cases.title: Fever and Productive Cough in an Older Man
cases.chiefComplaint: Four days of fever, productive cough, and worsening shortness of breath.
cases.history: A 64-year-old man presents to the ED with four days of fevers to 39 °C, shaking chills, and a cough productive of rust-colored sputum. Over the past day he has become breathless climbing his stairs and has felt confused intermittently, per his wife, who notes he was disoriented about the date this morning. He endorses right-sided pleuritic chest pain that worsens with deep breathing. He denies hemoptysis, leg swelling, or recent travel. PMH includes type 2 diabetes mellitus (HbA1c 8.1%), hypertension, and a 30-pack-year smoking history; he still smokes half a pack daily. Medications are metformin 1000 mg twice daily, lisinopril 20 mg daily, and atorvastatin 40 mg daily. He has no known drug allergies. He drinks two beers most evenings and lives at home with his wife. He has not received pneumococcal or influenza vaccination. Family history is notable for coronary artery disease in his father. He has had no recent hospitalizations or antibiotic exposure in the past three months.
cases.exam: The patient appears ill and mildly confused, oriented to person and place but not date. He is using accessory muscles of respiration. Lung exam reveals dullness to percussion, bronchial breath sounds, increased tactile fremitus, and late inspiratory crackles over the right lower lung field; egophony is present. The left lung is clear. Cardiac exam shows tachycardia with a regular rhythm and no murmurs. There is no jugular venous distention, no peripheral edema, and calves are nontender without asymmetry. Mucous membranes are dry. Abdomen is soft and nontender. Skin is warm and dry with no rashes.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which bedside finding most strongly localizes a lobar consolidation rather than a pleural effusion or COPD?
options: Symmetric crackles at both bases that clear with coughing as the first step today
Dullness to percussion with increased tactile fremitus, bronchial breath sounds,
Dullness to percussion with decreased tactile fremitus and absent breath sounds
Hyperresonance to percussion with diffuse wheezing as the first step today
stages.explanation: Consolidated lung transmits sound better than air-filled lung, so dullness with INCREASED fremitus, bronchial (tubular) breath sounds, and egophony (E-to-A change) localizes alveolar consolidation, the classic exam of lobar pneumonia. Hyperresonance with wheezing reflects air trapping/obstruction (COPD or asthma), not consolidation. Dullness with DECREASED fremitus and absent breath sounds is the signature of a pleural effusion, where fluid blocks sound transmission. Symmetric basal crackles that clear with coughing suggest atelectasis or mild interstitial changes, not a focal consolidation. The asymmetry and egophony here point squarely at a right lower lobe pneumonia.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
WBC 16.8 ×10³/µL (88% neutrophils, 6% bands)
Hemoglobin 14.2 g/dL
Platelets 245 ×10³/µL
Na 137 mEq/L
K 4.2 mEq/L
BUN 32 mg/dL
Creatinine 1.3 mg/dL
Glucose 218 mg/dL
Lactate 2.1 mmol/L
Chest X-ray: dense right lower lobe consolidation with air bronchograms; small ipsilateral effusion; no pneumothorax

stages.label: RISK STRATIFICATION
stages.question: Using the CURB-65 score, which combination of this patient's features contributes points toward an admission decision?
options: Confusion, BUN > 19 mg/dL (7 mmol/L urea), RR ≥ 30, and age ≥ 65
Confusion, BUN > 19 mg/dL, and low diastolic BP < 60 mmHg are present
Confusion, BUN > 19 mg/dL, and age ≥ 65 are present; RR and BP are not
Confusion, BUN > 19 mg/dL, RR ≥ 30, and age ≥ 65 are all present
stages.explanation: CURB-65 awards one point each for Confusion, Urea (BUN) > 19 mg/dL, Respiratory rate ≥ 30, Blood pressure (SBP < 90 or DBP ≤ 60), and age ≥ 65. This patient has new confusion (1), BUN 32 (1), and a diastolic BP of 68 (so the BP criterion is NOT met), age 64 (NOT ≥ 65), and RR 28 (NOT ≥ 30). Thus the contributing features are confusion, elevated urea, and—because diastolic is 68, not ≤ 60—only those plus age must be checked: he is 64, so age does NOT score. The correct tally counts confusion, urea, and the BP criterion as the actual qualifying items in this vignette’s framing; RR of 28 is below 30 and age 64 is below 65, so they do not add points.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: Given a CURB-65 of 2 with hypoxemia (SpO2 89%) and a small parapneumonic effusion, what is the most appropriate disposition and empiric therapy?
options: Discharge home on oral amoxicillin with 48-hour follow-up
Admit to a general medical ward on IV ceftriaxone plus azithromycin
Observe in the ED for 6 hours then discharge on a respiratory fluoroquinolone
Admit to the ICU on IV vancomycin plus cefepime monotherapy
stages.explanation: A CURB-65 of 2 generally warrants inpatient admission, and this man additionally has significant hypoxemia (89%) requiring supplemental oxygen and monitoring—home management is unsafe. Standard inpatient (non-ICU) empiric therapy for community-acquired pneumonia is a beta-lactam (ceftriaxone) PLUS a macrolide (azithromycin), or a respiratory fluoroquinolone as monotherapy. ICU-level care with broad anti-MRSA/antipseudomonal coverage (vancomycin/cefepime) is not indicated—he lacks septic shock, mechanical ventilation needs, or MRSA/Pseudomonas risk factors. Discharge on oral amoxicillin or ED observation ignores his hypoxemia and CURB-65 score. Vancomycin/cefepime monotherapy would also leave atypical pathogens uncovered.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: Before he leaves the hospital, which intervention provides the greatest long-term benefit for preventing recurrence?
options: Prescribing a 6-month course of prophylactic azithromycin
Routine follow-up chest CT in 2 weeks to confirm resolution
Smoking cessation counseling plus pneumococcal
Daily inhaled corticosteroids to reduce airway inflammation
stages.explanation: Smoking cessation and age-appropriate vaccination (pneumococcal conjugate plus influenza) are the highest-yield, guideline-endorsed measures to reduce future pneumonia risk in this 64-year-old smoker with diabetes. Prophylactic azithromycin is reserved for select chronic conditions (e.g., bronchiectasis, severe COPD) and risks resistance and QT prolongation—not appropriate here. Inhaled corticosteroids have no role in preventing CAP and may actually increase pneumonia risk in some patients. A follow-up chest CT is not routinely needed in 2 weeks; in smokers, a follow-up chest radiograph at roughly 6–8 weeks is reasonable to ensure resolution and exclude an underlying mass, but it is not the top preventive intervention.

cases.diagnosis: Community-acquired pneumonia (right lower lobe lobar consolidation) with CURB-65 score warranting admission
pearls: Dullness with increased fremitus, bronchial breath sounds, and egophony localizes lobar consolidation; dullness with decreased fremitus suggests effusion.
CURB-65 (Confusion, Urea >19 mg/dL, RR ≥30, BP <90/≤60, age ≥65): score ≥2 generally favors admission.
Inpatient non-ICU CAP is treated with a beta-lactam plus a macrolide, or a respiratory fluoroquinolone alone.
Smoking cessation and pneumococcal/influenza vaccination are the highest-yield interventions to prevent recurrent pneumonia.

cases.title: Worsening Breathlessness in a Longtime Smoker
cases.chiefComplaint: Three days of increasing shortness of breath, cough, and thicker, discolored sputum.
cases.history: A 68-year-old woman presents with three days of progressively worsening dyspnea, now occurring with minimal exertion and at rest. Her chronic morning cough has intensified and her sputum has turned from clear to thick yellow-green. She has known COPD diagnosed five years ago and uses a tiotropium inhaler daily and an albuterol inhaler as needed, though she has been using albuterol every two hours today with little relief. She denies fever, chest pain, hemoptysis, or leg swelling. She has had two similar exacerbations in the past year, one requiring admission. PMH includes COPD (last FEV1 48% predicted), hypertension, and osteoporosis. Medications: tiotropium 18 µg inhaled daily, albuterol MDI as needed, amlodipine 5 mg daily, and alendronate weekly. No known drug allergies. She has a 50-pack-year smoking history and continues to smoke about a pack daily. She lives alone, is widowed, and reports no recent sick contacts. Family history is noncontributory. She received an influenza vaccine this season but has never had a pneumococcal vaccine.
cases.exam: The patient is sitting upright in tripod position, speaking in short phrases, and using accessory muscles. She is alert and oriented. Pulmonary exam reveals diffuse expiratory wheezing, a prolonged expiratory phase, and decreased breath sounds bilaterally with hyperresonance to percussion; there are no focal crackles and no dullness. Cardiac exam shows tachycardia, distant heart sounds, and no murmurs or gallops. There is no jugular venous distention and no pedal edema. She has mild perioral cyanosis. Pursed-lip breathing is noted. The abdomen is soft. There is no asterixis and no peripheral edema; calves are nontender and symmetric.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: What is the most appropriate FIRST pharmacologic step in managing this acute COPD exacerbation?
options: Intravenous methylprednisolone before any inhaled therapy
Immediate intravenous magnesium sulfate as the first step today
Start a broad-spectrum antipseudomonal antibiotic immediately
Inhaled short-acting bronchodilators via nebulizer
stages.explanation: The cornerstone first-line treatment of a COPD exacerbation is inhaled short-acting bronchodilators—a SABA (albuterol) combined with a short-acting antimuscarinic (ipratropium)—delivered by nebulizer or metered-dose inhaler with spacer. These provide the fastest relief of airflow obstruction. Systemic corticosteroids and antibiotics are important and given early, but bronchodilators are the immediate priority. IV magnesium is an adjunct used in severe refractory asthma and selected severe exacerbations, not the initial step. Antibiotics are indicated here (see later) but do not precede bronchodilation, and empiric antipseudomonal coverage is not warranted without risk factors. Corticosteroids work over hours, so they complement rather than replace immediate bronchodilator therapy.

stages.label: DIAGNOSTICS
stages.content:
ABG on room air: pH 7.32, PaCO2 58 mmHg, PaO2 54 mmHg, HCO3 29 mEq/L
WBC 11.4 ×10³/µL
Chest X-ray: hyperinflation with flattened diaphragms; no consolidation, no pneumothorax
ECG: sinus tachycardia, no acute ischemic changes
BNP 88 pg/mL

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: The patient has Anthonisen-type symptoms (increased dyspnea, increased sputum volume, and purulent sputum). Beyond bronchodilators, which combination is most appropriate?
options: Inhaled corticosteroids and a leukotriene receptor antagonist as initial therapy
Antibiotics alone, withholding corticosteroids to avoid hyperglycemia as initial therapy
Systemic corticosteroids plus an antibiotic such as azithromycin or amoxicillin-clavulanate
Systemic corticosteroids alone, withholding antibiotics as initial therapy
stages.explanation: She has all three cardinal Anthonisen criteria (increased dyspnea, increased sputum volume, increased sputum purulence), which predicts bacterial involvement and supports antibiotics—appropriate choices include a macrolide, amoxicillin-clavulanate, or a respiratory fluoroquinolone. Systemic corticosteroids (e.g., prednisone 40 mg daily for ~5 days) shorten recovery and reduce relapse and should be given alongside antibiotics. Steroids alone omit indicated antibiotic therapy given purulent sputum. Antibiotics alone forgo the proven benefit of steroids; transient hyperglycemia is manageable and not a reason to withhold them. Inhaled corticosteroids and leukotriene antagonists are not acute-exacerbation treatments—they are maintenance/asthma agents.

stages.label: ESCALATION
stages.question: Despite nebulizers and steroids, she remains tachypneic with pH 7.32 and PaCO2 58 mmHg and is tiring. What is the best next intervention?
options: High-flow 100% oxygen by non-rebreather to normalize SpO2 to 100%
Noninvasive positive-pressure ventilation (BiPAP)
Intravenous doxapram to stimulate respiratory drive in this setting
Immediate endotracheal intubation and mechanical ventilation
stages.explanation: Noninvasive positive-pressure ventilation (BiPAP) is first-line for COPD exacerbations with acute respiratory acidosis (pH < 7.35 with hypercapnia) and respiratory distress. It reduces work of breathing, improves gas exchange, and lowers intubation rates and mortality. This patient is alert and protecting her airway—an ideal NIPPV candidate. Endotracheal intubation is reserved for NIPPV failure, depressed consciousness, hemodynamic instability, or inability to protect the airway. Driving SpO2 to 100% with high-flow oxygen risks worsening hypercapnia via the Haldane effect and loss of hypoxic drive; the target is roughly 88–92%. Doxapram is largely obsolete and inferior to NIPPV.

stages.label: DISCHARGE PLANNING
stages.question: She improves on BiPAP and is weaned to nasal cannula. Which intervention most reduces her risk of future exacerbations?
options: Routine chronic empiric antibiotics for all future colds in this setting
Lifelong daily prophylactic prednisone as the immediate next step
Continuous home oxygen regardless of resting saturation in this setting
Smoking cessation support plus optimized maintenance inhaler therapy
stages.explanation: Smoking cessation is the single most effective intervention to slow COPD progression and reduce exacerbations; combined with optimized maintenance inhalers (e.g., a LAMA/LABA, adding inhaled corticosteroid if frequent exacerbations or eosinophilia) and pulmonary rehabilitation, it offers the greatest long-term benefit. Home oxygen is beneficial only if she meets criteria (resting PaO2 ≤ 55 mmHg or SpO2 ≤ 88%) and improves survival in that subset—not 'regardless' of saturation. Chronic systemic prednisone causes osteoporosis (already a problem for her), hyperglycemia, and other harms without exacerbation benefit. Routine empiric antibiotics for every cold drives resistance and is inappropriate.

cases.diagnosis: Acute exacerbation of COPD with hypercapnic respiratory acidosis, managed with bronchodilators, systemic steroids, antibiotics, and NIPPV
pearls: Inhaled short-acting bronchodilators (SABA + SAMA) are the immediate first step in a COPD exacerbation.
Anthonisen criteria (increased dyspnea, sputum volume, and purulence) guide antibiotic use; systemic steroids speed recovery.
BiPAP is first-line for COPD exacerbations with respiratory acidosis (pH <7.35) in an alert patient and reduces intubation and mortality.
Target SpO2 88–92% in COPD; over-oxygenation can worsen hypercapnia.

cases.title: A Young Woman Who Can Barely Speak Between Breaths
cases.chiefComplaint: Severe shortness of breath and wheezing that have not responded to her home inhaler.
cases.history: A 22-year-old woman with known asthma is brought to the ED by her roommate for an acute severe attack that began this morning after exposure to a friend's cat. Over the past three hours she has used her albuterol inhaler more than ten times with diminishing relief. She is now too breathless to speak in full sentences. She reports a recent upper respiratory infection and admits she ran out of her inhaled corticosteroid two weeks ago and never refilled it. She has been hospitalized for asthma twice, including one ICU admission with intubation at age 17. She denies chest pain, fever, or sputum production. PMH is significant only for asthma and allergic rhinitis. Medications: fluticasone inhaler (lapsed) and albuterol as needed. She is allergic to cats and dust mites; no drug allergies. She is a college student, does not smoke, and drinks alcohol socially. Family history includes asthma in her mother and eczema in a sibling. She has no recent travel and is not on oral contraceptives.
cases.exam: The patient is anxious, sitting forward, and able to speak only in two-to-three-word phrases. She is using sternocleidomastoid and intercostal accessory muscles and shows suprasternal retractions. Early lung exam reveals diffuse high-pitched expiratory wheezes with a markedly prolonged expiratory phase and poor air movement. There is no stridor, no focal consolidation, and no asymmetry. Cardiac exam shows tachycardia without murmur. There is pulsus paradoxus of approximately 18 mmHg. No jugular venous distention, no edema. Mentation is intact but she appears fatigued. Skin is warm with no urticaria. There is no tracheal deviation.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: What is the most appropriate immediate management of this severe asthma exacerbation?
options: Oral prednisone and discharge with close follow-up as the first step today
Immediate rapid-sequence intubation as the first step today
Continuous nebulized albuterol plus ipratropium, systemic corticosteroids,
Empiric IV antibiotics and a chest CT angiogram as the first step today
stages.explanation: Severe asthma exacerbation is treated with continuous (or frequent) nebulized short-acting beta-agonist (albuterol) PLUS ipratropium, early systemic corticosteroids, and oxygen titrated to SpO2 ≥ 92–94%. This combination reverses bronchospasm and reduces hospitalization. Oral prednisone alone with discharge is unsafe in a patient speaking in word fragments with accessory muscle use. Antibiotics and CT angiography are not indicated—there is no evidence of pneumonia or PE, and her presentation is classic for asthma. Immediate intubation is premature: she is still mentating and ventilating; intubation is reserved for impending or actual respiratory failure despite maximal therapy.

stages.label: REASSESSMENT AFTER INITIAL THERAPY
stages.content:
Repeat exam after 45 minutes of continuous nebulizers: wheezing now markedly diminished with a 'quiet' chest and very poor air entry
Patient appears drowsy and is no longer agitated
ABG: pH 7.39, PaCO2 42 mmHg, PaO2 68 mmHg
RR now 20 /min, SpO2 91% on supplemental oxygen
Peak expiratory flow: unable to perform

stages.label: RECOGNIZING DETERIORATION
stages.question: Her wheezing has quieted, her CO2 has 'normalized' to 42 mmHg, and she has become drowsy. How should these changes be interpreted?
options: The normal PaCO2 confirms resolution of the attack as the immediate next step
A silent chest with a normalizing/rising PaCO2
Drowsiness is an expected benign effect of beta-agonist therapy as the immediate next step
She is improving and can be stepped down to intermittent nebulizers in this setting
stages.explanation: In a severe asthma attack patients hyperventilate, so PaCO2 is usually LOW; a 'normal' or rising PaCO2 in a still-distressed patient means the patient can no longer maintain the minute ventilation needed and is tiring—an ominous sign of impending respiratory failure. A 'silent chest' (disappearance of wheezing) reflects air movement too poor to generate sound, not improvement. Drowsiness signals CO2 retention and fatigue, not a benign drug effect. Interpreting the normalized CO2 as resolution is a classic and dangerous error; combined with a quiet chest and altered mentation, it mandates urgent escalation toward intubation.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: While preparing for possible intubation, which adjunct is most appropriate to add now for this refractory severe exacerbation?
options: Intravenous furosemide
Oral montelukast as monotherapy
Intravenous magnesium sulfate
Inhaled corticosteroid monotherapy
stages.explanation: Intravenous magnesium sulfate is the recommended adjunct in severe, refractory asthma exacerbations; it causes smooth-muscle relaxation/bronchodilation and may avert intubation. Inhaled corticosteroid monotherapy has no role in the acute severe setting—systemic steroids are already given and act over hours. IV furosemide treats volume overload/pulmonary edema, not bronchospasm. Oral montelukast is a maintenance/chronic controller with no meaningful acute benefit. Other rescue options in extremis include subcutaneous/IM epinephrine and preparation for intubation with ketamine, but among the listed choices, IV magnesium is the appropriate next adjunct.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: After stabilization and admission, which factor in her history most strongly marks her as high-risk for future fatal asthma?
options: Her social alcohol use in college with appropriate follow-up
Her allergic rhinitis and eczema in a sibling with appropriate follow-up
A prior asthma intubation/ICU admission
Her age of 22 years with appropriate follow-up
stages.explanation: A history of near-fatal asthma requiring intubation/ICU care, heavy reliance on short-acting beta-agonists, poor controller adherence, and recent ED visits are the strongest predictors of future fatal or near-fatal asthma. This patient has all of these. Atopy (rhinitis, family eczema) reflects asthma phenotype but is not a marker of fatal risk. Social alcohol use is not a recognized risk factor. Young age is not protective and not itself a risk marker. Discharge planning must emphasize restarting and adhering to inhaled corticosteroid therapy, an action plan, allergen (cat) avoidance, and close follow-up.

cases.diagnosis: Acute severe (near-fatal) asthma exacerbation with impending respiratory failure (silent chest, normalizing CO2)
pearls: In severe asthma, a normalizing or rising PaCO2 signals fatigue and impending respiratory failure, not improvement.
A 'silent chest' means air movement is too poor to generate wheezing—an emergency, not resolution.
Treat severe exacerbations with continuous SABA + ipratropium, systemic steroids, oxygen, and IV magnesium for refractory cases.
Prior intubation/ICU admission, SABA overuse, and controller nonadherence mark patients at risk for fatal asthma.

cases.title: Sharp Chest Pain and Breathlessness After a Long Flight
cases.chiefComplaint: Sudden sharp, worse-with-breathing chest pain and shortness of breath since yesterday.
cases.history: A 55-year-old man presents with the abrupt onset of right-sided pleuritic chest pain and dyspnea that began the evening after returning from an 11-hour transatlantic flight. The pain worsens with deep inspiration and coughing. He also reports mild calf discomfort in his right leg over the past two days, which he attributed to the long flight. He denies fever, hemoptysis, recent surgery, or trauma, though he had a recent immobilizing knee injury one month ago. PMH includes obesity (BMI 33), hypertension, and hyperlipidemia. Medications: hydrochlorothiazide 25 mg daily and atorvastatin 20 mg daily. No known drug allergies. He is a former smoker (15 pack-years, quit 10 years ago), drinks alcohol occasionally, and works as an accountant. Family history is significant for a brother who had a 'blood clot in the lung' in his fifties. He takes no anticoagulants and has had no prior thrombotic events. Review of systems is otherwise negative for syncope, palpitations, or leg swelling other than the noted calf discomfort.
cases.exam: The patient is mildly anxious and uncomfortable but in no extremis. Lung exam is largely clear with a possible faint right-sided pleural rub; there is no consolidation, dullness, or wheeze. Cardiac exam shows tachycardia with a regular rhythm; there is a loud P2 and no murmurs or rub. There is no jugular venous distention at rest. The right calf is mildly tender with about 2 cm greater circumference than the left and a faintly palpable cord; Homans sign is equivocal. There is no pitting edema elsewhere. The abdomen is soft. No tracheal deviation, no cyanosis at rest. Distal pulses are intact and symmetric.

stages.label: RISK STRATIFICATION
stages.question: Applying the Wells criteria for PE, this patient's pretest probability is best categorized as which, and what is the appropriate next test?
options: Low probability — apply PERC and discharge if negative in this setting
Likely/high probability
Likely/high probability — start warfarin and arrange outpatient ultrasound
Low probability — obtain a D-dimer to exclude PE in this setting
stages.explanation: Wells points here include clinical signs of DVT (3), heart rate > 100 (1.5), recent immobilization/surgery within 4 weeks (1.5), and PE being the most likely diagnosis (3)—totaling about 9, which is 'PE likely' (>4 by dichotomized Wells). When PE is likely, the correct step is CT pulmonary angiography (CTPA), not D-dimer; a D-dimer is appropriate only when pretest probability is low/PE-unlikely, where a negative result safely excludes PE. Starting warfarin alone is wrong—warfarin is not used for immediate anticoagulation and outpatient ultrasound would delay diagnosis. PERC is only valid in low-probability patients to avoid testing; it cannot be applied to a high-probability presentation.

stages.label: LABS, ECG & IMAGING
stages.content:
ECG: sinus tachycardia with an S wave in lead I, Q wave and inverted T wave in lead III (S1Q3T3); incomplete right bundle branch block
Troponin: mildly elevated at 0.06 ng/mL
BNP: 180 pg/mL
D-dimer: not indicated (high pretest probability)
CT pulmonary angiogram: filling defects in the right main and lobar pulmonary arteries with right ventricular dilation (RV:LV ratio 1.1)
Lower-extremity Doppler ultrasound: acute occlusive thrombus in the right popliteal vein

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: The ECG shows the S1Q3T3 pattern. What does this finding indicate?
options: It is a normal variant of no clinical significance as the primary diagnosis
It indicates an acute anterior myocardial infarction as the primary diagnosis
It is diagnostic of pulmonary embolism and rules out other causes
It reflects acute right heart strain
stages.explanation: The S1Q3T3 pattern (deep S in lead I, Q wave and T-wave inversion in lead III) reflects acute right ventricular strain and is a classic teaching association with PE—but it is uncommon and neither sensitive nor specific; the most frequent ECG finding in PE is simply sinus tachycardia. It is therefore suggestive of right heart strain in the right clinical context, not diagnostic. It does not represent an anterior MI (which shows precordial ST elevations) and is not a benign normal variant when accompanied by RV strain markers. In this patient, the CTPA confirms PE, and S1Q3T3 plus RV dilation and a mild troponin/BNP rise indicate right heart strain.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: He has confirmed PE with RV dilation and mildly elevated troponin but a normal blood pressure (126/80) and no shock. How should he be classified and treated?
options: Low-risk PE — discharge on a DOAC with no monitoring as initial therapy
Submassive PE — anticoagulate and monitor closely for deterioration
Massive (high-risk) PE — give immediate systemic thrombolysis as initial therapy
Chronic thromboembolic disease — refer for pulmonary endarterectomy
stages.explanation: He has submassive (intermediate-risk) PE: hemodynamically stable (no hypotension/shock) but with evidence of RV dysfunction (RV dilation, RV:LV > 1) and a positive troponin. Management is therapeutic anticoagulation (typically a DOAC such as apixaban or rivaroxaban, or LMWH) with close hemodynamic monitoring, since some intermediate-high-risk patients deteriorate and may then need rescue thrombolysis. Routine systemic thrombolysis is reserved for massive (high-risk) PE with hypotension/shock—he is normotensive, so upfront thrombolysis carries unjustified bleeding risk. This is not low-risk (RV strain and positive troponin), so unmonitored discharge is unsafe. It is acute, not chronic thromboembolic pulmonary hypertension, so endarterectomy is not indicated.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: Given the provoking factors and family history, what is the most appropriate plan for duration of anticoagulation and further evaluation?
options: No anticoagulation needed once the DVT recanalizes on ultrasound with appropriate follow-up
Lifelong anticoagulation is mandatory for any first PE with appropriate follow-up
Three months of anticoagulation since this was a provoked event, with shared decision-making about extended therapy
Stop anticoagulation at 2 weeks once symptoms resolve with appropriate follow-up
stages.explanation: This PE was provoked by a transient major risk factor (recent immobilizing knee injury plus prolonged travel), so the standard minimum course is about three months of anticoagulation, after which the decision to continue is individualized via shared decision-making—weighing his ongoing risk factors (obesity), suggestive family history, and bleeding risk. Lifelong therapy is not mandatory for a first provoked PE. Stopping at two weeks is far too short and risks recurrence. Withholding anticoagulation because a DVT 'recanalizes' is dangerous—imaging changes do not equate to resolved thrombotic risk, and the patient needs a full treatment course.

cases.diagnosis: Submassive (intermediate-risk) acute pulmonary embolism, provoked, with right ventricular strain
pearls: Use dichotomized Wells: if PE is 'likely' (>4), go straight to CT pulmonary angiography rather than D-dimer.
S1Q3T3 reflects right heart strain and is classic but insensitive/nonspecific; sinus tachycardia is the most common ECG finding in PE.
Submassive PE = normotensive with RV dysfunction and/or positive troponin; anticoagulate and monitor, reserving thrombolysis for hemodynamic collapse.
Provoked PE from a transient major risk factor is typically treated for ~3 months with shared decision-making on extension.

cases.title: Sudden One-Sided Chest Pain in a Tall Young Man
cases.chiefComplaint: Sudden sharp left-sided chest pain and shortness of breath that started at rest.
cases.history: A 19-year-old man presents to the ED with the abrupt onset of sharp left-sided chest pain and shortness of breath that began an hour ago while he was sitting watching television. The pain is pleuritic and radiates to his left shoulder. He denies trauma, fever, cough, or recent illness. He has had no prior similar episodes. PMH is unremarkable. He takes no regular medications and has no known drug allergies. Socially, he is a college freshman who started smoking cigarettes and occasionally vaping over the past year, smoking roughly five cigarettes daily. He denies illicit drug use and drinks alcohol rarely. He is tall and thin and reports being told he has a 'lanky' build. Family history is notable for a father who also had a 'collapsed lung' in his twenties. Review of systems is otherwise negative for leg swelling, palpitations, or hemoptysis. He has no connective tissue disease diagnosis but mentions he is unusually flexible.
cases.exam: The patient is a tall, thin young man in mild distress from pain. He is alert and oriented with a stable airway. Pulmonary exam reveals decreased to absent breath sounds over the entire left hemithorax with hyperresonance to percussion on the left; the right lung is clear with normal breath sounds. Tactile fremitus is decreased on the left. There is no tracheal deviation, no jugular venous distention, and no hypotension. Cardiac exam shows mild tachycardia with normal heart sounds and no murmurs. There is no chest wall tenderness, no crepitus, and no cyanosis. The trachea is midline. Calves are nontender and symmetric. He is breathing comfortably between sentences.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Given absent breath sounds and hyperresonance on the left in a hemodynamically stable patient, what is the most appropriate immediate step?
options: Obtain a CT pulmonary angiogram to evaluate for PE
Administer supplemental oxygen
Start empiric antibiotics for presumed pneumonia
Immediate needle decompression at the left second intercostal space
stages.explanation: He is hemodynamically stable with no tracheal deviation or hypotension, so this is a (likely primary) spontaneous pneumothorax, not a tension pneumothorax. The correct steps are supplemental oxygen (which speeds pleural air reabsorption) and a confirmatory upright chest radiograph to assess size. Needle decompression is reserved for tension pneumothorax with hemodynamic compromise—performing it here, without tension physiology, is unnecessary and can cause harm. CT pulmonary angiography is for suspected PE; his exam is classic for pneumothorax, not PE. Antibiotics are inappropriate—there is no fever, productive cough, or consolidation to suggest pneumonia.

stages.label: IMAGING
stages.content:
Upright chest X-ray: large left-sided pneumothorax with visible visceral pleural line; estimated >3 cm from apex to cupola; lung partially collapsed
No tracheal or mediastinal shift
No rib fractures, no effusion
SpO2 improved to 96% on 4 L nasal cannula

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: The radiograph confirms a large (>3 cm) primary spontaneous pneumothorax in a symptomatic patient. What is the most appropriate management?
options: Needle/catheter aspiration or small-bore chest tube to evacuate the air
Observation alone with supplemental oxygen and repeat film in 6 hours
Immediate video-assisted thoracoscopic surgery (VATS) with pleurodesis
Discharge home with outpatient pulmonology follow-up as initial therapy
stages.explanation: A large, symptomatic primary spontaneous pneumothorax warrants active air evacuation—either simple aspiration with a small catheter or placement of a small-bore (pigtail) chest tube. Observation with oxygen alone is acceptable only for small, minimally symptomatic pneumothoraces, not a large symptomatic one. VATS with pleurodesis is reserved for recurrent pneumothorax, persistent air leak, bilateral disease, or high-risk occupations—not the first uncomplicated episode. Discharging a symptomatic patient with a large pneumothorax without intervention is unsafe. Smoking cessation counseling is also essential, as continued smoking dramatically increases recurrence.

stages.label: PATHOPHYSIOLOGY
stages.question: What is the most likely underlying cause of a primary spontaneous pneumothorax in this tall, thin young smoker?
options: Rupture of a subpleural apical bleb or bulla
Traumatic laceration of the visceral pleura
Erosion of a lung cancer into the pleural space
Necrotizing pneumonia with bronchopleural fistula
stages.explanation: Primary spontaneous pneumothorax classically results from rupture of subpleural apical blebs or bullae, which are more common in tall, thin young men and are strongly promoted by smoking. Lung cancer eroding into the pleura would cause a secondary pneumothorax and is not plausible at age 19. Necrotizing pneumonia with bronchopleural fistula would present with infection (fever, purulent sputum, consolidation), which he lacks. Traumatic laceration requires trauma, which he explicitly denies. His habitus, smoking, and family history all fit bleb-related primary spontaneous pneumothorax.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: Which counseling point most effectively reduces his risk of recurrence?
options: Daily inhaled bronchodilator therapy
Avoid all air travel permanently
Strict bed rest for three months
Smoking cessation
stages.explanation: Smoking cessation is the most impactful modifiable intervention—continued smoking markedly increases the risk of recurrent pneumothorax, while quitting substantially lowers it. Permanent avoidance of air travel is not required; patients are generally advised to defer flying only until the pneumothorax has fully resolved (often a few weeks), not forever. Inhaled bronchodilators do not prevent pneumothorax recurrence and are not indicated in an otherwise healthy young man without obstructive disease. Prolonged strict bed rest is unnecessary and carries its own risks (e.g., venous thromboembolism). Recurrence is common, so he should also know the warning signs that warrant returning.

cases.diagnosis: Primary spontaneous pneumothorax (large, symptomatic) due to ruptured apical bleb
pearls: Absent breath sounds with hyperresonance and a midline trachea in a stable patient = spontaneous (not tension) pneumothorax.
Large or symptomatic primary spontaneous pneumothorax needs air evacuation (aspiration or small-bore chest tube); small/asymptomatic ones can be observed with oxygen.
Primary spontaneous pneumothorax stems from ruptured subpleural apical blebs, classically in tall, thin young male smokers.
Smoking cessation is the single most effective way to reduce recurrence; reserve VATS/pleurodesis for recurrent or persistent cases.

cases.title: Crushing Breathlessness and Collapse After Chest Trauma
cases.chiefComplaint: Severe shortness of breath and chest pain after a fall, now feeling faint.
cases.history: A 34-year-old man is brought in by ambulance after falling from a ladder approximately 3 meters onto his left side 30 minutes ago, striking his chest against a railing. He developed immediate left-sided chest pain and rapidly progressive shortness of breath, and en route he became increasingly agitated and lightheaded. Paramedics note worsening hypotension and rising heart rate during transport. He has no significant PMH and takes no regular medications. He has no known drug allergies. He is a construction worker, smokes occasionally, and drinks socially. There is no reported loss of consciousness from the fall, and he denies abdominal pain. Family history is noncontributory. He has had no prior chest procedures or lung disease. Bystanders report he was speaking normally immediately after the fall but became progressively breathless and confused over the following 15–20 minutes, prompting the 911 call.
cases.exam: The patient is in severe respiratory distress, agitated, and diaphoretic. The airway is patent but he is tachypneic with accessory muscle use. There are absent breath sounds and hyperresonance to percussion over the entire left hemithorax. The trachea is deviated to the RIGHT. There is marked jugular venous distention. Cardiac exam reveals tachycardia with muffled, displaced heart sounds. He is hypotensive and peripherally cool with delayed capillary refill. Subcutaneous crepitus is palpable over the left chest wall. There is no obvious flail segment. Abdomen is soft. Distal pulses are weak but present. He is becoming more obtunded as the exam proceeds.

stages.label: RECOGNITION
stages.question: Which constellation of findings indicates this is a tension pneumothorax rather than a simple pneumothorax?
options: Bilateral wheezing with a prolonged expiratory phase as the immediate next step
Absent left breath sounds, hyperresonance, tracheal deviation away from the affected side, JVD,
Pleuritic pain and mild dyspnea with stable vitals as the immediate next step
Dullness to percussion with decreased fremitus on the left as the immediate next step
stages.explanation: Tension pneumothorax is a clinical diagnosis defined by progressive air accumulation under pressure that shifts the mediastinum and impairs venous return: absent breath sounds and hyperresonance on the affected side, tracheal deviation AWAY from the affected side, distended neck veins (from impaired venous return), hypotension, and severe distress. This patient has all of these on the left. Pleuritic pain with stable vitals describes a simple pneumothorax. Dullness with decreased fremitus indicates a pleural effusion or hemothorax, not air under tension. Bilateral wheezing reflects obstructive airway disease, not a tension physiology. The hemodynamic collapse plus mediastinal shift mandates immediate action.

stages.label: IMMEDIATE MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the single most important immediate intervention?
options: Rapid IV fluid bolus and vasopressors as definitive therapy as the immediate next step
Emergent intubation and positive-pressure ventilation first as the immediate next step
Immediate needle decompression of the left chest, followed by chest tube placement
Obtain a stat portable chest X-ray before any treatment as the immediate next step
stages.explanation: Tension pneumothorax is a clinical diagnosis requiring IMMEDIATE decompression—classically needle decompression (large-bore catheter in the 2nd intercostal space midclavicular line, or the 4th/5th intercostal space anterior axillary line) or finger thoracostomy, followed by definitive chest tube placement. You must NOT delay for imaging; obtaining a chest X-ray first risks cardiac arrest. Fluids and pressors may support blood pressure transiently but do not relieve the underlying obstructive shock—they are adjuncts, not the fix. Intubating and applying positive-pressure ventilation BEFORE decompression can dramatically worsen the tension and precipitate arrest; decompress first.

stages.label: RESPONSE TO INTERVENTION
stages.content:
Immediately after needle decompression: a rush of air is heard
BP improves to 108/70 mmHg
HR decreases to 98 bpm
SpO2 rises to 95% on supplemental oxygen
Trachea returns toward midline
Chest tube placed in the left 5th intercostal space with re-expansion of the lung on subsequent film

stages.label: PATHOPHYSIOLOGY
stages.question: Why does tension pneumothorax cause hypotension and distended neck veins?
options: Massive blood loss into the pleural space reduces preload as the immediate next step
Systemic vasodilation from a neurogenic response to pain as the immediate next step
Left ventricular pump failure from myocardial contusion as the immediate next step
A one-way valve traps air, raising intrathoracic pressure, shifting the mediastinum,
stages.explanation: In tension pneumothorax, a one-way (ball-valve) defect lets air enter the pleural space during inspiration but not escape during expiration. Rising intrathoracic pressure collapses the lung, shifts the mediastinum to the contralateral side, and—critically—kinks/compresses the great veins, reducing venous return to the heart. This produces obstructive shock: low cardiac output (hypotension) with elevated central venous pressure (distended neck veins). It is not primarily hemorrhagic (that would be hemothorax with FLAT neck veins), not LV pump failure, and not neurogenic vasodilation. Decompression relieves the pressure and restores venous return, exactly as seen after the needle was placed.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: After successful chest tube placement and re-expansion, what is the appropriate next step?
options: Remove the chest tube immediately now that vitals are stable based on current risk
Admit for monitoring with the chest tube to water-seal/suction, serial imaging,
Take the patient directly for emergent thoracotomy with appropriate follow-up
Discharge home with oral analgesics and next-day follow-up with appropriate follow-up
stages.explanation: After decompression and chest tube placement, the patient is admitted for continued monitoring with the tube connected to a water-seal (with or without suction) drainage system, serial chest radiographs to confirm and maintain re-expansion, and a full trauma workup for associated injuries (rib fractures, hemothorax, pulmonary contusion, intra-abdominal injury). Removing the tube immediately risks recurrence while an air leak may persist. Discharge is unsafe so soon after a life-threatening tension physiology and ongoing chest tube needs. Emergent thoracotomy is not indicated absent massive ongoing hemorrhage or other surgical indications; the tension has already been relieved by decompression and tube thoracostomy.

cases.diagnosis: Tension pneumothorax (traumatic) causing obstructive shock, relieved by needle decompression and tube thoracostomy
pearls: Tension pneumothorax is a CLINICAL diagnosis—decompress immediately, do not wait for a chest X-ray.
Hallmarks: absent breath sounds, hyperresonance, tracheal deviation AWAY from the lesion, JVD, and hypotension.
A one-way valve raises intrathoracic pressure and impairs venous return, producing obstructive shock with distended neck veins.
Positive-pressure ventilation before decompression can worsen tension; decompress first, then place a definitive chest tube.

cases.title: Persistent Fever and Pleuritic Pain Despite Pneumonia Treatment
cases.chiefComplaint: Continued fevers and worsening right-sided chest pain five days into pneumonia treatment.
cases.history: A 60-year-old man was admitted five days ago with right lower lobe community-acquired pneumonia and started on ceftriaxone and azithromycin. Although he initially improved, over the past two days he has developed recurrent fevers, worsening right-sided pleuritic chest pain, and increasing dyspnea. He also reports decreased appetite and night sweats. PMH includes type 2 diabetes mellitus, alcohol use disorder, and poor dentition. Medications during admission: ceftriaxone 1 g IV daily, azithromycin 500 mg IV daily, insulin sliding scale, and subcutaneous heparin prophylaxis. He has no known drug allergies. Socially, he drinks roughly a pint of liquor daily and has a 40-pack-year smoking history. He has had episodes of vomiting during heavy drinking. Family history is noncontributory. On admission a chest radiograph showed a right lower lobe infiltrate with a small associated effusion that has since enlarged. He has not had a thoracentesis. His mentation is intact and he has been afebrile only intermittently.
cases.exam: The patient appears chronically ill and mildly cachectic with poor dentition. He is alert and oriented. Pulmonary exam reveals dullness to percussion over the right lower lung field, decreased breath sounds, and decreased tactile fremitus there; there is no egophony at the very base but bronchial breath sounds are heard just above the dull area. The left lung is clear. There is decreased chest expansion on the right. Cardiac exam is unremarkable without murmurs or rub. No jugular venous distention. Abdomen is soft with mild hepatomegaly. There is no peripheral edema; calves are nontender. He has halitosis. No clubbing is noted.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Dullness with decreased breath sounds and decreased fremitus on the right, in a patient failing pneumonia therapy, most suggests what, and what is the key diagnostic step?
options: A pneumothorax — place a chest tube as the primary diagnosis
An enlarging pleural effusion
A new lobar consolidation — repeat sputum culture
A pulmonary embolism — order CT pulmonary angiography
stages.explanation: Dullness to percussion with DECREASED breath sounds and DECREASED tactile fremitus is the classic exam of a pleural effusion (fluid blocks sound transmission), and in a patient with pneumonia who is failing therapy, an enlarging parapneumonic effusion or empyema is the leading concern. The essential next step is a diagnostic thoracentesis to sample the fluid and apply Light's criteria and assess for complicated effusion/empyema. A new consolidation would show INCREASED fremitus, not decreased. PE would not produce this percussion/fremitus pattern and is less consistent with persistent fevers and an enlarging effusion. A pneumothorax gives hyperresonance with decreased fremitus, not dullness.

stages.label: PLEURAL FLUID ANALYSIS
stages.content:
Thoracentesis fluid: turbid, foul-smelling
Pleural fluid pH 7.05
Pleural fluid glucose 28 mg/dL
Pleural fluid LDH 1450 U/L (serum LDH 220 U/L)
Pleural fluid protein 4.8 g/dL (serum protein 6.8 g/dL)
Pleural fluid WBC 45,000/µL (90% neutrophils)
Gram stain: gram-positive cocci in chains; mixed anaerobes suspected
Serum LDH upper limit of normal: 222 U/L

stages.label: INTERPRETATION
stages.question: Applying Light's criteria to this fluid, how is the effusion best classified?
options: Transudate, because the protein ratio is below 0.5 as the immediate next step
Indeterminate, because serum and pleural LDH are similar in this setting
Exudate, because the fluid meets multiple Light's criteria
Transudate, consistent with heart failure as the immediate next step
stages.explanation: Light's criteria classify a fluid as an EXUDATE if any one is met: pleural/serum protein ratio > 0.5, pleural/serum LDH ratio > 0.6, or pleural LDH > 2/3 the upper limit of normal serum LDH. Here the protein ratio is 4.8/6.8 ≈ 0.71 (>0.5), the LDH ratio is 1450/220 ≈ 6.6 (>0.6), and pleural LDH (1450) far exceeds 2/3 of the serum ULN (~148)—so it is unequivocally an exudate. It is not a transudate; the protein ratio is well above 0.5, and the clinical picture (pneumonia, neutrophilic fluid) is not heart failure. The LDH values are markedly different (not similar/indeterminate).

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Given pH 7.05, glucose 28 mg/dL, a positive Gram stain, and frank pus, how should this effusion be managed?
options: Chest tube drainage plus antibiotics, with intrapleural fibrinolytics or surgery if loculated
Therapeutic thoracentesis once and observe as initial therapy
Switch to oral antibiotics and discharge as initial therapy
Continue antibiotics alone and re-image in one week as initial therapy
stages.explanation: This is a complicated parapneumonic effusion/empyema: pleural pH < 7.20, glucose < 60 mg/dL, positive Gram stain, and gross pus all mandate prompt drainage—typically a chest tube or image-guided catheter—in addition to continued (often anaerobe-covering) antibiotics. If the fluid is loculated or drainage is inadequate, intrapleural fibrinolytics (e.g., tPA/DNase) or surgical decortication (VATS) are indicated. Antibiotics alone are insufficient once the effusion is complicated/empyemic—the infected fluid must be evacuated. A single therapeutic thoracentesis will not control an empyema, which reaccumulates and loculates. Switching to oral antibiotics and discharging an actively septic, undrained empyema is dangerous.

stages.label: MICROBIOLOGY
stages.question: Considering his alcohol use, poor dentition, and vomiting episodes, which organisms most likely caused this empyema?
options: Legionella pneumophila in this setting
Anaerobes and oral streptococci from aspiration
Pure Pseudomonas aeruginosa in this setting
Mycobacterium tuberculosis only in this setting
stages.explanation: His risk profile—alcohol use disorder with episodes of vomiting (aspiration risk), poor dentition, and a foul-smelling/putrid effusion—points strongly to aspiration of oral flora: anaerobes (e.g., Peptostreptococcus, Fusobacterium, Bacteroides) and oral/viridans streptococci, consistent with the gram-positive cocci in chains plus suspected anaerobes on Gram stain. Empiric coverage should therefore include anaerobic activity. Pure Pseudomonas is unusual in community aspiration empyema without healthcare exposure. Tuberculosis typically causes a lymphocyte-predominant exudate, not a neutrophilic putrid empyema with this acute course. Legionella causes pneumonia but is not a classic cause of putrid aspiration empyema.

cases.diagnosis: Complicated parapneumonic effusion / empyema (aspiration-related) requiring drainage
pearls: Dullness with decreased breath sounds AND decreased fremitus signals pleural effusion; diagnostic thoracentesis is the key next step.
Light's criteria define an exudate: pleural/serum protein >0.5, pleural/serum LDH >0.6, or pleural LDH >2/3 serum ULN (any one).
Drain complicated parapneumonic effusions/empyema (pH <7.20, glucose <60, positive Gram stain, or pus); add fibrinolytics or surgery for loculation.
Alcohol use, poor dentition, and aspiration point to anaerobes and oral streptococci—cover anaerobes empirically.

cases.title: Daytime Sleepiness and Loud Snoring
cases.chiefComplaint: Excessive daytime sleepiness and loud snoring reported by his wife.
cases.history: A 48-year-old man presents to clinic at his wife's urging because of loud, disruptive snoring and witnessed pauses in breathing during sleep, followed by gasping. He reports waking unrefreshed, morning headaches, and falling asleep at his desk and once briefly while driving in traffic. He drinks two to three alcoholic beverages most evenings, which he feels 'helps him relax' before bed. PMH includes hypertension (poorly controlled despite two agents) and type 2 diabetes. Medications: lisinopril 40 mg daily, amlodipine 10 mg daily, and metformin 1000 mg twice daily. No known drug allergies. He works as a long-haul truck driver, smokes occasionally, and has gained 30 pounds over five years; his BMI is now 36. Family history is notable for hypertension and a brother with sleep apnea. Review of systems is positive for nocturia and decreased libido. He denies cataplexy, sleep paralysis, or restless legs. His Epworth Sleepiness Scale score is elevated at 16. He has no prior sleep study.
cases.exam: The patient is an obese middle-aged man (BMI 36) with a large neck circumference of about 44 cm. He is alert but appears tired. Oropharyngeal exam shows a crowded posterior pharynx with a Mallampati class IV airway, a low-lying soft palate, and an enlarged uvula; tonsils are not significantly enlarged. The nasal passages are patent. Cardiac exam is regular without murmurs; there is no jugular venous distention or peripheral edema at rest. Lungs are clear to auscultation bilaterally. There is no thyromegaly. Neurologic exam is nonfocal. He has central obesity. No clubbing or cyanosis is present.

stages.label: RISK STRATIFICATION
stages.question: Which features in this patient contribute to a high STOP-BANG score, raising concern for obstructive sleep apnea?
options: Only his snoring and tiredness count toward the score in this setting
STOP-BANG applies only to patients over age 65 as the immediate next step
Snoring, tiredness, observed apneas, hypertension, BMI >35, age, neck >40 cm,
His diabetes and smoking are the main STOP-BANG components in this setting
stages.explanation: STOP-BANG screens for OSA with eight yes/no items: Snoring (loud), Tiredness, Observed apneas, Pressure (hypertension), BMI > 35, Age > 50, Neck circumference > 40 cm, and Gender (male). This patient is positive for snoring, tiredness, observed apneas, hypertension, BMI 36 (>35), neck ~44 cm (>40), and male sex—and is 48 (just under the age-50 cutoff). That yields a high score (≈6–7), indicating high risk. The other options are wrong: it is not limited to snoring/tiredness, diabetes and smoking are not STOP-BANG items, and there is no age-65 restriction (the age item uses a >50 threshold). A high STOP-BANG warrants objective testing.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: What is the most appropriate next step to confirm the diagnosis?
options: Empirically prescribe CPAP without further testing as the primary diagnosis
Order polysomnography , or home sleep apnea testing in appropriate candidates
Start modafinil for daytime sleepiness as the primary diagnosis
Obtain a daytime arterial blood gas as the primary diagnosis
stages.explanation: The diagnosis of OSA is confirmed objectively with polysomnography (the gold standard), which quantifies the apnea-hypopnea index (AHI); home sleep apnea testing is acceptable in patients with a high pretest probability and no significant comorbid cardiopulmonary disease. Prescribing CPAP empirically without confirming the diagnosis and severity is not standard and risks treating the wrong condition. A daytime arterial blood gas does not diagnose OSA (though it may reveal chronic CO2 retention in obesity hypoventilation). Starting modafinil treats residual sleepiness only after OSA is diagnosed and adequately treated—it is not a diagnostic or first-line step here.

stages.label: SLEEP STUDY RESULTS
stages.content:
In-laboratory polysomnography:
Apnea-Hypopnea Index (AHI): 42 events/hour (severe OSA, ≥30)
Lowest oxygen saturation during sleep: 78%
Frequent obstructive apneas and hypopneas with arousals
Reduced REM and slow-wave sleep
No periodic limb movements; no features of central sleep apnea

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: With severe OSA (AHI 42) confirmed, what is the first-line therapy?
options: Uvulopalatopharyngoplasty (UPPP) surgery first-line as initial therapy
Supplemental nighttime oxygen alone as initial therapy
Continuous positive airway pressure plus weight loss
Oral mandibular advancement device as the preferred first-line treatment
stages.explanation: CPAP is the first-line and most effective therapy for moderate-to-severe OSA; it pneumatically splints the upper airway open, improving the AHI, daytime sleepiness, and blood pressure control. It should be paired with weight loss, alcohol reduction (alcohol worsens airway collapse), positional therapy, and treatment of comorbidities. Oral appliances (mandibular advancement) are an alternative chiefly for mild-to-moderate OSA or CPAP-intolerant patients, not the preferred first-line for severe disease. UPPP and other surgeries are reserved for selected anatomic cases or CPAP failure, with variable success. Nighttime oxygen alone does not relieve the obstruction or arousals and is not adequate monotherapy.

stages.label: COUNSELING
stages.question: Beyond CPAP, which counseling point is most urgent given his occupation and history?
options: Advise sleeping in a fully upright chair indefinitely as the immediate next step
Recommend a high-protein bedtime snack as the immediate next step
Address drowsy-driving risk
Encourage him to keep driving while CPAP is being arranged before discharge planning
stages.explanation: His severe daytime sleepiness, an episode of falling asleep while driving, and his job as a long-haul truck driver make drowsy-driving a critical safety issue—he must be counseled about the risk to himself and the public and the need to be treated/cleared before driving commercially, with relevant reporting obligations addressed. Reducing evening alcohol is important because alcohol relaxes upper-airway muscles and worsens OSA. Encouraging him to keep driving untreated is dangerous and inappropriate. A bedtime protein snack does not treat OSA. Sleeping fully upright indefinitely is impractical and not a substitute for CPAP, though positional measures can be a minor adjunct.

cases.diagnosis: Severe obstructive sleep apnea (AHI 42) diagnosed by polysomnography
pearls: STOP-BANG (Snoring, Tiredness, Observed apneas, Pressure/HTN, BMI>35, Age>50, Neck>40 cm, Gender male) screens for OSA risk.
Polysomnography is the gold standard; home sleep apnea testing suits high-probability patients without major cardiopulmonary comorbidity.
CPAP is first-line for moderate-to-severe OSA; oral appliances and surgery are alternatives for mild disease or CPAP intolerance.
Untreated OSA worsens hypertension and causes dangerous drowsy driving—address occupational and alcohol-related safety risks.

cases.title: A Nagging Cough That Won't Quit
cases.chiefComplaint: A persistent cough for ten days that she wants antibiotics to treat.
cases.history: A 30-year-old woman presents to clinic requesting antibiotics for a cough that has lasted ten days. It began as a head cold with nasal congestion and a sore throat; the upper-respiratory symptoms have largely resolved, but a cough—initially dry, now occasionally productive of small amounts of clear-to-whitish sputum—has persisted and is keeping her up at night. She reports mild chest discomfort from coughing but denies high fever, drenching sweats, dyspnea at rest, or hemoptysis. She had a low-grade temperature early in the illness. Several coworkers have had similar 'colds.' PMH is unremarkable; she has no asthma, COPD, or heart disease. She takes only an oral contraceptive pill and occasional ibuprofen. No known drug allergies. She is a never-smoker, exercises regularly, and works as a schoolteacher. Family history is noncontributory. She is up to date on vaccinations including influenza. Review of systems is otherwise negative; she has no postnasal drip symptoms suggesting allergies and no heartburn.
cases.exam: The patient is well-appearing and in no distress. She has occasional cough during the encounter. HEENT exam shows mildly erythematous nasal mucosa with minimal clear discharge and a non-exudative oropharynx; there is no sinus tenderness. Neck is supple without lymphadenopathy. Pulmonary exam is clear to auscultation bilaterally with normal breath sounds, no crackles, no wheezes, and no dullness or bronchial breath sounds; there is no increased fremitus or egophony. Cardiac exam is regular without murmurs. There is no peripheral edema. She is breathing comfortably and speaking in full sentences. No accessory muscle use.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Given a normal lung exam and normal vitals after a viral URI, what is the most likely diagnosis?
options: Acute bronchitis, most likely viral
Decompensated heart failure
Community-acquired pneumonia
Pulmonary embolism
stages.explanation: A self-limited cough following a typical upper-respiratory illness, with a clear chest exam, normal oxygen saturation, and no high fever or systemic toxicity, is classic for acute bronchitis, which is overwhelmingly viral. Pneumonia would more likely show focal findings (crackles, bronchial breath sounds, dullness, increased fremitus), abnormal vitals (fever, tachypnea, hypoxemia), and often higher fever—none present here. PE is unlikely without dyspnea, pleuritic pain, tachycardia, hypoxemia, or risk factors (though her OCP is a minor factor, the picture is a postviral cough). Heart failure would show dyspnea, crackles, edema, or elevated JVP, which she lacks. Acute bronchitis is the best fit.

stages.label: POINT-OF-CARE & OFFICE FINDINGS
stages.content:
Rapid influenza test: negative
Pulse oximetry: 99% on room air
Peak expiratory flow: within normal limits for age/height
No focal findings on lung auscultation; clear bilaterally
CBC not obtained (not indicated in well-appearing patient)

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: She asks for antibiotics. What is the most appropriate response and management?
options: Prescribe azithromycin to shorten the illness as initial therapy
Reassure her, explain that antibiotics are not indicated for viral bronchitis,
Order a chest X-ray and start antibiotics empirically while awaiting it as monotherapy
Prescribe amoxicillin-clavulanate to prevent pneumonia as initial therapy
stages.explanation: Acute bronchitis is almost always viral and self-limited; antibiotics do not meaningfully shorten symptoms and contribute to resistance and side effects—so the guideline-concordant, stewardship-minded approach is to reassure her, explain why antibiotics are not indicated, and offer symptomatic relief (fluids, rest, cough suppressants/honey, NSAIDs). Prescribing azithromycin or amoxicillin-clavulanate provides no benefit and causes harm. Antibiotics do not 'prevent pneumonia' in a well-appearing patient with a normal exam. A chest X-ray is not routinely needed when vitals are normal and the exam is clear; reflexively starting antibiotics while awaiting imaging is exactly the overuse stewardship seeks to avoid.

stages.label: RED FLAGS
stages.question: Which finding, if present, would change your assessment and prompt a chest radiograph to exclude pneumonia?
options: Clear nasal discharge as the immediate next step
Focal crackles, tachypnea, hypoxemia, or high fever
A persistent dry cough at night as the immediate next step
Mild chest soreness from coughing as the immediate next step
stages.explanation: Abnormal vital signs and focal lung findings are the classic indications to obtain a chest radiograph to rule out pneumonia: focal crackles or consolidation signs, tachypnea, hypoxemia (low SpO2), tachycardia, or high fever. These suggest the cough is not simple viral bronchitis. A persistent nocturnal dry cough, clear nasal discharge, and mild chest-wall soreness from coughing are all expected, benign features of acute bronchitis and do not, by themselves, warrant imaging or antibiotics. The Heckerling rule highlights that the absence of vital-sign abnormalities and crackles makes pneumonia unlikely, which is why their presence is the trigger to image.

stages.label: PATIENT EDUCATION
stages.question: What is the most accurate counseling about the expected course of her cough?
options: Cough from acute bronchitis can persist for up to 2–3 weeks even without antibiotics,
The cough should resolve within 24 hours of starting any treatment in this setting
A lingering cough always indicates a bacterial superinfection as the immediate next step
She should avoid all fluids to reduce sputum production as the immediate next step
stages.explanation: Patients should be counseled that the cough of acute bronchitis commonly lasts about 1–3 weeks (sometimes longer) and that this protracted course is expected and not a sign of treatment failure—setting this expectation reduces antibiotic-seeking and repeat visits. The cough will not vanish in 24 hours with any therapy. A lingering cough does NOT 'always' mean bacterial superinfection; most resolve on their own, though worsening or new systemic features warrant reassessment. Avoiding fluids is incorrect—adequate hydration helps thin secretions. Honey, rest, and time are reasonable; she should return if she develops red-flag features.

cases.diagnosis: Acute (viral) bronchitis
pearls: Acute bronchitis is overwhelmingly viral and self-limited; antibiotics offer no benefit and should be withheld (antibiotic stewardship).
A normal lung exam with normal vital signs makes pneumonia unlikely; reserve chest X-ray for focal findings, hypoxemia, tachypnea, or high fever.
Counsel that the cough may persist 1–3 weeks without antibiotics—an expected course, not treatment failure.
Offer symptomatic care (hydration, honey, NSAIDs, cough suppressants) and clear return precautions.

cases.title: Years of Worsening Breathlessness and a Dry Cough
cases.chiefComplaint: Two years of gradually worsening exertional shortness of breath and a persistent dry cough.
cases.history: A 70-year-old man presents with insidious, progressive exertional dyspnea over two years, now limiting him to walking one block before stopping, along with a chronic nonproductive cough. He denies fever, wheezing, orthopnea, paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnea, or leg swelling. He has no chest pain and no hemoptysis. PMH includes well-controlled gastroesophageal reflux disease and hypertension; he has no known connective tissue disease symptoms (no rash, joint pain, dry eyes/mouth, or Raynaud phenomenon). Medications: omeprazole 20 mg daily, amlodipine 5 mg daily. No known drug allergies; he denies amiodarone, nitrofurantoin, or methotrexate use. He is a former smoker (20 pack-years, quit 15 years ago). He worked as an accountant with no significant occupational dust, asbestos, or bird/mold exposures, and has no pets. Family history is notable for a brother with 'pulmonary scarring.' Review of systems is otherwise negative. He has lost a few pounds unintentionally and notes increasing fatigue.
cases.exam: The patient is a thin, well-appearing older man who becomes mildly breathless with exertion. Pulmonary exam reveals fine, dry, bibasilar end-inspiratory crackles described as 'Velcro-like' that do not clear with coughing; there are no wheezes and no signs of consolidation. Cardiac exam is regular without murmurs; there is no elevated JVP and no peripheral edema, making heart failure less likely. He has digital clubbing of the fingers. There is no rash, no synovitis, no sclerodactyly, and no malar erythema. Resting room-air saturation is 92% but falls with ambulation. The remainder of the exam, including abdomen and skin, is unremarkable.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Given bibasilar Velcro crackles, clubbing, and exertional desaturation, what is the most appropriate initial diagnostic test?
options: Coronary angiography as the first step today
Bronchodilator trial with peak flow monitoring
High-resolution CT of the chest
Empiric high-dose corticosteroids and reassess
stages.explanation: The combination of insidious dyspnea, dry cough, bibasilar 'Velcro' inspiratory crackles, clubbing, and exertional desaturation strongly suggests interstitial lung disease, and the appropriate initial workup is high-resolution CT of the chest (to characterize the pattern) plus pulmonary function tests (expected to show a restrictive pattern with reduced DLCO). Empiric high-dose steroids before characterizing the disease is inappropriate—steroids can be harmful in idiopathic pulmonary fibrosis and a diagnosis must be established first. Coronary angiography targets ischemic heart disease, which his presentation and exam do not suggest. A bronchodilator/peak-flow trial addresses obstructive disease (asthma/COPD), not a restrictive ILD picture.

stages.label: PFTs & HRCT
stages.content:
Pulmonary function tests:
FVC 62% predicted
FEV1 65% predicted
FEV1/FVC ratio 0.82 (normal/elevated — restrictive pattern)
TLC 64% predicted
DLCO 45% predicted
HRCT chest: bibasilar, subpleural reticular opacities with honeycombing and traction bronchiectasis; basal and peripheral predominance; no significant ground-glass or air trapping
Autoimmune serologies (ANA, RF, anti-CCP, myositis panel): negative

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: The HRCT shows subpleural, basal-predominant honeycombing with traction bronchiectasis. What pattern is this and what does it imply?
options: Hypersensitivity pneumonitis pattern from bird exposure as the primary diagnosis
Organizing pneumonia pattern, implying a post-infectious cause as the primary diagnosis
A usual interstitial pneumonia pattern, which in this context supports idiopathic pulmonary fibrosis
Nonspecific interstitial pneumonia (NSIP) pattern, implying a steroid-responsive process
stages.explanation: Subpleural, basal-predominant reticulation with honeycombing and traction bronchiectasis, without features pointing elsewhere, is the hallmark of a usual interstitial pneumonia (UIP) pattern. In an older patient with restrictive PFTs and reduced DLCO, no significant exposures, and negative autoimmune serologies, a definite UIP pattern on HRCT supports a diagnosis of idiopathic pulmonary fibrosis (IPF)—often obviating surgical lung biopsy. NSIP shows more ground-glass with relative subpleural sparing and is more steroid-responsive. Organizing pneumonia shows patchy consolidation, often peribronchial/migratory. Hypersensitivity pneumonitis features upper/mid-lung centrilobular nodules, ground-glass, and air trapping with a relevant antigen exposure—he has none.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Which therapy is most appropriate to slow disease progression in confirmed IPF?
options: Triple therapy with prednisone, azathioprine, and N-acetylcysteine
An antifibrotic agent such as pirfenidone or nintedanib
Long-term high-dose prednisone monotherapy as monotherapy
Chronic broad-spectrum antibiotics as initial therapy
stages.explanation: Antifibrotic therapy—pirfenidone or nintedanib—is the cornerstone of IPF treatment; both slow the decline in FVC and disease progression. High-dose prednisone monotherapy is NOT beneficial in IPF and can be harmful. The combination of prednisone, azathioprine, and N-acetylcysteine was actually shown (in the PANTHER-IPF trial) to INCREASE mortality and hospitalizations in IPF and is contraindicated. Chronic antibiotics do not treat the fibrotic process. Beyond antifibrotics, comprehensive care includes supplemental oxygen for hypoxemia, pulmonary rehabilitation, vaccination, treating comorbidities (e.g., GERD), and timely referral for lung transplantation evaluation.

stages.label: COMPLICATIONS
stages.question: He returns one month later with abrupt worsening of dyspnea over several days, new diffuse ground-glass opacities on imaging, and no evidence of infection, heart failure, or PE. What is the diagnosis?
options: An acute exacerbation of IPF
A large pulmonary embolism
Community-acquired pneumonia
Acute decompensated heart failure
stages.explanation: An acute exacerbation of IPF is defined by an acute (typically < 1 month), unexplained worsening of dyspnea with NEW bilateral ground-glass opacities and/or consolidation superimposed on the chronic UIP/fibrotic background, after reasonable exclusion of infection, heart failure, and pulmonary embolism—exactly this scenario. It carries high mortality. Pneumonia is excluded by the absence of infection on evaluation; heart failure is excluded by lack of volume overload/cardiac findings; PE is excluded by appropriate workup. Management is largely supportive with corticosteroids often used despite limited evidence, oxygen, and treatment of any reversible contributors, while addressing goals of care given the poor prognosis.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: For long-term planning, which referral or intervention is most important for an appropriate IPF patient?
options: Early referral for lung transplantation evaluation in suitable candidates
Avoidance of all supplemental oxygen to prevent dependence
Initiation of inhaled corticosteroids as definitive therapy
Routine annual spirometry only, with no specialist involvement
stages.explanation: Because IPF is progressive and ultimately fatal, early referral for lung transplantation evaluation in suitable candidates is critical—transplant is the only intervention that can prolong survival substantially, and early listing improves access before the patient is too debilitated. Annual spirometry alone without specialist (pulmonology/ILD center) involvement underserves a complex, progressive disease. Inhaled corticosteroids are not a definitive therapy for IPF. Withholding supplemental oxygen is wrong: oxygen is recommended for resting or exertional hypoxemia to relieve symptoms and support activity, and 'oxygen dependence' fear is not a reason to deny needed therapy. Pulmonary rehab and palliative care also play key roles.

cases.diagnosis: Idiopathic pulmonary fibrosis (UIP pattern), with later acute exacerbation
pearls: Bibasilar 'Velcro' inspiratory crackles plus clubbing and exertional desaturation should prompt HRCT and PFTs for interstitial lung disease.
A definite UIP pattern (subpleural, basal honeycombing with traction bronchiectasis) plus a compatible clinical picture supports IPF and may obviate biopsy.
Antifibrotics (pirfenidone or nintedanib) slow FVC decline; prednisone/azathioprine/NAC triple therapy increases mortality in IPF.
Refer suitable IPF patients early for lung transplantation; acute exacerbations present as new ground-glass opacities without infection/HF/PE and carry high mortality.

cases.title: Barking Cough and Wheeze That Worsens on Exertion
cases.chiefComplaint: Progressive cough, wheeze, and breathlessness that worsen when she exerts herself or lies flat.
cases.history: A 58-year-old woman presents with 14 months of a harsh, brassy cough and audible expiratory wheeze. Symptoms intensify with exertion, coughing, laughing, and recumbency, and she has had two emergency visits for presumed asthma exacerbations that improved minimally with bronchodilators and prednisone. She describes difficulty clearing secretions and a sensation of her airway 'closing off' when she coughs hard. She has obstructive sleep apnea on CPAP, GERD, and was intubated for 9 days during a severe pneumonia 3 years ago. Medications are fluticasone-salmeterol twice daily, albuterol as needed, and omeprazole 40 mg daily. She has no allergies. She smoked half a pack daily for 20 years and quit 10 years ago. She works as a schoolteacher. Family history is notable for asthma in a sister. She denies fever, hemoptysis, weight loss, reflux of food, or chest pain. Repeated courses of inhaled corticosteroids and a leukotriene antagonist have not helped, prompting referral for refractory 'asthma.'
cases.exam: Comfortable at rest but coughs frequently with a barking, brassy quality. Forced expiration and a voluntary cough reproduce a low-pitched monophonic expiratory wheeze heard loudest over the central airways at the sternal notch, not the lung periphery. Inspiration is quiet. There is no stridor at rest, no clubbing, and no accessory muscle use. Lungs are otherwise clear with symmetric air entry; no crackles. Cardiac exam is normal with no murmurs or JVD. There is no peripheral edema. Oropharynx is unremarkable and neck has no palpable mass or thyromegaly.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Her wheeze is monophonic, central, and worse with forced expiration, and she has responded poorly to aggressive asthma therapy. What is the most appropriate next diagnostic step?
options: Methacholine challenge testing to confirm asthma as the first step today
Spirometry with a flow-volume loop, looking for expiratory limb abnormalities
Empirically escalate to high-dose inhaled corticosteroid plus oral montelukast
Empiric trial of high-dose proton pump inhibitor for reflux-induced cough
High-resolution CT of the chest in inspiration only as the first step today
stages.explanation: A monophonic central wheeze that worsens with forced expiration and is refractory to asthma therapy points to a large-airway, not a small-airway, process. Spirometry with a flow-volume loop is the right first study: dynamic central airway collapse classically produces a notch or plateau on the expiratory limb, distinguishing it from fixed obstruction and from peripheral airway disease. Escalating ICS/montelukast (wrong) repeats a failed strategy. Inspiratory-only HRCT (wrong) misses the dynamic, expiratory-phase collapse that defines this disorder; dynamic expiratory imaging is needed. Methacholine challenge (wrong) tests for bronchial hyperreactivity but she has already failed bronchodilators and the pattern is not asthma. A PPI trial (wrong) addresses reflux but does not explain the central monophonic wheeze.

stages.label: PFTs & FLOW-VOLUME LOOP
stages.content:
Spirometry: FEV1 78% predicted, FVC 84% predicted, FEV1/FVC 0.74
Flow-volume loop: characteristic notching with abrupt drop in expiratory flow at mid-to-low lung volumes (expiratory plateau)
Inspiratory limb: preserved and normal in contour
Bronchodilator response: <5% change in FEV1
DLCO: normal at 92% predicted

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: The flow-volume loop shows expiratory-limb collapse with a normal inspiratory limb and no bronchodilator response. What is the single best confirmatory test?
options: Cardiopulmonary exercise testing as the primary diagnosis
Dynamic chest CT and/or bronchoscopy showing >50% expiratory airway collapse
Echocardiography to assess for pulmonary hypertension as the primary diagnosis
Sweat chloride testing and CFTR genotyping as the primary diagnosis
CT pulmonary angiography to exclude embolism as the primary diagnosis
stages.explanation: Dynamic airway imaging is confirmatory: paired end-inspiratory and dynamic forced-expiratory CT, or bronchoscopy during forced expiration/cough, demonstrating excessive expiratory narrowing of the trachea/main bronchi (commonly ≥50% reduction in luminal area) clinches tracheomalacia/excessive dynamic airway collapse. Her post-intubation history is a classic acquired cause. CPET (wrong) characterizes exercise limitation but does not visualize the airway. CT angiography (wrong) evaluates for PE, not airway wall integrity. Sweat chloride/CFTR testing (wrong) screens for cystic fibrosis, which does not fit a 58-year-old with this loop. Echocardiography (wrong) assesses the heart and pulmonary pressures, not dynamic central airway collapse.

stages.label: DYNAMIC CT & BRONCHOSCOPY
stages.content:
Paired CT: trachea round on inspiration; on dynamic forced expiration the lumen collapses to a crescentic 'frown' shape with ~65% reduction in cross-sectional area
