Bronchoscopy: marked anterior-posterior collapse of the lower trachea and main bronchi during forced expiration and cough, with apposition of the posterior membrane
Airway mucosa: normal, no mass or stenosis
No extrinsic compression on CT

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: She is confirmed to have tracheomalacia with excessive dynamic airway collapse and moderate symptoms. What is the most appropriate initial management?
options: Immediate tracheal stent placement as initial therapy
Definitive tracheobronchoplasty (posterior membrane plication) as initial therapy
Continuous positive airway pressure as a pneumatic airway stent, plus optimize comorbidities
Lifelong oral corticosteroids as initial therapy
Rigid bronchoscopy with laser ablation of the posterior membrane as initial therapy
stages.explanation: Initial management of symptomatic dynamic airway collapse is conservative and physiologic: CPAP acts as a pneumatic stent splinting the airway open during expiration, and treating comorbidities (OSA, GERD, reflux), pulmonary hygiene, and airway-clearance techniques improves symptoms. She already uses CPAP, which can be optimized. A stent (wrong) is reserved for a short therapeutic trial in severe, refractory disease and carries migration/granulation/infection risks. Tracheobronchoplasty (wrong) is definitive surgery considered only after a positive stent trial in severely symptomatic, fit patients—not first-line. Chronic steroids (wrong) have no role and add harm. Laser ablation of the posterior membrane (wrong) is not a standard treatment and risks airway injury.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: After several months of optimized CPAP, pulmonary hygiene, and reflux control, she remains severely dyspneic with recurrent infections and poor quality of life. What is the appropriate next step before offering definitive surgery?
options: Permanent silicone stent placement as the long-term solution based on current risk
Restart high-dose inhaled corticosteroids and reassess in one year
A short-term internal stent trial to predict whether tracheobronchoplasty will help
Proceed directly to tracheobronchoplasty without further testing
Bilateral lung transplantation evaluation with appropriate follow-up
stages.explanation: Before committing a patient to tracheobronchoplasty, a short-term airway stent trial is used to confirm that mechanically splinting the airway open relieves symptoms and improves function; a clear positive response predicts surgical benefit and selects appropriate candidates. Going straight to surgery (wrong) risks a major operation in someone who may not benefit. A permanent stent (wrong) is not a durable solution given migration, granulation tissue, mucus plugging, and infection. Lung transplant (wrong) does not address central airway collapse and is not indicated. Returning to high-dose ICS (wrong) repeats an already-failed asthma-directed therapy and delays effective care.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: She undergoes the short-term stent trial. Which complication is most characteristic of indwelling central airway stents and should prompt close surveillance?
options: Stent migration, granulation tissue formation, mucus plugging,
Immediate fatal hemoptysis in nearly all patients
Esophageal varices from venous compression as the leading concern
Permanent vocal cord paralysis in most patients
Hyperthyroidism from stent material as the leading concern
stages.explanation: Indwelling airway stents are notorious for stent migration, granulation tissue at the stent margins, obstructing mucus plugging, and stent-related infection—which is exactly why permanent stenting is avoided and a stent trial is kept short. Hyperthyroidism (wrong) is unrelated to airway stent material. Near-universal fatal hemoptysis (wrong) overstates the (real but uncommon) risk of stent-related airway erosion. Permanent vocal cord paralysis in most patients (wrong) is not a typical stent complication and relates to recurrent laryngeal nerve injury from other causes. Esophageal varices (wrong) are a portal-hypertension phenomenon, not an airway-stent complication. Awareness of these issues drives diligent bronchoscopic surveillance during the trial.

cases.diagnosis: Tracheomalacia with excessive dynamic airway collapse (acquired, post-intubation)
pearls: Refractory 'asthma' with a monophonic central expiratory wheeze and a barking cough should raise suspicion for dynamic central airway collapse, not small-airway disease.
The flow-volume loop shows expiratory-limb notching/plateau; diagnosis is confirmed with dynamic expiratory CT or bronchoscopy showing excessive (≥50%) expiratory luminal collapse.
First-line therapy is CPAP as a pneumatic stent plus treatment of comorbidities, GERD, and pulmonary hygiene.
A short-term stent trial predicts response and selects candidates for definitive tracheobronchoplasty; permanent stents carry high complication rates.

cases.title: Breathlessness That Improves When She Lies Down
cases.chiefComplaint: Slowly worsening exertional breathlessness with low oxygen levels and recurrent nosebleeds.
cases.history: A 36-year-old woman presents with 2 years of exertional dyspnea and fatigue, plus exercise intolerance disproportionate to her fitness. She reports that her breathlessness is worse when standing or sitting upright and improves when she lies flat. She has had frequent, sometimes heavy, nosebleeds since adolescence and notes small red spots on her lips and fingertips. She has no cough, fever, chest pain, or orthopnea, and no leg swelling. Past history includes iron-deficiency anemia treated intermittently with oral iron. She takes no other regular medications and has no allergies. She does not smoke and drinks alcohol rarely. She works as a graphic designer. Family history is striking: her father and a brother both have recurrent epistaxis and 'lung shunts,' and a paternal aunt had a stroke at a young age. On directed questioning she reports occasional migraine-like headaches and one episode of transient right-arm numbness that resolved. She has never been pregnant. She denies recent travel, immobilization, or hemoptysis.
cases.exam: Comfortable supine. Mucocutaneous telangiectasias are visible on the lips, tongue, and fingertips, and nasal mucosa shows telangiectasias with dried blood. There is mild central cyanosis and early digital clubbing. A faint continuous bruit is audible over the right lower lung zone that increases with inspiration. Lungs are otherwise clear; no crackles or wheeze. Cardiac exam is normal with no murmur, no fixed split S2, normal JVP, and no peripheral edema. Abdomen is soft without hepatomegaly or bruit. Neurologic exam is nonfocal.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: A young woman has hypoxemia that worsens upright and improves supine (orthodeoxia), mucocutaneous telangiectasias, recurrent epistaxis, and a family history of the same. Which underlying physiology best explains her hypoxemia?
options: Alveolar hypoventilation from neuromuscular weakness
Carboxyhemoglobinemia from chronic carbon monoxide exposure
Diffusion impairment from interstitial fibrosis
Ventilation-perfusion mismatch from small-airway disease
Right-to-left shunt through a pulmonary arteriovenous malformation
stages.explanation: Orthodeoxia (desaturation when upright) plus telangiectasias, epistaxis, and an autosomal-dominant family pattern strongly suggest hereditary hemorrhagic telangiectasia with a pulmonary arteriovenous malformation creating an anatomic right-to-left shunt; basal-predominant PAVMs receive more flow when upright, worsening hypoxemia. V/Q mismatch from airway disease (wrong) would not cause a fixed shunt that fails to correct with oxygen or this orthodeoxia pattern. Diffusion impairment (wrong) does not produce orthodeoxia and her lungs are clear. Hypoventilation (wrong) would raise CO2 and is excluded by normal strength. Carboxyhemoglobinemia (wrong) causes falsely normal pulse oximetry, not true measured hypoxemia, and does not fit the syndrome.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: To confirm an intrapulmonary right-to-left shunt at the bedside before definitive imaging, which test is most appropriate?
options: D-dimer and lower-extremity venous duplex as the primary diagnosis
100% oxygen study and agitated-saline contrast transthoracic echocardiography
Right heart catheterization with vasodilator challenge in this clinical context
Spirometry with bronchodilator response as the primary diagnosis
Methacholine challenge test as the primary diagnosis
stages.explanation: Agitated-saline contrast echocardiography is the key screening test: bubbles appearing in the left atrium after 3–5 cardiac cycles indicate an intrapulmonary (rather than intracardiac) shunt, and a 100% oxygen study showing failure to fully correct hypoxemia supports a true anatomic shunt. Methacholine (wrong) tests airway reactivity. D-dimer/venous duplex (wrong) evaluate venous thromboembolism, not a congenital pulmonary shunt. Right heart catheterization with vasodilator challenge (wrong) assesses pulmonary arterial hypertension, which is not her physiology. Spirometry with bronchodilator (wrong) characterizes obstructive airway disease and would not detect a shunt.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
ABG on RA (seated): PaO2 58 mmHg, PaCO2 36 mmHg, pH 7.43
Bubble echo: microbubbles appear in the left atrium after 4 cardiac cycles, no intracardiac shunt
100% O2 study: PaO2 rises only to 180 mmHg (large shunt fraction)
Hgb 11.8 g/dL, MCV 76 fL, ferritin 9 ng/mL
Contrast chest CT: 1.8 cm serpiginous nidus in the right lower lobe with a single feeding artery ~3.5 mm and a draining vein

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: CT confirms a right-lower-lobe pulmonary AVM with a feeding artery of about 3.5 mm. What is the most appropriate definitive treatment?
options: Lifelong systemic anticoagulation as monotherapy
Surgical lobectomy of the right lower lobe
Pulmonary vasodilator therapy with sildenafil
Observation with annual CT given small size
Transcatheter embolization of the feeding artery
stages.explanation: Transcatheter embolization of the feeding artery (with coils or a vascular plug) is first-line for PAVMs with a feeding artery generally ≥2–3 mm; it eliminates the shunt, improves oxygenation, and reduces the risk of paradoxical embolic stroke and brain abscess. Anticoagulation (wrong) is contraindicated as a treatment and would worsen her bleeding/epistaxis. Lobectomy (wrong) is far more morbid and reserved for lesions not amenable to embolization. Observation (wrong) is inappropriate when the feeding artery is large enough to treat and she is symptomatic with neurologic risk. Sildenafil (wrong) treats pulmonary arterial hypertension, not an anatomic AVM.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: She is diagnosed with hereditary hemorrhagic telangiectasia. Beyond pulmonary AVMs, which complication should prompt directed screening because it can be clinically silent yet catastrophic?
options: Hypertrophic cardiomyopathy as the leading concern
Primary hyperaldosteronism as the leading concern
Cerebral arteriovenous malformations causing hemorrhage or paradoxical embolic stroke/abscess
Achalasia as the leading concern
Pulmonary alveolar proteinosis as the leading concern
stages.explanation: HHT is associated with cerebral AVMs (and right-to-left shunting through PAVMs predisposes to paradoxical embolic stroke and brain abscess), so brain MRI screening and antibiotic prophylaxis for procedures are recommended; hepatic and GI AVMs are also seen. Her prior transient neurologic event underscores this risk. Pulmonary alveolar proteinosis (wrong) is unrelated to HHT. Hypertrophic cardiomyopathy (wrong) is a sarcomeric disorder, not a vascular dysplasia. Primary hyperaldosteronism (wrong) and achalasia (wrong) have no link to HHT. Screening for cerebral and visceral AVMs and treating her iron-deficiency anemia from chronic epistaxis are central to her ongoing care.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: After successful embolization, how should her pulmonary AVMs be managed long term, and what daily precaution reduces her risk of brain abscess?
options: Begin chronic suppressive antibiotics indefinitely for all patients as the immediate next step
Periodic surveillance imaging for reperfusion or new AVMs, plus antibiotic prophylaxis before dental
Start lifelong warfarin to keep the treated AVM closed as the immediate next step
Repeat embolization every 3 months regardless of findings as the immediate next step
No further imaging is ever needed once the AVM is treated as the immediate next step
stages.explanation: PAVMs can reperfuse or new ones can grow, so periodic surveillance imaging (e.g., CT) is required after embolization; because the right-to-left shunt allows bacteria to bypass the pulmonary capillary filter, antibiotic prophylaxis before dental and other bacteremia-prone procedures reduces the risk of brain abscess. Saying no imaging is ever needed (wrong) ignores reperfusion/new lesions. Lifelong warfarin (wrong) is not used to keep AVMs closed and would worsen her epistaxis. Routine re-embolization every 3 months (wrong) is not indicated absent findings. Chronic suppressive antibiotics for everyone (wrong) is excessive; targeted procedural prophylaxis is the standard, alongside surveillance and management of HHT-related anemia.

cases.diagnosis: Pulmonary arteriovenous malformation in hereditary hemorrhagic telangiectasia (Osler-Weber-Rendu)
pearls: Orthodeoxia-platypnea plus telangiectasias, epistaxis, and a dominant family history is hereditary hemorrhagic telangiectasia until proven otherwise.
Agitated-saline bubble echo shows late (after 3–5 cycles) left-heart opacification for an intrapulmonary shunt; a 100% O2 study confirms a fixed anatomic shunt.
Transcatheter embolization is first-line for PAVMs with feeding arteries ≥2–3 mm and reduces paradoxical stroke and brain abscess risk.
Screen HHT patients for cerebral, hepatic, and GI AVMs, treat iron-deficiency anemia, and give antibiotic prophylaxis to prevent brain abscess.

cases.title: Singed Face and Black Sputum After a House Fire
cases.chiefComplaint: Brought in after rescue from an enclosed house fire with facial burns and a hoarse, sooty cough.
cases.history: A 44-year-old man was pulled from a burning single-story house after being trapped in a smoke-filled bedroom for an estimated 10–15 minutes. EMS reports he was found confused near the floor and received 100% oxygen by non-rebreather en route. He complains of a hoarse voice, sore throat, and a cough productive of black material, with a headache and lightheadedness. He has partial-thickness burns to the face and anterior neck and singed nasal hairs and eyebrows. Past history includes hypertension and a 25-pack-year smoking history. Medications are lisinopril 20 mg daily; he has no known allergies. He drinks socially. There is no family history of note. He denies chest trauma, vomiting, or loss of consciousness aside from initial confusion, though the timeline is uncertain. Total body surface area of cutaneous burns is estimated at about 12%. He arrives anxious, with progressively raspy speech and worsening throat tightness over the first 30 minutes of evaluation.
cases.exam: Anxious, tachypneic man with soot around the nostrils and mouth and carbonaceous sputum. Voice is hoarse and he has audible upper-airway stridor on inspiration. There are partial-thickness facial and anterior neck burns with intraoral and posterior pharyngeal soot and mucosal edema; singed nasal vibrissae. Lungs have scattered wheezes but otherwise fair air entry. Cardiac exam shows tachycardia, regular rhythm, no murmur. There is no chest-wall crepitus. Mentation is mildly altered with a headache; pupils equal and reactive. No focal neurologic deficit. Distal pulses intact; no circumferential chest or neck eschar limiting expansion at present.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: He has facial burns, singed nasal hairs, carbonaceous sputum, hoarseness, and developing inspiratory stridor after an enclosed-space fire. What is the most appropriate immediate action?
options: Place on BiPAP to support oxygenation as the first step today
Start high-dose IV corticosteroids to reduce airway edema as the first step today
Early endotracheal intubation for impending airway obstruction from inhalation injury
Administer nebulized albuterol and reassess in one hour as the first step today
Obtain a chest CT before any airway intervention as the first step today
stages.explanation: Signs of inhalation injury with progressive hoarseness and stridor signal impending upper-airway obstruction; airway edema worsens rapidly over hours, so early/prophylactic endotracheal intubation while the airway is still navigable is the priority—waiting risks a 'cannot intubate' emergency. CT first (wrong) dangerously delays securing the airway. Albuterol alone (wrong) treats bronchospasm but does nothing for life-threatening laryngeal edema. Corticosteroids (wrong) are not proven to reverse inhalation-related airway edema and increase infection risk. BiPAP (wrong) is contraindicated with an unprotected, edematous, threatened upper airway and risks aspiration and obstruction behind the mask.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: He was confused at the scene with a headache, and his pulse oximetry reads 100% despite his exposure. Which test best evaluates the most likely cause of his altered mentation?
options: CT head without contrast as the primary diagnosis
Lumbar puncture as the primary diagnosis
Pulse oximetry trend over time as the primary diagnosis
Standard arterial blood gas pH and PaO2 alone
Co-oximetry for carboxyhemoglobin
stages.explanation: Standard pulse oximetry cannot distinguish oxyhemoglobin from carboxyhemoglobin and reads falsely normal in carbon monoxide poisoning; co-oximetry directly measures carboxyhemoglobin and is essential, and concurrent cyanide toxicity (from combustion of synthetic materials) should be considered with a persistent lactic acidosis. A pulse-ox trend (wrong) remains falsely reassuring. CT head (wrong) is reasonable later but does not address the likely toxic-metabolic cause. Lumbar puncture (wrong) is not indicated. A routine ABG reporting only pH and PaO2 (wrong) can look normal because dissolved oxygen is unaffected by CO; you must specifically measure carboxyhemoglobin via co-oximetry.

stages.label: LABS & STUDIES
stages.content:
Co-oximetry: carboxyhemoglobin 24%
ABG: pH 7.28, PaO2 240 mmHg (on 100% O2), PaCO2 34 mmHg, lactate 9.2 mmol/L
Bedside fiberoptic laryngoscopy: supraglottic edema, soot, and mucosal sloughing
CXR: clear lung fields initially
Carboxyhemoglobin trending and persistent lactic acidosis raise concern for concurrent cyanide toxicity

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: His carboxyhemoglobin is 24% and he has a profound lactic acidosis suggesting concomitant cyanide poisoning. What is the most appropriate management combination?
options: Methylene blue and supplemental oxygen as initial therapy
Hyperbaric oxygen only, with no antidotal therapy as initial therapy
Switch to room air and observe as initial therapy
High-flow 100% oxygen for carbon monoxide plus hydroxocobalamin for suspected cyanide toxicity
Sodium thiosulfate alone with room air as initial therapy
stages.explanation: Treat carbon monoxide with high-flow 100% oxygen, which shortens the carboxyhemoglobin half-life, and treat suspected cyanide toxicity (severe lactic acidosis after enclosed-space fire) with hydroxocobalamin, which binds cyanide to form cyanocobalamin. Room air (wrong) prolongs CO clearance and ignores cyanide. Methylene blue (wrong) treats methemoglobinemia and can worsen tissue hypoxia in CO/cyanide poisoning. Sodium thiosulfate alone on room air (wrong) is an adjunct for cyanide but omits oxygen for CO and is slower-acting than hydroxocobalamin. Hyperbaric oxygen (wrong) may be considered for severe CO poisoning in select stable patients but does not address cyanide and is not appropriate as the sole therapy in an unstable patient with a threatened airway.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: He is intubated and stabilized in the ICU. Over the next 24–72 hours, which complication of lower-airway and parenchymal smoke injury should you most anticipate and monitor for?
options: Spontaneous pneumothorax from blebs as the leading concern
Acute respiratory distress syndrome with airway cast formation
Massive hemoptysis from bronchial artery erosion as the leading concern
Chronic obstructive lung disease developing acutely in this presentation
Immediate pulmonary fibrosis within 48 hours as the leading concern
stages.explanation: Lower-airway smoke injury causes mucosal sloughing, bronchospasm, and obstructing fibrinous airway casts, with chemical pneumonitis evolving over 24–72 hours into ARDS; lung-protective ventilation and aggressive pulmonary toilet (including therapeutic bronchoscopy to clear casts) are key, and superimposed pneumonia is a frequent later complication. Spontaneous pneumothorax from blebs (wrong) is unrelated. Pulmonary fibrosis (wrong) is a late, chronic sequela, not a 48-hour event. Massive hemoptysis from bronchial artery erosion (wrong) is not characteristic of acute inhalation injury. Acute COPD (wrong) is not a recognized acute consequence; the immediate threat is ARDS with airway obstruction from casts.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: He develops thick obstructing secretions with mucus casts and lobar atelectasis despite suctioning and chest physiotherapy. What is the most appropriate next intervention?
options: Routine prophylactic antibiotics for all inhalation-injury patients
Diuresis to reduce airway secretions with appropriate follow-up
Immediate tracheostomy regardless of airway status with appropriate follow-up
Therapeutic flexible bronchoscopy to remove airway casts
Empiric high-dose systemic corticosteroids to dissolve the casts
stages.explanation: Obstructing fibrinous casts causing lobar atelectasis are best cleared with therapeutic flexible bronchoscopy, which directly removes casts and debris and re-expands collapsed lung when routine suctioning and physiotherapy fail; nebulized agents and airway clearance are adjuncts. Systemic corticosteroids (wrong) do not dissolve casts and increase infection risk. Immediate tracheostomy (wrong) is not indicated solely for cast clearance and is unrelated to the obstruction mechanism. Routine prophylactic antibiotics for everyone (wrong) are not recommended; antibiotics are directed at documented infection, which is treated when it arises. Diuresis (wrong) addresses volume overload, not tenacious airway casts, and could harm a patient who needs adequate perfusion.

cases.diagnosis: Smoke inhalation injury with upper-airway edema, carbon monoxide poisoning, and suspected cyanide toxicity
pearls: Facial burns, singed nasal hairs, carbonaceous sputum, hoarseness, and stridor after an enclosed-space fire mandate early intubation before airway edema makes it impossible.
Pulse oximetry reads falsely normal in carbon monoxide poisoning—use co-oximetry to measure carboxyhemoglobin.
Persistent severe lactic acidosis after a fire suggests cyanide toxicity; treat with hydroxocobalamin plus 100% oxygen for CO.
Lower-airway injury causes obstructing airway casts and evolves into ARDS over 24–72 hours; manage with lung-protective ventilation and pulmonary toilet.

cases.title: Slowly Worsening Breathlessness in a Man on Heart Medication
cases.chiefComplaint: Several months of progressive breathlessness and a dry cough.
cases.history: A 69-year-old man presents with 5 months of insidious, progressive exertional dyspnea, a nonproductive cough, fatigue, and a 4 kg unintentional weight loss. He can no longer walk up a single flight without stopping. He has atrial fibrillation, for which he has taken amiodarone 200 mg daily for the past 2 years, along with apixaban, metoprolol, atorvastatin, and lisinopril. He has no known drug allergies. He is a former smoker (15 pack-years, quit 12 years ago) and drinks minimally. He worked in accounting with no occupational dust or asbestos exposure, keeps no birds, and has no mold exposure at home. He denies fever, night sweats, hemoptysis, joint pains, rash, or sick contacts. He has not traveled recently. Review of systems is notable for new photosensitivity and a slightly bluish-gray hue to his skin, plus mild tremor. There is no family history of interstitial lung disease. His symptoms have steadily worsened despite no change in cardiac status, prompting pulmonary referral for unexplained progressive dyspnea.
cases.exam: Thin man in no acute distress at rest but desaturates with walking. Skin has a subtle slate-gray discoloration over sun-exposed areas. Bibasilar fine 'Velcro' inspiratory crackles are heard with no wheeze. There is no clubbing and no peripheral edema. Cardiac exam reveals an irregularly irregular rhythm, no murmur, normal JVP, and no S3. There is no rash suggestive of connective tissue disease, no synovitis, and no lymphadenopathy. A fine resting tremor is present. Thyroid is non-tender and not enlarged. The remainder of the exam is unremarkable.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: A man on long-term amiodarone has subacute progressive dyspnea, dry cough, weight loss, bibasilar crackles, and exertional hypoxemia. What is the most appropriate next diagnostic step?
options: Coronary angiography as the first step today
Bronchoscopy with biopsy as the first test
Empiric diuresis for presumed heart failure
Empiric antibiotics for community-acquired pneumonia
High-resolution CT of the chest
stages.explanation: The picture suggests interstitial lung disease, and HRCT plus PFTs with DLCO is the appropriate next step: HRCT characterizes the pattern and distribution, while PFTs typically show a restrictive defect with reduced DLCO, establishing severity and a baseline. Empiric antibiotics (wrong) do not fit a subacute, afebrile, non-infectious course. Coronary angiography (wrong) targets ischemia, which does not explain crackles and a falling DLCO. Empiric diuresis (wrong) is unjustified without volume overload and normal JVP. Bronchoscopy first (wrong) is more invasive than warranted initially; noninvasive HRCT and PFTs guide whether further sampling is needed and help link the findings to his medication.

stages.label: PFTs & HRCT
stages.content:
PFTs: FVC 62% predicted, FEV1 64% predicted, FEV1/FVC 0.82 (restrictive), TLC 65% predicted, DLCO 41% predicted
HRCT: bilateral patchy ground-glass and reticular opacities with areas of high-attenuation (dense) parenchymal and pleural opacities
Noncontrast liver also appears dense (high attenuation)
No honeycombing; no large effusion; no consolidation suggesting infection
BNP normal; echocardiogram shows preserved EF and no significant valvular disease

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: HRCT shows high-attenuation pulmonary infiltrates and a dense liver, with restriction and a markedly reduced DLCO in a patient on chronic amiodarone. What is the most likely diagnosis?
options: Hypersensitivity pneumonitis from an avian antigen
Idiopathic pulmonary fibrosis
Amiodarone-induced pulmonary toxicity
Metastatic malignancy to the lungs
Heart failure with pulmonary edema
stages.explanation: High-attenuation pulmonary opacities and a dense liver reflect tissue accumulation of iodine-rich amiodarone and are characteristic of amiodarone-induced pulmonary toxicity; with 2 years of therapy, progressive dyspnea, restriction, and a low DLCO, this is the leading diagnosis. Idiopathic pulmonary fibrosis (wrong) shows basal honeycombing without high-attenuation deposits and no drug link. Hypersensitivity pneumonitis (wrong) requires antigen exposure he denies and lacks the high-attenuation sign. Heart failure (wrong) is excluded by normal BNP, preserved EF, and no congestion. Metastatic malignancy (wrong) produces discrete nodules/masses, not diffuse high-attenuation infiltrate with a dense liver, which is essentially pathognomonic of amiodarone deposition.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: He is diagnosed with amiodarone pulmonary toxicity. What is the cornerstone of management?
options: Initiate lung transplant evaluation immediately as initial therapy
Start broad-spectrum antibiotics and antifungals as initial therapy
Begin antifibrotic therapy with pirfenidone as first-line as initial therapy
Continue amiodarone but reduce the dose by half as initial therapy
Discontinue amiodarone
stages.explanation: The cornerstone is stopping the offending drug: discontinue amiodarone in coordination with cardiology (arranging alternative rhythm control/anticoagulation as needed), and add systemic corticosteroids for moderate-to-severe or progressive disease. Because amiodarone has an extremely long half-life, improvement can be slow, making steroids useful while the drug clears. Dose reduction while continuing the drug (wrong) is inadequate when toxicity is established. Antibiotics/antifungals (wrong) treat infection, which is excluded. Pirfenidone (wrong) is for idiopathic pulmonary fibrosis, not drug-induced toxicity. Immediate transplant evaluation (wrong) is premature because drug withdrawal and steroids often produce meaningful recovery.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: When counseling him about prognosis and monitoring after stopping amiodarone, which point is most accurate?
options: Symptoms should resolve within 48 hours because the drug clears rapidly with appropriate follow-up
He can safely restart amiodarone once symptoms improve with appropriate follow-up
A normal DLCO at baseline would have excluded the diagnosis entirely with appropriate follow-up
Because amiodarone's half-life is very long, recovery is gradual over weeks to months
No further pulmonary follow-up is needed after discharge with appropriate follow-up
stages.explanation: Amiodarone is highly lipophilic with a half-life measured in weeks to months, so tissue clearance and clinical recovery are gradual; serial PFTs (especially DLCO) and imaging track improvement and guide steroid tapering. Resolution in 48 hours (wrong) is impossible given the pharmacokinetics. Rechallenge (wrong) risks recurrence and is contraindicated. No follow-up (wrong) is unsafe given the slow course and need to monitor. The claim that a normal baseline DLCO excludes the diagnosis (wrong) is incorrect; a falling DLCO over time is the most sensitive monitoring tool, which is why baseline and serial measurements matter rather than a single value.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: As his cardiologist transitions him off amiodarone, which additional organ toxicities of long-term amiodarone warrant baseline and periodic monitoring?
options: No other organs are affected by amiodarone as the leading concern
Thyroid dysfunction , hepatotoxicity,
Adrenal insufficiency alone as the leading concern
Only renal function as the leading concern
Bone marrow aplasia exclusively as the leading concern
stages.explanation: Amiodarone is iodine-rich and lipophilic, accumulating in multiple tissues; beyond pulmonary toxicity it causes thyroid dysfunction (both hyper- and hypothyroidism), hepatotoxicity (transaminase elevation), corneal microdeposits and rare optic neuropathy, photosensitivity with blue-gray skin discoloration, and neurologic effects—warranting baseline and periodic thyroid and liver testing and eye/skin assessment. Monitoring only renal function (wrong) misses its principal toxicities; amiodarone is not chiefly nephrotoxic. Isolated bone marrow aplasia (wrong) and isolated adrenal insufficiency (wrong) are not its characteristic effects. Claiming no other organs are affected (wrong) is false given its well-known multi-organ toxicity, several features of which (slate-gray skin, tremor) this patient already shows.

cases.diagnosis: Amiodarone-induced pulmonary toxicity (drug-induced interstitial lung disease)
pearls: Consider amiodarone pulmonary toxicity in any patient on the drug with new progressive dyspnea, dry cough, restriction, and a falling DLCO.
High-attenuation pulmonary infiltrates plus a dense liver on CT reflect iodine-rich amiodarone tissue deposition and are highly suggestive.
Management centers on stopping the drug (with cardiology coordination) plus corticosteroids for significant disease.
Amiodarone's very long half-life means recovery is gradual; monitor with serial DLCO and imaging, and never rechallenge.

cases.title: Cough and Breathlessness After Cancer Treatment
cases.chiefComplaint: Dry cough and breathlessness developing a few weeks after completing chest radiation.
cases.history: A 60-year-old woman presents with 2 weeks of a dry cough, low-grade fevers, and progressive exertional dyspnea. She completed a 6-week course of thoracic external-beam radiotherapy for a right-sided stage II non-small-cell lung cancer 7 weeks ago. She reports mild pleuritic chest discomfort and fatigue. She has hypertension and hyperlipidemia and takes amlodipine and atorvastatin; she has no drug allergies. She is a former smoker with a 30-pack-year history who quit at diagnosis. She lives with her husband and has no pets, mold, or occupational exposures. She denies hemoptysis, leg swelling, calf pain, recent immobilization, or sick contacts, and she has not started any new medications. Family history is noncontributory. She is concerned that her cancer has progressed. On further questioning, her symptoms localize to the side that was irradiated, and she has not had purulent sputum. She completed chemotherapy concurrently but received no immune checkpoint inhibitor. She remains up to date on vaccinations including influenza and pneumococcal.
cases.exam: Mildly dyspneic with exertion. Inspiratory crackles are heard over the right upper-to-mid lung zone, corresponding to the prior radiation field; the contralateral lung is clear. There is faint dullness to percussion in the same region. No wheeze. Skin over the anterior right chest shows mild post-radiation erythema and hyperpigmentation. Cardiac exam is normal with no murmur, no JVD, and no peripheral edema; calves are nontender without asymmetry. There is no lymphadenopathy. The remainder of the exam is unremarkable.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: She has dry cough, dyspnea, and crackles localized to her prior radiation field about 7 weeks after thoracic radiotherapy. What is the most appropriate initial diagnostic step?
options: Empiric broad-spectrum antibiotics without imaging as the first step today
Bronchoscopy with transbronchial biopsy as the first test
PET scan to assess for tumor recurrence as the first step today
CT pulmonary angiogram to rule out pulmonary embolism first
Chest CT to characterize infiltrates
stages.explanation: A chest CT is the best initial step: radiation pneumonitis classically produces ground-glass/consolidative opacities that conform to the radiation portal rather than anatomic lung boundaries, and CT helps distinguish it from infection, tumor progression, and embolism. CT angiography (wrong) is reasonable only if PE is suspected, but she lacks risk features and her findings localize to the field. Empiric antibiotics without imaging (wrong) miss the likely diagnosis and delay appropriate care. PET (wrong) is confounded by post-radiation inflammation and is not the immediate step. Bronchoscopy with biopsy first (wrong) is more invasive than needed initially; CT often suffices when opacities match the treatment field.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
WBC 9.4 ×10³/µL
Procalcitonin: low
Chest CT: ground-glass and patchy consolidative opacities in the right upper/mid lung with a sharp straight-line border that does NOT follow anatomic fissures but conforms to the prior radiation field
No lobar consolidation outside the field; no large effusion
CT pulmonary angiogram (performed): no pulmonary embolism

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: CT shows ground-glass/consolidative opacities with a sharp non-anatomic border conforming to the radiation field, with low procalcitonin and no embolism. What is the most likely diagnosis?
options: Radiation pneumonitis
Lymphangitic spread of tumor
Bacterial pneumonia
Pulmonary embolism with infarction
Checkpoint-inhibitor pneumonitis
stages.explanation: Opacities that respect the radiation portal rather than anatomic lung divisions, arising in the typical 4–12 week window after thoracic radiotherapy with dry cough, low-grade fever, and dyspnea, are characteristic of radiation pneumonitis. Bacterial pneumonia (wrong) usually follows lobar/segmental anatomy with higher procalcitonin and purulent sputum. Lymphangitic tumor spread (wrong) shows nodular septal thickening, not a sharp field-conforming border, and would not appear so soon. PE with infarction (wrong) is excluded by the negative CT angiogram. Checkpoint-inhibitor pneumonitis (wrong) is possible with immunotherapy, but she received none; the field-conforming distribution and timing point squarely to radiation pneumonitis.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: She has symptomatic radiation pneumonitis with hypoxemia on exertion. What is the most appropriate treatment?
options: Systemic corticosteroids with a gradual taper
Observation alone with no pharmacologic therapy
Therapeutic anticoagulation as initial therapy
Immediate resumption of additional radiation to the area
Broad-spectrum antibiotics and antifungals as monotherapy
stages.explanation: Symptomatic radiation pneumonitis is treated with systemic corticosteroids such as prednisone, followed by a slow taper to prevent rebound; supportive oxygen and cough control are adjuncts. Observation alone (wrong) is appropriate only for mild, asymptomatic radiographic changes, not symptomatic disease with hypoxemia. Antibiotics/antifungals (wrong) treat infection, which is unlikely here given low procalcitonin and the field-conforming pattern. Anticoagulation (wrong) treats thromboembolism, which was excluded. Additional radiation (wrong) would worsen lung injury and is contraindicated. Steroids typically improve symptoms, though late radiation fibrosis can still develop in the treated region.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: Which statement best guides counseling about her longer-term pulmonary course?
options: Months later she may develop radiation fibrosis in the same region, with traction changes
Steroids should be stopped abruptly once she feels better to avoid side effects with appropriate follow-up
Radiation injury can never progress once pneumonitis resolves with appropriate follow-up
These findings confirm metastatic spread and warrant palliative-only care with appropriate follow-up
She should expect complete reversal of all imaging changes within one week of steroids based on current risk
stages.explanation: Radiation lung injury has two phases: acute pneumonitis (weeks to a few months) and later radiation fibrosis (months later) with traction bronchiectasis, scarring, and volume loss limited to the treated field; counseling should set this expectation and arrange follow-up imaging. Saying it can never progress (wrong) ignores the fibrotic phase. Complete reversal within a week (wrong) is unrealistic. Interpreting field-conforming inflammation as metastatic spread (wrong) is incorrect and would mislead management. Stopping steroids abruptly (wrong) risks rebound pneumonitis—corticosteroids must be tapered gradually.

cases.diagnosis: Radiation pneumonitis
pearls: Radiation pneumonitis typically appears 4–12 weeks after thoracic radiotherapy with dry cough, low-grade fever, and dyspnea.
Imaging hallmark is ground-glass/consolidative opacities with a sharp border that conforms to the radiation field, not anatomic lung boundaries.
Symptomatic disease is treated with corticosteroids and a slow taper; asymptomatic radiographic changes can be observed.
Late radiation fibrosis with traction changes and volume loss can develop months later within the treated field.

cases.title: Worsening Breathlessness in a Woman With Stiff, Swollen Hands
cases.chiefComplaint: Several months of progressive breathlessness and a dry cough.
cases.history: A 54-year-old woman with an 8-year history of seropositive rheumatoid arthritis presents with 6 months of slowly progressive exertional dyspnea and a persistent dry cough. Her joint disease, affecting the MCPs, wrists, and PIPs, has been moderately controlled on methotrexate 20 mg weekly with folic acid and intermittent low-dose prednisone. She has no drug allergies. She reports morning stiffness lasting over an hour, but no recent fever, hemoptysis, or chest pain. She is a never-smoker who works as a librarian, keeps no birds, and has no mold, asbestos, or other occupational exposures. She drinks rarely. Family history includes a mother with hypothyroidism. On review of systems she notes mild dry eyes and dry mouth and Raynaud-like color changes in cold weather. She is worried both about her lungs and about whether her arthritis medication could be responsible. Her exercise tolerance has declined from brisk walking to becoming winded climbing one flight. She has had no recent infections and no new home or travel exposures.
cases.exam: Comfortable at rest, mildly dyspneic with exertion. Bilateral fine 'Velcro' inspiratory crackles are heard at the lung bases. There is symmetric synovial thickening and ulnar deviation at the MCPs with mild active synovitis at the wrists; subcutaneous nodules are present at the elbows. No clubbing. Cardiac exam is normal: regular rhythm, no murmur, normal JVP, no peripheral edema, and no loud P2. Skin shows no rash or sclerodactyly. There is no lymphadenopathy. Eyes appear mildly dry. The remainder of the exam is unremarkable.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: A woman with seropositive RA on methotrexate has progressive dyspnea, dry cough, basal Velcro crackles, and exertional desaturation. What is the most appropriate initial workup?
options: Empiric antibiotics for atypical pneumonia as the first step today
High-resolution CT of the chest plus pulmonary function tests with DLCO
Stop methotrexate and reassess in 3 months before any testing as the first step today
Right heart catheterization to diagnose pulmonary hypertension as the first step today
Reassure her that RA does not affect the lungs and observe as the first step today
stages.explanation: Suspected connective tissue disease–associated interstitial lung disease warrants HRCT, PFTs with DLCO, and serologies (RF, anti-CCP, ANA, and an extended panel) to define pattern, severity, and disease activity. Stopping methotrexate without testing (wrong) delays diagnosis and assumes drug toxicity prematurely. Empiric antibiotics (wrong) do not fit a chronic, afebrile, non-infectious course. Right heart catheterization (wrong) is invasive and premature without evidence of pulmonary hypertension. Reassurance that RA spares the lungs (wrong) is false—RA commonly causes ILD and other pulmonary complications, so an objective evaluation is essential before attributing symptoms to medication or dismissing them.

stages.label: PFTs, SEROLOGIES & HRCT
stages.content:
PFTs: FVC 64% predicted, FEV1/FVC 0.84 (restrictive), TLC 66% predicted, DLCO 48% predicted
RF positive (high titer); anti-CCP strongly positive; ANA 1:320
HRCT: bilateral, basal- and peripheral-predominant ground-glass opacities with fine reticulation and traction bronchiectasis; relative subpleural sparing; minimal honeycombing
No significant lymphadenopathy or effusion
Echocardiogram: normal RV size and estimated pulmonary pressures

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Her HRCT shows basal/peripheral ground-glass with reticulation, traction bronchiectasis, relative subpleural sparing, and minimal honeycombing. Which radiographic pattern does this best represent?
options: Lymphangioleiomyomatosis with diffuse cysts
Organizing pneumonia with peripheral consolidations
Usual interstitial pneumonia (UIP) with extensive honeycombing
Hypersensitivity pneumonitis with mosaic air-trapping
Nonspecific interstitial pneumonia (NSIP) pattern
stages.explanation: Ground-glass with fine reticulation and traction bronchiectasis, relatively homogeneous and basal-predominant with characteristic relative subpleural sparing and little honeycombing, is the classic NSIP pattern—frequently seen in connective tissue disease–associated ILD, including RA. UIP (wrong) is defined by basal subpleural honeycombing and reticulation, which is only minimal here. Organizing pneumonia (wrong) shows patchy peripheral or peribronchial consolidations, not this ground-glass/reticular pattern. Hypersensitivity pneumonitis (wrong) features mosaic attenuation/air-trapping and an exposure history she lacks. Lymphangioleiomyomatosis (wrong) produces diffuse thin-walled cysts in younger women, not ground-glass with traction bronchiectasis.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: She is diagnosed with RA-associated ILD, NSIP pattern, with significant symptoms and impaired physiology. What is the most appropriate management approach?
options: Begin long-term broad-spectrum antibiotics as initial therapy
Continue methotrexate unchanged and add only inhaled bronchodilators as initial therapy
Hold methotrexate, treat with corticosteroids plus a steroid-sparing immunosuppressant ,
Start antifibrotic monotherapy with nintedanib and stop all immunosuppression as initial therapy
Proceed directly to lung transplantation as initial therapy
stages.explanation: CTD-ILD with an inflammatory NSIP pattern is treated with immunosuppression: corticosteroids plus a steroid-sparing agent such as mycophenolate, while optimizing control of the underlying rheumatoid arthritis with the rheumatologist; methotrexate is often held given its potential pulmonary toxicity and the need to reassess. Continuing methotrexate with only bronchodilators (wrong) ignores both possible drug toxicity and the inflammatory ILD. Antifibrotic monotherapy after stopping all immunosuppression (wrong) is inappropriate for an inflammatory NSIP pattern, though antifibrotics have a role in progressive fibrosing phenotypes. Long-term antibiotics (wrong) treat infection, not autoimmune ILD. Immediate transplant (wrong) is premature before a trial of immunosuppression.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: Despite immunosuppression and RA control, repeat testing over the next year shows progressive fibrosis with declining FVC and worsening reticulation. What is the most appropriate addition?
options: Switch to chronic azithromycin as monotherapy before discharge planning
Increase corticosteroids indefinitely to high dose in this setting
Discontinue all therapy and observe as the immediate next step
Initiate empiric antifungal therapy as the immediate next step
Add an antifibrotic agent for the progressive fibrosing ILD phenotype
stages.explanation: When CTD-ILD develops a progressive pulmonary fibrosis phenotype despite appropriate immunosuppression, adding an antifibrotic such as nintedanib slows the rate of FVC decline and is guideline-supported for progressive fibrosing ILD. Indefinite high-dose steroids (wrong) add toxicity without controlling progressive fibrosis and are not a long-term strategy. Stopping all therapy (wrong) abandons treatment of progressive disease. Azithromycin monotherapy (wrong) is not a disease-modifying treatment for fibrotic ILD. Empiric antifungals (wrong) treat infection, which is not the issue. Recognizing the transition to a progressive fibrosing phenotype and layering antifibrotic therapy onto immunosuppression is the key management step.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: She is on immunosuppression for RA-ILD. She develops a new fever, dry cough, and worsening hypoxemia with diffuse ground-glass opacities. Beyond an ILD flare, which complication of her therapy must be urgently excluded?
options: Benign anxiety-related hyperventilation as the leading concern
Acute gouty arthritis as the leading concern
Opportunistic infection, particularly Pneumocystis jirovecii pneumonia
Costochondritis as the leading concern
Medication-induced diabetes insipidus as the leading concern
stages.explanation: Patients on corticosteroids plus a steroid-sparing immunosuppressant are at risk for opportunistic infection; Pneumocystis jirovecii pneumonia classically presents with fever, dry cough, hypoxemia out of proportion to exam, and diffuse ground-glass opacities, mimicking an ILD flare—so it must be urgently excluded (and PCP prophylaxis considered) before simply escalating immunosuppression. Acute gout (wrong) causes joint inflammation, not diffuse lung opacities. Diabetes insipidus (wrong) is a water-balance disorder unrelated to this presentation. Anxiety hyperventilation (wrong) does not cause true hypoxemia or ground-glass changes. Costochondritis (wrong) is a chest-wall pain syndrome without imaging findings. Distinguishing infection from a flare is critical because the treatments diverge sharply.

cases.diagnosis: Rheumatoid arthritis–associated interstitial lung disease (NSIP pattern)
pearls: Suspect CTD-ILD when a patient with autoimmune disease develops progressive dyspnea, dry cough, basal Velcro crackles, and a restrictive pattern with low DLCO.
NSIP—ground-glass with fine reticulation, traction bronchiectasis, and relative subpleural sparing—is the most common CTD-ILD pattern, including in RA.
Inflammatory CTD-ILD is treated with corticosteroids plus a steroid-sparing immunosuppressant such as mycophenolate, alongside control of the underlying disease.
If fibrosis progresses despite immunosuppression, add an antifibrotic (e.g., nintedanib) for the progressive fibrosing phenotype.

cases.title: Breathless When Sitting Up in a Man With Liver Disease
cases.chiefComplaint: Breathlessness that gets worse when he sits or stands and improves when he lies down.
cases.history: A 56-year-old man with cirrhosis from prior alcohol use and hepatitis C (now treated and cured) presents with 8 months of progressive dyspnea and fatigue. He notes that his breathlessness and lightheadedness worsen on standing and improve when supine, and he tires quickly when walking. He has compensated cirrhosis with prior small-volume ascites controlled on diuretics, and he abstains from alcohol. Medications are spironolactone, furosemide, propranolol for varices, and lactulose as needed; he has no drug allergies. He does not smoke and has no occupational dust exposure or birds at home. Family history is noncontributory. He denies cough, fever, chest pain, paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnea, leg swelling beyond baseline, or hemoptysis. He has noted that his fingertips look bluish and his nails have changed shape. He is being evaluated as part of a liver transplant assessment, and his team is concerned about his low oxygen saturations, which seem out of proportion to his examination and chest imaging.
cases.exam: Chronically ill-appearing but comfortable lying flat; visibly more dyspneic when upright. There is central cyanosis, digital clubbing, and several spider angiomata over the chest, with palmar erythema. Lungs are clear with no crackles or wheeze. Cardiac exam is normal: regular rhythm, no murmur, normal JVP, no loud P2, and no peripheral edema beyond trace. Abdomen is soft with mild splenomegaly and no tense ascites. There is no asterixis. The remainder of the exam is unremarkable, and the breathlessness is notably positional.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: A man with cirrhosis has hypoxemia that worsens upright and improves supine (platypnea-orthodeoxia), clubbing, and cyanosis with clear lungs. What is the best initial test to identify the mechanism?
options: Exercise stress test for ischemia as the first step today
Spirometry with bronchodilator testing as the first step today
CT pulmonary angiography for embolism as the first step today
Arterial blood gas to calculate the alveolar–arterial gradient, followed by agitated-saline contrast echocardiography
Right heart catheterization as the first step today
stages.explanation: Platypnea-orthodeoxia with clear lungs in cirrhosis suggests intrapulmonary vascular dilatation; the workup starts with an ABG to document hypoxemia and a widened A–a gradient, then agitated-saline contrast echocardiography to detect a right-to-left intrapulmonary shunt (late left-heart bubbles). Right heart catheterization (wrong) is invasive and tests for pulmonary hypertension (portopulmonary), which is a different entity. CT angiography (wrong) targets embolism, not microvascular dilatation. Spirometry/bronchodilator testing (wrong) evaluates airway disease, which his clear lungs argue against. An exercise stress test (wrong) assesses ischemia and does not explain positional hypoxemia from intrapulmonary shunting.

stages.label: ABG & BUBBLE ECHO
stages.content:
ABG (seated, room air): PaO2 60 mmHg, PaCO2 35 mmHg, pH 7.44
A–a gradient: markedly widened
Standing ABG: PaO2 falls to 52 mmHg (orthodeoxia)
Agitated-saline contrast echocardiography: microbubbles appear in the left atrium after 5 cardiac cycles, indicating an intrapulmonary shunt (no intracardiac shunt)
Chest CT: no significant parenchymal disease; no pulmonary embolism

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: He has chronic liver disease, a widened A–a gradient with orthodeoxia, and delayed left-atrial bubble appearance on contrast echo. What is the diagnosis?
options: Chronic thromboembolic pulmonary hypertension
Hepatic hydrothorax as the primary diagnosis
Intracardiac right-to-left shunt (patent foramen ovale)
Portopulmonary hypertension
Hepatopulmonary syndrome
stages.explanation: The triad of liver disease, an elevated A–a gradient/hypoxemia, and evidence of intrapulmonary vascular dilatation (late left-atrial bubbles after 3–5 cycles) defines hepatopulmonary syndrome, with platypnea-orthodeoxia from basal-predominant dilated vessels. Portopulmonary hypertension (wrong) is elevated pulmonary arterial pressure from portal hypertension, with a different physiology and no intrapulmonary shunt. An intracardiac shunt/PFO (wrong) shows immediate (within 3 cycles) left-heart bubbles, not delayed. Hepatic hydrothorax (wrong) is a transudative pleural effusion, absent here on imaging. CTEPH (wrong) is excluded by the negative CT and does not cause delayed intrapulmonary bubble transit.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: He is diagnosed with hepatopulmonary syndrome and has resting hypoxemia. What is the definitive treatment, and what is the appropriate interim measure?
options: Long-term systemic corticosteroids as initial therapy
Transcatheter closure device for the intrapulmonary shunt as monotherapy
Liver transplantation is definitive
Lifelong anticoagulation to resolve the shunt as initial therapy
Pulmonary vasodilators such as epoprostenol as definitive therapy
stages.explanation: Hepatopulmonary syndrome can resolve after liver transplantation, which is the definitive treatment; meanwhile supplemental oxygen treats hypoxemia and is the mainstay of supportive care, and significant hypoxemia from HPS can actually upgrade transplant priority. Pulmonary vasodilators (wrong) are used in pulmonary arterial hypertension, not HPS, and could worsen shunting. Anticoagulation (wrong) does not treat dilated intrapulmonary vessels. A closure device (wrong) is impossible because HPS involves diffuse microvascular dilatation, not a single discrete shunt. Systemic corticosteroids (wrong) have no established role in HPS. Recognizing transplant as definitive while supporting oxygenation is the central concept.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: Which point is most important when counseling him and coordinating with the transplant team?
options: Severe hypoxemia from HPS may increase transplant priority,
Hepatopulmonary syndrome is a contraindication to liver transplantation with appropriate follow-up
No follow-up of oxygenation is needed once he is listed with appropriate follow-up
His hypoxemia will resolve immediately on the day of transplant with appropriate follow-up
Oxygen therapy will cure the underlying shunting permanently with appropriate follow-up
stages.explanation: HPS is not a contraindication—rather, qualifying hypoxemia can grant additional priority (exception points) for transplantation, and oxygenation typically improves gradually over weeks to months after a successful transplant as the intrapulmonary dilatations regress. Calling HPS a contraindication (wrong) is incorrect and would deny him definitive therapy. Expecting immediate resolution on transplant day (wrong) misstates the gradual recovery. Oxygen as a cure (wrong) is only supportive and does not reverse the vascular abnormality. No follow-up once listed (wrong) is unsafe given his progressive hypoxemia and the need to monitor candidacy and oxygen requirements.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: During transplant evaluation, the team also screens for the other major pulmonary vascular complication of portal hypertension. How does portopulmonary hypertension differ from hepatopulmonary syndrome and why does it matter?
options: Portopulmonary hypertension is elevated pulmonary arterial pressure and, if severe
Portopulmonary hypertension causes platypnea-orthodeoxia identical to HPS as the leading concern
Portopulmonary hypertension is diagnosed solely by bubble echocardiography as the leading concern
They are identical conditions with the same treatment as the leading concern
Portopulmonary hypertension always improves with supplemental oxygen alone as the leading concern
stages.explanation: Portopulmonary hypertension is pulmonary arterial hypertension arising in the setting of portal hypertension—elevated pulmonary pressures and vascular resistance, not the intrapulmonary vascular dilatation/shunt of HPS—and severe, uncontrolled portopulmonary hypertension can be a contraindication to liver transplant because of perioperative right-heart risk, sometimes requiring pulmonary vasodilator therapy first. Calling them identical (wrong) ignores opposite physiology and prognosis. Improving with oxygen alone (wrong) is false; it needs PAH-directed therapy. Producing identical platypnea-orthodeoxia (wrong) misattributes an HPS feature. Diagnosis solely by bubble echo (wrong) is incorrect—right heart catheterization confirms the pressures. Distinguishing the two is essential because management and transplant candidacy diverge.

cases.diagnosis: Hepatopulmonary syndrome
pearls: Suspect hepatopulmonary syndrome with the triad of liver disease, a widened A–a gradient/hypoxemia, and intrapulmonary vascular dilatation.
Platypnea-orthodeoxia (worse upright, better supine) and clubbing/cyanosis with clear lungs are classic clues.
Contrast echo shows delayed (after 3–5 cardiac cycles) left-atrial bubbles, distinguishing an intrapulmonary shunt from an immediate intracardiac shunt.
Liver transplantation is definitive and severe hypoxemia can grant priority; supplemental oxygen is the supportive bridge.

cases.title: Persistent Fever and Chest Pain After Pneumonia Treatment
cases.chiefComplaint: Continued fevers, pleuritic chest pain, and breathlessness despite antibiotics for pneumonia.
cases.history: A 50-year-old man was admitted 6 days ago with community-acquired pneumonia and started on ceftriaxone and azithromycin. Although he initially improved slightly, he now has persistent fevers, worsening right-sided pleuritic chest pain, breathlessness, and night sweats. He has poorly controlled type 2 diabetes (last HbA1c 9.2%) and a history of heavy alcohol use, with several recent episodes he describes as choking on food while intoxicated. Medications are metformin, the inpatient antibiotics, and subcutaneous insulin. He has no drug allergies. He smokes one pack daily. He lives alone and has poor dentition. Family history is noncontributory. He denies hemoptysis but reports producing foul-tasting sputum. His appetite is poor and he has lost several pounds. The team is concerned that he is failing to respond to appropriate antibiotics, and a repeat chest radiograph shows a new, enlarging right-sided pleural effusion that was not present on admission.
cases.exam: Ill-appearing, diaphoretic man. There is dullness to percussion, decreased breath sounds, and reduced tactile fremitus over the right lower hemithorax, with bronchial breath sounds and crackles just above the dullness. No wheeze on the left. Cardiac exam shows tachycardia, regular rhythm, no murmur, and normal JVP. Dentition is poor with gingival disease. There is no peripheral edema and calves are nontender. Abdomen is soft and nontender. The remainder of the exam is unremarkable aside from his toxic appearance.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: He has persistent fever and an enlarging pleural effusion despite appropriate antibiotics for pneumonia. What is the most important next step?
options: CT pulmonary angiography for pulmonary embolism in this clinical context
Diagnostic and therapeutic thoracentesis with pleural fluid analysis
Start anticoagulation empirically as the first step today
Broaden antibiotics and observe for another 48 hours without sampling the fluid
Repeat blood cultures only and continue current therapy
stages.explanation: A new or enlarging effusion in a patient failing antibiotics for pneumonia must be sampled: diagnostic (and often therapeutic) thoracentesis with pleural fluid analysis—pH, glucose, LDH, protein, cell count, Gram stain, and culture—determines whether this is a simple parapneumonic effusion, a complicated effusion, or empyema requiring drainage. Broadening antibiotics without sampling (wrong) delays source control and misses a drainable infection. CT angiography (wrong) targets embolism, which does not explain a fever and enlarging effusion. Empiric anticoagulation (wrong) is unindicated and potentially harmful. Repeating blood cultures alone (wrong) does not characterize the pleural space, where the actionable diagnosis lies.

stages.label: PLEURAL FLUID & IMAGING
stages.content:
Thoracentesis: frankly purulent, foul-smelling fluid aspirated
Pleural fluid pH 6.9
Pleural fluid glucose 28 mg/dL
Pleural fluid LDH 2,400 IU/L
Gram stain: mixed organisms including gram-positive cocci and gram-negative rods
Contrast chest CT: loculated right pleural collection with pleural enhancement and septations ('split pleura' sign)

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: The pleural fluid is frankly purulent with pH 6.9, glucose 28 mg/dL, high LDH, positive Gram stain, and a loculated, septated collection on CT. What is the diagnosis?
options: Transudative effusion from heart failure
Malignant pleural effusion in this clinical context
Empyema
Uncomplicated (simple) parapneumonic effusion
Chylothorax as the primary diagnosis
stages.explanation: Frank pus, pleural fluid pH <7.2 (here 6.9), low glucose, very high LDH, a positive Gram stain, and a loculated/septated collection with the split-pleura sign define an empyema. A simple parapneumonic effusion (wrong) is free-flowing with pH >7.2, normal glucose, and a negative Gram stain. A malignant effusion (wrong) is typically exudative but not frankly purulent and would show malignant cells. A transudate from heart failure (wrong) has low LDH/protein and is not purulent. Chylothorax (wrong) is milky and triglyceride-rich, not purulent with a low pH. The fluid characteristics mandate drainage, not antibiotics alone.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: He has a frank empyema with loculations. Beyond continuing appropriate IV antibiotics, what is the most appropriate initial intervention?
options: Pleurodesis with talc as initial therapy
Immediate pneumonectomy as initial therapy
Antibiotics alone, as drainage is unnecessary for empyema as initial therapy
Chest tube placement, with intrapleural tPA/DNase if drainage is inadequate due to loculations
Repeated needle thoracenteses only as initial therapy
stages.explanation: Empyema requires source control with tube thoracostomy plus appropriate antibiotics; when loculations impair drainage, intrapleural fibrinolytic therapy with tPA combined with DNase improves fluid clearance and reduces the need for surgery. Antibiotics alone (wrong) cannot clear a loculated purulent space. Repeated needle thoracenteses (wrong) are inadequate for an organizing, septated empyema. Immediate pneumonectomy (wrong) is far too aggressive as an initial step. Talc pleurodesis (wrong) is for recurrent malignant or persistent benign effusions, not active infection, and would be inappropriate in an infected pleural space. Surgery (VATS decortication) is reserved for failure of drainage and fibrinolytics.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: After 3 days of chest-tube drainage with intrapleural tPA/DNase, he still has persistent loculated collections, ongoing sepsis, and trapped lung on imaging. What is the most appropriate next step?
options: Continue the same fibrinolytic regimen for two more weeks
Surgical referral for video-assisted thoracoscopic surgery with decortication
Remove the chest tube and switch to oral antibiotics based on current risk
Begin talc pleurodesis through the existing tube with appropriate follow-up
Start systemic corticosteroids with appropriate follow-up
stages.explanation: When tube drainage plus intrapleural fibrinolytics fail to control a loculated empyema and the patient remains septic with trapped/entrapped lung, surgical management with VATS (debridement and decortication) is indicated to evacuate the infected, organized collection and re-expand the lung. Removing the tube and going to oral antibiotics (wrong) abandons source control during active sepsis. Continuing the same failed regimen for weeks (wrong) delays definitive treatment. Corticosteroids (wrong) have no role in empyema. Talc pleurodesis (wrong) is contraindicated in an actively infected space and does not address the organized rind causing trapped lung.

cases.diagnosis: Empyema (complicated, loculated post-pneumonic pleural effusion)
pearls: A new or enlarging effusion in a patient failing antibiotics for pneumonia requires prompt diagnostic thoracentesis.
Empyema is defined by frank pus or pleural fluid pH <7.2, low glucose, high LDH, a positive Gram stain/culture, and loculation with the split-pleura sign.
Treatment is antibiotics plus drainage; intrapleural tPA/DNase aids clearance of loculated collections.
Failure of drainage and fibrinolytics with ongoing sepsis or trapped lung warrants VATS decortication.

cases.title: Sudden Chest Pain After Repeated Vomiting
cases.chiefComplaint: Sudden chest pain and neck fullness after a night of forceful vomiting.
cases.history: A 20-year-old man presents to the emergency department with acute substernal chest pain that began after several hours of forceful, repeated vomiting following heavy alcohol intake at a party. The pain is sharp, worse with deep breathing and swallowing, and radiates to his neck and back. He also reports a 'crackling' sensation in his neck and a change in his voice. He is otherwise healthy, takes no regular medications, and has no allergies. He smokes occasionally and uses no inhaled drugs. He denies trauma, recent travel, fever, or cough, and has had no prior similar episodes. Family history is noncontributory. He is anxious but able to speak in full sentences. He specifically denies hematemesis, but the vomiting was severe and protracted. There is no history of asthma or recent diving, and he has not used a ventilator. On further questioning he notes that swallowing both food and liquids worsens the pain, raising concern in the treating team about possible esophageal injury.
cases.exam: Anxious young man, speaking in full sentences. There is palpable subcutaneous crepitus over the anterior neck and supraclavicular fossae. On cardiac auscultation a crunching, crackling sound synchronous with the heartbeat is heard over the precordium (Hamman sign). Lungs are clear bilaterally with equal breath sounds and no tracheal deviation; there is no tension physiology. The abdomen is soft and nontender without guarding or rigidity. There is no facial or chest-wall trauma. Peripheral pulses are equal, and there is no jugular venous distension.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: A young man has chest pain, neck crepitus, and a crunching sound synchronous with the heartbeat (Hamman sign) after forceful vomiting. What is the best initial imaging step?
options: Chest radiograph
Head CT as the first step today
V/Q scan as the first step today
Abdominal ultrasound
Immediate transthoracic echocardiography
stages.explanation: Subcutaneous emphysema and a Hamman crunch after forceful vomiting suggest air in the mediastinum; a chest radiograph is the appropriate first study, often showing lucent streaks of air outlining mediastinal structures, subcutaneous air, or a continuous diaphragm sign. Echocardiography (wrong) does not reliably detect mediastinal air and is not the initial test. Abdominal ultrasound (wrong) does not evaluate the mediastinum. Head CT (wrong) is unrelated to his thoracic presentation. A V/Q scan (wrong) assesses for pulmonary embolism, which does not explain crepitus and a Hamman crunch. The CXR is fast, available, and frequently diagnostic, guiding the need for further evaluation.

stages.label: IMAGING
stages.content:
CXR: lucent streaks of air outlining the mediastinal borders and along the left heart border; subcutaneous emphysema in the neck and supraclavicular soft tissues; no large pneumothorax; lungs otherwise clear
WBC 11.0 ×10³/µL
Troponin: negative
ECG: sinus tachycardia, no ischemic changes

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: The CXR confirms air in the mediastinum and subcutaneous tissues. Given the protracted vomiting and pain worsened by swallowing, what is the most critical condition that must be excluded?
options: Acute myocardial infarction
Spontaneous pneumothorax
Aortic dissection in this clinical context
Esophageal perforation
Pericarditis as the primary diagnosis
stages.explanation: Pneumomediastinum after forceful vomiting can be benign (spontaneous), but the protracted emesis and pain on swallowing raise the possibility of esophageal rupture (Boerhaave syndrome), a life-threatening cause of mediastinal air that must be excluded—typically with a water-soluble contrast esophagram or CT esophagography. Myocardial infarction (wrong) is excluded by a negative troponin and nonischemic ECG and does not cause crepitus. Spontaneous pneumothorax (wrong) is not the primary concern given mediastinal/subcutaneous air without a large pleural collection. Aortic dissection (wrong) is unlikely in a healthy 20-year-old and does not produce subcutaneous emphysema. Pericarditis (wrong) does not cause mediastinal free air.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: A water-soluble contrast esophagram shows no leak and CT confirms isolated pneumomediastinum without esophageal injury. What is the most appropriate management?
options: Immediate chest tube placement as initial therapy
Emergent surgical exploration of the mediastinum as initial therapy
Broad-spectrum antibiotics and urgent thoracotomy as initial therapy
Conservative management with rest, analgesia, antiemetics, observation,
Therapeutic anticoagulation as initial therapy
stages.explanation: Spontaneous (uncomplicated) pneumomediastinum with no esophageal injury is benign and self-limited; management is conservative—rest, analgesia, antiemetics to stop further straining, observation, and avoidance of maneuvers that raise airway pressure (Valsalva)—with the air reabsorbing over days. Surgical exploration (wrong) is unnecessary without perforation. A chest tube (wrong) treats pneumothorax, not isolated mediastinal air. Broad-spectrum antibiotics and thoracotomy (wrong) are reserved for esophageal perforation/mediastinitis, which was excluded here. Anticoagulation (wrong) has no role and risks harm. Confirming the esophagus is intact is what allows safe conservative management.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: He is being observed and improving. Which finding during observation would most strongly indicate a missed esophageal perforation requiring escalation?
options: Transient sinus tachycardia that resolves with hydration
Mild residual neck crepitus that is stable based on current risk
New high fever, rising leukocytosis, sepsis,
Continued mild pain with swallowing that is improving
A small amount of subcutaneous air on repeat film that is decreasing
stages.explanation: Esophageal perforation leading to mediastinitis presents with worsening systemic toxicity—high fever, rising leukocytosis, sepsis, and a pleural effusion (often left-sided)—and demands urgent escalation with broad-spectrum antibiotics and surgical/endoscopic intervention. Stable mild neck crepitus (wrong) is expected and reassuring in benign pneumomediastinum. Transient tachycardia resolving with fluids (wrong) is nonspecific and not alarming. Improving pain with swallowing (wrong) suggests a benign course. Decreasing subcutaneous air on repeat imaging (wrong) indicates resolution. The danger sign is evolving sepsis with an effusion, signaling that the initially excluded perforation may have been missed or evolved.

cases.diagnosis: Spontaneous pneumomediastinum (with exclusion of esophageal perforation/Boerhaave syndrome)
pearls: Subcutaneous neck crepitus and a Hamman crunch after forceful vomiting suggest pneumomediastinum; CXR is the first imaging test.
Always exclude esophageal perforation (Boerhaave) after protracted vomiting using a water-soluble contrast esophagram or CT esophagography.
Isolated spontaneous pneumomediastinum is benign and managed conservatively with rest, analgesia, antiemetics, and avoidance of Valsalva.
Evolving fever, leukocytosis, sepsis, and a (often left-sided) pleural effusion signal mediastinitis from a missed perforation and require urgent escalation.

cases.title: Headache, Unsteadiness, and Confusion on a Mountain Trek
cases.chiefComplaint: Worsening headache, nausea, and now stumbling and confusion during a high-altitude trek.
cases.history: A 35-year-old woman is evaluated at a high-altitude trekking clinic at approximately 4,300 m. She ascended rapidly over 3 days from a low-altitude city to base camp without acclimatization days. For 2 days she has had a throbbing headache, nausea, poor appetite, fatigue, and disrupted sleep, which she attributed to exertion. Today her companions report she became increasingly clumsy, unsteady on her feet, and confused, with slurred responses and difficulty following simple instructions. She is otherwise healthy, takes only an oral contraceptive, and has no allergies. She does not smoke and rarely drinks. She has no history of migraines, seizures, or prior altitude illness, and has not taken acetazolamide. Family history is noncontributory. There is no head trauma, fever, neck stiffness, or recent illness, and no diarrhea or dehydrating illness. Her companions note she has not been drinking carbonated alcoholic beverages and that her decline has accelerated over the past several hours despite resting.
cases.exam: Drowsy and disoriented to time and place, with slowed, slurred speech. She has a clearly ataxic gait, unable to perform tandem walking, and dysmetria on finger-to-nose testing. Pupils are equal and reactive; there is no focal motor deficit and no neck rigidity. Funduscopy is limited but suggests mild disc blurring. Lungs are clear without crackles or wheeze, and there is no frothy sputum. Cardiac exam shows tachycardia, regular rhythm, no murmur, and normal JVP. There is no peripheral edema. The neurologic decline with ataxia and altered mentation is the dominant finding.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: After rapid ascent to 4,300 m, she progressed from headache and nausea to ataxia and confusion. What is the most likely diagnosis driving immediate management?
options: High-altitude cerebral edema , progressing from acute mountain sickness
Bacterial meningitis as the first step today
Ischemic stroke as the first step today
High-altitude pulmonary edema (HAPE) as the first step today
Hypoglycemia as the first step today
stages.explanation: Acute mountain sickness (headache, nausea, fatigue, poor sleep) that progresses to ataxia and altered mental status defines high-altitude cerebral edema (HACE)—truncal ataxia and encephalopathy are the hallmark signs, and recognition drives immediate treatment. HAPE (wrong) presents with dyspnea, cough, and crackles/frothy sputum, which she lacks (clear lungs). Bacterial meningitis (wrong) typically features fever and neck stiffness, both absent. Ischemic stroke (wrong) usually causes focal deficits, not the global ataxic encephalopathy after rapid ascent. Hypoglycemia (wrong) is possible but does not fit the classic altitude-illness progression and would be quickly excluded by a glucose check; the syndrome and setting point to HACE.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: She has HACE at 4,300 m with worsening ataxia and confusion. What is the single most important intervention?
options: Empiric IV antibiotics for presumed meningitis as initial therapy
Acetazolamide monotherapy and continued observation at altitude
Continue ascent to reach definitive care faster as initial therapy
Lumbar puncture before any treatment as initial therapy
Immediate descent to lower altitude , with supplemental oxygen
stages.explanation: The cornerstone of HACE management is immediate descent—the single most effective intervention—combined with supplemental oxygen and dexamethasone to reduce cerebral edema; a portable hyperbaric (Gamow) bag can temporize if descent is delayed. Continuing to ascend (wrong) is dangerous and accelerates deterioration. Acetazolamide alone with continued observation at altitude (wrong) is inadequate for established HACE; acetazolamide is mainly for AMS prevention/treatment, not as sole therapy for cerebral edema, and staying at altitude is unsafe. Lumbar puncture first (wrong) delays life-saving descent and is not indicated. Empiric antibiotics for meningitis (wrong) misdiagnoses an altitude syndrome lacking fever or meningismus.

stages.label: FIELD ASSESSMENT & RESPONSE
stages.content:
Point-of-care glucose: 92 mg/dL (normal)
Dexamethasone administered and supplemental oxygen started; descent initiated
SpO2 improves to 90% on oxygen during descent
After descending ~1,000 m, mentation begins to clear and gait steadies
No crackles, no frothy sputum, lungs remain clear throughout

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: She improves with descent, oxygen, and dexamethasone. For future high-altitude travel, what is the most appropriate counseling for prevention?
options: Ascend as quickly as possible to minimize time at altitude with appropriate follow-up
Take dexamethasone daily indefinitely as routine prophylaxis at all altitudes with appropriate follow-up
Use graded ascent with acclimatization days,
Resume the original itinerary immediately once she feels better with appropriate follow-up
Avoid all supplemental oxygen because it impairs acclimatization with appropriate follow-up
stages.explanation: Prevention centers on gradual, graded ascent with rest/acclimatization days ('climb high, sleep low'), avoiding overly rapid altitude gain, and considering acetazolamide prophylaxis for those at risk; the cardinal rule is to never continue ascending while symptomatic and to descend if symptoms worsen. Ascending as fast as possible (wrong) precipitates altitude illness.
