Comprehensive multidisciplinary CF care integrates pulmonary, nutritional, and modulator therapy.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: Given his pancreatic insufficiency and CF, for which additional complication should he be routinely screened?
options: Cystic fibrosis-related diabetes via annual oral glucose tolerance testing
Diabetes insipidus from posterior pituitary failure based on current risk
Type 1 autoimmune diabetes from islet-cell antibodies in this clinical context
Primary hyperaldosteronism with appropriate follow-up
stages.explanation: Cystic fibrosis-related diabetes (CFRD) is a common complication that increases with age and pancreatic insufficiency, arising from progressive pancreatic fibrosis with insulin deficiency (plus a variable insulin-resistance component during illness). Guidelines recommend annual screening with an oral glucose tolerance test starting in adolescence, because CFRD worsens nutrition and lung function and benefits from insulin therapy. It is not classic type 1 autoimmune diabetes, the mechanism is destruction of islet architecture by pancreatic fibrosis, not islet-cell autoimmunity. Primary hyperaldosteronism is unrelated to CF pathophysiology. Diabetes insipidus involves ADH deficiency from the posterior pituitary and is not a recognized CF complication. Recognizing and screening for CFRD, along with monitoring for CF-related bone disease and liver disease, is part of comprehensive multisystem CF care.

cases.diagnosis: Cystic fibrosis (F508del homozygous) with recurrent Pseudomonas exacerbations
pearls: Defective CFTR causes thick dehydrated secretions and impaired mucociliary clearance, driving chronic Pseudomonas aeruginosa biofilm infection.
Manage CF exacerbations with sputum culture-directed anti-pseudomonal antibiotics (often dual-agent), intensified airway clearance, and nutrition.
Elexacaftor/tezacaftor/ivacaftor benefits patients with at least one F508del allele; ivacaftor monotherapy is for gating mutations like G551D.
Nutrition is core to CF outcomes: pancreatic enzymes, fat-soluble vitamins (A,D,E,K), and a high-calorie diet; continue airway clearance even on modulators.

cases.title: Worsening Asthma With Brown Mucus Plugs
cases.chiefComplaint: Worsening cough, wheeze, and recurrent brown mucus plugs over the past three months.
cases.history: A 33-year-old man with childhood-onset asthma presents with progressively worsening wheeze, productive cough, and low-grade fevers over three months. He reports coughing up thick brown-flecked mucus plugs and occasional streaks of blood. His asthma, previously controlled on a low-dose inhaled corticosteroid, now requires frequent albuterol and two oral prednisone bursts in the past two months. He has had three episodes described as 'pneumonia' on chest films over the past year, each with shifting locations that resolved with steroids. He denies sick contacts, recent travel, or weight loss. Medications: fluticasone-salmeterol 250/50 one puff twice daily, albuterol PRN, and a recently completed prednisone taper. No known drug allergies. He works as an accountant, has never smoked, and has no pets. Family history is notable for atopy and eczema in his mother. He reports allergic rhinitis since adolescence. Review of systems is positive for malaise and intermittent right-sided pleuritic chest discomfort but negative for night sweats.
cases.exam: The patient is in no acute distress but coughs frequently during the encounter. Lung exam reveals scattered expiratory wheezes bilaterally and coarse crackles at the right upper lung field. There is no clubbing, cyanosis, or peripheral edema. Cardiac exam shows a normal S1 and S2 without murmurs or right ventricular heave. There is no jugular venous distention. Nasal mucosa is pale and boggy with clear discharge consistent with allergic rhinitis. The skin shows healed flexural eczematous changes. No lymphadenopathy is palpated.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which feature of this presentation is most concerning for a complication beyond poorly controlled asthma?
options: Pale, boggy nasal mucosa with clear rhinorrhea as the first step today
Scattered expiratory wheezes on lung auscultation as the first step today
A history of childhood-onset asthma requiring inhaled corticosteroids
Recurrent fleeting infiltrates in different locations with brown mucus plugs
stages.explanation: Recurrent migratory ('fleeting') infiltrates plus expectoration of brown mucus plugs in an asthmatic strongly suggests allergic bronchopulmonary aspergillosis (ABPA), in which Aspergillus colonizes the airways and triggers an intense immune response with mucoid impaction. Pale boggy nasal mucosa simply reflects coexisting allergic rhinitis, an expected atopic feature. Childhood-onset asthma on inhaled steroids is the baseline disease, not a complication. Scattered expiratory wheezes are nonspecific and seen in any asthma exacerbation. The combination of steroid-dependent asthma, migratory infiltrates, and mucus plugs is the classic clinical triad that should prompt specific serologic and imaging workup for ABPA.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which initial test is most useful to screen for the suspected diagnosis?
options: Total serum IgE level
Sputum acid-fast bacilli smear and culture
Alpha-1 antitrypsin level in this clinical context
High-resolution CT of the chest without contrast
stages.explanation: A markedly elevated total serum IgE is a sensitive screening test for ABPA; a normal total IgE (when off steroids) makes active ABPA very unlikely, so it is an excellent first step. Sputum AFB screens for tuberculosis, which is in the differential for migratory infiltrates but does not fit the atopic, steroid-responsive picture. HRCT is valuable later to detect central bronchiectasis and mucoid impaction but is not the initial screen. Alpha-1 antitrypsin deficiency causes basilar panacinar emphysema, not migratory infiltrates with mucus plugging. Once IgE is elevated, confirmatory testing with Aspergillus-specific IgE/IgG and skin testing follows.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
Total serum IgE 2,840 IU/mL (normal <100)
Absolute eosinophil count 1,450 cells/µL
Aspergillus fumigatus-specific IgE: elevated
Aspergillus fumigatus-specific IgG (precipitins): positive
Aspergillus skin prick test: immediate wheal-and-flare positive
HRCT chest: central (perihilar) bronchiectasis with mucoid impaction creating finger-in-glove opacities; patchy right upper lobe infiltrate
Sputum culture: scant Aspergillus fumigatus

stages.label: CONFIRMATION
stages.question: Which CT finding is most specific for the suspected diagnosis in this asthmatic patient?
options: Central bronchiectasis with mucoid impaction
Bilateral lower-lobe honeycombing in this setting
Diffuse ground-glass opacities with septal thickening
Apical bullae with paraseptal emphysema in this setting
stages.explanation: Central bronchiectasis predominantly involving the perihilar/medial airways, often with mucoid impaction producing a 'finger-in-glove' appearance, is highly characteristic of ABPA and distinguishes it from other causes of bronchiectasis, which typically spare the central airways. Apical bullae and paraseptal emphysema suggest smoking-related or alpha-1 disease. Diffuse ground-glass with septal thickening suggests hypersensitivity pneumonitis or edema. Lower-lobe honeycombing indicates usual interstitial pneumonia/IPF. Combined with elevated IgE, positive Aspergillus serologies, eosinophilia, and the asthma history, central bronchiectasis satisfies the diagnostic criteria for ABPA and guides treatment.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate first-line treatment for this acute exacerbation of ABPA?
options: Surgical lobectomy of the affected segment as initial therapy
Oral corticosteroids, with consideration of adjunctive itraconazole
Inhaled tobramycin as initial therapy
Long-term azithromycin monotherapy as initial therapy
stages.explanation: Systemic corticosteroids are the cornerstone of treatment for an ABPA exacerbation: they suppress the hypersensitivity response, clear infiltrates, and lower IgE. Adjunctive antifungal therapy with itraconazole reduces the fungal antigen burden, allows steroid sparing, and decreases exacerbations, so it is commonly added. Inhaled tobramycin treats chronic gram-negative airway infection (e.g., in cystic fibrosis or non-CF bronchiectasis), not the eosinophilic immune response of ABPA. Lobectomy is not indicated for diffuse, immunologically driven disease. Azithromycin monotherapy has anti-inflammatory benefit in bronchiectasis but does not control the acute hypersensitivity reaction driving this patient's exacerbation.

stages.label: FOLLOW-UP
stages.question: Which parameter is best for monitoring treatment response and detecting future exacerbations?
options: Serial D-dimer levels in this setting
Serial total serum IgE levels
Serial absolute neutrophil counts
Serial procalcitonin levels
stages.explanation: Total serum IgE tracks ABPA disease activity: a 25-50% decline with treatment indicates response, and a subsequent rise often heralds an exacerbation, so it is the standard monitoring marker along with symptoms and imaging. Absolute neutrophil count reflects bacterial infection or steroid effect, not ABPA activity. Procalcitonin rises with bacterial sepsis and is not useful for this eosinophilic, fungal-driven process. D-dimer assesses thromboembolism. Establishing the patient's baseline IgE after the acute response allows clinicians to define a threshold above which recurrent flares are suspected and treatment is reintensified.

cases.diagnosis: Allergic bronchopulmonary aspergillosis (ABPA)
pearls: Suspect ABPA in any asthmatic (or cystic fibrosis patient) with recurrent fleeting infiltrates, brown mucus plugs, and steroid dependence.
Diagnostic pillars: markedly elevated total IgE (often >1,000 IU/mL), positive Aspergillus-specific IgE/IgG and skin test, eosinophilia, and central bronchiectasis on HRCT.
Oral corticosteroids are first-line; add itraconazole to reduce fungal burden and spare steroids.
Follow serial total IgE to gauge response and anticipate exacerbations.

cases.title: Subacute Breathlessness in a Bird Keeper
cases.chiefComplaint: Progressive shortness of breath and dry cough over the past two months.
cases.history: A 50-year-old man presents with two months of gradually worsening exertional dyspnea and a nonproductive cough. He notes that he feels mildly feverish and fatigued, with symptoms partially improving when he travels for work and returning when he is home. He breeds and shows pigeons and parakeets, spending hours daily in a poorly ventilated aviary attached to his home, and recently expanded his flock. He denies wheeze, chest pain, orthopnea, or leg swelling. He has lost about 4 kg unintentionally. Past medical history is unremarkable; he takes no regular medications and has no allergies. He has never smoked and drinks alcohol socially. There are no pets indoors besides the birds and no recent travel abroad. Family history is noncontributory. Review of systems is negative for joint pain, rash, hemoptysis, and night sweats. He has not noticed symptoms among other household members, though his spouse rarely enters the aviary. He has not tried any treatment beyond over-the-counter cough remedies without benefit.
cases.exam: The patient appears mildly dyspneic with exertion when walking to the exam room. Lung auscultation reveals bibasilar fine inspiratory 'velcro' crackles without wheeze; mid-inspiratory squeaks are appreciated. There is no clubbing. Cardiac exam is normal with no murmurs, gallops, or right ventricular heave, and jugular venous pressure is normal. There is no peripheral edema, rash, or synovitis. Oxygen saturation drops to 86% after ambulating in the hallway. The remainder of the exam, including the abdomen and skin, is unremarkable.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which element of the history is most important in framing the differential diagnosis?
options: Symptom improvement away from home
Social alcohol use as the first step today
Unremarkable past medical history in this clinical context
Absence of indoor pets other than birds
stages.explanation: A temporal link between symptoms and a specific environmental exposure - improving away from home and recurring with aviary contact - is the hallmark history for hypersensitivity pneumonitis (HP), here 'bird fancier's lung' from avian proteins. This exposure-response relationship is the single most diagnostically valuable clue. Social alcohol use is not relevant to this interstitial process. The absence of other indoor pets does not advance the differential and actually reinforces that birds are the dominant antigen source. An unremarkable past medical history is reassuring but nonspecific. Recognizing the antigen-symptom temporal pattern directs targeted testing and the central intervention of antigen avoidance.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
WBC 9.8 ×10³/µL with normal differential
CBC otherwise unremarkable; eosinophils normal
Serum avian (pigeon/budgerigar) precipitating IgG antibodies: positive
HRCT chest: diffuse centrilobular ground-glass nodules with mosaic attenuation and air trapping on expiratory images; mid-to-upper zone predominance
No honeycombing or pleural effusion
Spirometry: FVC 68% predicted, FEV1 70% predicted, FEV1/FVC 0.82 (restrictive); DLCO 55% predicted

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: The HRCT shows mosaic attenuation with expiratory air trapping and centrilobular ground-glass nodules. What does this pattern most strongly indicate?
options: Small-airway involvement typical of subacute hypersensitivity pneumonitis
Centrilobular emphysema from tobacco use as the primary diagnosis
Chronic pulmonary thromboembolic disease as the primary diagnosis
Lymphangitic spread of malignancy as the primary diagnosis
stages.explanation: Centrilobular ground-glass nodules with mosaic attenuation and expiratory air trapping reflect cellular bronchiolitis and small-airway obstruction characteristic of subacute HP. The mid-to-upper zone predominance and absence of honeycombing fit the nonfibrotic form. Centrilobular emphysema produces low-attenuation areas without nodules and occurs in smokers - this patient never smoked. Lymphangitic carcinomatosis causes nodular interstitial/septal thickening, not air trapping with centrilobular nodules. Chronic thromboembolic disease can cause mosaic perfusion, but it lacks centrilobular ground-glass nodules and is accompanied by pulmonary hypertension and vascular findings. The pattern, plus positive avian precipitins and exposure history, supports HP.

stages.label: CONFIRMATION
stages.question: If diagnostic uncertainty remained, which test would most help confirm hypersensitivity pneumonitis?
options: Methacholine challenge testing as the immediate next step
Sputum cytology for malignant cells in this setting
Serum brain natriuretic peptide before discharge planning
Bronchoalveolar lavage demonstrating marked lymphocytosis
stages.explanation: Bronchoalveolar lavage in HP characteristically shows a striking lymphocytosis (often >30-50%), supporting the diagnosis when imaging and serology are not definitive; transbronchial biopsy may show poorly formed non-necrotizing granulomas with bronchiolocentric inflammation. Sputum cytology evaluates malignancy, which is not suggested here. BNP assesses heart failure, inconsistent with this exposure-linked interstitial pattern and normal cardiac exam. Methacholine challenge tests for airway hyperreactivity in asthma, not interstitial lung disease. BAL lymphocytosis, combined with antigen exposure, compatible HRCT, and positive precipitins, strongly establishes HP and avoids the need for surgical lung biopsy in many cases.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the single most important intervention for this patient?
options: Inhaled bronchodilator monotherapy as initial therapy
Lifelong broad-spectrum antibiotics as initial therapy
Complete and permanent avoidance of the inciting antigen
Immediate referral for lung transplantation
stages.explanation: Permanent removal from the offending antigen - in this case rehoming the birds and thoroughly cleaning or vacating the contaminated environment - is the cornerstone of HP management and the strongest determinant of long-term outcome. Without antigen avoidance, disease progresses toward fibrosis despite other therapy. A course of corticosteroids can accelerate recovery in symptomatic subacute disease but does not change long-term prognosis if exposure continues. Antibiotics treat infection, which is not the mechanism here. Bronchodilators may give minor symptomatic relief but do not address the immunologic process. Transplantation is reserved for end-stage fibrotic disease, far beyond this reversible subacute stage.

stages.label: PROGNOSIS
stages.question: What is the major long-term risk if antigen exposure continues despite treatment?
options: Progression to severe persistent asthma
Onset of chronic eosinophilic pneumonia
Development of irreversible pulmonary fibrosis
Transformation into small-cell lung cancer
stages.explanation: Continued antigen exposure drives chronic HP, which can evolve into irreversible pulmonary fibrosis with traction bronchiectasis and honeycombing, carrying a prognosis approaching that of idiopathic pulmonary fibrosis. This is why permanent avoidance is emphasized. HP is not a precursor to asthma; airway hyperreactivity is not its defining mechanism. There is no established progression of HP to small-cell lung cancer. Chronic eosinophilic pneumonia is a distinct eosinophil-driven disease unrelated to avian antigen sensitization and is not a sequela of HP. Early recognition and antigen removal in the subacute, cellular phase offer the best chance to prevent fibrotic transformation.

cases.diagnosis: Hypersensitivity pneumonitis (bird fancier's lung)
pearls: An exposure-symptom temporal relationship (better away, worse on re-exposure) is the key clue to hypersensitivity pneumonitis.
Subacute HP shows centrilobular ground-glass nodules, mosaic attenuation, and expiratory air trapping with upper/mid-zone predominance.
BAL lymphocytosis and positive serum precipitins support the diagnosis; honeycombing signals fibrotic chronic HP.
Permanent antigen avoidance is the most important intervention; steroids only speed symptomatic recovery.

cases.title: Slowly Progressive Breathlessness in a Former Shipyard Worker
cases.chiefComplaint: Gradually worsening shortness of breath on exertion over several years.
cases.history: A 72-year-old man presents with insidious, progressive exertional dyspnea over the past five years, now limiting him to walking one block before stopping. He has a dry cough but denies hemoptysis, chest pain, fever, or weight loss. He worked for 30 years in a naval shipyard insulating pipes and boilers, retiring at age 60; he recalls heavy dust exposure and rarely wearing a respirator. He smoked one pack daily for 20 years and quit at age 55. Medications include lisinopril 10 mg daily for hypertension and as-needed acetaminophen. He has no known allergies. He lives with his wife and is independent in activities of daily living. Family history includes coronary disease in his father. Review of systems is negative for orthopnea, paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnea, joint pain, and skin changes. He has had no prior pulmonary evaluation. He reports that several former coworkers developed lung problems. He denies asbestos exposure in any hobby and has no history of recurrent pneumonias.
cases.exam: The patient is comfortable at rest but becomes dyspneic with exertion. Lung auscultation reveals fine, dry, late-inspiratory 'velcro' crackles at both lung bases. Digital clubbing is present. There is no wheeze. Cardiac exam shows a regular rhythm without murmurs; there is no jugular venous distention, right ventricular heave, or peripheral edema. No cyanosis is noted at rest. The remainder of the exam, including the skin and joints, is unremarkable. Oxygen saturation falls to 88% with ambulation in the hallway.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which historical detail is most critical in guiding the workup?
options: His father's coronary artery disease as the first step today
His 20-pack-year remote smoking history as the first step today
Decades of occupational insulation dust exposure with a long latency before symptoms
Use of lisinopril for hypertension as the first step today
stages.explanation: Thirty years of pipe/boiler insulation work in a shipyard is a classic high-risk asbestos exposure, and the 10-20+ year latency before fibrosis appears is characteristic of asbestos-related disease. This occupational history most directly shapes the differential toward asbestosis and pleural disease. The smoking history is relevant (it multiplies lung cancer risk and can cause emphysema) but does not explain basilar fibrosis with clubbing as specifically. The father's coronary disease is unrelated to this interstitial process. Lisinopril can cause cough but not progressive fibrotic dyspnea with clubbing and basilar crackles. Eliciting and quantifying the asbestos exposure latency is the pivotal step.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
Chest radiograph: bilateral lower-zone reticular opacities and calcified pleural plaques along the diaphragm and lateral chest wall
HRCT chest: subpleural, lower-lobe-predominant reticulation with septal thickening and early honeycombing; bilateral calcified pleural plaques; no dominant mass or effusion
Spirometry: FVC 64% predicted, FEV1 66% predicted, FEV1/FVC 0.84; DLCO 52% predicted
BNP 70 pg/mL (normal)

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which combination of findings best supports the diagnosis of asbestosis specifically (rather than another fibrotic lung disease)?
options: Mosaic attenuation with expiratory air trapping as the primary diagnosis
Upper-lobe nodules with eggshell-calcified hilar nodes as the primary diagnosis
Lower-lobe-predominant fibrosis accompanied by bilateral calcified pleural plaques
Migratory infiltrates with peripheral eosinophilia as the primary diagnosis
stages.explanation: Asbestosis produces lower-lobe-predominant, subpleural interstitial fibrosis, and the coexistence of bilateral calcified pleural plaques (a marker of prior asbestos exposure) strongly anchors the diagnosis and distinguishes it from idiopathic pulmonary fibrosis, which lacks plaques. Upper-lobe nodules with eggshell-calcified nodes describe silicosis. Migratory infiltrates with eosinophilia suggest ABPA or eosinophilic pneumonia. Mosaic attenuation with air trapping points to hypersensitivity pneumonitis. The restrictive PFTs with reduced DLCO confirm interstitial fibrosis, and the pleural plaques plus exposure latency complete the asbestosis picture rather than an idiopathic or inhalational alternative.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: Given his combined asbestos exposure and smoking history, which malignancy risk is most dramatically increased?
options: Chronic lymphocytic leukemia
Bronchogenic carcinoma
Renal cell carcinoma
Hepatocellular carcinoma
stages.explanation: Asbestos and tobacco smoke act synergistically (multiplicatively) to raise the risk of bronchogenic carcinoma, which is the most common asbestos-related cancer and far exceeds the risk of mesothelioma in absolute numbers. This synergy makes lung cancer his greatest malignancy concern and underscores the importance of continued smoking abstinence and vigilance. Renal cell carcinoma is linked to smoking but not asbestos synergy. Chronic lymphocytic leukemia and hepatocellular carcinoma are not characteristically associated with asbestos. Notably, mesothelioma risk is increased by asbestos but is not amplified by smoking; lung cancer is the dominant combined risk in this patient.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate management approach for his asbestosis?
options: Empiric antifungal therapy as initial therapy
Supportive care, smoking-cessation reinforcement, vaccinations,
High-dose oral corticosteroids to reverse the fibrosis as monotherapy
Therapeutic plasma exchange as initial therapy
stages.explanation: Asbestosis has no disease-reversing therapy, so management is supportive: ensuring continued smoking abstinence, administering influenza and pneumococcal vaccines, providing supplemental oxygen for hypoxemia, pulmonary rehabilitation, and surveillance for lung cancer and mesothelioma. Corticosteroids do not reverse established fibrotic pneumoconiosis and add adverse effects. Antifungal therapy treats fungal disease such as ABPA, not asbestosis. Plasma exchange is used for antibody-mediated disorders (e.g., anti-GBM disease), not pneumoconiosis. Emphasizing smoking cessation is especially important given the multiplicative lung cancer risk, and counseling about the irreversible, slowly progressive nature of the disease guides realistic expectations.

cases.diagnosis: Asbestosis with pleural plaques
pearls: Asbestosis causes lower-lobe-predominant fibrosis; bilateral calcified pleural plaques are the fingerprint of prior asbestos exposure.
Expect a long latency (often >15-20 years) between exposure and clinical disease.
Asbestos and smoking multiply lung cancer risk; smoking does NOT raise mesothelioma risk.
No therapy reverses asbestosis; management is supportive with smoking cessation, vaccines, oxygen, and cancer surveillance.

cases.title: Exertional Breathlessness in a Sandblaster
cases.chiefComplaint: Worsening shortness of breath and dry cough over the past two years.
cases.history: A 55-year-old man presents with two years of progressive exertional dyspnea and a persistent dry cough. He has worked for 25 years as a sandblaster and stone cutter in monument fabrication, often in enclosed spaces with inconsistent respirator use. He denies hemoptysis, fever, night sweats, or weight loss, though he reports some recent fatigue. He has never been screened for tuberculosis. He smoked half a pack daily for 15 years and quit two years ago. Medications: none regularly. No known allergies. He lives alone and has no pets. Family history is noncontributory. Review of systems is otherwise negative for chest pain, orthopnea, and leg swelling. He recalls being told years ago that a routine chest x-ray showed 'spots' but did not follow up. He has had no prior pulmonary function testing. He drinks alcohol occasionally and denies illicit drug use. He has not traveled internationally and has no known TB contacts, but he is concerned because a coworker was recently diagnosed with tuberculosis.
cases.exam: The patient is well-appearing and in no distress. Lung auscultation reveals mildly diminished breath sounds in the upper zones with scattered fine crackles; no wheeze. There is no clubbing or cyanosis. Cardiac exam is normal without murmurs or right ventricular heave, and there is no jugular venous distention or peripheral edema. There is no cervical or supraclavicular lymphadenopathy. The remainder of the exam is unremarkable. Oxygen saturation is preserved at rest and falls modestly to 91% with exertion.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
Chest radiograph: numerous small rounded nodules predominating in the upper lung zones; bilateral hilar lymphadenopathy with peripheral ('eggshell') calcification
HRCT chest: multiple small upper-zone centrilobular and subpleural nodules, some coalescing; eggshell-calcified hilar and mediastinal nodes
Spirometry: FVC 78% predicted, FEV1 80% predicted, FEV1/FVC 0.83; DLCO 70% predicted
QuantiFERON-TB Gold: pending

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which imaging finding is most characteristic of the suspected occupational lung disease?
options: Cavitary upper-lobe consolidation with air-fluid levels
Diffuse ground-glass opacities with mosaic attenuation
Lower-lobe honeycombing with pleural plaques
Upper-zone nodules with eggshell-calcified hilar lymph nodes
stages.explanation: Upper-lung-zone small nodules accompanied by eggshell (peripheral rim) calcification of hilar lymph nodes are classic for silicosis, fitting this sandblaster's heavy crystalline silica exposure. Lower-lobe honeycombing with pleural plaques describes asbestosis. Diffuse ground-glass with mosaic attenuation suggests hypersensitivity pneumonitis. Cavitary upper-lobe consolidation with air-fluid levels suggests a lung abscess or reactivation tuberculosis - relevant because silicosis increases TB risk, but the chronic nodular pattern with eggshell nodes points to the underlying pneumoconiosis. Recognizing eggshell calcification helps differentiate silicosis (and sometimes sarcoidosis) from other dust diseases.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: Because of this diagnosis, for which infection should this patient be specifically screened?
options: Cytomegalovirus
Mycobacterium tuberculosis
Pneumocystis jirovecii
Legionella pneumophila
stages.explanation: Silica impairs macrophage function, markedly increasing susceptibility to tuberculosis (and other mycobacteria); all silicosis patients should be screened for latent and active TB, with treatment of latent infection given the elevated reactivation risk. His coworker's recent TB diagnosis further heightens concern. Pneumocystis jirovecii pneumonia occurs in severe T-cell immunodeficiency (e.g., advanced HIV), not silicosis. CMV disease arises in transplant or severely immunocompromised hosts. Legionella causes acute pneumonia and is not specifically linked to silicosis. The silica-tuberculosis association ('silicotuberculosis') is high-yield: a QuantiFERON or tuberculin skin test plus symptom review and imaging is essential here.

stages.label: FOLLOW-UP TESTING
stages.content:
QuantiFERON-TB Gold: positive
Three sputum AFB smears: negative
Sputum mycobacterial cultures: pending
Repeat chest CT: no cavitation or new consolidation; stable upper-zone nodules
HIV antigen/antibody: negative
Liver function tests: within normal limits

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Given a positive QuantiFERON with negative AFB smears and no active disease, what is the most appropriate next step?
options: No treatment; repeat testing in one year as initial therapy
Treat for latent tuberculosis infection after excluding active disease
Begin four-drug therapy for active tuberculosis immediately
Start high-dose corticosteroids for the silicosis as initial therapy
stages.explanation: A positive interferon-gamma release assay with negative smears and no clinical or radiographic evidence of active TB indicates latent tuberculosis infection. In silicosis, the reactivation risk is high, so latent TB treatment (e.g., isoniazid plus rifapentine, or isoniazid) is strongly indicated once active disease is excluded. Doing nothing ignores the elevated reactivation risk conferred by silica. Four-drug active-TB therapy is reserved for confirmed or strongly suspected active disease, which is not present here (negative smears, no cavitation, cultures still pending). Corticosteroids do not treat silicosis and would further impair host defenses against TB, worsening the risk.

stages.label: PREVENTION
stages.question: What is the most effective measure to prevent progression of his underlying lung disease?
options: Prophylactic lobectomy as the immediate next step
Lifelong inhaled corticosteroids as the immediate next step
Routine therapeutic phlebotomy as the immediate next step
Elimination of further silica exposure with proper workplace controls
stages.explanation: There is no cure for silicosis, so preventing further dust exposure is paramount - through engineering controls, wet-cutting techniques, adequate ventilation, fitted respirators, and ultimately removal from ongoing exposure. This halts additional silica accumulation that drives progression, including to progressive massive fibrosis. Inhaled corticosteroids do not alter the fibrotic course of silicosis. Prophylactic lobectomy is not a recognized strategy for diffuse pneumoconiosis. Therapeutic phlebotomy treats polycythemia or iron overload, not silicosis. Coupling exposure cessation with TB screening, vaccination, and smoking abstinence offers the best chance to limit disease progression and complications.

cases.diagnosis: Silicosis (chronic) with latent tuberculosis infection
pearls: Silicosis classically shows upper-zone nodules and eggshell-calcified hilar nodes; sandblasting and stone-cutting are high-risk jobs.
Silica impairs macrophages, sharply raising TB risk - screen every silicosis patient for tuberculosis.
Treat latent TB in silicosis once active disease is excluded, given the high reactivation rate.
No therapy reverses silicosis; preventing further exposure is the key to limiting progression.

cases.title: Chronic Cough and Breathlessness in a Former Miner
cases.chiefComplaint: Long-standing cough with black-tinged sputum and worsening breathlessness.
cases.history: A 68-year-old man presents with chronic cough productive of black-tinged sputum and progressive exertional dyspnea over the past several years. He worked underground in coal mines for 28 years, retiring at age 58, and recalls dense dust without consistent respiratory protection. He denies fever, hemoptysis, or significant weight loss, although exercise tolerance has declined notably in the past year. He smoked one pack daily for 30 years and quit five years ago. Medications: tiotropium inhaler daily and albuterol as needed. No known allergies. He lives with his spouse in a former mining town. Family history is noncontributory. Review of systems is negative for orthopnea, leg swelling, and chest pain. He has not had recent imaging. He notes that several former coworkers have similar breathing problems and that one received compensation for 'black lung.' He denies known tuberculosis exposure and has had no recent travel. He drinks alcohol rarely and denies illicit drug use.
cases.exam: The patient appears chronically ill but is in no acute distress. Lung auscultation reveals diminished breath sounds with scattered crackles and a prolonged expiratory phase; mild expiratory wheeze is present. There is no clubbing. Cardiac exam shows a regular rhythm with a slightly accentuated pulmonic component of the second heart sound but no overt right ventricular heave or peripheral edema; jugular venous pressure is normal. The remainder of the exam is unremarkable. Oxygen saturation declines to 87% with ambulation.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which historical and clinical features most strongly suggest an occupational pneumoconiosis as the cause of his symptoms?
options: Decades of underground coal-dust exposure with chronic black-tinged sputum
Quitting smoking five years ago as the first step today
Occasional alcohol use and living in a former mining town
His use of a daily tiotropium inhaler as the first step today
stages.explanation: Prolonged underground coal-dust exposure plus chronic productive cough with black-tinged sputum (from coal-laden macrophages) is the hallmark of coal workers' pneumoconiosis. This occupational-clinical combination most strongly directs the workup. Living in a mining town or drinking alcohol is contextual but not diagnostic. Daily tiotropium reflects coexisting obstructive disease (he is also a former smoker) but does not point to a pneumoconiosis. Smoking cessation is beneficial but does not explain the current findings. The exposure duration and characteristic sputum, together with his imaging, will differentiate simple from complicated (progressive massive fibrosis) disease.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
Chest radiograph: large opacities (>1 cm) in both upper zones with surrounding smaller nodules; background of diffuse small nodular opacities
HRCT chest: bilateral upper-lobe conglomerate masses with adjacent paracicatricial emphysema; numerous small nodules; no cavitation
Spirometry: FEV1 58% predicted, FVC 74% predicted, FEV1/FVC 0.62 (obstructive with restrictive component); DLCO 60% predicted
QuantiFERON-TB Gold: negative
BNP 90 pg/mL

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: The large coalescent upper-zone opacities on imaging indicate which complication of his pneumoconiosis?
options: Progressive massive fibrosis
Metastatic adenocarcinoma in this clinical context
Bilateral lobar pneumonia in this clinical context
Pulmonary alveolar proteinosis
stages.explanation: Coalescence of nodules into large (>1 cm) upper-zone conglomerate masses defines progressive massive fibrosis (PMF), the 'complicated' form of coal workers' pneumoconiosis, which causes greater functional impairment than simple nodular disease. Metastatic adenocarcinoma typically produces discrete, often randomly distributed nodules or masses rather than symmetric upper-zone conglomeration on a background of pneumoconiotic nodules. Bilateral lobar pneumonia is an acute consolidative process with fever and a different time course. Pulmonary alveolar proteinosis shows a 'crazy-paving' ground-glass pattern, not conglomerate fibrotic masses. The PMF designation has prognostic and compensation implications for this miner.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate management strategy for his disease?
options: Empiric antituberculous therapy as initial therapy
Supportive care: exposure cessation, smoking abstinence, bronchodilators, oxygen, vaccinations,
High-dose corticosteroids to dissolve the fibrotic masses as initial therapy
Immediate bilateral lung resection as initial therapy
stages.explanation: Coal workers' pneumoconiosis, including PMF, has no curative or fibrosis-reversing therapy, so management is supportive: removing further dust exposure, reinforcing smoking cessation, optimizing airflow with bronchodilators, providing supplemental oxygen for hypoxemia, vaccinating against influenza and pneumococcus, and offering pulmonary rehabilitation. Corticosteroids do not dissolve established fibrosis. Empiric antituberculous therapy is unwarranted with a negative IGRA and no evidence of active TB, though TB remains a consideration in pneumoconiosis. Lung resection is not used for diffuse fibrotic pneumoconiosis. Assisting with occupational compensation ('black lung benefits') and monitoring for cor pulmonale are also appropriate components of care.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: Which long-term cardiopulmonary complication should be monitored for given his advanced disease and hypoxemia?
options: Constrictive pericarditis as the leading concern
Cor pulmonale from chronic hypoxemic pulmonary hypertension
Left-sided systolic heart failure from coronary disease
Aortic stenosis as the leading concern
stages.explanation: Chronic hypoxemia and parenchymal destruction in advanced pneumoconiosis drive pulmonary hypertension, which over time produces right ventricular hypertrophy and failure - cor pulmonale. His accentuated pulmonic second sound and exertional desaturation are early clues, so monitoring for right heart strain (edema, elevated JVP, echocardiographic RV changes) is warranted. Left-sided systolic failure from coronary disease is possible in any older patient but is not the disease-specific complication here. Constrictive pericarditis and aortic stenosis are unrelated to pneumoconiosis and lack supporting features in this case. Recognizing and managing hypoxemia with oxygen can help slow progression to cor pulmonale.

cases.diagnosis: Coal workers' pneumoconiosis with progressive massive fibrosis
pearls: Chronic coal-dust exposure with black-tinged sputum suggests coal workers' pneumoconiosis; black sputum reflects coal-laden macrophages.
Coalescence of nodules into upper-zone masses >1 cm defines progressive massive fibrosis (complicated disease).
No therapy reverses the fibrosis; management is supportive with exposure cessation, smoking abstinence, oxygen, and rehab.
Advanced disease with chronic hypoxemia can progress to pulmonary hypertension and cor pulmonale.

cases.title: Chest Pain and Breathlessness With a Bloody Effusion
cases.chiefComplaint: Several months of worsening right-sided chest pain and shortness of breath.
cases.history: A 74-year-old man presents with three months of progressive right-sided chest wall pain and exertional dyspnea, accompanied by a 6 kg unintentional weight loss and fatigue. The pain is dull, constant, and not clearly pleuritic, and it has gradually intensified. He denies fever or productive cough. He worked for over two decades in shipbuilding and boiler repair, with substantial asbestos exposure, and recalls no consistent respiratory protection; he retired more than 30 years ago. He smoked a half pack daily for 10 years and quit decades ago. Medications: amlodipine 5 mg daily and atorvastatin 20 mg daily. No known allergies. He lives independently with his wife. Family history is noncontributory. Review of systems is positive for decreased appetite and night sweats but negative for hemoptysis, orthopnea, and leg swelling. He has not had recent imaging. He notes the chest discomfort is now interfering with sleep. He denies recent infections or trauma and has had no prior thoracic procedures.
cases.exam: The patient appears cachectic and mildly dyspneic. Examination of the right hemithorax reveals dullness to percussion, decreased tactile fremitus, and markedly diminished breath sounds over the lower two-thirds, consistent with a pleural effusion. There is mild reduced expansion on the right. No clubbing is noted. Cardiac exam is unremarkable without murmurs or right ventricular heave; jugular venous pressure is normal and there is no peripheral edema. No palpable supraclavicular lymphadenopathy. There is point tenderness along the right lateral chest wall. The remainder of the exam is unremarkable.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Given his asbestos history and unilateral effusion with chest wall pain and weight loss, what is the most appropriate initial diagnostic step?
options: Immediate thoracotomy as the first step today
Outpatient observation with repeat exam in three months
Empiric antibiotics for parapneumonic effusion
Diagnostic thoracentesis with pleural fluid analysis
stages.explanation: A new unilateral pleural effusion with constitutional symptoms and chest wall pain warrants diagnostic thoracentesis to characterize the fluid (exudate vs transudate), obtain cytology, and guide further workup. This is the appropriate first invasive step and can relieve symptoms. Empiric antibiotics are inappropriate without evidence of infection and would delay diagnosis of a likely malignant process. Immediate thoracotomy is premature before the effusion is characterized and tissue strategy is planned. Observation is unsafe given red-flag features (weight loss, night sweats, persistent pain) in a patient with heavy asbestos exposure, which raises concern for malignant pleural mesothelioma or metastatic disease. Imaging (CT) and thoracentesis together direct the next steps.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
CT chest with contrast: circumferential, nodular right-sided pleural thickening encasing the lung with mediastinal pleural involvement; moderate right pleural effusion; ipsilateral volume loss; calcified pleural plaques on the left
Thoracentesis: serosanguineous (bloody) fluid
Pleural fluid: exudative by Light's criteria; protein 5.2 g/dL; LDH elevated; lymphocyte-predominant; glucose 55 mg/dL
Pleural fluid cytology: atypical mesothelial cells, indeterminate
No organisms on Gram stain or AFB

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: The CT shows circumferential nodular pleural thickening encasing the lung with mediastinal involvement. Which diagnosis does this most strongly suggest?
options: Congestive heart failure effusion
Malignant pleural mesothelioma
Tuberculous pleuritis in this clinical context
Simple asbestos-related benign pleural effusion
stages.explanation: Circumferential, nodular pleural thickening that encases the lung, involves the mediastinal pleura, and is associated with ipsilateral volume loss is highly suggestive of malignant pleural mesothelioma, particularly with this asbestos history and a bloody exudative effusion. A benign asbestos-related effusion does not produce nodular encasement or such constitutional symptoms. Tuberculous pleuritis can cause a lymphocytic exudate but typically lacks the rind-like nodular encasement and mediastinal pleural involvement, and AFB/clinical context here do not fit. A heart failure effusion is a bilateral transudate without nodular pleural disease. The imaging pattern plus exposure history strongly points to mesothelioma, requiring tissue confirmation.

stages.label: CONFIRMATION
stages.question: Pleural fluid cytology is indeterminate. What is the best next step to establish a definitive tissue diagnosis?
options: Repeat thoracentesis for cytology two more times in this setting
Bronchoscopy with endobronchial biopsy as the immediate next step
Thoracoscopy or image-guided pleural biopsy for histology
Sputum cytology as the immediate next step
stages.explanation: Mesothelioma is notoriously difficult to diagnose on fluid cytology alone; a definitive diagnosis requires adequate pleural tissue, best obtained by thoracoscopy (VATS pleural biopsy) or image-guided core biopsy, with immunohistochemistry (e.g., calretinin, WT-1, cytokeratin 5/6 positive; loss of BAP1) to distinguish mesothelioma from metastatic adenocarcinoma. Repeating thoracentesis has low diagnostic yield and delays care. Sputum cytology evaluates airway-centered tumors, not pleural malignancy. Bronchoscopy with endobronchial biopsy targets endobronchial lung cancer, not a pleural-based process. Thoracoscopy also permits pleurodesis for the recurrent effusion, addressing both diagnosis and symptom control.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: After biopsy confirms epithelioid malignant pleural mesothelioma, which statement best reflects appropriate management and prognosis counseling?
options: Management is largely multimodal
No treatment affects symptoms or survival, so only hospice is appropriate at diagnosis as initial therapy
Radiation alone reliably cures most patients as initial therapy
It is highly curable with antibiotics alone as initial therapy
stages.explanation: Malignant pleural mesothelioma carries a generally poor prognosis; care is multidisciplinary and frequently palliative, integrating systemic therapy (e.g., platinum-pemetrexed chemotherapy, with immunotherapy combinations now used), pleurodesis or indwelling catheter for effusion control, radiation for pain or procedure-site seeding, and surgery in highly selected early-stage patients. Antibiotics have no role in this malignancy. Radiation alone does not cure mesothelioma. While prognosis is poor, active therapies do improve symptoms and modestly extend survival, so dismissing all treatment in favor of immediate hospice at diagnosis is incorrect; goals-of-care discussion and palliative support are important alongside disease-directed options.

stages.label: PUBLIC HEALTH
stages.question: Regarding the relationship between his exposures and this cancer, which statement is correct?
options: Only direct miners, never bystanders or family members, are at risk as the immediate next step
Mesothelioma typically develops within one to two years of asbestos exposure in this setting
Cigarette smoking dramatically increases mesothelioma risk synergistically with asbestos
Asbestos exposure is the dominant risk factor,
stages.explanation: Asbestos is the dominant cause of malignant mesothelioma, and unlike bronchogenic carcinoma, smoking does NOT meaningfully increase mesothelioma risk - this distinction is high-yield. The latency is long, typically 20-40 years, not one to two years. Risk is not limited to direct workers: household contacts exposed to fibers on clothing and bystanders can develop disease, so 'only miners' is false. Recognizing the long latency explains why a man who retired decades ago now presents with mesothelioma. Counseling should also address occupational/legal resources, given the strong, well-established asbestos causation behind this tumor.

cases.diagnosis: Malignant pleural mesothelioma
pearls: A unilateral bloody exudative effusion with nodular pleural encasement and asbestos history is mesothelioma until proven otherwise.
Fluid cytology is often indeterminate; pleural tissue with immunohistochemistry (calretinin, WT-1) is needed for diagnosis.
Latency from asbestos exposure is long (20-40 years); smoking does NOT raise mesothelioma risk (unlike lung cancer).
Management is multimodal and often palliative; pleurodesis controls recurrent effusions.

cases.title: Progressive Breathlessness and a Loud Second Heart Sound
cases.chiefComplaint: Worsening shortness of breath on exertion and episodes of near-fainting over several months.
cases.history: A 40-year-old woman presents with six months of progressive exertional dyspnea, now occurring with mild activity such as climbing one flight of stairs. She reports exertional lightheadedness, two episodes of near-syncope, and occasional substernal chest tightness with exercise. She denies fever, cough, orthopnea, or leg swelling initially, though she has noticed mild ankle swelling recently. She has no history of asthma, deep vein thrombosis, or pulmonary embolism. She takes no anorexigens and uses only an oral contraceptive pill. No known allergies. She does not smoke or use illicit drugs and drinks alcohol rarely. She works as a teacher. Family history is notable for a maternal aunt with 'lung blood pressure problems.' Review of systems is negative for joint pain, rash, Raynaud phenomenon, hemoptysis, and snoring. She has had a recent normal complete blood count and thyroid studies. She has not had prior echocardiography. She reports no recent travel or prolonged immobilization.
cases.exam: The patient is comfortable at rest. Cardiac exam reveals a loud, palpable pulmonic component of the second heart sound (loud P2), a left parasternal (right ventricular) heave, and a soft systolic murmur at the left lower sternal border that increases with inspiration, consistent with tricuspid regurgitation. Jugular venous pressure is elevated with prominent a waves. Lungs are clear to auscultation bilaterally without crackles or wheeze. There is mild bilateral pedal edema. No clubbing, cyanosis at rest, rash, or sclerodactyly is noted. Abdominal exam is benign without hepatomegaly or ascites.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which constellation of exam findings best explains her presentation and points toward the underlying disorder?
options: Bibasilar crackles and an S3 gallop indicating left heart failure as the first step today
A diastolic decrescendo murmur indicating aortic regurgitation as the first step today
Loud P2, right ventricular heave, elevated JVP,
Diminished breath sounds and dullness indicating pleural effusion as the first step today
stages.explanation: A loud P2, right ventricular (parasternal) heave, elevated JVP with prominent a waves, and a tricuspid regurgitation murmur that augments with inspiration together signify elevated pulmonary artery pressure with right ventricular pressure overload - the signature of pulmonary hypertension. Clear lungs argue against left heart failure or parenchymal disease, and there is no S3 or bibasilar crackles to indicate left-sided failure. A diastolic decrescendo murmur of aortic regurgitation is not described and would not explain right heart findings. Diminished breath sounds with dullness would indicate effusion, which is absent. These right-heart signs, with exertional syncope, raise strong concern for pulmonary arterial hypertension.

stages.label: INITIAL WORKUP
stages.content:
ECG: right axis deviation, right ventricular hypertrophy with strain, right atrial enlargement
Chest radiograph: enlarged central pulmonary arteries with peripheral 'pruning'; clear lung fields; no effusion
Transthoracic echocardiogram: estimated right ventricular systolic pressure 70 mmHg; dilated, hypokinetic right ventricle; flattened interventricular septum; normal left ventricular size and function; no significant valvular stenosis or left-sided disease
BNP 480 pg/mL

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Echo suggests pulmonary hypertension. What is the most appropriate next step to confirm the diagnosis and define the hemodynamics?
options: Coronary angiography as the primary diagnosis
Right heart catheterization
Repeat echocardiography in six months
Empiric high-dose calcium channel blocker without further testing
stages.explanation: Right heart catheterization is the gold standard to confirm pulmonary hypertension and characterize it: it directly measures mean pulmonary artery pressure, pulmonary capillary wedge pressure (to exclude left heart disease), and pulmonary vascular resistance, and allows acute vasoreactivity testing. Echo only estimates pressures and cannot distinguish pre- from post-capillary disease. Coronary angiography evaluates coronary artery disease, not pulmonary vascular hemodynamics. Starting high-dose calcium channel blockers empirically is dangerous and inappropriate without vasoreactivity testing, since most patients are non-responders and can deteriorate. Deferring with repeat echo delays diagnosis in a symptomatic patient with syncope and RV strain. Catheterization is essential before classifying and treating.

stages.label: RIGHT HEART CATHETERIZATION
stages.content:
Mean pulmonary artery pressure 48 mmHg (normal <20)
Pulmonary capillary wedge pressure 10 mmHg (normal)
Pulmonary vascular resistance 8 Wood units (elevated)
Cardiac index reduced
Acute vasoreactivity testing: negative (no significant fall in mean PAP)
Ventilation-perfusion scan: no segmental mismatched defects
Autoimmune serologies and HIV: negative; LFTs normal

stages.label: CLASSIFICATION
stages.question: Based on a mean PAP of 48 mmHg, a normal wedge pressure, elevated PVR, and a negative VQ scan, how is her pulmonary hypertension best classified?
options: Group 2 (due to left heart disease) in this setting
Group 1 pulmonary arterial hypertension
Group 3 (due to chronic lung disease/hypoxia)
Group 4 (chronic thromboembolic) in this setting
stages.explanation: Elevated mean PAP with a normal (≤15 mmHg) wedge pressure and elevated PVR defines pre-capillary pulmonary hypertension; with left heart disease, chronic lung disease, thromboembolic disease, and other causes excluded, this is Group 1 pulmonary arterial hypertension (idiopathic or, given the family history, possibly heritable). A normal wedge pressure excludes Group 2 (left heart disease, which raises wedge pressure). A negative VQ scan excludes Group 4 (CTEPH). Clear lungs and absence of significant hypoxic lung disease argue against Group 3. Correct WHO group classification is essential because PAH-specific vasodilator therapy is appropriate for Group 1 but can be harmful in some other groups.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Her acute vasoreactivity test is negative. Which treatment approach is most appropriate?
options: Systemic anticoagulation as the sole therapy as initial therapy
Beta-blocker therapy to reduce pulmonary pressures as initial therapy
High-dose calcium channel blocker monotherapy as initial therapy
Pulmonary arterial hypertension-specific therapy targeting the endothelin, nitric oxide,
stages.explanation: Because she is a vasoreactivity non-responder, calcium channel blockers are not indicated; instead she should receive PAH-specific therapy directed at the three pathways - endothelin receptor antagonists (e.g., ambrisentan), phosphodiesterase-5 inhibitors or guanylate cyclase stimulators (nitric oxide pathway), and prostacyclin analogues/agonists - increasingly used as initial combination therapy in higher-risk patients. High-dose calcium channel blockers benefit only the minority who are vasoreactive responders and can harm non-responders. Anticoagulation alone does not treat the vasculopathy of Group 1 PAH and is not the primary therapy. Beta-blockers do not lower pulmonary pressures and may impair the RV-dependent cardiac output, so they are avoided.

cases.diagnosis: Pulmonary arterial hypertension (WHO Group 1)
pearls: Loud P2, RV heave, elevated JVP, and tricuspid regurgitation signal pulmonary hypertension with RV pressure overload.
Right heart catheterization is the gold standard: mean PAP elevated with NORMAL wedge pressure and high PVR defines pre-capillary (Group 1) PAH.
Acute vasoreactivity testing determines therapy - only responders benefit from calcium channel blockers.
Non-responders receive PAH-specific drugs targeting endothelin, nitric oxide, and prostacyclin pathways, often in combination.

cases.title: Persistent Breathlessness After a Prior Clot
cases.chiefComplaint: Shortness of breath that has not improved months after a pulmonary embolism.
cases.history: A 60-year-old woman presents with persistent exertional dyspnea eight months after an unprovoked pulmonary embolism that was treated with anticoagulation. Initially she improved, but over the past several months her breathlessness has worsened and she now tires after walking half a block. She reports exertional lightheadedness and mild leg swelling but no chest pain, fever, or hemoptysis. She has been adherent to apixaban 5 mg twice daily. Past medical history includes hypertension and prior unprovoked PE; no known thrombophilia testing has been done. Medications: apixaban 5 mg twice daily, amlodipine 5 mg daily. No known allergies. She is a never-smoker, drinks alcohol rarely, and works part-time. Family history includes a brother with a deep vein thrombosis. Review of systems is negative for cough, orthopnea, rash, joint pains, and Raynaud phenomenon. She has had no recent surgery or immobilization. She is frustrated that she has not returned to her baseline despite completing the expected treatment course.
cases.exam: The patient is comfortable at rest but dyspneic with minimal exertion. Cardiac exam reveals a loud pulmonic component of S2, a right ventricular heave, and a soft tricuspid regurgitation murmur that increases with inspiration. Jugular venous pressure is mildly elevated. A pulmonary flow murmur or bruit is suspected over the lung fields. Lungs are clear to auscultation without crackles or wheeze. There is mild bilateral pedal edema. No calf tenderness, cords, or asymmetric leg swelling suggesting acute DVT. No clubbing or cyanosis at rest. Abdomen is soft without hepatomegaly.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Given persistent dyspnea and signs of pulmonary hypertension months after a treated PE, what condition should be suspected?
options: Chronic thromboembolic pulmonary hypertension
Asthma as the first step today
Anxiety-related hyperventilation
Recurrent acute pulmonary embolism only
stages.explanation: Persistent or progressive dyspnea with new signs of pulmonary hypertension (loud P2, RV heave, tricuspid regurgitation, elevated JVP) months after a pulmonary embolism, despite adequate anticoagulation, should raise strong suspicion for chronic thromboembolic pulmonary hypertension - organized, fibrotic thromboembolic material obstructing the pulmonary vasculature. While acute recurrent PE is possible, her adherence to anticoagulation and the chronic progressive course with established PH signs fit CTEPH. Asthma causes wheeze and airflow obstruction, not these right-heart findings. Anxiety-related hyperventilation would not produce a loud P2, RV heave, hypoxemia, and edema. CTEPH is an important, potentially curable cause of PH that must be actively sought after PE.

stages.label: INITIAL WORKUP
stages.content:
ECG: right axis deviation and right ventricular hypertrophy
Transthoracic echocardiogram: estimated RV systolic pressure 65 mmHg; dilated, hypokinetic right ventricle; septal flattening; preserved left ventricular function
Ventilation-perfusion (V/Q) scan: multiple bilateral segmental perfusion defects with normal ventilation (mismatched defects)
BNP 360 pg/mL
Repeat lower-extremity Doppler: no acute DVT

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Why is the ventilation-perfusion scan the preferred screening test over CT pulmonary angiography for this suspected diagnosis?
options: CT pulmonary angiography cannot detect any pulmonary emboli as the primary diagnosis
VQ scan is cheaper and requires no isotope as the primary diagnosis
VQ scanning measures pulmonary artery pressure directly as the primary diagnosis
VQ scan is more sensitive for detecting the segmental perfusion defects of chronic thromboembolic disease
stages.explanation: The V/Q scan is the recommended screening test for CTEPH because it is more sensitive than CT pulmonary angiography for detecting the chronic, organized, often peripheral segmental perfusion defects; a normal V/Q scan essentially excludes CTEPH. Her scan shows classic multiple bilateral mismatched defects. The V/Q scan does use a radioisotope, so 'no isotope' is incorrect. CTPA can detect emboli and is useful to define surgical accessibility of disease, but it can miss subtle chronic disease, making V/Q the better screen. V/Q scanning images perfusion/ventilation distribution and does not directly measure pulmonary artery pressure - that requires right heart catheterization.

stages.label: CONFIRMATION
stages.question: After an abnormal V/Q scan, what is the next step to confirm CTEPH and assess operability?
options: Right heart catheterization with pulmonary angiography
Bronchoscopy with biopsy as the immediate next step
Empiric thrombolysis as the immediate next step
Cardiac stress testing as the immediate next step
stages.explanation: Confirming CTEPH requires right heart catheterization to document pre-capillary pulmonary hypertension (elevated mean PAP with normal wedge pressure) and pulmonary angiography (with CT angiography) to delineate the location and surgical accessibility of the chronic thromboembolic material. This combination establishes the diagnosis and determines candidacy for pulmonary endarterectomy. Bronchoscopy evaluates airway and parenchymal disease, not pulmonary vascular obstruction. Cardiac stress testing assesses ischemia and would not diagnose CTEPH. Empiric thrombolysis is ineffective against organized, fibrotic chronic thrombus and carries bleeding risk; acute lytic therapy does not treat chronic disease. Accurate hemodynamic and anatomic characterization is essential before deciding on surgery, balloon angioplasty, or medical therapy.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Confirmatory testing shows surgically accessible proximal chronic thromboembolic disease. What is the definitive, potentially curative treatment?
options: Single-agent diuretic therapy as initial therapy
Pulmonary thromboendarterectomy
Inhaled bronchodilators as initial therapy
Lifelong calcium channel blockers as monotherapy
stages.explanation: For operable CTEPH with surgically accessible (proximal) disease, pulmonary thromboendarterectomy is the treatment of choice and is potentially curative, removing organized thromboembolic material and normalizing pulmonary hemodynamics in many patients. Lifelong anticoagulation is also required, but it does not by itself relieve fixed chronic obstruction. Calcium channel blockers treat vasoreactive Group 1 PAH, not the mechanical obstruction of CTEPH. Inhaled bronchodilators treat airflow obstruction, irrelevant here. Diuretics manage volume/right heart congestion symptomatically but are not definitive. For inoperable disease or persistent PH after surgery, balloon pulmonary angioplasty and medical therapy such as riociguat are used, but operable patients should be referred for endarterectomy.

stages.label: LONG-TERM CARE
stages.question: Regardless of whether she undergoes surgery, which therapy must be continued long-term?
options: Lifelong antiplatelet monotherapy with aspirin
Lifelong inhaled corticosteroids
Lifelong therapeutic anticoagulation
Lifelong systemic corticosteroids
stages.explanation: All patients with CTEPH require lifelong therapeutic anticoagulation to prevent recurrent or in situ thrombosis, whether or not they undergo pulmonary endarterectomy or angioplasty. Corticosteroids, systemic or inhaled, have no role in CTEPH, which is a thromboembolic vasculopathy rather than an inflammatory airway disease. Aspirin monotherapy is insufficient for thromboembolic prophylaxis in CTEPH; full anticoagulation is needed. Given her unprovoked PE, recurrent thromboembolic disease, and family history, indefinite anticoagulation is appropriate, and a thrombophilia evaluation (e.g., antiphospholipid syndrome) should be considered. Anticoagulation is foundational therapy underpinning all other CTEPH management.

cases.diagnosis: Chronic thromboembolic pulmonary hypertension (CTEPH, WHO Group 4)
pearls: Suspect CTEPH when dyspnea and pulmonary hypertension persist months after a treated PE despite anticoagulation.
V/Q scan is the preferred screen (more sensitive than CTPA); a normal V/Q scan excludes CTEPH.
Right heart cath plus pulmonary angiography confirm the diagnosis and assess operability.
Pulmonary thromboendarterectomy is potentially curative for proximal disease; lifelong anticoagulation is required for all patients.

cases.title: Leg Swelling and Breathlessness in a COPD Patient
cases.chiefComplaint: Worsening leg swelling, abdominal fullness, and shortness of breath over two weeks.
cases.history: A 66-year-old man with severe COPD is admitted with two weeks of progressive bilateral leg swelling, abdominal distension, and worsening dyspnea. He has a 50-pack-year smoking history and continues to smoke. His COPD is managed with tiotropium and a budesonide-formoterol inhaler, and he uses home oxygen at 2 L/min for chronic hypoxemia. He denies chest pain, fever, or productive purulent sputum suggesting acute infection, though he reports increased baseline breathlessness. He has had two COPD exacerbations in the past year. Medications: tiotropium daily, budesonide-formoterol twice daily, albuterol PRN, and home oxygen. No known allergies. He lives with his daughter. Family history is noncontributory. Review of systems is positive for early satiety, weight gain from swelling, and exertional fatigue, and negative for orthopnea due primarily to left heart disease, paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnea, and hemoptysis. He has not had recent echocardiography. He admits to poor adherence to oxygen at night.
cases.exam: The patient is dyspneic and using accessory muscles. He has markedly elevated jugular venous pressure with a positive hepatojugular reflux. Cardiac exam reveals a left parasternal (right ventricular) heave, a loud pulmonic component of S2, and a holosystolic murmur at the left lower sternal border that increases with inspiration (tricuspid regurgitation). Lung exam shows distant breath sounds with a prolonged expiratory phase and scattered wheezes consistent with COPD; no focal consolidation. The abdomen is distended with a pulsatile, tender, enlarged liver and shifting dullness suggesting ascites. There is 3+ pitting edema to the thighs. No focal calf tenderness.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which diagnosis best unifies his elevated JVP, RV heave, loud P2, tricuspid regurgitation, hepatomegaly, ascites, and peripheral edema?
options: Left ventricular systolic heart failure
Cor pulmonale
Cirrhosis from alcohol use as the first step today
Nephrotic syndrome as the first step today
stages.explanation: The combination of right-sided congestion (elevated JVP, hepatojugular reflux, congestive hepatomegaly, ascites, peripheral edema) with signs of pulmonary hypertension and RV pressure overload (RV heave, loud P2, tricuspid regurgitation) in a patient with severe chronic hypoxemic COPD is classic for cor pulmonale - right heart failure from a primary pulmonary process. Left ventricular systolic failure would typically present with pulmonary congestion/crackles, orthopnea, and an S3, which are not the dominant features. Nephrotic syndrome causes edema via proteinuria and hypoalbuminemia, not these right-heart findings. Cirrhosis can cause ascites and edema but would not produce a loud P2, RV heave, and elevated JVP from pulmonary hypertension.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
ECG: right axis deviation, P pulmonale (peaked P waves), right ventricular hypertrophy
Chest radiograph: hyperinflation with flattened diaphragms; enlarged central pulmonary arteries; no infiltrate or effusion
ABG: pH 7.35, PaCO2 55 mmHg, PaO2 56 mmHg on 2 L/min
Echocardiogram: dilated, hypertrophied, hypokinetic right ventricle; estimated RV systolic pressure 60 mmHg; normal left ventricular size and ejection fraction; no significant left-sided valvular disease
BNP 520 pg/mL

stages.label: PATHOPHYSIOLOGY
stages.question: What is the principal mechanism by which his COPD led to right heart failure?
options: Pericardial constriction impairing right ventricular filling in this setting
Chronic alveolar hypoxia causing pulmonary vasoconstriction
Left ventricular dilation reducing right-sided output in this setting
Coronary artery occlusion causing right ventricular infarction in this setting
stages.explanation: In COPD, chronic alveolar hypoxia triggers hypoxic pulmonary vasoconstriction and, over time, vascular remodeling and destruction of the pulmonary capillary bed, raising pulmonary vascular resistance and pulmonary artery pressure. The right ventricle hypertrophies and ultimately fails under this sustained pressure load, producing cor pulmonale. Left ventricular dilation is not the mechanism here - the echo shows normal LV function, and cor pulmonale is defined by right heart failure from lung/pulmonary vascular disease, not left heart disease. Right ventricular infarction from coronary occlusion is an acute ischemic event, not this chronic hypoxic process. Pericardial constriction is a distinct restrictive condition not supported by the imaging or history.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Which intervention most directly targets the underlying driver of his cor pulmonale and improves long-term survival?
options: Routine therapeutic phlebotomy regardless of hematocrit as initial therapy
High-dose loop diuretics as the sole therapy as initial therapy
Correcting chronic hypoxemia with adequate long-term oxygen therapy
Initiating a beta-blocker to lower pulmonary pressures as initial therapy
stages.explanation: Long-term oxygen therapy that corrects chronic hypoxemia reduces hypoxic pulmonary vasoconstriction, lowers pulmonary artery pressure, and is the intervention shown to improve survival in hypoxemic COPD, directly addressing the cause of cor pulmonale; optimizing COPD and smoking cessation are essential adjuncts. Loop diuretics relieve congestion and edema symptomatically but must be used cautiously to avoid reducing RV preload excessively and do not treat the underlying hypoxic vasoconstriction. Beta-blockers do not lower pulmonary pressures and may impair RV output. Therapeutic phlebotomy is reserved for symptomatic severe secondary polycythemia, not routine use. Ensuring nocturnal oxygen adherence is particularly important in this patient.

stages.label: SUPPORTIVE CARE
stages.question: Which additional measure is appropriate to manage his volume overload symptoms while avoiding harm?
options: Cautious diuresis with monitoring of renal function
High-dose calcium channel blockers for all patients with cor pulmonale
Aggressive intravenous fluid boluses as the immediate next step
Strict bed rest with no pharmacologic therapy as the immediate next step
stages.explanation: Cautious diuresis relieves the congestive symptoms of right heart failure (edema, ascites, hepatic congestion) but must be carefully titrated because the failing, pressure-loaded right ventricle is preload-dependent; over-diuresis can drop cardiac output and worsen renal function, so monitoring electrolytes and renal function is essential. Intravenous fluid boluses would worsen volume overload in a congested patient. High-dose calcium channel blockers are appropriate only for vasoreactive Group 1 PAH, not routine cor pulmonale, and can cause harm. Bed rest with no therapy neglects treatable congestion and the underlying hypoxemia. The cornerstone remains oxygenation and COPD optimization, with judicious diuretics for symptom control.

cases.diagnosis: Cor pulmonale secondary to COPD
pearls: Cor pulmonale is right heart failure from a primary lung/pulmonary vascular process - look for elevated JVP, RV heave, loud P2, TR, hepatomegaly, ascites, and edema with PRESERVED left heart function.
Chronic alveolar hypoxia drives hypoxic pulmonary vasoconstriction, pulmonary hypertension, and RV failure.
Long-term oxygen therapy correcting hypoxemia improves survival and directly addresses the cause.
Use diuretics cautiously - the pressure-loaded RV is preload-dependent and over-diuresis lowers output.

cases.title: Coughing Up Large Volumes of Blood
cases.chiefComplaint: Coughing up large amounts of bright red blood over the past few hours.
cases.history: A 58-year-old man is brought to the ICU after coughing up large volumes of bright red blood at home, estimated at more than a cupful over a few hours, with ongoing expectoration. He has a history of bronchiectasis from prior tuberculosis and recurrent respiratory infections, with chronic cough and occasional blood streaking, but never this severe. He smoked one pack daily for 35 years. Medications: an inhaled bronchodilator and as-needed mucolytics. He takes no anticoagulants or antiplatelet agents. No known allergies. He lives alone. Family history is noncontributory. Review of systems is positive for low-grade fevers and increased sputum in the preceding week, suggesting an infective exacerbation, and negative for hematemesis, melena, epistaxis, and gum bleeding. He has had no recent trauma or procedures. On arrival he is anxious, coughing forcefully, and continuing to bring up blood, with audible gurgling. He is able to speak in short phrases between coughing episodes.
cases.exam: The patient is anxious, tachypneic, and actively coughing bright red blood. There is audible gurgling and coarse crackles heard predominantly over the right lung field, with relatively clearer sounds on the left. He has accessory muscle use and is mildly diaphoretic. Cardiac exam shows tachycardia without murmurs or right ventricular heave; jugular venous pressure is normal. There is no peripheral edema, cyanosis at rest beyond hypoxemia, or clubbing change from baseline. The oropharynx shows blood but no clear nasopharyngeal or upper GI source. Abdomen is soft and nontender.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: In this patient with massive hemoptysis, what is the immediate priority?
options: Secure the airway and protect the unaffected lung, ensuring oxygenation and ventilation
Give a large intravenous fluid bolus and discharge once bleeding slows
Immediately start systemic thrombolysis as the first step today
Obtain an outpatient CT scan before any intervention as the first step today
stages.explanation: In massive hemoptysis, death usually results from asphyxiation due to blood flooding the airways, not exsanguination, so the immediate priority is airway protection and maintaining oxygenation/ventilation - including positioning the patient bleeding-side down to protect the healthy lung, suctioning, and early consideration of intubation (with a large endotracheal tube to allow bronchoscopy and possible selective intubation). Deferring stabilization for an outpatient scan is unsafe in active bleeding. Systemic thrombolysis would worsen bleeding and is contraindicated. A fluid bolus alone does not address the airway threat, and discharge is dangerous given the high mortality of massive hemoptysis. Airway, breathing, and circulation come first, with the airway paramount.

stages.label: STABILIZATION
stages.question: Crackles and bleeding localize to the right lung. How should the patient be positioned, and why?
options: Trendelenburg (head down) to increase cerebral perfusion as the immediate next step
Right lateral decubitus to protect the unaffected left lung from blood spillage
Left lateral decubitus (bleeding side up) to drain blood away from the right lung
Strict supine flat positioning to stabilize the spine as the immediate next step
stages.explanation: When the bleeding source is lateralized, the patient should be placed with the bleeding side DOWN (here, right lateral decubitus) so gravity keeps blood within the affected lung and prevents spillage into and flooding of the unaffected (left) lung, preserving gas exchange in the healthy lung. Positioning bleeding-side up would allow blood to drain into the good lung, worsening asphyxiation. Supine positioning offers no airway protection and can let blood distribute to both lungs. Trendelenburg does not protect the airway and may promote aspiration into both lungs. Lateralizing the bleed (by exam, imaging, or bronchoscopy) is therefore a key early step that guides both positioning and definitive therapy.

stages.label: WORKUP
stages.content:
Hemoglobin 11.8 g/dL (down from baseline)
Platelets 240 ×10³/µL
INR 1.0; aPTT normal
Type and crossmatch sent
CT angiography of the chest: right lower lobe bronchiectasis with active contrast extravasation; hypertrophied, tortuous right bronchial arteries
Bronchoscopy: brisk bleeding originating from the right lower lobe; airway otherwise patent after suctioning

stages.label: DEFINITIVE THERAPY
stages.question: Bleeding is localized to a hypertrophied right bronchial artery. What is the most appropriate definitive treatment in a stabilized patient?
options: Observation with cough suppressants alone
Bronchial artery embolization
Systemic heparin infusion in this setting
Emergent bilateral lung transplantation
stages.explanation: Most massive hemoptysis arises from the high-pressure bronchial (systemic) circulation, and bronchial artery embolization is the first-line definitive therapy: it is minimally invasive, rapidly controls bleeding, and is effective for bronchiectasis-related and other bronchial arterial sources, as identified on this CT angiogram. Lung transplantation is not an emergency treatment for acute hemoptysis. Systemic heparin would worsen hemorrhage and is contraindicated. Observation with cough suppressants alone is inadequate for ongoing massive bleeding and risks fatal asphyxiation. After embolization, treating the underlying cause (e.g., the infective bronchiectasis exacerbation) and considering surgery for refractory bleeding or failed embolization complete management.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: If bronchial artery embolization fails to control persistent, life-threatening bleeding from a single localized site, what is the next definitive option?
options: Long-term oral corticosteroids based on current risk
Discharge with outpatient follow-up
Surgical resection of the bleeding lung segment/lobe
Repeat systemic anticoagulation in this clinical context
stages.explanation: When embolization fails to control life-threatening hemoptysis from a localized source, surgical resection (segmentectomy or lobectomy) of the affected, bleeding portion of lung is the definitive rescue option in a patient with adequate pulmonary reserve and a clearly lateralized source, as in this right lower lobe disease. Anticoagulation would exacerbate bleeding and is contraindicated. Corticosteroids do not control mechanical bronchial arterial bleeding and are not appropriate here.
