Discharge is dangerous with ongoing massive hemoptysis. Surgery is higher-risk than embolization, so it is reserved for refractory cases or specific indications (e.g., aspergilloma, certain trauma); ensuring the patient can tolerate resection and the source is localized is essential before proceeding.

cases.diagnosis: Massive hemoptysis (from bronchiectasis with bronchial artery bleeding)
pearls: In massive hemoptysis, death is usually from asphyxiation, not blood loss - airway protection and oxygenation come first.
Position the patient bleeding-side DOWN to protect the unaffected lung; consider intubation with a large tube and lung isolation.
Most bleeding originates from the high-pressure bronchial (systemic) arteries; bronchial artery embolization is first-line definitive therapy.
Surgical resection is the rescue option for refractory, localized bleeding; avoid anticoagulants.

cases.title: Nighttime Cough and Wheeze with Sports
cases.chiefComplaint: Recurrent nighttime cough and wheezing that worsens when he runs at recess.
cases.history: A 9-year-old boy is brought to clinic by his mother for several months of recurrent dry cough that is worst at night and after running during soccer practice. Episodes include audible wheeze and chest tightness that resolve with rest within 20-30 minutes. He has missed school twice this month for cough. Symptoms flare with viral colds and when the family cat sleeps in his room. He has no fever, weight loss, or sputum production. Past medical history is notable for eczema as a toddler and seasonal allergic rhinitis treated with intranasal fluticasone. He takes no daily respiratory medications and has used a friend's albuterol inhaler once with relief. No known drug allergies. He lives with his parents, a younger sister, and two cats; no tobacco smoke exposure in the home, though his grandfather smokes outdoors. Immunizations are up to date. Family history is significant for asthma in his father and atopic dermatitis in his mother. Development and growth have been normal, tracking along the 50th percentile.
cases.exam: Well-appearing boy in no distress, speaking in full sentences. HEENT: boggy, pale nasal turbinates with clear discharge; oropharynx clear. Mild allergic shiners noted. Lungs: good air entry bilaterally with scattered end-expiratory wheezes on forced exhalation; no crackles, no stridor, and no accessory muscle use. Mild prolongation of the expiratory phase. Cardiac: regular rate and rhythm without murmur. Skin: faint flexural dryness at the antecubital fossae, no active eczema. No clubbing, no cyanosis. No digital clubbing or chest wall deformity.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which feature of this presentation most strongly suggests asthma rather than another cause of chronic cough?
options: Episodic, reversible symptoms triggered by exercise
Productive cough with purulent sputum each morning as the first step today
Digital clubbing with failure to thrive as the first step today
Cough that is constant throughout the day and unrelated to activity
stages.explanation: Asthma is characterized by episodic, reversible airflow obstruction triggered by exercise, allergens, and viral illness, and it clusters with atopy (eczema, allergic rhinitis, family history) — exactly this boy's pattern. A productive morning cough with purulent sputum points toward a suppurative process such as bronchiectasis or chronic infection, not classic asthma. A constant, activity-independent cough suggests habit cough or a non-airway etiology. Digital clubbing with failure to thrive raises concern for cystic fibrosis or another chronic suppurative or interstitial disease, none of which fit this well-grown, atopic child with intermittent reversible wheeze.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: What is the most appropriate next diagnostic test to confirm the suspected diagnosis in this 9-year-old?
options: Methacholine challenge as the first-line test
Spirometry before and after bronchodilator
High-resolution CT of the chest
Sweat chloride testing in this clinical context
stages.explanation: Spirometry with pre- and post-bronchodilator measurements is the standard confirmatory test in a school-aged child who can perform the maneuver; demonstrating an obstructive pattern (reduced FEV1/FVC) that reverses by greater than or equal to 12% after albuterol confirms reversible obstruction. HRCT is not indicated for typical asthma and carries radiation risk. Methacholine challenge is reserved for cases where spirometry is normal but asthma is still suspected — it is not the first-line test. Sweat chloride testing evaluates for cystic fibrosis, which is not suggested here given normal growth and the absence of suppurative or GI features.

stages.label: SPIROMETRY
stages.content:
FEV1: 78% predicted
FVC: 95% predicted
FEV1/FVC: 0.72 (below lower limit of normal)
Post-bronchodilator FEV1: increased by 14% (260 mL)
Flow-volume loop: concave expiratory limb consistent with obstruction
No paradoxical inspiratory flattening

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Spirometry confirms reversible obstruction. Given symptoms more than twice weekly and frequent nighttime awakenings, what is the most appropriate controller therapy?
options: Daily oral montelukast as monotherapy with no inhaled corticosteroid
Daily low-dose inhaled corticosteroid plus as-needed albuterol
Albuterol as needed only, with no controller as initial therapy
Long-acting beta-agonist monotherapy daily as initial therapy
stages.explanation: This child has persistent asthma (daytime symptoms more than twice weekly and frequent nocturnal awakenings), so a daily low-dose inhaled corticosteroid (ICS) is the preferred controller, paired with as-needed albuterol for rescue. As-needed albuterol alone is appropriate only for intermittent asthma, which this is not. Montelukast monotherapy is inferior to ICS and is reserved for patients who cannot use ICS or have prominent allergic rhinitis as an add-on. LABA monotherapy is contraindicated because of increased risk of severe exacerbations and death; a LABA may only be used in combination with an ICS.

stages.label: PREVENTION
stages.question: For his exercise-induced symptoms during soccer, what is the most appropriate recommendation in addition to his controller?
options: Switch to a long-acting beta-agonist used only before exercise
Use inhaled albuterol 10-15 minutes before exercise
Take a daily oral antihistamine before games as the immediate next step
Avoid all physical activity and sports permanently in this setting
stages.explanation: Pre-exercise short-acting beta-agonist (albuterol) taken 10-15 minutes before activity prevents exercise-induced bronchoconstriction, and good adherence to the daily ICS reduces underlying airway inflammation so exercise is better tolerated. Children with asthma should be encouraged to participate in sports, not avoid them — well-controlled asthma allows normal activity. Oral antihistamines treat allergic rhinitis but do not prevent exercise-induced bronchoconstriction. Pre-exercise LABA monotherapy is not appropriate; LABAs must be combined with an ICS and are not used as standalone pre-exercise agents in children.

cases.diagnosis: Asthma (new diagnosis), persistent — atopic, with exercise-induced bronchoconstriction
pearls: Episodic, reversible wheeze and cough triggered by exercise, allergens, and viral illness, with atopy and family history, is the classic asthma pattern.
Confirm asthma in cooperative school-aged children with spirometry showing obstruction plus greater than or equal to 12% post-bronchodilator reversibility.
Daily low-dose ICS is first-line controller for persistent asthma; LABA must never be used as monotherapy.
Pre-exercise albuterol prevents exercise-induced bronchoconstriction — children with asthma should stay active, not avoid sports.

cases.title: Toddler with a Barking Cough Overnight
cases.chiefComplaint: Harsh barking cough and noisy breathing that came on suddenly during the night.
cases.history: A 2-year-old boy is brought to the ED at 2 a.m. by his parents for a harsh, seal-like barking cough and noisy breathing. He developed rhinorrhea and a low-grade fever two days ago, and tonight the cough worsened abruptly with a high-pitched sound when he breathes in. He is fussy but consolable and continues to take sips of fluid. Parents deny choking, foreign-body ingestion, or sudden onset while eating. He has had no prior episodes like this and is fully immunized, including Hib and DTaP. There is no history of intubation, prematurity, or congenital airway anomaly. He attends daycare where several children have had colds. Past medical history is unremarkable; he takes no medications and has no known allergies. He lives with both parents and an older sibling; no household tobacco smoke. Birth history was term and uncomplicated, with normal growth and development. Family history is noncontributory. There is no drooling, and he can swallow his secretions.
cases.exam: Alert, fussy toddler who is consolable in mother's arms, sitting comfortably. Audible inspiratory stridor at rest that softens when calm. Voice and cry are hoarse. HEENT: clear rhinorrhea; oropharynx without exudate, no drooling, normal-appearing epiglottis area on visual inspection of the mouth; he handles secretions well. Neck supple without lymphadenopathy. Lungs: stridor transmitted to the chest; mild suprasternal and intercostal retractions; good air entry bilaterally without focal wheeze or crackles. Cardiac: tachycardic, regular, no murmur. No cyanosis. Capillary refill brisk.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Based on the barking cough, hoarse voice, and inspiratory stridor in this immunized toddler, what is the most likely diagnosis?
options: Epiglottitis in this clinical context
Croup
Foreign body aspiration
Bacterial tracheitis
stages.explanation: The seal-like barking cough, hoarse voice, and inspiratory stridor following a viral prodrome in a 2-year-old are classic for croup, most often caused by parainfluenza virus and reflecting subglottic inflammation. Epiglottitis presents with high fever, toxic appearance, drooling, and a muffled rather than barking cough — and is rare in immunized children. Bacterial tracheitis causes high fever and a toxic, rapidly deteriorating child with thick airway secretions, not a typical barking cough. Foreign body aspiration usually has an abrupt choking event and focal findings; this child had a gradual viral prodrome and no aspiration history.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Croup is largely a clinical diagnosis. If a neck radiograph were obtained, which finding would be expected?
options: Widened retropharyngeal space in this clinical context
Hyperinflation of one lung on expiratory film
Thumbprint sign on lateral neck film
Steeple sign on AP neck film
stages.explanation: The steeple sign — tapered subglottic narrowing on an AP neck radiograph — reflects the subglottic edema of croup, though imaging is usually unnecessary because the diagnosis is clinical. The thumbprint sign on a lateral neck film indicates a swollen epiglottis (epiglottitis). Unilateral hyperinflation on an expiratory film suggests a bronchial foreign body with ball-valve obstruction. A widened retropharyngeal space points to a retropharyngeal abscess. Imaging should never delay airway management in a child with significant distress.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: He has stridor at rest with mild retractions. What is the most appropriate initial pharmacologic treatment?
options: A single dose of oral or IM dexamethasone
Inhaled albuterol as initial therapy
Nebulized hypertonic saline
Empiric IV ceftriaxone and vancomycin
stages.explanation: A single dose of dexamethasone (typically 0.6 mg/kg, oral, IM, or IV) is the cornerstone of croup management for all severities, including mild disease, because it reduces airway edema and the need for return visits. Empiric broad-spectrum IV antibiotics are not indicated for viral croup; they would be reserved for bacterial tracheitis or epiglottitis. Nebulized hypertonic saline is used in bronchiolitis, not croup, and lacks proven benefit here. Albuterol targets lower-airway bronchospasm and does not relieve the subglottic obstruction of croup.

stages.label: ESCALATION
stages.question: An hour later he develops stridor at rest with marked retractions and increasing agitation. What is the most appropriate next intervention?
options: Nebulized racemic epinephrine with observation for at least 3-4 hours
Oral amoxicillin and discharge as the immediate next step
Immediate intubation in the ED as the immediate next step
Discharge home now that dexamethasone has been given in this setting
stages.explanation: For moderate-to-severe croup with stridor at rest and significant retractions, nebulized racemic epinephrine rapidly reduces airway edema; because its effect wanes in about 2 hours, the child must be observed for at least 3-4 hours to watch for rebound stridor before any discharge. Discharging now would be unsafe given worsening obstruction. Immediate intubation is reserved for impending respiratory failure or failure of medical therapy, not first-line for worsening but still-compensated distress. Oral amoxicillin treats bacterial infection and has no role in viral croup.

cases.diagnosis: Croup (viral laryngotracheobronchitis), moderate severity
pearls: Barking cough, hoarse voice, and inspiratory stridor after a viral prodrome in a toddler is classic croup, usually from parainfluenza.
Dexamethasone is given for ALL severities of croup, including mild disease, to reduce edema and return visits.
Nebulized racemic epinephrine is for moderate-to-severe croup; observe 3-4 hours afterward for rebound stridor.
The steeple sign (subglottic narrowing) is the radiographic correlate, but croup is a clinical diagnosis — never delay airway care for imaging.

cases.title: Wheezing Infant with Cold Symptoms
cases.chiefComplaint: Worsening cough, congestion, and fast breathing with poor feeding over the past two days.
cases.history: A 6-month-old girl is brought to the ED in February with three days of rhinorrhea and cough that has progressed to fast, labored breathing and decreased feeding. Her parents report wheezing and a wet cough, and she has taken only about half her usual volume of formula, with fewer wet diapers today. She had a low-grade fever to 38.0 °C at home. There has been no apnea reported, but she pauses and works harder to breathe during feeds. She was born at 38 weeks without complications and has been healthy. Immunizations are up to date for age. She takes no medications and has no allergies. She lives with her parents and a 3-year-old brother who started daycare and currently has a cold; the family does not smoke. There is no family history of asthma or atopy in first-degree relatives, though an aunt has eczema. Birth weight and growth have been normal. No prior wheezing episodes and no history of intubation or chronic lung disease.
cases.exam: Alert but fussy infant with audible congestion. HEENT: copious clear nasal secretions; moist mucous membranes but slightly tacky. Respiratory: tachypnea with nasal flaring, subcostal and intercostal retractions; diffuse bilateral wheezes and fine crackles with a prolonged expiratory phase; air entry adequate. Cardiac: tachycardic, regular, no murmur. Abdomen soft, nondistended. No rash. Capillary refill 2 seconds. Anterior fontanelle soft and flat. She remains interactive and consolable between coughing spells, with intermittent grunting at the height of distress.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Given the seasonal viral prodrome, diffuse wheezes, and crackles in this 6-month-old, what is the most likely diagnosis?
options: Bronchiolitis
First presentation of asthma
Bacterial pneumonia
Congestive heart failure
stages.explanation: Bronchiolitis — most commonly caused by RSV in winter — typically presents in infants under 2 years with a viral upper-respiratory prodrome followed by diffuse wheezes and crackles, tachypnea, retractions, and feeding difficulty, exactly as seen here. Bacterial pneumonia more often causes focal findings and higher fever and is less likely to produce diffuse bilateral wheeze. CHF would usually show a murmur, hepatomegaly, or a cardiac history and is much less common. Asthma is rarely diagnosed at 6 months on a first wheezing episode, and the clear viral context makes bronchiolitis far more likely.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: What is the most appropriate diagnostic approach for this infant with classic bronchiolitis?
options: Blood culture and lumbar puncture in this clinical context
Immediate RSV PCR to guide whether to give antibiotics
Routine chest radiograph and CBC for all cases
Clinical diagnosis without routine labs or imaging
stages.explanation: Bronchiolitis is a clinical diagnosis; routine chest radiographs, CBC, and viral testing are not recommended because they rarely change management and may lead to unnecessary antibiotics. A chest x-ray is reserved for atypical or severe presentations. RSV PCR does not alter management in a stable outpatient or ward setting and would not justify antibiotics, since bronchiolitis is viral. Blood culture and lumbar puncture are appropriate for a septic-appearing or very young febrile infant, but this well-appearing, interactive 6-month-old with a clear viral syndrome does not warrant a full sepsis evaluation.

stages.label: REASSESSMENT
stages.content:
Repeat SpO2 after nasal suctioning: 94% RA
Respiratory rate after suctioning: 56 /min
Rapid RSV antigen: positive
Feeding trial: takes 40 mL then tires
Capillary blood gas: pH 7.36, pCO2 41 mmHg
Urine output: 2 wet diapers in last 12 hours

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: After nasal suctioning she is more comfortable with SpO2 94%. What is the cornerstone of management for RSV bronchiolitis?
options: Systemic corticosteroids as initial therapy
Supportive care: nasal suctioning, hydration,
Nebulized albuterol scheduled every 4 hours as monotherapy
Empiric oral amoxicillin as initial therapy
stages.explanation: Management of bronchiolitis is supportive: nasal suctioning to clear secretions, maintaining hydration (oral or IV/NG if feeding is inadequate), and supplemental oxygen for hypoxemia. Bronchodilators such as albuterol are not routinely recommended because they do not change the disease course in bronchiolitis. Antibiotics are unhelpful for this viral illness unless a bacterial co-infection is documented. Systemic corticosteroids do not improve outcomes in bronchiolitis and are not recommended. Hypertonic saline and chest physiotherapy are likewise not standard. Careful monitoring of feeding, hydration, and work of breathing guides disposition.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: She continues to feed poorly, tires during a feeding trial, and needs low-flow oxygen to keep SpO2 above 90%. What is the most appropriate disposition?
options: Admit for supportive care including supplemental oxygen
Discharge home with albuterol metered-dose inhaler
Discharge with oral antibiotics and follow-up in 48 hours
Admit to the ICU for immediate intubation with appropriate follow-up
stages.explanation: Admission is appropriate because she has persistent hypoxemia requiring oxygen and inadequate oral intake with feeding fatigue — both standard indications for inpatient supportive care, including supplemental oxygen and hydration via NG or IV as needed. Discharge home is unsafe given her oxygen requirement and poor feeding, and an albuterol inhaler would not help. Oral antibiotics are not indicated for viral bronchiolitis. ICU admission for intubation is premature: her blood gas is reassuring (normal pCO2, no acidosis) and she is not in impending respiratory failure, so ward-level supportive care is correct.

cases.diagnosis: Bronchiolitis due to respiratory syncytial virus (RSV)
pearls: Bronchiolitis is a clinical diagnosis in infants under 2 with a viral prodrome plus diffuse wheezes and crackles — routine x-ray, CBC, and viral testing are not needed.
Management is supportive: nasal suctioning, hydration, and oxygen; bronchodilators, steroids, and antibiotics are not routinely recommended.
Admit for hypoxemia requiring oxygen or for inability to maintain hydration due to feeding fatigue.
RSV is the most common cause and peaks in winter; nasal suctioning often dramatically improves the infant's comfort and oxygenation.

cases.title: Sudden High Fever and Drooling in a Child
cases.chiefComplaint: Rapidly worsening sore throat, high fever, drooling, and difficulty breathing over several hours.
cases.history: A 5-year-old boy is brought to the ED by ambulance for a few hours of rapidly progressive sore throat, high fever, and difficulty swallowing. He now refuses to lie down, leans forward, and is drooling. His parents recently moved from a country with limited vaccine access, and his immunization records are incomplete; they believe he may not have received Hib vaccine. He was completely well yesterday. He has no cough and no preceding coryza. There is no choking history or known foreign-body exposure. Past medical history is unremarkable and he takes no medications; no known drug allergies. He lives with his parents and attends kindergarten. Birth and developmental history are normal. No prior airway problems. Family history is noncontributory. On arrival he appears anxious and toxic, sitting upright and resisting movement. The triage nurse notes a muffled voice. Staff have kept him calm in his mother's lap and have not attempted to examine the throat with a tongue depressor.
cases.exam: Toxic-appearing boy sitting in the tripod position, leaning forward with his chin thrust out and mouth open, drooling. Voice is muffled ("hot potato"). Soft inspiratory stridor is audible. He is anxious and minimizes movement. No barking cough. HEENT: throat NOT examined with a tongue blade to avoid precipitating obstruction; no oral foreign body visible externally. Neck: mild tenderness over the anterior neck, no obvious mass. Lungs: air entry decreased with transmitted upper-airway stridor; no focal crackles. Cardiac: tachycardic, regular. Skin warm, well-perfused. He is using mild accessory muscles and is reluctant to speak.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: A toxic 5-year-old with incomplete Hib vaccination presents with high fever, drooling, tripod posture, and a muffled voice. What is the most appropriate immediate action?
options: Examine the oropharynx with a tongue depressor to confirm the diagnosis in this clinical context
Obtain an immediate lateral neck radiograph in the radiology suite as the first step today
Place a large-bore IV and draw blood cultures before anything else as the first step today
Keep the child calm and undisturbed while urgently mobilizing the difficult-airway team
stages.explanation: This is epiglottitis until proven otherwise, an airway emergency. The priority is to keep the child calm and undisturbed and to immediately mobilize the team capable of securing a difficult airway (anesthesia and ENT) for controlled intubation in the OR. Examining the throat with a tongue depressor or any agitating maneuver can precipitate complete laryngospasm and obstruction and is contraindicated. Sending an unstable child to radiology removes them from airway support and risks decompensation. Placing an IV or drawing blood before the airway is secured may agitate the child and is not the first priority — airway comes first.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: If the child were stable enough for imaging, which radiographic finding would support the suspected diagnosis?
options: Bilateral patchy infiltrates on chest film
Thumbprint sign on lateral neck film
Pneumomediastinum as the primary diagnosis
Steeple sign on AP neck film in this clinical context
stages.explanation: Epiglottitis produces the thumbprint sign — an enlarged, rounded epiglottis resembling a thumb — on a lateral neck radiograph, reflecting epiglottic and aryepiglottic fold swelling. The steeple sign (subglottic narrowing) is characteristic of croup, a different and far less emergent entity. Bilateral patchy infiltrates suggest a parenchymal pneumonia, not supraglottic obstruction. Pneumomediastinum can complicate severe airway disease or asthma but is not the diagnostic finding for epiglottitis. Imaging should only be pursued if the airway is stable and the child is accompanied by personnel ready to intervene.

stages.label: AIRWAY MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Where and how should the airway be definitively secured in this child?
options: Rapid-sequence intubation in the ED resuscitation bay by the emergency physician
Controlled intubation in the operating room with ENT prepared for emergent surgical airway
Observation with humidified oxygen and reassessment in 2 hours as the immediate next step
Awake nasotracheal intubation at the bedside without backup as the immediate next step
stages.explanation: Definitive management is controlled intubation in the operating room, with the most experienced provider (often anesthesia) and ENT standing by to perform an emergent surgical airway (tracheostomy or cricothyrotomy) if intubation fails. Attempting routine rapid-sequence intubation in the ED risks losing a tenuous airway without surgical backup. Observation alone is dangerous in a child with stridor, drooling, and a toxic appearance, given the risk of sudden complete obstruction. Bedside awake intubation without surgical backup is unsafe; a failed attempt could be fatal in a critically narrowed supraglottic airway.

stages.label: OPERATIVE & LAB FINDINGS
stages.content:
Direct laryngoscopy in OR: cherry-red, markedly swollen epiglottis
Airway secured: endotracheal intubation successful
WBC: 19.5 ×10³/µL with left shift
Blood culture: pending, later grows Haemophilus influenzae type b
Lactate: 1.8 mmol/L
Vaccination record confirmed: no Hib series documented

stages.label: DEFINITIVE MANAGEMENT
stages.question: The airway is secured and cultures suggest Haemophilus influenzae type b. What is the most appropriate antibiotic therapy?
options: Oral amoxicillin as the immediate next step
Intravenous vancomycin alone in this setting
Nebulized racemic epinephrine and dexamethasone only
Intravenous ceftriaxone
stages.explanation: Empiric IV therapy with a third-generation cephalosporin such as ceftriaxone covers Haemophilus influenzae type b, the classic cause of epiglottitis, including beta-lactamase-producing strains; this is the standard of care. Oral amoxicillin is inadequate for a life-threatening invasive infection and does not reliably cover beta-lactamase producers. Vancomycin alone covers gram-positive organisms (added when MRSA or invasive strep is a concern) but does not cover H. influenzae and is not appropriate as monotherapy. Racemic epinephrine and steroids address airway edema but do not treat the underlying bacterial infection and cannot substitute for antibiotics.

stages.label: PUBLIC HEALTH
stages.question: Beyond treating the patient, what additional public-health measure is indicated for close contacts in confirmed Hib epiglottitis?
options: A 10-day course of amoxicillin for all classmates as the immediate next step
No prophylaxis is ever needed for contacts as the immediate next step
Rifampin prophylaxis for certain close/household contacts, especially with under-immunized young children
Immediate quarantine of the entire kindergarten for 14 days as the immediate next step
stages.explanation: Invasive Hib disease warrants rifampin chemoprophylaxis for selected close contacts — particularly households with incompletely immunized children under 4 years — to eradicate nasopharyngeal carriage and prevent secondary cases; local public-health guidance directs exactly who qualifies. "No prophylaxis ever" is incorrect because Hib is transmissible and secondary cases occur. Empiric amoxicillin for all classmates is not the recommended regimen; rifampin is the standard agent for eradicating carriage. Blanket quarantine of the school is excessive and not how Hib exposures are managed. The case also underscores the value of completing Hib immunization.

cases.diagnosis: Acute epiglottitis due to Haemophilus influenzae type b — airway emergency
pearls: Epiglottitis is a clinical airway emergency: toxic child, high fever, drooling, dysphagia, tripod posture, and muffled voice — keep the child calm and do NOT examine the throat or agitate them.
Secure the airway by controlled intubation in the OR with ENT ready for an emergent surgical airway; the thumbprint sign on lateral neck film supports the diagnosis if stable.
Treat empirically with IV third-generation cephalosporin (ceftriaxone) to cover Hib; add vancomycin if MRSA/invasive strep is a concern.
Invasive Hib disease triggers rifampin prophylaxis for select close contacts; widespread Hib vaccination has made this disease rare.

cases.title: Sudden Choking Then Persistent Cough in a Toddler
cases.chiefComplaint: Sudden coughing and gagging while playing, followed by ongoing cough and noisy breathing.
cases.history: An 18-month-old girl is brought to the ED after a sudden coughing and gagging episode that began while she was playing near her older brother's snack of peanuts about two hours ago. The parents witnessed a brief choking spell with color change that self-resolved, after which she has had persistent intermittent cough and noisy breathing on one side. She had no preceding cold symptoms and no fever. She is otherwise well, developmentally normal, fully immunized, and on no medications, with no known allergies. She lives with her parents and a 4-year-old brother; no household smoke exposure. Birth history was unremarkable. Family history is noncontributory. Since the event, she has been active and is in no severe distress but continues to cough, and the parents note an occasional wheeze they can hear without a stethoscope. There has been no drooling, no stridor at rest, and she is feeding and handling secretions normally. No history of recurrent pneumonia or chronic respiratory symptoms.
cases.exam: Active toddler in mild distress with intermittent coughing, able to play. HEENT: no drooling, oropharynx clear, no stridor at rest. Respiratory: asymmetric breath sounds with decreased air entry and monophonic expiratory wheeze localized to the right lung field; the left lung is clear. Mild intercostal retractions during coughing spells only. No crackles. Cardiac: tachycardic, regular, no murmur. Trachea midline. No cyanosis. She remains interactive and well-perfused. Repeat auscultation confirms the wheeze is unilateral and right-sided, contrasting with the clear contralateral lung.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Given the witnessed choking event and now-localized unilateral wheeze, what is the most likely diagnosis?
options: Viral bronchiolitis in this clinical context
Community-acquired pneumonia
Reactive airway disease / first asthma episode
Foreign body aspiration into a bronchus
stages.explanation: A sudden witnessed choking episode followed by persistent cough and a localized, unilateral monophonic wheeze is the classic picture of bronchial foreign body aspiration, especially with a high-risk food (peanuts) in a toddler. Bronchiolitis causes diffuse, bilateral wheezes and crackles after a viral prodrome, not a sudden choking event with unilateral findings. Pneumonia typically produces fever and focal crackles, and lacks the abrupt aspiration history. A first asthma episode would not begin with a discrete choking event and usually produces bilateral, polyphonic wheeze rather than a fixed unilateral monophonic wheeze.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: She is stable. What is the most appropriate initial imaging study?
options: CT pulmonary angiography as the primary diagnosis
No imaging — proceed directly to MRI of the chest
Lateral neck radiograph as the primary diagnosis
Inspiratory and expiratory chest radiographs
stages.explanation: In a stable child, inspiratory and expiratory chest radiographs (or bilateral decubitus films in an uncooperative toddler) are the appropriate first study; a radiolucent food foreign body acts as a ball valve, trapping air so the affected lung stays hyperinflated on expiration with mediastinal shift away from the affected side. CT angiography evaluates for pulmonary embolism and is not indicated here. A lateral neck film assesses the upper airway (e.g., epiglottitis), not a bronchial foreign body. MRI is not used acutely for airway foreign bodies and is impractical in this setting.

stages.label: IMAGING
stages.content:
Inspiratory film: lungs appear nearly symmetric
Expiratory film: hyperinflation of the right lung with air trapping
Mediastinal shift toward the LEFT on expiration
No radiopaque foreign body visualized
No focal consolidation or pneumothorax
Right hemidiaphragm flattened relative to left

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Imaging shows right-sided air trapping and mediastinal shift, consistent with a radiolucent bronchial foreign body. What is the definitive management?
options: Empiric antibiotics and discharge with follow-up
Blind finger sweep and back blows as initial therapy
Rigid bronchoscopy for removal of the foreign body
Trial of nebulized albuterol and observation
stages.explanation: Rigid bronchoscopy is the definitive diagnostic and therapeutic procedure for a suspected bronchial foreign body, allowing direct visualization and controlled extraction while protecting the airway. A bronchodilator trial does not remove a foreign body and delays definitive care. Empiric antibiotics with discharge is inappropriate because the foreign body remains and risks post-obstructive pneumonia and worsening obstruction. Blind finger sweeps and back blows are reserved for the acutely choking, obstructed patient and can convert a partial to a complete obstruction in a stable, conscious child — they are not appropriate management for a lodged bronchial foreign body.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: If this foreign body had gone unrecognized for weeks, which complication would be most expected?
options: Development of generalized atopic asthma as the leading concern
Recurrent or persistent post-obstructive pneumonia
Complete resolution with no sequelae in all cases as the leading concern
Spontaneous pneumothorax with tension physiology as the leading concern
stages.explanation: A retained bronchial foreign body classically leads to recurrent or non-resolving post-obstructive pneumonia in the same segment and, over time, can cause localized bronchiectasis from chronic obstruction and inflammation — a key reason aspiration should be considered in recurrent same-site pneumonia. Tension pneumothorax is not the typical chronic outcome of a lodged organic foreign body. Aspiration does not cause generalized atopic asthma. Spontaneous resolution "in all cases" is false; organic material like peanut incites significant inflammation and rarely passes on its own, making timely bronchoscopic removal important.

cases.diagnosis: Foreign body aspiration (radiolucent bronchial foreign body, peanut)
pearls: Sudden choking followed by persistent cough and a localized, unilateral monophonic wheeze is foreign body aspiration until proven otherwise.
A radiolucent foreign body causes ball-valve air trapping — expiratory or decubitus films show hyperinflation of the affected lung with mediastinal shift away from it.
Rigid bronchoscopy is the definitive diagnostic and therapeutic step; a normal chest x-ray does NOT exclude aspiration.
Retained foreign bodies cause recurrent same-site pneumonia and can lead to bronchiectasis — consider aspiration in non-resolving focal infection.

cases.title: Newborn with Fits of Coughing and Pauses in Breathing
cases.chiefComplaint: Severe coughing fits with color change and episodes of stopping breathing.
cases.history: A 4-week-old infant is admitted to the pediatric ward for two weeks of progressively worsening cough that has evolved from mild rhinorrhea into severe paroxysms. During fits the infant coughs repeatedly, turns red then dusky, and afterward gags and occasionally vomits; parents have witnessed two brief episodes where the baby stopped breathing and went limp before recovering. There is minimal fever. The baby was born at term and is exclusively breastfed but is now feeding poorly during paroxysms. Immunizations are not yet started given age; the first DTaP is due at 2 months. The mother did not receive Tdap during pregnancy. An older sibling has had a lingering cough for several weeks. The infant takes no medications and has no known allergies. The family does not smoke. Birth history was uncomplicated. Family history is otherwise noncontributory. The parents note the cough comes in clusters, sometimes ending with a high-pitched sound, and that the infant appears well between paroxysms early on but increasingly tired.
cases.exam: Young infant, well-appearing between spells but tired. During an observed paroxysm: repetitive staccato coughing with facial plethora progressing to perioral cyanosis, followed by post-tussive gagging; no classic whoop heard this time. HEENT: scant clear rhinorrhea, mild conjunctival petechiae from coughing. Lungs: largely clear to auscultation between paroxysms, without focal crackles or wheeze. Cardiac: tachycardic, regular, no murmur. Abdomen soft. Neuro: appropriately responsive between spells, normal tone. Anterior fontanelle soft. Capillary refill brisk. The relatively benign lung exam contrasts sharply with the severity of the paroxysms.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: A 4-week-old with paroxysmal cough, post-tussive emesis, apnea, and an under-immunized exposure most likely has which diagnosis?
options: Gastroesophageal reflux disease
Pertussis (whooping cough)
Foreign body aspiration
Respiratory syncytial virus bronchiolitis
stages.explanation: Pertussis is the best fit: a catarrhal prodrome evolving into severe paroxysmal coughing fits with post-tussive emesis and apnea, in an infant too young to be vaccinated, with a household contact who has a prolonged cough and no maternal Tdap. Young infants often lack the classic "whoop" and instead present with apnea and cyanosis. RSV bronchiolitis usually has more diffuse wheeze and crackles and a different cough quality. GERD does not cause apneic paroxysmal coughing fits with conjunctival petechiae. Foreign body aspiration would have an acute choking event and focal findings, not a two-week evolving paroxysmal cough.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which test best confirms the diagnosis at this stage of illness (paroxysmal phase, about 2 weeks of cough)?
options: Serology for pertussis toxin IgG only
Nasopharyngeal PCR for Bordetella pertussis
Sputum Gram stain and culture
Chest CT with contrast in this clinical context
stages.explanation: Nasopharyngeal PCR for Bordetella pertussis is the most sensitive confirmatory test in the first few weeks of cough and is the test of choice in this paroxysmal-phase infant; culture is specific but less sensitive and slow. Pertussis serology (anti-PT IgG) is useful later in the illness (after several weeks) and is not ideal for confirming acute disease in a young infant, especially one too young to mount or distinguish vaccine antibody. A sputum Gram stain/culture is impractical in an infant and not specific for Bordetella. Chest CT is not used to diagnose pertussis and adds radiation without diagnostic benefit.

stages.label: LABS
stages.content:
WBC: 32 ×10³/µL
Lymphocytes: 78% (marked lymphocytosis)
Nasopharyngeal PCR: positive for Bordetella pertussis
Chest radiograph: mild perihilar infiltrate, no consolidation
Electrolytes: within normal limits
Observed apnea with desaturation to 78% during paroxysm on monitor

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: PCR confirms pertussis and there is marked lymphocytosis. What is the most appropriate antibiotic treatment?
options: Amoxicillin as initial therapy
No antibiotic, since antibiotics never alter the course
Oral corticosteroids as monotherapy
A macrolide such as azithromycin
stages.explanation: A macrolide — azithromycin is preferred in young infants — is the treatment of choice for pertussis; while antibiotics given in the paroxysmal phase may not shorten cough duration much, they reduce transmissibility and are recommended, and earlier treatment can blunt severity. Amoxicillin does not reliably eradicate Bordetella and is not a recommended agent. Corticosteroids are not standard pertussis therapy and lack proven benefit. Saying antibiotics never alter the course is incorrect: macrolides decrease infectivity and are indicated, and treatment of close contacts (post-exposure prophylaxis) is also recommended.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: This 4-week-old has documented apnea and desaturation during paroxysms. What is the most appropriate level of monitoring?
options: Discharge home with oral antibiotics and outpatient follow-up
Observation in the ED for 2 hours then discharge based on current risk
Inpatient admission with continuous cardiorespiratory
Routine ward bed with vitals every 8 hours only with appropriate follow-up
stages.explanation: Infants under a few months old with pertussis — especially with observed apnea, cyanosis, and feeding difficulty — require inpatient admission with continuous cardiorespiratory and oximetry monitoring because of the risk of life-threatening apnea, secondary pneumonia, and pulmonary hypertension. Home discharge is unsafe given documented apneic spells. Routine vitals every 8 hours would miss sudden apneic events that need rapid intervention. A brief ED observation followed by discharge does not provide the sustained monitoring this high-risk young infant needs. Severe cases may require ICU-level care.

cases.diagnosis: Pertussis (whooping cough) in a young infant
pearls: Young infants with pertussis often lack the whoop and instead present with apnea, cyanosis, and post-tussive emesis — a relatively clear lung exam contrasts with severe paroxysms.
Marked lymphocytosis is a classic clue; nasopharyngeal PCR is the confirmatory test of choice in the first few weeks.
Azithromycin (a macrolide) is first-line and reduces transmissibility; provide post-exposure prophylaxis to close contacts.
Admit young infants for continuous monitoring due to apnea risk; maternal Tdap in pregnancy and timely infant DTaP prevent disease.

cases.title: Fever, Body Aches, and Worsening Shortness of Breath
cases.chiefComplaint: Three days of fever, myalgias, and dry cough now with new shortness of breath.
cases.history: A 45-year-old woman presents to the ED in January with three days of abrupt fever, severe myalgias, headache, and a dry cough that began suddenly. Over the past 24 hours she has developed worsening shortness of breath and pleuritic chest discomfort with deep breaths. Several coworkers and her school-aged children have had similar febrile respiratory illnesses, and influenza is circulating widely in the community. She did not receive the influenza vaccine this season. Past medical history includes well-controlled asthma, for which she uses an inhaled corticosteroid daily and albuterol as needed. She has no other chronic conditions. Medications are budesonide inhaler and PRN albuterol; no known drug allergies. She is a never-smoker, works as a teacher, and drinks alcohol socially. Family history is unremarkable. She has had no recent travel and no sick contacts with tuberculosis. She reports decreased appetite and fatigue but no hemoptysis. She is mildly dyspneic at rest and feels her asthma is also somewhat flared.
cases.exam: Ill-appearing but alert woman, mildly tachypneic. HEENT: dry mucous membranes, no pharyngeal exudate. Respiratory: bibasilar crackles, more prominent at the right base, with scattered expiratory wheezes; mild increased work of breathing, no accessory muscle use at rest. Cardiac: tachycardic, regular, no murmur or rub. Abdomen benign. Skin warm. No peripheral edema, no calf tenderness. She speaks in full sentences. The combination of crackles and a flared wheeze reflects both parenchymal involvement and her underlying reactive airways.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Given the abrupt febrile illness during flu season with new hypoxemia and crackles, what is the most likely diagnosis?
options: Influenza complicated by viral pneumonia
Pulmonary embolism as the first step today
Decompensated heart failure in this clinical context
Acute asthma exacerbation alone with no infection
stages.explanation: An abrupt onset of fever, myalgias, headache, and dry cough during peak influenza season, with sick contacts and new hypoxemia plus crackles, points to influenza complicated by viral pneumonia. Pulmonary embolism is possible with pleuritic pain and hypoxemia but lacks the febrile viral syndrome and community exposure pattern, and there are no risk factors or calf findings. Heart failure would typically show edema, elevated JVP, or a cardiac history. An isolated asthma exacerbation would not explain the high fever, systemic myalgias, and bibasilar crackles; her airway flare is secondary to the viral infection.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: What is the most appropriate initial diagnostic workup?
options: Influenza PCR (nasopharyngeal) and chest radiograph
CT pulmonary angiography as the first test for everyone
Echocardiogram before any other testing
Sputum acid-fast bacilli smears for tuberculosis
stages.explanation: Influenza PCR (rapid molecular nasopharyngeal testing) confirms the etiology and a chest radiograph evaluates for pneumonia or alternative findings — together these are the appropriate initial workup for a febrile patient with hypoxemia during flu season. CT pulmonary angiography is reserved for cases where PE is genuinely suspected after risk stratification, not as a blanket first test here. AFB smears target tuberculosis, which is not suggested by this acute febrile syndrome without TB risk factors. An echocardiogram is unnecessary first-line given the absence of features suggesting primary cardiac disease.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
Influenza A PCR: positive
WBC: 6.8 ×10³/µL
Procalcitonin: 0.15 ng/mL (low)
Chest radiograph: bilateral interstitial and patchy airspace opacities, right base predominant
SpO2: 92% on room air, improves to 96% on 2 L nasal cannula
Blood cultures: drawn, pending

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Influenza A is confirmed with viral pneumonia on imaging. Regarding antiviral therapy, what is the most appropriate statement and action?
options: Withhold oseltamivir because symptoms began more than 48 hours ago as initial therapy
Defer all treatment and observe without antivirals as initial therapy
Start oseltamivir now, since antiviral therapy is recommended for hospitalized or severe influenza even beyond 48 hours of symptom onset
Antibiotics alone are sufficient; antivirals are never indicated in pneumonia as initial therapy
stages.explanation: Oseltamivir should be started now. Although the greatest benefit of neuraminidase inhibitors is within 48 hours of symptom onset for uncomplicated influenza, antiviral therapy is recommended for hospitalized patients and those with severe or complicated influenza (such as viral pneumonia) regardless of symptom duration, because it can still reduce complications and mortality. Withholding it solely because she is past 48 hours is incorrect in this severe presentation. Antibiotics do not treat the virus, and antivirals are clearly indicated here. Deferring treatment in a hypoxemic patient with influenza pneumonia is inappropriate.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: Two days later she worsens with rising fever, a new lobar consolidation, and a leukocytosis with elevated procalcitonin. What complication is most likely?
options: Spontaneous pneumothorax as the leading concern
Drug reaction to oseltamivir as the leading concern
Pulmonary embolism as the leading concern
Secondary bacterial pneumonia
stages.explanation: Clinical worsening after initial influenza, with a new focal lobar consolidation, rising leukocytosis, and an elevated procalcitonin, is the classic picture of secondary bacterial pneumonia — commonly Streptococcus pneumoniae and, notably, Staphylococcus aureus (including MRSA) — and warrants adding empiric antibiotics. Pulmonary embolism would not explain the new consolidation and rising inflammatory markers. Spontaneous pneumothorax causes sudden pleuritic pain and decreased breath sounds, not consolidation with leukocytosis. An oseltamivir drug reaction would not produce a lobar infiltrate with elevated procalcitonin; this biphasic deterioration is a hallmark of post-influenza bacterial superinfection.

cases.diagnosis: Influenza A complicated by viral pneumonia (with risk of secondary bacterial pneumonia)
pearls: Abrupt fever, myalgias, and dry cough during flu season with sick contacts and new hypoxemia suggests influenza with viral pneumonia.
Start oseltamivir for hospitalized or severe/complicated influenza even beyond 48 hours from symptom onset.
A biphasic course — initial improvement then worsening with focal consolidation and rising procalcitonin — signals secondary bacterial pneumonia (S. pneumoniae, S. aureus/MRSA).
Annual influenza vaccination is the key preventive measure, especially in those with asthma and other chronic conditions.

cases.title: Progressive Breathlessness with Low Oxygen but Few Symptoms
cases.chiefComplaint: One week of dry cough and progressive shortness of breath with surprisingly low oxygen levels.
cases.history: A 57-year-old man is admitted to the medical ward with one week of dry cough, low-grade fevers, fatigue, and progressive exertional dyspnea. He reports loss of taste over the past several days. Despite notably low oxygen saturation on arrival, he appears relatively comfortable and is not in obvious distress. He has hypertension and type 2 diabetes. Medications are lisinopril 20 mg daily and metformin 1000 mg twice daily; no known drug allergies. He is a former smoker with a 15-pack-year history, quit 10 years ago, and drinks alcohol occasionally. He works in an office and has had recent exposure to a coworker with a confirmed respiratory viral infection. He has not received recent respiratory vaccinations. Family history includes coronary disease in his father. He denies chest pain, hemoptysis, leg swelling, or recent immobilization or travel. He reports decreased appetite. There is a regional surge of a respiratory virus in the community. He notes his breathing feels worse with exertion such as walking to the bathroom but is tolerable at rest.
cases.exam: Middle-aged man who looks deceptively well given his oxygen saturation, conversing in full sentences. Respiratory: mild tachypnea; fine bibasilar inspiratory crackles bilaterally, slightly more on the right; no wheeze, no focal dullness. Cardiac: regular rate and rhythm, no murmur, no rub, JVP not elevated. Extremities: no edema, no calf tenderness or asymmetry. Skin warm and well-perfused, no cyanosis evident despite low saturation. Abdomen benign. He is alert and oriented. The striking discordance between his hypoxemia and his comfortable appearance is notable on exam.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: A patient with a week of dry cough, anosmia, fever, and SpO2 86% yet a comfortable appearance during a community viral surge most likely has what?
options: Bacterial lobar pneumonia
Acute decompensated heart failure
Pulmonary embolism in this clinical context
COVID-19 pneumonia with "happy hypoxemia"
stages.explanation: Loss of taste/smell, dry cough, fevers, bilateral crackles, and profound hypoxemia with a deceptively comfortable appearance — "happy" or "silent" hypoxemia — during a community surge strongly suggest COVID-19 pneumonia. Heart failure would typically show elevated JVP, edema, and an S3 or cardiac history, which are absent. Pulmonary embolism is possible with hypoxemia but lacks the febrile viral prodrome, anosmia, and bilateral crackles, and he has no thromboembolic risk factors or leg findings. Bacterial lobar pneumonia usually causes focal consolidation and a more toxic appearance rather than bilateral fine crackles with anosmia.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: What is the most appropriate confirmatory and initial imaging workup?
options: Sputum culture and AFB smear only in this clinical context
D-dimer alone to rule everything out in this clinical context
Echocardiogram as the single best test
SARS-CoV-2 PCR and a chest radiograph or CT chest
stages.explanation: SARS-CoV-2 PCR confirms the diagnosis, and chest imaging (radiograph or CT) characterizes the pneumonia — together the correct initial workup. On CT, COVID-19 classically shows bilateral peripheral ground-glass opacities. A D-dimer alone cannot establish the diagnosis and is nonspecific, though COVID does raise thrombosis risk. Sputum culture and AFB smear target bacterial pneumonia and tuberculosis, which are not the leading concern here. An echocardiogram does not diagnose viral pneumonia and is not indicated as the primary test given the absence of cardiac findings.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
SARS-CoV-2 PCR: positive
CT chest: bilateral peripheral ground-glass opacities
WBC: 5.2 ×10³/µL with lymphopenia (lymphocytes 0.7 ×10³/µL)
C-reactive protein: 88 mg/L
LDH: 410 U/L
D-dimer: 620 ng/mL (mildly elevated)
SpO2: 86% RA, improves to 94% on 4 L nasal cannula

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: PCR confirms COVID-19 pneumonia and he now requires supplemental oxygen (4 L to reach SpO2 94%). What is the most appropriate evidence-based therapy?
options: Systemic dexamethasone, with consideration of remdesivir
High-dose inhaled albuterol only as initial therapy
Azithromycin plus hydroxychloroquine as initial therapy
No treatment beyond room-air observation as initial therapy
stages.explanation: For COVID-19 pneumonia requiring supplemental oxygen, systemic corticosteroids (dexamethasone) reduce mortality and are indicated; the antiviral remdesivir is considered in hospitalized patients with oxygen needs, and pharmacologic VTE prophylaxis is standard given the prothrombotic state. Azithromycin plus hydroxychloroquine has been shown ineffective and is not recommended. Inhaled albuterol alone does not treat the underlying inflammatory pneumonia and oxygenation deficit. Room-air observation without therapy is inappropriate for a hypoxemic patient who now requires oxygen — this is precisely the group that benefits from dexamethasone.

stages.label: ESCALATION
stages.question: Despite therapy he requires escalating oxygen and tires with high-flow nasal cannula at high settings, with rising respiratory rate. What is the most appropriate next step?
options: Switch to oral antibiotics and plan discharge as the immediate next step
Reassess for ICU-level care including possible awake proning
Give a fluid bolus of 2 liters normal saline as the immediate next step
Stop dexamethasone immediately as the immediate next step
stages.explanation: Worsening hypoxemia with increasing work of breathing despite high-flow oxygen warrants reassessment for ICU-level care, including measures such as awake proning to improve oxygenation and timely escalation of respiratory support (up to noninvasive ventilation or intubation if needed). Stopping dexamethasone removes a mortality-reducing therapy and is wrong. Switching to oral antibiotics with discharge is unsafe in a deteriorating hypoxemic patient and does not address the viral pneumonia. A large fluid bolus risks worsening pulmonary edema and oxygenation in ARDS-prone COVID pneumonia and is not indicated without evidence of hypoperfusion.

cases.diagnosis: COVID-19 pneumonia with hypoxemic respiratory failure ("happy hypoxemia")
pearls: "Happy hypoxemia" — marked hypoxemia with a comfortable appearance — is a hallmark of COVID-19 pneumonia; trust the pulse oximeter.
CT classically shows bilateral peripheral ground-glass opacities; labs often show lymphopenia with elevated CRP and LDH.
Dexamethasone reduces mortality in COVID-19 patients requiring supplemental oxygen; add remdesivir consideration and VTE prophylaxis.
Watch for deterioration toward ARDS — escalate to ICU care, consider awake proning, and avoid aggressive fluids.

cases.title: Gradual Breathlessness and Fatigue in a Man with HIV
cases.chiefComplaint: Several weeks of slowly worsening dry cough, fatigue, and breathlessness on exertion.
cases.history: A 36-year-old man with HIV is admitted with about three weeks of insidious, progressively worsening dry cough, low-grade fevers, night sweats, and exertional dyspnea, with noticeable fatigue and a 4-kg weight loss. He was diagnosed with HIV years ago but admits he stopped his antiretroviral therapy several months ago and has not taken any prophylaxis. He reports no chest pain or hemoptysis. Past medical history is otherwise unremarkable. Medications: none currently (previously on antiretroviral therapy). No known drug allergies. He has a remote smoking history and no recent travel. He lives alone; he is sexually active with men and has a prior history of oral thrush. Family history is noncontributory. He reports his exercise tolerance has steadily declined — he now becomes short of breath climbing a single flight of stairs, whereas a month ago he was asymptomatic. He has had no recent TB exposure that he knows of. He notes a sense of breathlessness that is markedly worse with any exertion.

vitals.SpO2: 91% RA at rest, 84% after ambulating in the hallway

cases.exam: Thin, chronically ill-appearing man, mildly tachypneic at rest. HEENT: oral thrush (white plaques on the tongue and buccal mucosa). Respiratory: lungs are nearly clear to auscultation despite significant hypoxemia, with perhaps fine bibasilar crackles; no wheeze, no focal consolidation. Cardiac: tachycardic, regular, no murmur. Abdomen soft, no organomegaly. Skin: no rash, no Kaposi lesions seen. The hallmark finding is profound exertional desaturation (SpO2 falling to the 80s with ambulation) despite an unremarkable lung exam — a key clue. No clubbing.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: An HIV patient off therapy with subacute dry cough, a near-normal lung exam, and marked exertional desaturation most likely has which infection?
options: Pneumocystis jirovecii pneumonia (PJP)
Pulmonary tuberculosis with cavitary disease
Bacterial lobar pneumonia
Acute pulmonary embolism
stages.explanation: Insidious dry cough, low-grade fever, and profound exertional hypoxemia with a relatively clear lung exam in an HIV patient off antiretroviral therapy and prophylaxis is the classic picture of Pneumocystis jirovecii pneumonia (PJP), an AIDS-defining opportunistic infection. Bacterial lobar pneumonia is more acute with focal consolidation and a toxic course. Pulmonary TB can occur in HIV but typically shows more chronic constitutional symptoms with possible cavitation and abnormal imaging, and the exam-imaging discordance here favors PJP. Pulmonary embolism would be more acute and lacks the subacute febrile, immunosuppression-driven context with thrush.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which combination of findings would best support the suspected diagnosis on initial workup?
options: Lobar consolidation on chest x-ray with high procalcitonin in this clinical context
Normal chest imaging with a normal LDH as the primary diagnosis
Diffuse bilateral interstitial/ground-glass infiltrates with an elevated LDH
Unilateral pleural effusion with lymphocytic exudate as the primary diagnosis
stages.explanation: PJP characteristically produces diffuse bilateral interstitial or ground-glass infiltrates (often perihilar) with an elevated serum LDH, and it occurs predominantly when the CD4 count is below 200 cells/µL — all of which support the diagnosis. Lobar consolidation with high procalcitonin points to bacterial pneumonia. Completely normal imaging with a normal LDH argues against significant PJP. A unilateral lymphocytic pleural effusion is more suggestive of tuberculosis. Definitive diagnosis often requires identifying the organism (induced sputum or bronchoalveolar lavage with appropriate staining or PCR).

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
CD4 count: 65 cells/µL
HIV viral load: high (off therapy)
LDH: 520 U/L (elevated)
Chest radiograph: diffuse bilateral interstitial infiltrates, perihilar predominance
ABG on room air: PaO2 62 mmHg, A-a gradient 42 mmHg
Induced sputum / BAL: Pneumocystis jirovecii identified on staining

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: PJP is confirmed. What is the first-line antimicrobial therapy?
options: Isoniazid, rifampin, pyrazinamide, ethambutol
Azithromycin as initial therapy
Trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole
Fluconazole as initial therapy
stages.explanation: Trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole (TMP-SMX) is the first-line treatment for PJP and is dosed by the trimethoprim component, typically for 21 days in HIV-associated disease. Azithromycin treats atypical bacterial and mycobacterium avium complex disease, not Pneumocystis. The four-drug RIPE regimen treats tuberculosis, which is a different opportunistic infection. Fluconazole treats candidal and some other fungal infections (and his thrush), but it is not effective therapy for Pneumocystis. For sulfa-allergic or intolerant patients, alternatives such as clindamycin-primaquine or atovaquone exist, but TMP-SMX remains first-line.

stages.label: ADJUNCTIVE THERAPY
stages.question: His room-air ABG shows PaO2 62 mmHg and an A-a gradient of 42 mmHg. What adjunctive therapy is indicated?
options: Immediate start of full-dose antiretroviral therapy before any other treatment
Inhaled bronchodilators only as the immediate next step
Adjunctive corticosteroids (e.g., prednisone)
No adjunctive therapy regardless of oxygenation
stages.explanation: Adjunctive corticosteroids are indicated in moderate-to-severe PJP — defined by a room-air PaO2 below 70 mmHg or an A-a gradient of at least 35 mmHg — because they reduce mortality and respiratory failure by blunting the inflammatory response triggered by organism killing; both his PaO2 (62) and A-a gradient (42) meet criteria. Inhaled bronchodilators do not address PJP-related inflammation. Withholding steroids despite meeting hypoxemia criteria forgoes a proven mortality benefit. Antiretroviral therapy is important but is generally started after initiating PJP treatment (within a couple of weeks) to avoid severe immune reconstitution inflammatory syndrome and is not the immediate adjunct here.

cases.diagnosis: Pneumocystis jirovecii pneumonia (PJP) in advanced HIV/AIDS
pearls: Suspect PJP in HIV patients with CD4 below 200 who have subacute dry cough and marked exertional desaturation with a near-normal lung exam.
Elevated LDH and diffuse bilateral ground-glass/interstitial infiltrates are classic; confirm via induced sputum or BAL staining/PCR.
First-line treatment is TMP-SMX for 21 days; alternatives exist for sulfa intolerance (clindamycin-primaquine, atovaquone).
Add corticosteroids for moderate-to-severe disease (room-air PaO2 < 70 mmHg or A-a gradient ≥ 35 mmHg) — they reduce mortality.

cases.title: Coughing Up Blood Years After Treated Lung Infection
cases.chiefComplaint: Intermittent coughing up of blood over the past two months.
cases.history: A 62-year-old man presents to pulmonary clinic with two months of intermittent hemoptysis, ranging from blood-streaked sputum to occasional larger amounts. He reports mild fatigue but denies fevers, night sweats, or significant weight loss currently. He was treated for pulmonary tuberculosis 30 years ago and was told he had residual scarring with a cavity in the right upper lobe. He has chronic obstructive pulmonary disease from a 40-pack-year smoking history and continues to smoke. Medications: tiotropium inhaler daily and albuterol as needed; no anticoagulants. No known drug allergies. He worked in construction and lives with his wife. Family history is noncontributory. He has not traveled recently. He denies chest pain and has no leg swelling. He is concerned the blood may signal recurrent TB or cancer. He reports the hemoptysis is becoming somewhat more frequent. He has not had any recent respiratory infection and is up to date on routine vaccinations. He has no known immunodeficiency.
cases.exam: Well-appearing older man in no acute distress, speaking comfortably. HEENT: no oral lesions; no blood currently in the oropharynx. Respiratory: decreased breath sounds and coarse crackles at the right upper lung field; prolonged expiratory phase with scattered wheezes consistent with COPD; no current active bleeding visualized. Cardiac: regular rate and rhythm, no murmur. Extremities: no clubbing, no edema, no calf tenderness. No lymphadenopathy. The chronic right-upper-lobe findings correspond to his old cavitary disease. He is hemodynamically stable with no signs of massive ongoing hemorrhage at this visit.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: In a man with an old TB cavity in the right upper lobe now presenting with hemoptysis, which diagnosis should be high on the differential alongside recurrent TB and malignancy?
options: Pulmonary alveolar proteinosis
Aspergilloma within the pre-existing cavity
Idiopathic pulmonary fibrosis
Sarcoidosis as the first step today
stages.explanation: An aspergilloma — a mycetoma of Aspergillus colonizing a pre-existing cavity — characteristically forms in old tuberculous cavities and is a classic cause of hemoptysis in this setting, alongside reactivation TB and lung cancer, all of which must be considered. Pulmonary alveolar proteinosis causes diffuse alveolar filling and a "crazy-paving" CT pattern, not a cavitary fungus ball with hemoptysis. Sarcoidosis typically produces hilar adenopathy and upper-lobe reticulonodular changes, not a fungus ball. Idiopathic pulmonary fibrosis causes basilar fibrosis and honeycombing, not cavitary disease with a mobile intracavitary mass.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which imaging finding would most specifically support an aspergilloma?
options: A solitary peripheral spiculated nodule with no cavity as the primary diagnosis
A mobile intracavitary mass with surrounding air , often shifting with patient position
Diffuse bilateral ground-glass opacities as the primary diagnosis
Bilateral hilar lymphadenopathy as the primary diagnosis
stages.explanation: The air-crescent sign — a rounded intracavitary mass (the fungus ball) separated from the cavity wall by a crescent of air, classically mobile when the patient changes position — is the hallmark radiographic finding of an aspergilloma. Diffuse bilateral ground-glass opacities suggest an interstitial or alveolar process such as a viral or atypical pneumonia, not a mycetoma. A solitary spiculated nodule without a cavity is more concerning for malignancy. Bilateral hilar lymphadenopathy suggests sarcoidosis or lymphoma. CT chest best demonstrates the mass, the surrounding air crescent, and its mobility.

stages.label: IMAGING & LABS
stages.content:
CT chest: rounded soft-tissue mass within a right-upper-lobe cavity, separated from the wall by a crescent of air (air-crescent sign); mass moves with repositioning
Sputum AFB smears: negative ×3
Sputum fungal culture: Aspergillus fumigatus
Serum Aspergillus IgG (precipitins): positive
CBC: within normal limits
Sputum cytology: no malignant cells

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Imaging and cultures confirm an aspergilloma, and his hemoptysis is mild and intermittent. What is the most appropriate management approach for a simple, mildly symptomatic aspergilloma?
options: A short course of broad-spectrum antibacterial antibiotics as initial therapy
Conservative observation with monitoring
Long-term oral corticosteroids alone as initial therapy
Immediate lobectomy for everyone regardless of symptoms or operability as initial therapy
stages.explanation: For a simple aspergilloma with mild symptoms, observation with monitoring is reasonable because antifungals penetrate the fungus ball poorly and asymptomatic lesions may be watched; the definitive treatment for significant or recurrent hemoptysis is surgical resection in patients with adequate pulmonary reserve. Routine immediate lobectomy for everyone ignores operative risk in patients with poor lung function (like his COPD). Corticosteroids would suppress immunity without treating the fungus ball and are not appropriate. Antibacterial antibiotics do not treat a fungal mycetoma. Antifungal therapy has a limited role for simple aspergilloma given poor cavity penetration.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: He returns weeks later with massive hemoptysis (large-volume bleeding) and is hemodynamically stabilized. Besides airway protection, what is the most appropriate next intervention to control bleeding when surgery is not immediately feasible?
options: Start voriconazole and observe without further intervention
Systemic corticosteroids as the leading concern
Oral tranexamic acid alone and discharge
Bronchial artery embolization
stages.explanation: In massive hemoptysis, after securing the airway and stabilizing the patient (and positioning bleeding-side down to protect the contralateral lung), bronchial artery embolization is the key intervention to rapidly control bleeding, especially when emergent surgery is not feasible or the patient has limited pulmonary reserve. Oral tranexamic acid alone with discharge is inadequate for life-threatening bleeding. Systemic corticosteroids do not control hemorrhage and may worsen infection. Starting voriconazole without controlling the bleed does not address the acute hemorrhage, since antifungals penetrate the mycetoma poorly and act too slowly for an emergency; definitive surgical resection follows once the patient is stabilized.

stages.label: DEFINITIVE PLAN
stages.question: After bleeding is controlled, what is the definitive treatment for a symptomatic aspergilloma in a patient with adequate pulmonary reserve?
options: No further treatment is ever warranted
Lifelong inhaled corticosteroids in this setting
Surgical resection of the involved lung segment/lobe
Repeat courses of antibacterial antibiotics
stages.explanation: Surgical resection (segmentectomy or lobectomy) of the cavity containing the fungus ball is the definitive treatment for a symptomatic aspergilloma — particularly after life-threatening hemoptysis — in patients with sufficient pulmonary reserve to tolerate the operation. Lifelong inhaled corticosteroids do not eradicate the mycetoma and may impair local defenses. Repeated antibacterial antibiotic courses do not treat the fungal mass. "No further treatment" is incorrect after significant hemoptysis, given the high risk of recurrent, potentially fatal bleeding. In poor surgical candidates, bronchial artery embolization and antifungal therapy serve as palliative or temporizing options.

cases.diagnosis: Aspergilloma (fungus ball) in a chronic post-tuberculous cavity
pearls: Hemoptysis in a patient with an old TB cavity should prompt consideration of aspergilloma alongside reactivation TB and malignancy.
The air-crescent sign — a mobile intracavitary mass with surrounding air — is the radiographic hallmark; positive Aspergillus precipitins support the diagnosis.
Antifungals penetrate the fungus ball poorly; simple, mildly symptomatic aspergillomas may be observed, while surgical resection is definitive for significant disease.
For massive hemoptysis, protect the airway, position bleeding-side down, and use bronchial artery embolization to temporize before definitive surgery.

cases.title: Persistent Fever in a Transplant Recipient
cases.chiefComplaint: Fever and pleuritic chest pain unresponsive to broad-spectrum antibiotics.
cases.history: A 48-year-old man is in the ICU on day 18 after an allogeneic hematopoietic stem-cell transplant for acute myeloid leukemia. He has been profoundly neutropenic (ANC <200/µL) for 12 days. He developed fever to 39.1 °C six days ago and was started on cefepime, then meropenem and vancomycin two days later for persistent fever without an identified source. Despite 96 hours of broad-spectrum antibacterials he remains febrile and has developed new right-sided pleuritic chest pain and a dry cough. He denies hemoptysis, sinus pain, or skin lesions. PMH: AML in remission, prior CMV reactivation. Meds: meropenem, vancomycin, acyclovir prophylaxis, tacrolimus, methylprednisolone for grade II GVHD, and posaconazole prophylaxis that was held 4 days for an interaction. No known drug allergies. Social: nonsmoker, works as a landscaper, no recent travel. Family history noncontributory. He received red cell and platelet transfusions this week.
cases.exam: Ill-appearing, fatigued man. Mucous membranes show mild mucositis without thrush. Lungs: decreased breath sounds and a faint pleural friction rub at the right base; no diffuse crackles or wheeze. Cardiac: tachycardic, regular, no murmur. No sinus tenderness, no nasal eschar, palate intact. Skin: no nodules, ecthyma, or necrotic lesions. No focal neurologic deficit. No lymphadenopathy. Mild lower-extremity edema attributed to fluids. Central line site clean without erythema or discharge.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Given persistent neutropenic fever despite 96 hours of broad-spectrum antibacterials, what is the most appropriate next diagnostic step?
options: Switch meropenem to ceftazidime-avibactam for resistant gram-negatives
Order a transthoracic echocardiogram to evaluate for endocarditis
Obtain a high-resolution CT of the chest and send serum galactomannan
Empirically add trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole for Pneumocystis
Repeat blood cultures only and continue current antibiotics for 48 more hours
stages.explanation: Persistent neutropenic fever beyond 4-7 days of broad-spectrum antibacterials, especially with new respiratory findings, should prompt evaluation for invasive fungal disease (IFD) — chest CT plus serum galactomannan is the standard next step and can reveal early angioinvasive lesions. Repeating cultures alone delays diagnosis of a likely mold infection. Empiric TMP-SMX targets Pneumocystis, which is less likely here given the focal pleuritic findings and rare on a recipient with antibacterial focus; it does not address the leading concern. Switching gram-negative coverage ignores that bacterial coverage is already broad and fever is fungal until proven otherwise. Echocardiography is reasonable only if endocarditis is suspected, and the line site and exam are benign.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
WBC 0.4 ×10³/µL
ANC 120 /µL
Platelets 28 ×10³/µL
Na 137 mEq/L
Creatinine 1.0 mg/dL
Serum galactomannan index 1.8 (positive, ≥0.5)
1,3-beta-D-glucan 280 pg/mL (positive)
HRCT chest: 3.2 cm right-upper-lobe nodule with surrounding ground-glass halo; second subpleural nodule in the right lower lobe
Blood cultures: no growth at 96 hours

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: The CT shows a nodule with a surrounding ground-glass halo. What does this 'halo sign' most directly represent in this patient?
options: Perinodular hemorrhage from angioinvasion by a mold
Eosinophilic infiltration from a hypersensitivity reaction
Reactive lymphangitic spread of metastatic leukemia
Organizing pneumonia surrounding a bacterial abscess
stages.explanation: In a neutropenic host, the halo sign is the early radiographic hallmark of angioinvasive aspergillosis: hyphae invade pulmonary vessels, causing infarction and surrounding alveolar hemorrhage that appears as ground-glass attenuation around a solid nodule. It is an early, transient finding most visible while the patient is neutropenic. Lymphangitic carcinomatosis produces interlobular septal thickening, not a discrete halo nodule, and leukemic relapse rarely presents this way acutely. A bacterial abscess with organizing pneumonia would typically cavitate with an air-fluid level, not a hemorrhagic halo, and blood cultures are negative. Eosinophilic infiltrates produce migratory peripheral consolidation, not a vascular-invasion halo, and there is no eosinophilia here.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Which antifungal is the first-line agent for probable invasive pulmonary aspergillosis in this patient?
options: Liposomal amphotericin B
Fluconazole as initial therapy
Voriconazole
Caspofungin monotherapy
Posaconazole at the prior prophylactic dose
stages.explanation: Voriconazole is the first-line treatment for invasive aspergillosis based on superior survival versus amphotericin B in the landmark trial; isavuconazole is an equivalent first-line alternative. Liposomal amphotericin B is an effective alternative when triazoles are contraindicated or not tolerated, but it is not preferred first-line. Fluconazole has no meaningful activity against Aspergillus. Caspofungin (an echinocandin) is salvage or combination therapy, not first-line monotherapy. Resuming posaconazole at a prophylactic dose is inadequate — breakthrough infection on a mold-active azole signals treatment-dose therapy is required, ideally with a different agent and therapeutic drug monitoring. Check a baseline ECG (QT) and remember the tacrolimus interaction with voriconazole.

stages.label: DRUG INTERACTION
stages.question: After starting voriconazole, what is the most important pharmacologic consideration given his transplant regimen?
options: Voriconazole and tacrolimus have no clinically relevant interaction in this setting
Tacrolimus should be stopped permanently while on voriconazole in this setting
Voriconazole induces tacrolimus metabolism, requiring a higher tacrolimus dose
Voriconazole inhibits CYP3A4, raising tacrolimus levels
stages.explanation: Voriconazole is a potent CYP3A4 inhibitor, and tacrolimus is metabolized by CYP3A4, so co-administration markedly raises tacrolimus levels and the risk of nephrotoxicity and neurotoxicity. The standard approach is to reduce the tacrolimus dose substantially (often to roughly one-third) when starting voriconazole and to monitor tacrolimus trough levels closely. Voriconazole inhibits rather than induces this pathway, so increasing tacrolimus would cause dangerous toxicity. The interaction is clinically significant and cannot be ignored. Stopping tacrolimus permanently is unnecessary and would risk graft-versus-host disease flare; the goal is dose adjustment with monitoring, not discontinuation. Voriconazole levels themselves should also be monitored for efficacy and toxicity.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: Beyond antifungal therapy, what adjunctive measure most improves the likelihood of clearing this infection?
options: Adding high-dose corticosteroids to reduce pulmonary inflammation
Immediate surgical lobectomy of all involved segments with appropriate follow-up
Strict fluid restriction to minimize alveolar hemorrhage with appropriate follow-up
Recovery of the neutrophil count, supported by reducing immunosuppression
stages.explanation: Neutrophil recovery is the single most important determinant of outcome in invasive aspergillosis; tapering corticosteroids and other immunosuppression where feasible and supporting count recovery (e.g., with G-CSF in selected cases) directly improves host defense. Adding corticosteroids would worsen immunosuppression and is contraindicated unless needed for another indication. Fluid restriction does not treat angioinvasive disease and risks hypoperfusion.
