Surgical resection is reserved for specific situations — lesions abutting great vessels, massive hemoptysis, or persistent localized disease before further chemotherapy — not as routine therapy for multifocal disease that should respond to antifungals once neutropenia resolves.

cases.diagnosis: Invasive pulmonary aspergillosis (probable, EORTC/MSG criteria) in a neutropenic allogeneic stem-cell transplant recipient
pearls: Persistent neutropenic fever beyond 4-7 days of broad-spectrum antibacterials should trigger a CT chest and serum galactomannan to evaluate for invasive mold.
The halo sign (nodule with ground-glass rim) is an early, transient marker of angioinvasive aspergillosis; the air-crescent sign appears later during neutrophil recovery.
Voriconazole (or isavuconazole) is first-line for invasive aspergillosis; voriconazole inhibits CYP3A4 and sharply raises tacrolimus levels.
Neutrophil recovery and reduction of immunosuppression are the strongest drivers of survival in invasive aspergillosis.

cases.title: Mediastinal Fullness in a Spelunker
cases.chiefComplaint: Three weeks of low-grade fevers, dry cough, and fatigue after a caving trip.
cases.history: A 39-year-old man presents to clinic with three weeks of malaise, low-grade fevers, dry cough, and pleuritic chest discomfort. He is an avid cave explorer and returned five weeks ago from a multi-day caving expedition along the Ohio River valley, where he crawled through bat-roosting chambers thick with guano. Several others on the trip developed similar symptoms. He initially attributed it to a 'summer cold' but now reports drenching night sweats and a 4-pound weight loss. He denies hemoptysis, joint pains, or rash. PMH: none. Meds: none. No allergies. Social: occasional alcohol, never smoker, monogamous, no IV drug use. Works as a software engineer. Family history unremarkable. He has no known immunocompromise and an HIV test last year was negative. On review of systems he notes a vague substernal ache and mild dysphagia when eating quickly. No recent antibiotics. No sick contacts other than his caving companions. Travel otherwise limited to the Midwest United States.
cases.exam: Well-appearing man in no distress. HEENT: no oral lesions or thrush; no cervical or supraclavicular lymphadenopathy. Lungs: clear to auscultation with no crackles or wheeze. Cardiac: regular rate and rhythm, no rub or murmur. Abdomen: soft, nontender; liver and spleen not enlarged. Skin: no erythema nodosum or other lesions. No clubbing. No peripheral lymphadenopathy elsewhere. Neurologic exam normal.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which exposure detail most strongly shapes the differential for this subacute pulmonary illness?
options: Caving in bat-roosting chambers in the Ohio River valley
Recent negative HIV test one year ago
Mild intermittent dysphagia with quick eating
Software engineering occupation with sedentary lifestyle
stages.explanation: Caving in bat- and bird-dropping-laden soil in the Ohio and Mississippi River valleys is the classic exposure for Histoplasma capsulatum; disturbed guano aerosolizes microconidia that are inhaled. A cluster of symptomatic companions further supports a point-source environmental exposure. The sedentary occupation is irrelevant to the infectious differential. A negative HIV test a year ago is reassuring but does not pinpoint the diagnosis and does not exclude exposure-driven disease in an immunocompetent host. The dysphagia is a clue to mediastinal pathology but is a downstream effect rather than the exposure that frames the differential; the geography and guano contact are the decisive epidemiologic features here.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
WBC 6.8 ×10³/µL
Hemoglobin 14.1 g/dL
LDH 240 U/L (mildly elevated)
Chest X-ray: right paratracheal and subcarinal mediastinal widening; patchy right-middle-lobe infiltrate
CT chest: enlarged mediastinal and hilar lymph nodes (largest 2.6 cm) with central low attenuation; small calcified granuloma in the lung
Urine Histoplasma antigen: positive
HIV antigen/antibody: negative

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which test offers the highest yield for rapidly confirming acute pulmonary histoplasmosis in this presentation?
options: Fungal blood cultures held for 4-6 weeks
Urine and serum Histoplasma antigen testing
Tuberculin skin test (PPD) in this clinical context
Histoplasma complement-fixation serology alone
stages.explanation: Urine (and serum) Histoplasma polysaccharide antigen is the highest-yield rapid test for acute, moderate-to-severe pulmonary or disseminated disease and is already positive here. Fungal cultures are definitive but slow (weeks) and have low sensitivity in acute self-limited pulmonary disease, so they are not the rapid confirmatory test. Complement-fixation serology is useful and supportive but takes weeks to rise, can cross-react with other endemic mycoses, and is less sensitive early; antigen testing turns around faster. A PPD evaluates for tuberculosis, which is on the differential for mediastinal adenopathy but does not confirm histoplasmosis. Antigen testing combined with the classic exposure and imaging clinches the diagnosis quickly.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: He has had symptoms for three weeks with persistent fevers and mediastinal adenopathy but is hemodynamically stable with normal oxygenation. What is the most appropriate management?
options: No antifungal therapy; observe as most cases self-resolve
Itraconazole for 6-12 weeks
Liposomal amphotericin B followed by lifelong suppression
Empiric four-drug antitubercular therapy as initial therapy
stages.explanation: For acute pulmonary histoplasmosis with symptoms persisting beyond about one month (he is at three weeks and worsening) or with significant adenopathy, itraconazole for 6-12 weeks is recommended; mild disease under four weeks in an immunocompetent host can be observed. Pure observation is reasonable only for brief, mild disease, which this is not given prolonged fevers and bulky nodes. Liposomal amphotericin B is reserved for severe or disseminated disease, not stable moderate pulmonary disease, and lifelong suppression applies to certain immunocompromised hosts. Empiric antitubercular therapy is inappropriate given a positive Histoplasma antigen and the classic guano exposure, and would not treat the confirmed fungal infection. Itraconazole levels should be monitored.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: Months later he returns with worsening dysphagia and a sensation of food sticking. CT shows progressive, dense soft tissue encasing mediastinal structures. What complication should be considered?
options: Sarcoidosis with bulky adenopathy
Fibrosing mediastinitis
Esophageal adenocarcinoma
Reactivation tuberculosis
stages.explanation: Fibrosing (sclerosing) mediastinitis is a rare but classic late complication of histoplasmosis: an exuberant fibrotic response encases mediastinal structures, compressing the esophagus, airways, superior vena cava, or pulmonary vessels and producing progressive dysphagia or SVC syndrome. It is driven by the host fibrotic reaction rather than active infection, so antifungals are often ineffective and management is largely supportive or endovascular/surgical for symptomatic obstruction. Reactivation tuberculosis can cause adenopathy but would not produce this dense fibrotic encasement pattern and is not supported by his prior antigen-positive diagnosis. Esophageal adenocarcinoma is unlikely at his age without chronic reflux and would show a mucosal mass, not diffuse fibrosis. Sarcoidosis causes adenopathy but not this progressive constrictive fibrosis.

cases.diagnosis: Acute pulmonary histoplasmosis with mediastinal lymphadenopathy
pearls: Histoplasmosis is endemic to the Ohio and Mississippi River valleys; cave exposure and disturbed bird or bat droppings are classic risks.
Urine and serum Histoplasma antigen testing is the fastest high-yield diagnostic in acute moderate-to-severe or disseminated disease.
Itraconazole is first-line for symptoms lasting beyond ~1 month or for bulky adenopathy; severe/disseminated disease warrants liposomal amphotericin B.
Fibrosing mediastinitis is a late, fibrosis-driven complication that often does not respond to antifungals.

cases.title: Cough and Leg Rash After a Desert Trip
cases.chiefComplaint: Three weeks of cough, fatigue, and tender red leg bumps after travel to the Southwest.
cases.history: A 44-year-old man from the Midwest presents with three weeks of dry cough, low-grade fevers, fatigue, and arthralgias. Two weeks before symptom onset he spent a week in the deserts around Phoenix and Tucson, Arizona, riding ATVs through dusty terrain and hiking after a windstorm. Over the past few days he developed tender, raised red nodules on both shins. He also reports drenching night sweats and chest discomfort. He denies hemoptysis, weight loss, or headache. PMH: none. Meds: ibuprofen as needed. No allergies. Social: never smoker, social drinker, works in insurance, no IV drug use. He is not immunocompromised; HIV testing is negative. Family history noncontributory. No sick contacts. He recalls thick dust storms during the trip and did not wear a mask. No recent hospitalization or antibiotics. He has no history of asthma. He is otherwise healthy and active and has not traveled internationally.
cases.exam: Well-appearing, mildly fatigued. Lungs: scattered faint crackles at the right base, no wheeze. Cardiac: regular, no murmur or rub. Skin: multiple tender, warm, erythematous subcutaneous nodules over the anterior shins bilaterally consistent with erythema nodosum; no other rash. Joints: mild diffuse arthralgia without effusion or synovitis. No cervical or supraclavicular adenopathy. Abdomen soft, no organomegaly. Neurologic exam normal; no meningismus.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: What initial laboratory finding would best fit the suspected diagnosis given his exposure and erythema nodosum?
options: Microcytic anemia with low ferritin
Marked neutrophilia with toxic granulation
Profound lymphopenia with atypical lymphocytes
Peripheral eosinophilia
stages.explanation: Peripheral eosinophilia is characteristic of coccidioidomycosis, particularly in the setting of erythema nodosum and other hypersensitivity manifestations of the acute infection. The clinical picture — desert dust exposure in Arizona, subacute respiratory illness, arthralgias, and erythema nodosum (so-called 'desert rheumatism' / valley fever) — points to Coccidioides. Marked neutrophilia with toxic granulation suggests acute bacterial infection, not this immune-mediated fungal syndrome. Profound lymphopenia with atypical lymphocytes points toward viral processes such as acute HIV or EBV, not coccidioidomycosis. Microcytic anemia reflects iron deficiency and is unrelated to this acute presentation. Eosinophilia plus erythema nodosum in a returning desert traveler is a classic combination.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
WBC 9.6 ×10³/µL with 12% eosinophils (absolute 1,150 /µL)
ESR 58 mm/hr
Chest X-ray: focal right-lower-lobe consolidation with small ipsilateral hilar adenopathy
Coccidioides EIA IgM and IgG: positive
Coccidioides complement fixation titer: 1:8
HIV antigen/antibody: negative

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: How is the diagnosis of coccidioidomycosis most commonly confirmed in this clinical setting?
options: Acid-fast staining of sputum as the primary diagnosis
Urine antigen testing as the sole modality
Serology
Interferon-gamma release assay in this clinical context
stages.explanation: Coccidioidal serology — enzyme immunoassay for IgM/IgG, confirmed by immunodiffusion, with complement-fixation titers for severity and monitoring — is the most widely used and practical means of diagnosis in primary pulmonary disease, and is already positive here. Rising complement-fixation titers correlate with disseminated disease and guide follow-up. Acid-fast staining targets mycobacteria and is irrelevant to a fungus. Urine antigen testing exists for Coccidioides but is less sensitive in mild-to-moderate disease and cross-reacts with other endemic mycoses, so it is not the primary stand-alone confirmatory test. Interferon-gamma release assays test for tuberculosis, not coccidioidomycosis. Culture and direct visualization of spherules are confirmatory but serology is the usual route to diagnosis.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: He is immunocompetent with mild-to-moderate primary pulmonary coccidioidomycosis and stable vitals. What is the most appropriate management?
options: Lifelong fluconazole regardless of severity as initial therapy
Empiric antitubercular therapy until cultures finalize as initial therapy
Liposomal amphotericin B induction for all primary pulmonary cases as initial therapy
Observation with close follow-up, since many immunocompetent primary infections resolve without antifungals
stages.explanation: Most cases of primary pulmonary coccidioidomycosis in immunocompetent patients are self-limited and can be managed with observation and close follow-up; antifungal therapy (typically fluconazole or itraconazole) is reserved for severe, prolonged, or extensive disease, or for patients at higher risk for dissemination (pregnancy, diabetes, immunosuppression, certain ethnic groups, very high CF titers). Liposomal amphotericin B is reserved for severe or disseminated disease, not routine primary infection. Lifelong therapy is not indicated for uncomplicated primary disease and is reserved for situations such as coccidioidal meningitis. Empiric antitubercular therapy is wrong given confirmed positive coccidioidal serology. Erythema nodosum here actually portends a robust immune response and lower dissemination risk.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: Which follow-up parameter best monitors for the development of disseminated coccidioidomycosis?
options: Repeated tuberculin skin tests
Serial D-dimer levels in this clinical context
Serial complement-fixation antibody titers
Serial absolute eosinophil counts
stages.explanation: Serial complement-fixation (CF) titers are the key serologic marker for monitoring coccidioidomycosis: rising or persistently high CF titers (often a threshold around 1:16 or higher) correlate with disseminated disease and warrant evaluation for extrapulmonary spread, including meningitis. The eosinophil count reflects the acute hypersensitivity response but does not reliably track dissemination over time. Tuberculin skin testing is for tuberculosis and has no role in monitoring Coccidioides. D-dimer is a nonspecific marker of thrombosis or inflammation and does not track fungal dissemination. Following CF titers, along with symptoms and imaging, is the standard approach to detect progression and decide on antifungal therapy.

cases.diagnosis: Primary pulmonary coccidioidomycosis (valley fever) with erythema nodosum
pearls: Coccidioidomycosis is endemic to the Southwestern US deserts; dust exposure plus cough, arthralgias, and erythema nodosum equals 'valley fever' / desert rheumatism.
Peripheral eosinophilia and erythema nodosum are classic immune-mediated features and actually signal a lower risk of dissemination.
Serology (EIA/immunodiffusion plus complement-fixation titers) is the practical diagnostic mainstay; most immunocompetent primary cases are self-limited.
Rising complement-fixation titers (often ≥1:16) suggest dissemination and should prompt evaluation, including for meningitis.

cases.title: Foul Cough in a Man With Bad Teeth
cases.chiefComplaint: Two weeks of fever, productive cough with foul-smelling sputum, and weight loss.
cases.history: A 54-year-old man is admitted with two weeks of fevers, drenching night sweats, productive cough with copious foul-smelling sputum, and a 7-pound weight loss. He reports an episode of heavy alcohol intoxication about three weeks ago after which he 'slept it off' on his back; he recalls choking and coughing the next morning. He has severe dental caries and gingivitis and has not seen a dentist in years. He denies hemoptysis, sick contacts, or recent travel. PMH: alcohol use disorder, untreated GERD, prior generalized seizure during withdrawal. Meds: none consistently. No known allergies. Social: drinks a pint of liquor daily, smokes half a pack per day for 30 years, lives alone. Family history noncontributory. He has had progressive fatigue and decreased appetite. No prior tuberculosis exposure that he knows of, and no HIV risk factors he acknowledges. He has had no recent hospitalization. The sputum is described by nursing as putrid.
cases.exam: Cachectic, malodorous breath. Oral exam: severe caries, multiple loose teeth, purulent gingivitis. Lungs: bronchial breath sounds and coarse crackles over the right posterior mid-zone; egophony in that area; no diffuse wheeze. Cardiac: tachycardic, regular, no murmur. Clubbing not present. No cervical lymphadenopathy. Abdomen soft, mild hepatomegaly. No peripheral edema. Neurologic exam nonfocal; not encephalopathic at present.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Given aspiration after intoxication and foul sputum, which lung region is most likely involved if he aspirated while supine?
options: Anterior basal segment of the left lower lobe
Lingula of the left upper lobe as the first step today
Superior segment of the right lower lobe
Right middle lobe as the first step today
stages.explanation: Aspiration follows gravity. In a supine patient, aspirated material drains to the most dependent segments — classically the superior segments of the lower lobes and the posterior segments of the upper lobes, with right-sided predominance because the right mainstem bronchus is wider and more vertical. His exam findings localize to the right posterior mid-zone, fitting this pattern. The lingula and right middle lobe are dependent in the upright position, not supine. The anterior basal segment of the left lower lobe is not a classic aspiration location and is on the less-favored left side. Recognizing the typical aspiration segments helps anticipate where a cavitary lesion will appear on imaging.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
WBC 16.4 ×10³/µL (neutrophil predominant)
Hemoglobin 11.8 g/dL
Chest X-ray: cavitary lesion with an air-fluid level in the right lung, posterior distribution
CT chest: 5 cm thick-walled cavity with an internal air-fluid level in the superior segment of the right lower lobe; surrounding consolidation
Sputum Gram stain: mixed flora with numerous PMNs
HIV antigen/antibody: negative

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: The CT shows a thick-walled cavity with an air-fluid level. Which organisms are the most likely cause in this aspiration setting?
options: Mycobacterium tuberculosis as the primary diagnosis
Anaerobes and oral streptococci from the gingival flora
Methicillin-resistant Staphylococcus aureus
Pseudomonas aeruginosa as the primary diagnosis
stages.explanation: A primary lung abscess from aspiration in a patient with poor dentition is most often polymicrobial, dominated by anaerobes (Peptostreptococcus, Prevotella, Fusobacterium, Bacteroides) and microaerophilic oral streptococci that originate in the diseased gingiva. The indolent course, putrid sputum, and dental disease are classic. Pseudomonas is more typical of hospital-acquired or structurally damaged lungs, not community aspiration in this setting. MRSA can cause necrotizing pneumonia, often post-influenza, but is less likely than oral anaerobes here. Tuberculosis causes upper-lobe cavities and chronic constitutional symptoms but typically lacks the foul-smelling sputum and the acute aspiration history; it remains on the differential and warrants exclusion, but the anaerobic oral flora are the leading cause.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate empiric antibiotic regimen for this anaerobic lung abscess?
options: Ampicillin-sulbactam
Ceftriaxone alone as initial therapy
Azithromycin monotherapy as initial therapy
Oral metronidazole alone as initial therapy
stages.explanation: Ampicillin-sulbactam is a preferred regimen because it covers oral anaerobes plus the microaerophilic streptococci that typify aspiration lung abscess; carbapenems are alternatives. Clindamycin is the classic option for penicillin-allergic patients, though increasing resistance has made beta-lactam/beta-lactamase inhibitors preferred. Azithromycin alone provides inadequate anaerobic coverage. Metronidazole monotherapy notoriously fails because it does not cover the microaerophilic and aerobic streptococci that coexist in these polymicrobial abscesses, leading to treatment failure. Ceftriaxone alone lacks reliable anaerobic activity for this indication. Therapy is prolonged — often weeks — guided by clinical and radiographic improvement, and good dental care is an essential adjunct.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: After several days of appropriate antibiotics he remains febrile with persistent large cavity and minimal sputum drainage. What is the most appropriate next step?
options: Proceed directly to pneumonectomy with appropriate follow-up
Immediately switch to antitubercular therapy with appropriate follow-up
Continue current antibiotics unchanged for six weeks regardless of response
Pursue drainage — bronchoscopy to evaluate for obstruction
stages.explanation: Most lung abscesses drain spontaneously via the airway and respond to antibiotics, but failure to improve after several days warrants evaluation for an obstructing lesion (foreign body or endobronchial tumor) with bronchoscopy and consideration of drainage when the cavity is large and not communicating. Image-guided percutaneous or endobronchial drainage is appropriate for refractory cases. Switching to antitubercular therapy is premature without microbiologic evidence of TB. Blindly continuing the same regimen without investigating non-response risks missing obstruction or an inadequate source-control problem. Pneumonectomy or surgical resection is reserved for true treatment failure, massive hemoptysis, or suspected malignancy after less invasive measures fail, not as an immediate next step.

cases.diagnosis: Primary anaerobic lung abscess from aspiration (poor dentition)
pearls: Aspiration in a supine patient targets the superior segments of the lower lobes and posterior segments of the upper lobes, right side favored.
Foul-smelling sputum plus poor dentition points to polymicrobial anaerobic and oral streptococcal infection.
Ampicillin-sulbactam (or clindamycin) is preferred; metronidazole monotherapy fails by missing microaerophilic streptococci.
Lack of response should prompt bronchoscopy to exclude an obstructing tumor or foreign body before escalating to drainage or surgery.

cases.title: A College Student With a Lingering Cough
cases.chiefComplaint: Two weeks of dry, hacking cough, low-grade fever, and headache.
cases.history: A 21-year-old college student presents with two weeks of a worsening dry, hacking cough, low-grade fevers, malaise, headache, and a sore throat that has since improved. Several roommates and classmates in his dormitory have had similar prolonged coughs. He feels 'not that sick' but the cough is disrupting sleep and persists despite over-the-counter cough syrup. He denies dyspnea at rest, pleuritic pain, hemoptysis, or rash. PMH: healthy, up to date on vaccinations including a recent influenza vaccine. Meds: none. No allergies. Social: nonsmoker, occasional alcohol, lives in a crowded dormitory, no recent travel. Family history noncontributory. He recalls one episode of an ear ache. No known immunodeficiency. No recent antibiotics. He has continued attending classes. He has had no chest tightness or wheezing and no prior asthma. Appetite is slightly reduced but he is tolerating oral intake well and is well hydrated.
cases.exam: Well-appearing young man, comfortable, speaking in full sentences. HEENT: mild pharyngeal erythema, left tympanic membrane with bullae and erythema (bullous myringitis). Lungs: scattered faint crackles in the left lower zone but generally clear; no focal dullness, no wheeze, breath sounds otherwise normal — exam is notably mild relative to symptom severity. Cardiac: regular, no murmur. No rash, no lymphadenopathy. Abdomen soft, nontender. Neurologic exam normal.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which feature best characterizes this 'walking pneumonia' presentation?
options: Acute hypoxic respiratory failure within 24 hours as the first step today
A relatively mild lung exam that is out of proportion to a patchy infiltrate, in a young person from a crowded setting
Pleuritic chest pain with a large pleural effusion as the first step today
Lobar consolidation with rusty sputum and high fevers as the first step today
stages.explanation: Mycoplasma pneumoniae classically causes 'walking pneumonia': a subacute, mild illness in young, otherwise healthy people living in close quarters (dorms, barracks), with a chest film that often looks worse (patchy or reticulonodular infiltrates) than the modest physical exam suggests. Lobar consolidation with rusty sputum and high spiking fevers is the picture of typical pneumococcal pneumonia, not atypical Mycoplasma. Acute hypoxic respiratory failure within a day would be atypical for uncomplicated Mycoplasma in a healthy young adult. A large pleural effusion with pleuritic pain is more characteristic of bacterial empyema. The disconnect between mild findings and a busy film, plus the dorm cluster and bullous myringitis, points to Mycoplasma.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
WBC 9.2 ×10³/µL
Hemoglobin 13.8 g/dL
Chest X-ray: patchy reticulonodular infiltrate in the left lower lobe, more extensive than the exam suggested
Cold agglutinin titer: elevated
Mycoplasma PCR (nasopharyngeal): positive
SpO2: 98% on room air

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which extrapulmonary finding is most classically associated with this organism?
options: Aortic root aneurysm in this clinical context
Migratory large-joint septic arthritis
Acute glomerulonephritis with red cell casts
Cold agglutinin–mediated hemolytic anemia
stages.explanation: Mycoplasma pneumoniae is the classic cause of cold agglutinin disease, in which IgM autoantibodies can cause a cold-induced autoimmune hemolytic anemia; the cold agglutinin titer is elevated here. Other recognized extrapulmonary manifestations include bullous myringitis, erythema multiforme/Stevens-Johnson-like mucocutaneous eruptions, encephalitis, and Guillain-Barré syndrome. Acute glomerulonephritis with red cell casts is associated with post-streptococcal disease and vasculitides, not Mycoplasma. Migratory septic arthritis suggests disseminated gonococcal infection. Aortic root aneurysm is associated with tertiary syphilis and connective tissue disease. The combination of bullous myringitis and cold agglutinins is a high-yield Mycoplasma association.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate empiric outpatient antibiotic for this healthy young adult?
options: Amoxicillin as initial therapy
Vancomycin as initial therapy
A macrolide such as azithromycin
Ceftriaxone as initial therapy
stages.explanation: Mycoplasma lacks a cell wall, so beta-lactams are ineffective. A macrolide such as azithromycin — or alternatively doxycycline or a respiratory fluoroquinolone — is the appropriate choice and provides reliable atypical coverage for an otherwise healthy outpatient. Amoxicillin targets the cell wall and has no activity against Mycoplasma, which is why beta-lactams fail here. Ceftriaxone is also a beta-lactam and ineffective for this organism; it would be reserved for typical bacterial pneumonia requiring hospitalization. Vancomycin targets gram-positive cell-wall organisms (e.g., MRSA) and has no role in atypical pneumonia. For a young, low-comorbidity outpatient, azithromycin or doxycycline is standard and well tolerated.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: What is the most appropriate disposition for this patient?
options: Admit for IV antibiotics and oxygen based on current risk
Discharge home on oral antibiotics with return precautions
Admit to the ICU for close monitoring in this clinical context
Refer for urgent bronchoscopy with appropriate follow-up
stages.explanation: He is a young, well-appearing patient with normal oxygenation, stable vitals, intact oral intake, and a low severity score (e.g., CURB-65 of 0) — ideal for outpatient management with oral antibiotics and clear return precautions for worsening dyspnea, persistent high fever, or inability to tolerate oral intake. Admission for IV antibiotics and oxygen is unwarranted given his normal SpO2 and mild illness. ICU admission is far too aggressive for a stable patient without respiratory failure or sepsis. Urgent bronchoscopy has no role in straightforward atypical pneumonia with an established PCR diagnosis; it would be invasive and unnecessary. Outpatient care with follow-up is the guideline-concordant, cost-effective choice.

cases.diagnosis: Mycoplasma pneumoniae pneumonia (atypical 'walking pneumonia')
pearls: Mycoplasma causes walking pneumonia in young people in crowded settings, with a chest film worse than the physical exam.
Bullous myringitis and cold-agglutinin autoimmune hemolytic anemia are classic extrapulmonary clues.
Mycoplasma has no cell wall, so beta-lactams fail; treat with a macrolide, doxycycline, or a respiratory fluoroquinolone.
Low-severity, well-oxygenated outpatients are managed at home with oral therapy and return precautions.

cases.title: High Fever and Confusion With Loose Stools
cases.chiefComplaint: Four days of high fevers, cough, watery diarrhea, and new confusion.
cases.history: A 60-year-old man is admitted with four days of high fevers, malaise, a dry-to-productive cough, and watery diarrhea. His family notes he has become lethargic and intermittently confused over the past day. He recently returned from a week-long stay at a riverside hotel with an indoor whirlpool spa. He denies recent sick contacts. PMH: type 2 diabetes, hypertension, a 40-pack-year smoking history. Meds: metformin 1000 mg twice daily, lisinopril 20 mg daily. No known allergies. Social: current smoker, occasional alcohol, retired plumber. Family history: coronary disease. Review of systems notable for headache and mild abdominal cramping. He has not been hospitalized recently and has had no antibiotics. He reports no dysuria. He has had decreased oral intake over two days. No recent travel outside the country. No known tuberculosis exposure. The diarrhea is non-bloody and he has had several episodes per day.
cases.exam: Ill-appearing, mildly confused but arousable. Lungs: crackles over the right middle and lower zones with bronchial breath sounds; no wheeze. Cardiac: relatively bradycardic for the degree of fever (pulse-temperature dissociation), regular, no murmur. Abdomen: soft with mild diffuse tenderness, no rebound or guarding; normal bowel sounds. No meningismus; no focal neurologic deficit. Skin warm, no rash. No peripheral edema. Mucous membranes mildly dry.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which constellation of features most strongly raises suspicion for this specific atypical pneumonia?
options: High fever with relative bradycardia, diarrhea, confusion,
Productive purulent sputum with lobar consolidation and leukocytosis only
Subacute cough in a young person from a dormitory with bullous myringitis
Cavitary upper-lobe disease with night sweats and weight loss as the first step today
stages.explanation: Legionella pneumonia classically presents with high fevers, relative bradycardia (pulse-temperature dissociation), prominent gastrointestinal symptoms (watery diarrhea, abdominal pain), neurologic features (confusion, headache), and hyponatremia, often after exposure to aerosolized water (cooling towers, whirlpool spas, plumbing). Purulent sputum with lobar consolidation and isolated leukocytosis describes typical pneumococcal disease. A dormitory cluster with bullous myringitis points to Mycoplasma. Cavitary upper-lobe disease with night sweats and weight loss suggests tuberculosis. The combination of extrapulmonary GI and CNS involvement, relative bradycardia, and a contaminated-water source is the hallmark Legionella picture and should prompt specific testing.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
WBC 13.8 ×10³/µL
Na 128 mEq/L
K 3.9 mEq/L
Creatinine 1.4 mg/dL
AST 78 U/L / ALT 70 U/L
CRP 220 mg/L
Chest X-ray: patchy right-sided consolidation progressing to involve the lower lobe
Urine Legionella antigen: positive
Procalcitonin: elevated

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: The urinary antigen is positive. What is an important limitation of this test to keep in mind?
options: It is positive in the majority of healthy carriers, limiting specificity
It detects only Legionella pneumophila serogroup 1
It cross-reacts heavily with Streptococcus pneumoniae as the primary diagnosis
It becomes positive only after four weeks of illness as the primary diagnosis
stages.explanation: The Legionella urinary antigen test is rapid, convenient, and remains positive even after antibiotics are started, but it detects predominantly Legionella pneumophila serogroup 1 — the most common cause of disease — and can miss other serogroups and non-pneumophila species. When clinical suspicion remains high despite a negative antigen, culture on buffered charcoal yeast extract (BCYE) agar or PCR of respiratory specimens should be pursued. It does not require four weeks to turn positive; it is typically positive early in illness. It is not commonly positive in healthy carriers, so it retains good specificity. It does not cross-react substantially with pneumococcus. Understanding the serogroup limitation prevents falsely excluding Legionella.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate antibiotic therapy for confirmed Legionella pneumonia?
options: Amoxicillin-clavulanate as initial therapy
A respiratory fluoroquinolone or a macrolide
Ceftriaxone monotherapy as initial therapy
Trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole as initial therapy
stages.explanation: Legionella is an intracellular organism, so effective therapy requires agents that penetrate cells: a respiratory fluoroquinolone such as levofloxacin or a macrolide such as azithromycin is first-line, with fluoroquinolones often favored in severe disease. Amoxicillin-clavulanate and ceftriaxone are beta-lactams that do not achieve adequate intracellular concentrations and are ineffective against Legionella, which is why standard pneumococcal beta-lactam regimens fail when Legionella is the cause. Trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole is not a first-line agent for Legionella pneumonia. Recognizing the need for intracellular-active drugs is the key teaching point, and severe or immunocompromised cases warrant a longer treatment course with close monitoring of oxygenation and organ function.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: His SpO2 is 90% on room air, RR 24, he is confused, and his blood pressure is borderline. Using severity assessment, what is the most appropriate disposition?
options: Admit to a higher level of care/ICU given confusion, hypoxemia,
Discharge home on oral levofloxacin with appropriate follow-up
Admit to a general medical ward only, with no escalation planning
Observe in the emergency department for 6 hours then reassess for discharge
stages.explanation: This patient has multiple severity markers — confusion, hypoxemia (SpO2 90%), tachypnea, borderline hypotension, age, and comorbidities — yielding a high CURB-65/severity score and signs of severe pneumonia with possible early sepsis. He warrants admission to a higher level of care or the ICU for close monitoring, oxygen support, and IV antibiotics, with readiness to escalate respiratory support. Discharge home is unsafe given altered mentation and hypoxemia. A general ward without escalation planning underestimates his trajectory; Legionella can progress to respiratory failure. A brief ED observation followed by discharge ignores his confusion and oxygen requirement. Severe Legionella pneumonia frequently requires intensive monitoring.

cases.diagnosis: Legionella pneumophila pneumonia (Legionnaires' disease)
pearls: Suspect Legionella with high fever, relative bradycardia, diarrhea, confusion, and hyponatremia, especially after aerosolized-water exposure.
Urinary antigen detects mainly L. pneumophila serogroup 1; pair with BCYE culture or PCR when suspicion is high and antigen is negative.
Treat with a respiratory fluoroquinolone or azithromycin — beta-lactams fail against this intracellular organism.
Confusion, hypoxemia, and hemodynamic instability mark severe disease warranting ICU-level care.

cases.title: New Fever and Falling Oxygen on the Ventilator
cases.chiefComplaint: New fever, increased secretions, and worsening oxygenation on day 6 of mechanical ventilation.
cases.history: A 70-year-old man has been intubated and mechanically ventilated for six days in the ICU following an exploratory laparotomy complicated by ileus and prolonged sedation. On hospital day 6 (ventilator day 6) the nurse reports a new fever, increased purulent endotracheal secretions, and rising oxygen requirements over the past 24 hours. He had been weaning toward extubation. PMH: COPD, hypertension, type 2 diabetes, prior MI. Home meds: tiotropium, amlodipine, metformin, aspirin. No known allergies. He received cefazolin perioperatively. Social: former smoker (35 pack-years). Family history noncontributory. He has had a central venous catheter and an indwelling urinary catheter since surgery. Sedation has been interrupted daily. He has had no diarrhea. No prior multidrug-resistant organism colonization is documented, though he was hospitalized two months ago. The ventilator now shows worsening compliance and increasing FiO2 requirement.
cases.exam: Sedated, intubated man. Lungs: new coarse crackles and bronchial breath sounds over the right lower zone with copious purulent secretions suctioned from the endotracheal tube; decreased breath sounds at the right base. Cardiac: tachycardic, regular, no murmur. Abdomen: surgical incision clean, mild distention, no peritoneal signs. No new rash. Central line site without erythema. Extremities warm; no edema. No obvious alternative source of fever on exam.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: What combination best supports a diagnosis of ventilator-associated pneumonia (VAP) in this patient?
options: Fever alone after 48 hours of ventilation as the first step today
Positive urine culture in a catheterized patient as the first step today
Isolated tachycardia with normal chest imaging as the first step today
A new or progressive infiltrate plus at least two of fever, leukocytosis/leukopenia,
stages.explanation: VAP is defined as pneumonia developing more than 48 hours after endotracheal intubation, supported clinically by a new or progressive radiographic infiltrate accompanied by at least two of fever, leukocytosis or leukopenia, and purulent tracheobronchial secretions, often with worsening oxygenation. Fever alone is nonspecific and could reflect many ICU processes (line infection, deep vein thrombosis, drug fever). A positive urinary culture points to a urinary source, not pneumonia. Isolated tachycardia with a normal chest film does not meet criteria. The combination of timing (>48 hours intubated), new infiltrate, fever, purulent secretions, and declining oxygenation is what establishes the clinical suspicion that should trigger respiratory sampling and empiric therapy.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
WBC 15.6 ×10³/µL (left shift)
Lactate 2.1 mmol/L
Chest X-ray: new right-lower-lobe infiltrate not present 2 days ago
PaO2/FiO2 ratio: 150
Endotracheal aspirate Gram stain: many PMNs with gram-negative rods
Blood cultures: pending
Procalcitonin: rising

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Before starting antibiotics, what is the most appropriate diagnostic step?
options: Obtain a lower respiratory tract sample for culture
Obtain sputum from a spontaneous cough as the primary diagnosis
Start empiric antibiotics and never culture, to avoid delays in this clinical context
Perform open lung biopsy as the primary diagnosis
stages.explanation: A lower respiratory tract specimen for culture and Gram stain — typically an endotracheal aspirate, or a bronchoalveolar lavage in selected cases — should be obtained before or at the time of starting antibiotics to identify the pathogen and allow later de-escalation. Cultures should not be skipped: in the ICU, narrowing therapy based on results is essential for stewardship and to avoid prolonged broad-spectrum exposure. A spontaneous-cough sputum is impossible and inappropriate in an intubated, sedated patient. Open lung biopsy is far too invasive and not indicated for suspected VAP. Obtaining a respiratory sample promptly, then starting empiric therapy without waiting for results, is the correct sequence in suspected VAP.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: He has risk factors for multidrug-resistant organisms (prolonged ventilation, recent hospitalization, an ICU with high resistance rates). What empiric regimen is most appropriate?
options: Coverage for Pseudomonas plus MRSA
Ceftriaxone plus azithromycin as initial therapy
Ampicillin-sulbactam alone as initial therapy
Oral doxycycline as initial therapy
stages.explanation: For VAP with risk factors for multidrug-resistant organisms, empiric therapy must cover Pseudomonas aeruginosa and other resistant gram-negatives plus MRSA: an antipseudomonal beta-lactam (piperacillin-tazobactam, cefepime, meropenem, etc.) combined with an anti-MRSA agent (vancomycin or linezolid), with a second antipseudomonal agent considered in units with high resistance. Ceftriaxone plus azithromycin is a community-acquired pneumonia regimen lacking antipseudomonal and MRSA coverage. Oral doxycycline is inadequate and inappropriate for a critically ill ventilated patient. Ampicillin-sulbactam alone does not reliably cover Pseudomonas or MRSA. Therapy should be de-escalated promptly once culture and susceptibility data return, typically targeting a short, guideline-based total course.

stages.label: PREVENTION
stages.question: Which bundle element most directly reduces the risk of future ventilator-associated pneumonia?
options: Changing the ventilator circuit every 24 hours regardless of soiling as the immediate next step
Elevating the head of bed 30-45 degrees, daily sedation interruption with spontaneous breathing trials,
Routine prophylactic systemic antibiotics for all ventilated patients as the immediate next step
Keeping the patient flat (supine) at all times as the immediate next step
stages.explanation: Evidence-based VAP prevention bundles emphasize semi-recumbent positioning (head of bed elevated 30-45 degrees) to reduce aspiration, daily sedation interruption paired with spontaneous breathing trials to shorten ventilation time, oral care/decontamination, and minimizing ventilator days. Keeping the patient supine increases aspiration risk and is the opposite of best practice. Routine prophylactic systemic antibiotics do not prevent VAP and drive resistance, so they are not recommended. Frequent scheduled ventilator-circuit changes do not reduce VAP and may increase it; circuits should be changed only when visibly soiled or malfunctioning. The combination of positioning, sedation/ventilation minimization, and oral care is the cornerstone of prevention.

cases.diagnosis: Ventilator-associated pneumonia (hospital-acquired pneumonia) with MDR risk factors
pearls: VAP is pneumonia >48 hours after intubation: new/progressive infiltrate plus fever, abnormal WBC, purulent secretions, and worsening oxygenation.
Obtain a lower-respiratory specimen for culture before/at antibiotic initiation to enable later de-escalation.
With MDR risk factors, cover Pseudomonas plus MRSA empirically, then narrow based on susceptibilities.
Prevention bundle: head-of-bed elevation, daily sedation interruption with SBTs, oral care, and minimizing ventilator days.

cases.title: Worsening Breathlessness in a Smoker
cases.chiefComplaint: Three days of worsening dyspnea, cough, and increased sputum with progressive drowsiness.
cases.history: A 65-year-old woman with severe COPD (FEV1 35% predicted) is brought to the ED with three days of worsening dyspnea, cough productive of yellow sputum, and wheeze following an upper respiratory infection. Over the last several hours her family noticed increasing drowsiness and confusion. She uses home oxygen at 2 L/min. PMH: COPD with prior exacerbations, two previous ICU admissions (one requiring intubation), hypertension, osteoporosis. Meds: tiotropium, budesonide-formoterol inhaler, albuterol as needed, home oxygen, amlodipine. Allergies: none. Social: current smoker, 50-pack-year history, lives with her daughter. Family history noncontributory. She has had no chest pain or hemoptysis. She has been using her rescue inhaler frequently with little relief. She received a nebulizer en route. She is now barely keeping her eyes open and is hard to rouse for history; her daughter provides most of it.
cases.exam: Drowsy, using accessory muscles, speaking only single words. Lungs: markedly diminished air entry with diffuse expiratory wheeze and a prolonged expiratory phase; no focal consolidation. Cardiac: tachycardic, regular. She has a flapping tremor (asterixis) when arms are extended. No focal neurologic deficit otherwise; pupils equal and reactive. Mild peripheral cyanosis. No JVD or peripheral edema noted. She is diaphoretic and restless between periods of somnolence.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: What is the most important immediate diagnostic test to characterize her respiratory failure?
options: Pulmonary function testing with spirometry
Arterial blood gas
Brain natriuretic peptide
D-dimer as the first step today
stages.explanation: An arterial blood gas is the single most important immediate test: it directly measures PaCO2, pH, and PaO2, distinguishing hypercapnic (type 2) from hypoxemic respiratory failure and quantifying the degree of acidosis that drives management decisions about NIPPV versus intubation. Her drowsiness and asterixis suggest CO2 narcosis, which only an ABG can confirm. A D-dimer would be nonspecific and is not the priority given a clear COPD exacerbation picture. BNP evaluates heart failure, which is not the leading diagnosis here without JVD or edema. Spirometry is impossible and inappropriate in an obtunded patient in extremis. The ABG defines the physiology and urgency of the intervention.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
ABG (on 2 L NC): pH 7.24, PaCO2 78 mmHg, PaO2 54 mmHg, HCO3 32 mEq/L
WBC 13.0 ×10³/µL
Chest X-ray: hyperinflation, flattened diaphragms, no focal consolidation or pneumothorax
Lactate 1.6 mmol/L
Glasgow Coma Scale: 13 (drowsy but rousable, follows commands intermittently)

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: With pH 7.24, PaCO2 78, and a drowsy but rousable patient who can follow commands, what is the most appropriate next intervention?
options: Sedation to reduce work of breathing as initial therapy
High-flow 100% oxygen by non-rebreather to correct hypoxemia
Initiate noninvasive positive-pressure ventilation with close monitoring
Immediate endotracheal intubation as initial therapy
stages.explanation: In a hypercapnic COPD exacerbation with respiratory acidosis (pH 7.24, PaCO2 78) in a patient still able to protect her airway and follow commands, noninvasive positive-pressure ventilation (BiPAP) is first-line and reduces intubation rates, mortality, and length of stay. She must be monitored closely with a repeat ABG to confirm improvement. Immediate intubation is reserved for failure of NIPPV, inability to protect the airway, or impending arrest — she does not yet meet those criteria. Unfettered high-flow 100% oxygen can blunt hypoxic respiratory drive and worsen hypercapnia; oxygen should be titrated to a target SpO2 of about 88-92%. Sedation would suppress respiratory drive and is dangerous here.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: Along with ventilatory support, which pharmacologic measures target the underlying exacerbation?
options: Inhaled bronchodilators , systemic corticosteroids,
Empiric anticoagulation for pulmonary embolism as the leading concern
IV beta-blockers to control the heart rate as the leading concern
Loop diuretics and nitroglycerin for presumed heart failure as the leading concern
stages.explanation: Acute COPD exacerbation management combines inhaled short-acting bronchodilators (albuterol plus ipratropium), systemic corticosteroids to reduce airway inflammation and shorten recovery, and antibiotics when there are signs of bacterial exacerbation (increased sputum purulence and volume, as here). IV beta-blockers would worsen bronchospasm and are contraindicated for rate control in this setting. Loop diuretics and nitroglycerin treat cardiogenic pulmonary edema, which is not supported here given hyperinflation without consolidation and no JVD or edema. Empiric anticoagulation is not indicated without evidence of pulmonary embolism and would expose her to unnecessary bleeding risk. Bronchodilators, steroids, and targeted antibiotics directly address the exacerbation physiology while NIPPV supports ventilation.

stages.label: REASSESSMENT
stages.question: After two hours on BiPAP, a repeat ABG shows pH 7.20, PaCO2 88 mmHg, and she is now barely rousable and not following commands. What is the most appropriate next step?
options: Continue current BiPAP settings unchanged and recheck in 4 hours
Proceed to endotracheal intubation
Remove ventilatory support and provide oxygen alone in this setting
Add a benzodiazepine to relieve her agitation in this setting
stages.explanation: Worsening acidosis (pH falling to 7.20, PaCO2 rising to 88) and deteriorating mental status with loss of the ability to follow commands signal failure of noninvasive ventilation and inability to protect the airway — clear indications for endotracheal intubation and invasive mechanical ventilation. Continuing unchanged BiPAP while she deteriorates risks aspiration and arrest. Removing support and giving oxygen alone would accelerate CO2 retention and respiratory arrest. Adding a benzodiazepine would further suppress respiratory drive and is dangerous in worsening hypercapnia. Recognizing NIPPV failure promptly — by trending pH, PaCO2, and mental status — and escalating to intubation is the key decision point that prevents a respiratory arrest.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: Once stabilized and recovering, which intervention most reduces her risk of future exacerbations and mortality?
options: Routine long-term benzodiazepines for dyspnea with appropriate follow-up
Chronic prophylactic broad-spectrum antibiotics for everyone with appropriate follow-up
Long-term systemic corticosteroids with appropriate follow-up
Smoking cessation support
stages.explanation: The interventions with the greatest impact on COPD outcomes are smoking cessation (the only measure proven to slow FEV1 decline) and, where indicated, long-term oxygen therapy for chronic hypoxemia (which improves survival), alongside optimized long-acting inhaler therapy and pulmonary rehabilitation to reduce exacerbations and improve function and quality of life. Long-term systemic corticosteroids cause significant harm (osteoporosis, hyperglycemia, infection) and are not recommended for chronic management. Chronic prophylactic broad-spectrum antibiotics for all patients drive resistance; only selected patients benefit from specific macrolide regimens. Routine long-term benzodiazepines suppress respiratory drive and are inappropriate. A combination of cessation, inhaler optimization, oxygen assessment, and rehabilitation is the evidence-based plan.

cases.diagnosis: Acute hypercapnic (type 2) respiratory failure due to severe COPD exacerbation
pearls: An ABG defines respiratory failure type and severity; rising PaCO2 with acidemia and asterixis indicates CO2 narcosis.
BiPAP is first-line for hypercapnic COPD exacerbation when the patient can protect the airway; titrate oxygen to SpO2 88-92%.
NIPPV failure (worsening pH/PaCO2, declining mental status, airway compromise) mandates prompt intubation.
Smoking cessation and long-term oxygen for chronic hypoxemia are the COPD interventions that improve survival.

cases.title: A Cough That Won't Quit
cases.chiefComplaint: Cough lasting more than three months without a clear cause.
cases.history: A 50-year-old woman presents with a cough lasting more than three months. It is worse at night and in the early morning, and she frequently clears her throat and feels mucus dripping down the back of her throat. She also notes occasional heartburn and a sour taste, and the cough worsens after large meals and when lying down. She denies fevers, weight loss, hemoptysis, or significant dyspnea, though she occasionally feels mild chest tightness with exertion. She has seasonal nasal congestion. PMH: allergic rhinitis, mild intermittent reflux. Meds: occasional loratadine, antacids as needed. No allergies to medications. Importantly, she takes no ACE inhibitor. Social: never smoker, no occupational dust exposure, works as an accountant. Family history: mother with asthma. Her chest exam and chest X-ray done elsewhere were normal. She has tried over-the-counter cough suppressants without relief. No recent respiratory infection preceded the cough. No new pets or home dampness.
cases.exam: Well-appearing woman. HEENT: boggy, pale nasal turbinates with clear mucus; cobblestoning of the posterior oropharynx consistent with postnasal drainage. Lungs: clear to auscultation with no wheeze, crackles, or prolonged expiration at rest. Cardiac: regular rate and rhythm, no murmur. No clubbing, no lymphadenopathy. Abdomen soft, nontender, no epigastric mass. No peripheral edema. The remainder of the exam is unremarkable.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: In a nonsmoker with a normal chest X-ray and a cough lasting more than 8 weeks, what is the first thing to confirm or exclude?
options: That she does not have tuberculosis, via empiric four-drug therapy
That she has heart failure, via immediate echocardiography
That she does not have lung cancer, via immediate bronchoscopy
That she is not taking an ACE inhibitor and has no red-flag features
stages.explanation: For chronic cough (>8 weeks) in a nonsmoker with a normal chest film, the first steps are to confirm the absence of an ACE inhibitor (a very common, easily reversible cause) and to screen for red-flag features (hemoptysis, weight loss, significant dyspnea, smoking history) that would warrant escalated evaluation. Once those are excluded, the workup targets the three most common causes: upper airway cough syndrome (postnasal drip), asthma/cough-variant asthma, and gastroesophageal reflux. Immediate bronchoscopy is not indicated without red flags or imaging abnormalities. Empiric antitubercular therapy is inappropriate without evidence of TB. Immediate echocardiography for heart failure is unwarranted given no signs of cardiac disease. The stepwise approach starts with the simple, high-yield exclusions.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Her history of postnasal drip, reflux symptoms, and a family history of asthma suggests the classic triad of chronic cough causes. What is the most reasonable initial diagnostic-therapeutic strategy?
options: Empiric sequential treatment targeting upper airway cough syndrome, asthma,
Immediate 24-hour esophageal pH study before any treatment as the primary diagnosis
High-resolution chest CT and bronchoscopy first in all patients as the primary diagnosis
Long-term oral corticosteroids empirically as the primary diagnosis
stages.explanation: The guideline-endorsed approach to chronic cough with a normal chest film is empiric, often sequential or combined, treatment directed at the most common causes — upper airway cough syndrome (e.g., a first-generation antihistamine or intranasal steroid), asthma/cough-variant asthma (inhaled corticosteroid with bronchodilator), and GERD (lifestyle measures plus acid suppression) — with reassessment of the response to confirm the diagnosis by therapeutic trial. Routine HRCT and bronchoscopy in everyone are unnecessary and not cost-effective without red flags. Long-term systemic corticosteroids are inappropriate empiric therapy given their toxicity. An immediate pH study before any treatment is not first-line; reflux is typically addressed empirically first, reserving pH/impedance testing for refractory cases. Empiric, targeted therapy is the standard.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
Chest X-ray (reviewed): normal lung fields, no infiltrate or mass
Spirometry: FEV1/FVC normal at baseline; mild improvement in FEV1 after bronchodilator
Methacholine challenge: positive (suggesting airway hyperresponsiveness)
Sinus exam/history: consistent with allergic rhinitis and postnasal drainage
Response: cough improved substantially after intranasal steroid + antihistamine and an inhaled corticosteroid, with further benefit from reflux measures

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: The methacholine challenge is positive and her cough partly responded to inhaled corticosteroids. What does this most likely indicate?
options: Chronic thromboembolic disease as initial therapy
Vocal cord dysfunction as the sole cause
Cough-variant asthma contributing to her chronic cough
Idiopathic pulmonary fibrosis as initial therapy
stages.explanation: A positive methacholine challenge demonstrates airway hyperresponsiveness, and a cough that improves with inhaled corticosteroid therapy supports cough-variant asthma — a common cause of chronic cough in which cough is the predominant or only symptom without classic wheeze. Combined with her postnasal drip and reflux features, this fits the typical multifactorial chronic cough. Idiopathic pulmonary fibrosis would show restrictive physiology, basilar crackles, and reticular changes on imaging, none of which are present. Chronic thromboembolic disease causes dyspnea and pulmonary hypertension, not an isolated chronic cough with airway hyperresponsiveness. Vocal cord dysfunction produces inspiratory symptoms and would not respond to inhaled steroids or yield a positive methacholine test in this manner; it is not the sole explanation here.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: If she had instead failed to improve after thorough evaluation and adequate empiric trials for all three common causes, what is the appropriate next consideration?
options: Proceed directly to lung transplantation evaluation with appropriate follow-up
Reassess adherence and adequacy of trials, then evaluate for less common causes and consider refractory/unexplained chronic cough management
Reassure her nothing more can be done and stop the workup with appropriate follow-up
Start long-term opioids as first-line therapy immediately with appropriate follow-up
stages.explanation: When chronic cough persists despite adequate, adherent trials for the common causes, the right approach is to first confirm that each trial was optimized and adherence was good, then broaden the evaluation for less common etiologies (e.g., non-asthmatic eosinophilic bronchitis, medication effects, bronchiectasis) and consider management of refractory or unexplained chronic cough, which may include cough-suppression speech therapy and neuromodulator agents (such as gabapentin) in selected patients. Telling her nothing can be done abandons evidence-based options. Long-term opioids are not appropriate first-line therapy. Lung transplantation evaluation is absurd for isolated chronic cough without parenchymal disease. Systematic reassessment and stepwise escalation are the standard for refractory cough.

cases.diagnosis: Chronic cough due to the classic triad — upper airway cough syndrome, cough-variant asthma, and GERD
pearls: Chronic cough is >8 weeks; in a nonsmoker with a normal CXR, first exclude ACE inhibitor use and red-flag features.
The three most common causes are upper airway cough syndrome, asthma (including cough-variant), and GERD — often coexisting.
Empiric, targeted therapy with reassessment of response is both diagnostic and therapeutic; reserve extensive testing for refractory cases.
Refractory/unexplained chronic cough may respond to speech therapy and neuromodulators after adequate trials and reassessment.

cases.title: Sudden Throat-Tightening Attacks Mistaken for Asthma
cases.chiefComplaint: Recurrent episodes of sudden throat tightness and noisy breathing not relieved by asthma inhalers.
cases.history: A 26-year-old competitive runner presents with recurrent attacks of sudden throat tightness, a choking sensation, and noisy breathing that come on abruptly — often during exercise, stress, or exposure to strong perfumes — and resolve within minutes. She has been labeled with 'severe refractory asthma' and is on escalating inhaled corticosteroids, long-acting beta-agonist, and frequent oral prednisone bursts, plus albuterol that she says 'does nothing' during attacks. She has had several ED visits with normal oxygen saturations even while distressed, and one near-intubation that was deferred when symptoms abruptly resolved. She points to her throat, not her chest, as the site of trouble. PMH: anxiety, GERD, prior asthma label. Meds: high-dose ICS-LABA, montelukast, PRN albuterol, intermittent prednisone, omeprazole. Allergies: none. Social: nonsmoker, college athlete. Family history: mother with anxiety. She describes difficulty getting air in rather than out. Between episodes she is completely asymptomatic.
cases.exam: During a captured episode in clinic: audible inspiratory stridor localized to the throat/larynx, with the noise loudest over the neck rather than the chest; she appears distressed and grips her throat. Lungs: chest auscultation reveals good air entry without significant expiratory wheeze; no prolonged expiratory phase. Cardiac: regular, no murmur. SpO2 remains 98-99% throughout the episode. Between episodes the exam is entirely normal with no wheeze. No clubbing, no cyanosis, no accessory muscle use of the chest.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which features most strongly suggest vocal cord dysfunction rather than asthma?
options: Slowly progressive dyspnea over months with bibasilar crackles as the first step today
Inspiratory stridor localized to the throat/larynx, normal oxygen saturation during attacks, abrupt onset/offset,
Nocturnal cough with reversible airflow obstruction on spirometry as the first step today
Expiratory wheeze with prolonged expiration and dropping oxygen saturation as the first step today
stages.explanation: Vocal cord dysfunction (inducible laryngeal obstruction) is characterized by paradoxical adduction of the vocal cords during inspiration, producing inspiratory stridor localized to the larynx/throat, abrupt onset and resolution, preserved oxygen saturation even when the patient appears severely distressed, and a lack of response to bronchodilators — often triggered by exercise, irritants, or stress. Expiratory wheeze with prolonged expiration and falling SpO2 is the picture of asthma/lower-airway obstruction. Slowly progressive dyspnea with bibasilar crackles suggests interstitial lung disease. Nocturnal cough with reversible obstruction on spirometry indicates asthma. The throat-localized inspiratory noise, normal saturations during distress, and bronchodilator non-response distinguish VCD from true asthma and explain her 'refractory' label.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
Spirometry (flow-volume loop): truncated/flattened INSPIRATORY limb with relatively preserved expiratory limb (variable extrathoracic obstruction pattern)
Methacholine challenge: negative
SpO2 during symptomatic episode: 99%
Chest X-ray: normal
Laryngoscopy during a provoked episode: paradoxical adduction of the vocal cords on inspiration with a posterior glottic chink

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: What is the gold-standard test that confirms vocal cord dysfunction?
options: Exhaled nitric oxide measurement as the primary diagnosis
Bronchial biopsy as the primary diagnosis
Direct visualization of paradoxical vocal cord motion on laryngoscopy during an episode
High-resolution chest CT as the primary diagnosis
stages.explanation: The diagnostic gold standard for vocal cord dysfunction is direct laryngoscopy capturing paradoxical adduction (inappropriate inspiratory closure) of the true vocal cords during a symptomatic or provoked episode, classically leaving a posterior glottic chink. The flow-volume loop finding of a flattened inspiratory limb (variable extrathoracic obstruction) is highly supportive and seen here, and a negative methacholine challenge argues against asthma, but laryngoscopic visualization is definitive. High-resolution chest CT evaluates parenchymal and airway disease, not laryngeal motion. Bronchial biopsy assesses lower-airway tissue and has no role. Exhaled nitric oxide reflects eosinophilic airway inflammation in asthma and would not confirm VCD. Catching the paradoxical motion on scope, ideally during provocation, clinches the diagnosis.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the cornerstone of long-term management for vocal cord dysfunction?
options: Long-term daily oral corticosteroids as initial therapy
Permanent tracheostomy as initial therapy
Escalating inhaled corticosteroids and long-acting beta-agonists as initial therapy
Speech-language therapy with breathing/laryngeal-control techniques, plus treating triggers
stages.explanation: The mainstay of vocal cord dysfunction management is speech-language therapy teaching laryngeal-control and relaxed-throat breathing techniques (e.g., diaphragmatic breathing, rescue breathing maneuvers) that abort and prevent episodes, combined with addressing contributing factors — reflux, postnasal drip/rhinitis, anxiety, and exertional triggers. Escalating asthma controllers is exactly the trap to avoid: she does not have uncontrolled asthma, and intensifying ICS-LABA and prednisone exposes her to steroid harm without benefit. Long-term daily oral corticosteroids are inappropriate and harmful for VCD. A tracheostomy is reserved only for rare, life-threatening refractory cases and is not standard management. Recognizing VCD allows de-escalation of unnecessary asthma medications and a shift to behavioral therapy.

stages.label: ACUTE EPISODE
stages.question: She presents to the ED mid-attack with stridor and obvious distress but an SpO2 of 99%. What is the most appropriate initial intervention to abort the episode?
options: Bolus of IV benzodiazepine to sedate her completely as the immediate next step
Immediate endotracheal intubation for airway protection as the immediate next step
Reassurance with coached relaxed-breathing maneuvers
A stat dose of IV corticosteroids and continuous albuterol nebulization as the immediate next step
stages.explanation: An acute VCD episode is best aborted with calm reassurance and coached laryngeal-relaxation breathing techniques — panting, pursed-lip breathing, or a 'sniffing' maneuver that opens the glottis — since the airway is not truly compromised, as shown by the normal oxygen saturation. Heliox (helium-oxygen) can reduce turbulent flow and help refractory episodes. Endotracheal intubation is unnecessary and harmful when oxygenation is preserved and the obstruction is at the cords; it should be avoided unless there is genuine airway failure. IV corticosteroids and continuous albuterol treat asthma, not VCD, and will not relieve paradoxical cord adduction. Deep sedation with a benzodiazepine bolus risks respiratory depression and does not address the laryngeal mechanism; light anxiolysis is only an adjunct in select cases.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: Given the new diagnosis, what is the most appropriate adjustment to her current medication regimen?
options: Continue all asthma medications indefinitely as a precaution with appropriate follow-up
Add a second long-acting beta-agonist with appropriate follow-up
Start chronic daily opioids for symptom control with appropriate follow-up
Reassess the asthma diagnosis
stages.explanation: With objective evidence against asthma (negative methacholine challenge, normal saturations during distress, flattened inspiratory loop) and confirmed paradoxical vocal cord motion, the appropriate step is to reassess the asthma label and carefully de-escalate the controllers and recurrent prednisone bursts that are not helping, while starting speech-language therapy and managing triggers. Continuing all asthma medications indefinitely perpetuates avoidable steroid toxicity and mislabels her condition. Adding another long-acting beta-agonist treats a disease she likely does not have and risks harm. Chronic daily opioids are not indicated and pose dependence and respiratory risks. Confirming VCD enables both effective treatment and the discontinuation of ineffective, potentially harmful asthma therapy.

cases.diagnosis: Vocal cord dysfunction (inducible laryngeal obstruction) mimicking refractory asthma
pearls: VCD causes inspiratory stridor localized to the throat, abrupt onset/offset, normal SpO2 during distress, and no bronchodilator response.
The flow-volume loop shows a flattened inspiratory limb (variable extrathoracic obstruction); methacholine challenge is typically negative.
Laryngoscopy showing paradoxical inspiratory vocal cord adduction during an episode is the diagnostic gold standard.
Treat with speech-language therapy and trigger/comorbidity control, and de-escalate unnecessary asthma controllers and steroids.

cases.title: Sinus Crusting, Bloody Sputum, and Falling Kidney Function
cases.chiefComplaint: Worsening cough with blood-streaked sputum, fatigue, and decreasing urine output over several weeks.
cases.history: A 47-year-old man is admitted for a two-month history of nasal crusting, recurrent epistaxis, and a 'sinus infection' that failed two antibiotic courses. Over the last three weeks he developed a productive cough with intermittent hemoptysis, migratory arthralgias, malaise, and a 5 kg weight loss. He now notes frothy, tea-colored urine and ankle swelling. PMH is unremarkable. Medications: amoxicillin-clavulanate (recently completed) and ibuprofen 400 mg as needed. No known allergies. He works as a high-school teacher, has never smoked, drinks socially, and denies illicit drug use or recent travel. Family history is noncontributory. Review of systems is positive for new-onset hearing reduction, red painful eyes, and crusty skin nodules on the elbows. He denies fevers but has felt feverish at home. No sick contacts. He has had no prior renal or pulmonary disease and no occupational dust or silica exposure.
cases.exam: Ill-appearing man in no acute distress. Nasal exam shows mucosal crusting and a small septal perforation; a subtle saddle-nose contour is noted. Scleral injection of the right eye consistent with scleritis. Lungs have scattered crackles in both bases without wheeze. Cardiac exam is normal. Abdomen is soft and nontender. Bilateral pitting ankle edema is present. Skin shows palpable purpura on the shins and an ulcerated nodule on the left elbow. No lymphadenopathy. No oral ulcers. Neurologic exam is grossly intact with no focal deficits; gross hearing is reduced bilaterally.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which constellation of findings most strongly unifies this presentation into a single systemic diagnosis?
options: Upper airway disease, lower airway hemoptysis,
Isolated chronic bacterial sinusitis with secondary pneumonia
Allergic rhinitis with reactive airway disease as the first step today
Community-acquired pneumonia with septic emboli as the first step today
stages.explanation: The triad of upper respiratory tract involvement (crusting, septal perforation, saddle nose), lower respiratory disease (hemoptysis, crackles), and renal involvement (hematuria, hypertension, edema) defines a pulmonary-renal syndrome with prominent ENT disease, classic for granulomatosis with polyangiitis. Isolated bacterial sinusitis with pneumonia would not explain the glomerulonephritis, purpura, scleritis, or saddle nose. Allergic rhinitis with asthma causes neither destructive nasal disease nor nephritis. Septic emboli from endocarditis can cause pulmonary and renal lesions but would not produce destructive upper-airway granulomatous disease, scleritis, and the indolent multi-week course described here.

stages.label: INITIAL LABS
stages.content:
WBC 13.8 ×10³/µL
Hemoglobin 9.6 g/dL
Platelets 478 ×10³/µL
Creatinine 2.9 mg/dL (baseline 0.9)
BUN 44 mg/dL
Urinalysis: 3+ blood, 2+ protein, dysmorphic RBCs, RBC casts
ESR 92 mm/hr
CRP 14.2 mg/dL
C3/C4: normal

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which serologic test is most likely to be positive and best supports the leading diagnosis?
options: Anti-glomerular basement membrane (anti-GBM) antibodies
Cytoplasmic ANCA (c-ANCA) with anti-proteinase-3 antibodies
Perinuclear ANCA (p-ANCA) with anti-myeloperoxidase antibodies
Anti-double-stranded DNA antibodies with low complement
stages.explanation: Granulomatosis with polyangiitis is most commonly associated with c-ANCA directed against proteinase-3 (PR3), present in roughly 80-90% of generalized disease. The normal complement levels seen here argue against immune-complex disease. p-ANCA/MPO is more typical of microscopic polyangiitis and eosinophilic granulomatosis with polyangiitis, not the destructive ENT-predominant picture here. Anti-GBM defines Goodpasture syndrome, which causes pulmonary-renal disease but lacks sinus destruction, saddle nose, and nodular skin lesions. Anti-dsDNA with low complement indicates lupus nephritis; this patient has normal C3/C4 (pauci-immune pattern), making lupus unlikely.

stages.label: IMAGING & SEROLOGY
stages.content:
CT chest: multiple bilateral nodules, several cavitating, up to 3 cm; patchy ground-glass opacities
c-ANCA: positive, titer 1:320
Anti-PR3 antibodies: strongly positive
Anti-GBM antibodies: negative
BAL: hemosiderin-laden macrophages, no organisms on stain/culture
Kidney biopsy: pauci-immune necrotizing crescentic glomerulonephritis

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: He has rising creatinine and active alveolar hemorrhage. What is the most appropriate induction therapy?
options: Glucocorticoids alone with close monitoring as initial therapy
Broad-spectrum antibiotics and antifungals for cavitary nodules
High-dose glucocorticoids plus rituximab
Trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole monotherapy as initial therapy
stages.explanation: Organ- or life-threatening GPA (rapidly progressive glomerulonephritis, alveolar hemorrhage) requires aggressive remission-induction with high-dose glucocorticoids combined with either rituximab or cyclophosphamide; plasma exchange may be considered for severe renal failure or DAH. Glucocorticoids alone are inadequate for severe disease and risk progression to dialysis dependence. Antibiotics/antifungals treat infection, but biopsy confirmed pauci-immune crescentic GN and BAL showed no organisms—cavitary nodules here are granulomatous, not infectious. Trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole has a role in limited upper-airway disease and as Pneumocystis prophylaxis during immunosuppression, but it is not induction therapy for severe systemic disease.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: Once remission is achieved and the patient is on maintenance immunosuppression, which preventive measure is most important to add?
options: Routine monthly intravenous immunoglobulin as the leading concern
Lifelong therapeutic anticoagulation as the leading concern
Pneumocystis jirovecii prophylaxis with trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole
Empiric antifungal prophylaxis with voriconazole in this presentation
stages.explanation: Patients on cyclophosphamide or rituximab plus glucocorticoids are at substantial risk of Pneumocystis jirovecii pneumonia; prophylaxis with trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole is standard and also reduces certain GPA relapses involving the upper airway. Lifelong anticoagulation is not indicated absent thromboembolism, though active vasculitis modestly raises VTE risk. Routine antifungal prophylaxis is not standard for GPA maintenance and carries toxicity. Monthly IVIG is reserved for selected refractory or infection-complicated cases, not routine maintenance. Vaccination, monitoring for relapse with ANCA trends/urinalysis, and glucocorticoid taper are also key components of ongoing care.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: Which surveillance strategy best detects clinically silent relapse during long-term follow-up of GPA?
options: Monitoring serum complement levels as the primary relapse marker with appropriate follow-up
Repeat kidney biopsy every 6 months regardless of symptoms with appropriate follow-up
Periodic urinalysis with microscopy plus clinical assessment, using rising ANCA titers as a supportive signal
Serial chest radiographs alone every 3 months with appropriate follow-up
stages.explanation: Relapse in GPA often first manifests as recurrent glomerular inflammation, so periodic urinalysis with microscopy (looking for new hematuria, dysmorphic RBCs, or casts) plus symptom-directed clinical assessment is central to surveillance; a rising ANCA titer can support suspicion of relapse but should not by itself trigger treatment escalation. Chest radiographs alone miss renal and ENT relapse and lack sensitivity for early disease. Serial protocol kidney biopsies are invasive and not standard surveillance. Complement levels are typically normal in this pauci-immune disease and are not useful relapse markers, unlike in immune-complex glomerulonephritis such as lupus nephritis.

cases.diagnosis: Granulomatosis with polyangiitis (pulmonary-renal syndrome with cavitary nodules and c-ANCA/anti-PR3)
pearls: GPA classically combines upper-airway destruction (saddle nose, septal perforation), cavitary lung nodules, and pauci-immune crescentic glomerulonephritis.
c-ANCA/anti-PR3 is the characteristic serology; complement levels are typically normal in this pauci-immune vasculitis.
Severe (organ-threatening) disease is induced with glucocorticoids plus rituximab or cyclophosphamide; consider plasma exchange for severe renal failure or DAH.
Add Pneumocystis prophylaxis with trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole during intensive immunosuppression.

cases.title: Coughing Up Blood with Crashing Oxygen Levels
cases.chiefComplaint: Massive hemoptysis with rapidly worsening shortness of breath over two days.
cases.history: A 29-year-old man is brought to the ICU after coughing up bright-red blood and becoming acutely dyspneic over 48 hours. For the past week he had fatigue, frothy dark urine, and mild ankle swelling that he attributed to a 'cold.' He recently recovered from an upper respiratory infection. PMH is notable only for seasonal allergies. He smokes half a pack of cigarettes daily and reports recent recreational use of inhaled cocaine at a party two weeks ago. No prescription medications; no known drug allergies. He works as a warehouse stocker with possible hydrocarbon solvent exposure. Family history is unremarkable for kidney or lung disease. Review of systems is positive for pallor, exertional dyspnea, and reduced urine output, and negative for fever, chest pain, sinus symptoms, rashes, joint pain, and oral ulcers.
