A multiple sleep latency test evaluates for narcolepsy and is inappropriate when an obvious cause of hypersomnolence (sleep-disordered breathing) is untreated. An echocardiogram is reasonable to assess for pulmonary hypertension and cor pulmonale given her loud P2 and edema, but it does not diagnose or treat the sleep-breathing disorder. Right heart catheterization is invasive and premature before noninvasive assessment and treatment of hypoventilation.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Her polysomnogram confirms severe obstructive sleep apnea with sustained nocturnal hypoventilation. Beyond positive airway pressure therapy, which intervention provides the greatest long-term benefit for her underlying disease?
options: Daily acetazolamide to lower bicarbonate as initial therapy
Therapeutic phlebotomy as primary treatment as initial therapy
Sustained weight loss, including consideration of bariatric surgery
Long-term supplemental oxygen alone as initial therapy
stages.explanation: Weight reduction targets the root cause of OHS; substantial, sustained weight loss can reverse hypoventilation, improve gas exchange, and reduce mortality, and bariatric surgery is appropriate to consider at her BMI of 49. Positive airway pressure (typically initiated as CPAP, escalated to bilevel if hypoventilation persists) is first-line, but durable benefit requires addressing obesity. Supplemental oxygen alone can blunt hypoxic drive and worsen hypercapnia if given without ventilatory support, so it is not adequate monotherapy. Phlebotomy treats the secondary erythrocytosis symptomatically but ignores the cause; correcting hypoxemia with PAP and weight loss is preferred. Acetazolamide is not a primary therapy for OHS and risks metabolic complications without fixing the underlying mechanics.

cases.diagnosis: Obesity hypoventilation syndrome with secondary erythrocytosis and coexisting severe obstructive sleep apnea
pearls: OHS requires obesity, awake hypercapnia (PaCO2 ≥45 mmHg), and sleep-disordered breathing after excluding other causes; an awake ABG is the cornerstone test.
An elevated serum bicarbonate in an obese, sleepy patient is a clue to chronic CO2 retention and should prompt an ABG.
Secondary erythrocytosis (high Hgb/Hct) in OHS is an appropriate response to chronic hypoxemia, not polycythemia vera.
PAP therapy plus sustained weight loss is the mainstay; supplemental oxygen alone can worsen hypercapnia by suppressing ventilatory drive.

cases.title: Restless Nights and a Waxing-Waning Breath
cases.chiefComplaint: Frequent nighttime awakenings, daytime fatigue, and a partner's report of an unusual breathing pattern during sleep.
cases.history: A 67-year-old man with chronic systolic heart failure presents to clinic with fragmented sleep and daytime fatigue worsening over six months. His wife describes a striking sleep pattern: his breathing gradually deepens, then fades to a complete pause lasting several seconds, then resumes and crescendos again, cycling all night. She denies the loud, choking gasps typical of his brother's sleep apnea. He has mild orthopnea (two pillows) at baseline that is unchanged, no recent weight gain, and no leg swelling. PMH: ischemic cardiomyopathy with LVEF 28%, prior anterior MI, atrial fibrillation, hypertension. Medications: sacubitril-valsartan 97/103 mg twice daily, carvedilol 25 mg twice daily, spironolactone 25 mg daily, furosemide 40 mg daily, apixaban 5 mg twice daily. Allergies: none. Social: former smoker (quit 10 years ago, 20 pack-years), no alcohol or opioids. Family history: brother with obstructive sleep apnea. He is not obese (BMI 26). He denies witnessed snoring or morning headaches but feels unrefreshed.
cases.exam: Thin, well-appearing man in no distress. Jugular venous pressure approximately 8 cm H2O. Lungs clear with no crackles or wheeze. Cardiac exam reveals an irregularly irregular rhythm, a soft S3 gallop, and a displaced apical impulse; no significant murmur. Abdomen soft, nontender, no hepatomegaly. Extremities warm with trace ankle edema and good pulses. Oropharynx is not crowded (Mallampati II); neck circumference is normal. No cyanosis or clubbing. Neurologic exam nonfocal. Overall he appears euvolemic with compensated heart failure.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: His wife's description of crescendo-decrescendo breathing with central pauses, in a non-obese man with low-EF heart failure, most strongly suggests which mechanism over classic obstructive sleep apnea?
options: Ventilatory control instability with high loop gain
Upper-airway collapse during inspiration
Diaphragmatic paralysis as the first step today
Nocturnal bronchospasm as the first step today
stages.explanation: The crescendo-decrescendo (waxing-waning) pattern with central apneas is Cheyne-Stokes respiration, driven by ventilatory control instability—high loop gain—in which prolonged circulation time and heightened chemoreceptor sensitivity cause oscillating CO2 levels and periodic breathing. This is characteristic of central sleep apnea in heart failure. Upper-airway collapse defines obstructive sleep apnea, which produces snoring and continued respiratory effort against a closed airway, not the absent-effort central pauses described. Diaphragmatic paralysis causes orthopnea and paradoxical abdominal motion, not periodic crescendo-decrescendo cycling. Nocturnal bronchospasm causes wheeze and cough, not rhythmic central apneas. His normal habitus and lack of snoring further argue against OSA as the dominant problem.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: What is the definitive diagnostic test to confirm the type of sleep-disordered breathing in this patient?
options: Home sleep apnea test (type III portable monitor)
Daytime arterial blood gas
Attended in-laboratory polysomnography
Overnight oximetry in this clinical context
stages.explanation: Attended in-laboratory polysomnography is the gold standard and is required here because it records airflow, respiratory effort (by chest/abdominal belts), and EEG to distinguish central from obstructive events—the crucial distinction. Demonstrating apneas with absent respiratory effort confirms central sleep apnea and can quantify Cheyne-Stokes periodicity. Home sleep apnea tests are validated mainly for uncomplicated obstructive sleep apnea and are unreliable for central events and complex heart-failure physiology, risking misclassification. Overnight oximetry shows a cyclical desaturation pattern that is suggestive but cannot prove the events are central. A daytime ABG may reveal chronic hyperventilation/hypocapnia that predisposes to periodic breathing but does not diagnose the nocturnal event type.

stages.label: SLEEP STUDY RESULTS
stages.content:
Polysomnography:
Apnea-Hypopnea Index: 32 events/hour
Central apneas: 27 events/hour (84% of events)
Obstructive apneas: minimal
Pattern: crescendo-decrescendo airflow with cyclical absence of thoracoabdominal effort (Cheyne-Stokes respiration)
Nadir SpO2: 84%
Arousal index: 28/hour
No significant snoring recorded
Repeat echocardiogram: LVEF 27%, no new wall-motion abnormality

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Polysomnography confirms predominant central sleep apnea with Cheyne-Stokes respiration in a patient with LVEF 27%. What is the most appropriate first-line management?
options: Supplemental nocturnal oxygen as definitive therapy
Adaptive servo-ventilation (ASV) as initial therapy
Diaphragmatic pacing as initial therapy
Optimization of guideline-directed heart failure therapy
stages.explanation: In central sleep apnea due to heart failure, optimizing guideline-directed medical therapy (and treating volume overload, arrhythmia, and ischemia) is first-line, because improving cardiac output and circulation time can reduce or resolve Cheyne-Stokes breathing. Adaptive servo-ventilation is contraindicated in symptomatic patients with reduced LVEF ≤45%—the SERVE-HF trial showed increased cardiovascular and all-cause mortality—so ASV must be avoided in this man with EF 27%. Supplemental nocturnal oxygen can lessen central events and is a reasonable adjunct, but it is not definitive therapy and does not address the underlying hemodynamics. Diaphragmatic (phrenic nerve) stimulation is an option for refractory central sleep apnea but is not first-line before HF optimization.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: His central sleep apnea persists despite optimized heart failure therapy. He remains symptomatic with an EF of 27%. Which therapy is now most appropriate and safe?
options: Transvenous phrenic nerve stimulation
High-flow nasal cannula at home
Adaptive servo-ventilation
Theophylline infusion
stages.explanation: Transvenous phrenic nerve stimulation is FDA-approved for moderate-to-severe central sleep apnea and is appropriate for symptomatic patients who fail medical optimization, including those with reduced EF in whom ASV is unsafe. It restores a more physiologic breathing pattern without the mortality signal seen with ASV. Adaptive servo-ventilation remains contraindicated given his EF ≤45% (SERVE-HF). High-flow nasal cannula is not an established home therapy for central sleep apnea and lacks supporting outcome data. Respiratory stimulants such as theophylline or acetazolamide have limited, low-quality evidence, narrow therapeutic windows, and proarrhythmic risk—particularly concerning in a patient with atrial fibrillation—so they are not preferred over phrenic nerve stimulation.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: How should the presence of Cheyne-Stokes central sleep apnea be interpreted in the context of his heart failure prognosis and ongoing care?
options: It is a marker of more advanced heart failure
It is a benign incidental finding with no prognostic relevance with appropriate follow-up
It mandates immediate cardiac transplantation regardless of other factors with appropriate follow-up
It indicates the heart failure has resolved with appropriate follow-up
stages.explanation: Cheyne-Stokes respiration with central sleep apnea is a marker of more advanced heart failure and is independently associated with worse outcomes and increased mortality; its presence should prompt aggressive optimization of guideline-directed heart failure therapy, attention to volume status and arrhythmia, and close multidisciplinary follow-up. It is not a benign incidental finding—it reflects the severity of the underlying cardiac dysfunction and circulatory delay. It certainly does not indicate that the heart failure has resolved; rather, the opposite. While advanced heart failure may eventually prompt evaluation for advanced therapies, the finding of CSA alone does not mandate immediate transplantation; transplant decisions depend on comprehensive criteria including functional status, hemodynamics, and refractoriness to optimal therapy.

cases.diagnosis: Central sleep apnea with Cheyne-Stokes respiration secondary to heart failure with reduced ejection fraction
pearls: Cheyne-Stokes respiration—crescendo-decrescendo breathing with central apneas—reflects high loop gain from prolonged circulation time in low-EF heart failure.
Attended polysomnography distinguishes central (absent respiratory effort) from obstructive events; home tests are unreliable for central apnea.
Adaptive servo-ventilation is contraindicated in symptomatic central sleep apnea with LVEF ≤45% due to increased mortality (SERVE-HF).
Optimizing guideline-directed heart failure therapy is first-line for CSA; phrenic nerve stimulation is an option when symptoms persist.

cases.title: Worsening Breathing the Day After a Crash
cases.chiefComplaint: Progressive shortness of breath and falling oxygen levels the day after a high-speed motor vehicle collision.
cases.history: A 28-year-old man is admitted to the ICU after a high-speed motor vehicle collision in which his chest struck the steering wheel; he was restrained but the airbag deployed. In the trauma bay his initial chest X-ray was nearly clear and his oxygenation was adequate. Over the next 18-24 hours he develops worsening dyspnea, tachypnea, and progressively increasing oxygen requirements. He reports right-sided chest pain that worsens with deep breaths and cough productive of blood-tinged sputum. He denies prior lung disease, fever, or sick contacts. PMH: none. Medications: none before admission; now on scheduled acetaminophen and a fentanyl PCA for pain. Allergies: none. Social: occasional alcohol, no tobacco or drugs, works in construction. Family history: noncontributory. Trauma survey was notable for right chest-wall bruising and tenderness; FAST exam negative for intra-abdominal free fluid; pelvis and long bones intact. He has remained hemodynamically stable without ongoing hemorrhage, but his work of breathing is rising.
cases.exam: Young man in moderate respiratory distress, using accessory muscles. Ecchymosis and tenderness over the right anterolateral chest wall; no palpable crepitus or obvious chest-wall instability or paradoxical movement. Trachea midline. Right basal and mid-lung crackles with decreased breath sounds at the right base; left lung clear. Heart sounds normal, tachycardic, no murmur or muffling. Abdomen soft and nontender. No jugular venous distension. Extremities without edema; distal pulses intact. Oxygen saturation improves only modestly with increased FiO2.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: His chest was nearly clear initially but he is now hypoxemic with a localized right infiltrate 18-24 hours after blunt chest trauma. Which test best characterizes the lung injury?
options: Repeat chest X-ray and CT chest
CT pulmonary angiography for PE
Echocardiography for cardiac contusion
Bronchoscopy with lavage cultures
stages.explanation: Repeat chest imaging—portable chest X-ray and, ideally, CT of the chest—is the best next step because pulmonary contusion characteristically evolves over 24-48 hours, producing a non-segmental infiltrate that was not apparent on the initial film. CT is more sensitive and defines the extent of parenchymal injury and any associated injuries (pneumothorax, rib fractures, hemothorax). CT pulmonary angiography pursues pulmonary embolism, which is uncommon this early and would not explain a localized infiltrate matching the impact site. Bronchoscopy with cultures targets infection or airway injury; pneumonia would be unusual within a day and his picture fits contusion. Echocardiography evaluates cardiac contusion but does not explain the focal pulmonary infiltrate or hypoxemia.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
CT chest: patchy, non-segmental ground-glass and consolidative opacities in the right middle and lower lobes that do not respect anatomic boundaries; underlying right rib fractures (ribs 5-7); small right pneumothorax; no large hemothorax
ABG (on 4 L): pH 7.44, PaCO2 34 mmHg, PaO2 58 mmHg
WBC: 11.8 ×10³/µL
Hemoglobin: 13.6 g/dL
Lactate: 1.4 mmol/L
Troponin: within normal limits
ECG: sinus tachycardia

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: The CT shows a patchy, non-anatomic infiltrate beneath the rib fractures that developed over a day. What is the most likely diagnosis?
options: Pulmonary contusion
Aspiration pneumonia
Acute respiratory distress syndrome from sepsis
Pulmonary embolism
stages.explanation: Pulmonary contusion is the diagnosis: blunt chest trauma causes alveolar hemorrhage and edema directly beneath the site of impact, producing a non-segmental infiltrate that crosses anatomic boundaries and classically blossoms 24-48 hours after injury, exactly matching the timeline, location under the rib fractures, hemoptysis, and delayed hypoxemia. Aspiration pneumonia typically follows a depressed level of consciousness and localizes to dependent segments with a more infectious time course and rising WBC/fever. Pulmonary embolism causes hypoxemia but not a focal consolidation aligned with the trauma site, and is unusual this soon. Sepsis-related ARDS produces bilateral diffuse infiltrates from a systemic insult, whereas his findings are unilateral and directly trauma-related.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: He is maintaining adequate ventilation but remains hypoxemic. What is the cornerstone of management for his pulmonary contusion?
options: Aggressive crystalloid resuscitation to keep him well ahead as monotherapy
Empiric broad-spectrum antibiotics for all contusions as initial therapy
Systemic corticosteroids to reduce lung inflammation as initial therapy
Supportive care: supplemental oxygen, judicious fluids, analgesia,
stages.explanation: Management of pulmonary contusion is supportive: supplemental oxygen titrated to adequate saturation, careful (not excessive) fluid management to avoid worsening alveolar edema, effective analgesia to permit deep breathing and coughing, and pulmonary toilet/incentive spirometry to prevent atelectasis and pneumonia. Escalation to noninvasive or invasive ventilation is reserved for respiratory failure. Routine prophylactic antibiotics are not indicated and do not prevent infection; antibiotics are used only if pneumonia develops. Aggressive crystalloid loading worsens contusion edema and oxygenation—fluids should be judicious. Corticosteroids have no proven benefit in pulmonary contusion and may increase infection risk, so they are not recommended.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: Why is ICU-level monitoring particularly important for this patient over the next 24-72 hours?
options: Antibiotic levels must be monitored hourly with appropriate follow-up
Hypoxemia and respiratory compromise often peak 24-72 hours after injury, risking ARDS and need for ventilation
He is at imminent risk of massive hemoptysis requiring lobectomy with appropriate follow-up
Contusions resolve within hours, so monitoring can be brief with appropriate follow-up
stages.explanation: Pulmonary contusion characteristically worsens before it improves: gas exchange and respiratory mechanics often deteriorate maximally at 24-72 hours as edema and inflammation peak, with risk of progression to ARDS and need for escalating respiratory support, so close ICU monitoring is essential. Contusions do not resolve within hours; they typically take several days to a week to clear radiographically. Massive hemoptysis requiring lobectomy is rare; hemoptysis here is mild and self-limited. Hourly antibiotic monitoring is irrelevant since prophylactic antibiotics are not indicated. Recognizing the predictable deterioration window allows timely escalation to noninvasive or mechanical ventilation and lung-protective strategies if respiratory failure develops.

cases.diagnosis: Pulmonary contusion following blunt chest trauma (with associated rib fractures and small pneumothorax)
pearls: Pulmonary contusion produces a non-segmental infiltrate beneath the impact site that classically evolves and worsens over 24-72 hours after blunt trauma.
Management is supportive—oxygen, judicious fluids, analgesia, and pulmonary toilet; prophylactic antibiotics and steroids are not indicated.
Avoid fluid overload, which exacerbates alveolar edema and hypoxemia in contused lung.
Watch for progression to ARDS and the need for ventilatory support during the peak window.

cases.title: A Chest Wall That Moves the Wrong Way
cases.chiefComplaint: Severe chest pain and labored breathing after a crush injury to the chest.
cases.history: A 50-year-old man is brought to the ICU after a forklift pinned his chest against a loading dock at work. He has severe right-sided chest pain that is markedly worse with each breath, and he is breathing rapidly and shallowly. Paramedics noted an abnormal segment of his chest wall moving opposite to the rest during respiration. He is alert and protecting his airway. He denies abdominal pain or limb deformity. PMH: hypertension and a 30 pack-year smoking history. Medications: amlodipine 10 mg daily. Allergies: penicillin (rash). Social: current smoker, moderate alcohol use, works in a warehouse. Family history: noncontributory. On arrival he received IV fentanyl with partial relief. Trauma evaluation showed no head, abdominal, or pelvic injury, and FAST was negative. His main problems are pain-limited ventilation and an unstable segment of chest wall, with rising oxygen requirement and a tendency toward shallow breaths to avoid pain. He has not been intubated and is being monitored closely.
cases.exam: Anxious man in respiratory distress, splinting and taking shallow breaths. Inspection of the right anterolateral chest reveals a segment that sinks inward during inspiration and bulges outward during expiration—paradoxical movement. Marked tenderness and crepitus over multiple right ribs; no obvious open wound. Trachea midline. Breath sounds diminished at the right base; no hyperresonance to suggest tension pneumothorax at present. Heart tachycardic, regular, no murmur. Abdomen soft, nontender. Extremities intact with good pulses. No jugular venous distension. He cannot take a deep breath because of pain.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: A chest-wall segment moving paradoxically with respiration after multiple rib fractures defines which condition, and what is the minimum fracture pattern required?
options: Open pneumothorax; any rib fracture with a skin defect as the first step today
Flail chest; three or more contiguous ribs each fractured in two or more places
Sternal fracture; a single transverse sternal break as the first step today
Simple rib fractures; a single fractured rib as the first step today
stages.explanation: Flail chest is defined by paradoxical movement of a free-floating chest-wall segment, which occurs when three or more contiguous ribs are each fractured in two or more places, isolating that segment from the rest of the thoracic cage. During inspiration the negative intrathoracic pressure pulls the segment inward while the rest of the chest expands, reversing during expiration. A single fractured rib cannot create a free segment and will not float paradoxically. An open pneumothorax involves a chest-wall defect communicating with the pleural space ('sucking chest wound') and is a different entity. A sternal fracture alone does not produce the multi-rib free segment that defines flail chest, though it can coexist.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
Chest X-ray: fractures of right ribs 4-8, several with two fracture sites each; underlying right lung opacity consistent with contusion; small right pneumothorax
CT chest: confirms segmental flail of right ribs 4-8 with displaced fragments; moderate right pulmonary contusion; no aortic injury
ABG (on 6 L): pH 7.31, PaCO2 50 mmHg, PaO2 56 mmHg
Hemoglobin: 12.8 g/dL
Lactate: 1.8 mmol/L

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Beyond the visible paradoxical motion, what is the principal driver of his hypoxemia and respiratory failure in flail chest?
options: The underlying pulmonary contusion plus pain-limited ventilation
A massive hemothorax compressing the lung as the primary diagnosis
Air embolism from the rib fractures as the primary diagnosis
The paradoxical segment itself impairing tidal volume
stages.explanation: The dominant cause of respiratory compromise in flail chest is the underlying pulmonary contusion combined with pain-induced hypoventilation and resulting atelectasis—not the mechanical paradox alone. Classic teaching once emphasized the flail segment's pendelluft, but modern understanding attributes most of the gas-exchange impairment to the bruised lung beneath and to splinting from pain, which his rising PaCO2 and contusion on CT confirm. A massive hemothorax would show a large opacity and hemodynamic instability, absent here (only a small pneumothorax noted). Air embolism is rare and would present with sudden cardiovascular or neurologic collapse. This understanding shifts management toward aggressive analgesia and pulmonary support rather than merely stabilizing the chest wall.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: His PaCO2 is rising and he cannot breathe deeply because of pain. What is the most important initial management priority?
options: Empiric chest tube and antibiotics for the contusion as initial therapy
Immediate sandbag/external stabilization of the flail segment as initial therapy
Emergency open reduction and internal fixation of all ribs as initial therapy
Aggressive multimodal analgesia with pulmonary toilet
stages.explanation: The cornerstone of flail chest management is aggressive pain control—multimodal analgesia, with thoracic epidural analgesia particularly effective—paired with pulmonary toilet, incentive spirometry, and escalating respiratory support (high-flow or noninvasive ventilation, and intubation if needed). Adequate analgesia allows deeper breathing, reduces atelectasis, and prevents progression to respiratory failure. External stabilization with sandbags is obsolete and harmful, as it restricts ventilation and worsens atelectasis. Surgical rib fixation has a role in selected patients (e.g., severe displacement, failure to wean), but it is not the initial priority before pain control and respiratory support are optimized. A chest tube is indicated for significant pneumothorax/hemothorax, and prophylactic antibiotics for contusion are not warranted.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: Despite optimal analgesia and noninvasive support, his PaCO2 climbs to 60 mmHg with worsening hypoxemia and fatigue. He requires intubation and mechanical ventilation. In which subset of flail-chest patients is surgical rib fixation most clearly beneficial?
options: Patients with severe displacement or those failing to wean from mechanical ventilation
Only patients without any pulmonary contusion as the leading concern
Patients who are already extubating successfully as the leading concern
All patients with any rib fracture, regardless of course as the leading concern
stages.explanation: Surgical stabilization of rib fractures (rib fixation) provides the clearest benefit in patients with severely displaced flail segments and in those who fail to wean from mechanical ventilation, where it can shorten ventilator days, reduce pneumonia, decrease ICU length of stay, and improve chest-wall mechanics. It is not indicated for every rib fracture—most simple fractures heal with supportive care. The presence of pulmonary contusion does not by itself contraindicate fixation; selection is based on chest-wall instability and ventilatory failure, not the absence of contusion. Patients who are weaning and extubating successfully do not need operative fixation, since the goal—liberation from the ventilator—is being achieved nonoperatively.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: After rib fixation he is extubated and improving. What measure most reduces his risk of pneumonia and long-term morbidity during recovery?
options: Continued multimodal analgesia, early mobilization,
Routine daily chest CT scans with appropriate follow-up
Prolonged prophylactic broad-spectrum antibiotics in this clinical context
Strict bed rest and breath-holding to protect the repair
stages.explanation: Recovery hinges on sustained pain control that enables deep breathing and coughing, early mobilization, and aggressive pulmonary hygiene (incentive spirometry, chest physiotherapy), which together prevent atelectasis and pneumonia—the leading drivers of morbidity after flail chest. Bed rest and breath-holding promote atelectasis and venous thromboembolism and are harmful. Prolonged prophylactic antibiotics do not prevent pneumonia and select for resistant organisms; antibiotics are reserved for documented infection. Routine daily CT scans add radiation and cost without changing management; imaging should be driven by clinical change. Smoking cessation counseling is also valuable given his history. The unifying theme is keeping the lung expanded and the patient moving.

cases.diagnosis: Flail chest with underlying pulmonary contusion causing hypercapnic respiratory failure
pearls: Flail chest = three or more contiguous ribs each fractured in two or more places, producing a free segment that moves paradoxically.
The main driver of respiratory failure is the underlying pulmonary contusion plus pain-limited ventilation, not the paradoxical motion alone.
Aggressive analgesia (thoracic epidural is highly effective) plus pulmonary toilet and respiratory support is the cornerstone; external stabilization with sandbags is obsolete.
Surgical rib fixation benefits patients with severe displacement or those failing to wean from the ventilator.

cases.title: A White-Out After a Stabbing
cases.chiefComplaint: Stab wound to the left chest with shortness of breath and chest pain.
cases.history: A 36-year-old man is brought to the emergency department after being stabbed in the left lateral chest during an altercation roughly 40 minutes ago. He complains of left-sided chest pain and progressive shortness of breath. He is awake and able to speak in short sentences. He denies abdominal pain and has no other wounds on exposure. PMH: none. Medications: none. Allergies: none. Social: occasional alcohol, smokes half a pack daily, no anticoagulants. Family history: noncontributory. Last meal several hours ago. On primary survey his airway is patent and he is protecting it; breathing is labored with reduced air entry on the left; circulation shows mild tachycardia and borderline-low blood pressure that responds to initial fluids. A single 3 cm wound is present in the left fifth intercostal space, mid-axillary line, with minimal external bleeding. He has received two large-bore IVs, is on the monitor, and blood has been sent for type and crossmatch. He remains anxious and dyspneic, and his oxygen requirement is climbing.
cases.exam: Diaphoretic young man in moderate distress. A 3 cm stab wound in the left fifth intercostal space at the mid-axillary line with minimal external bleeding and no air bubbling. Trachea midline. Markedly decreased breath sounds and dullness to percussion over the left lower hemithorax (not hyperresonance); right lung clear. Heart sounds normal and tachycardic without muffling; no jugular venous distension. Abdomen soft and nontender. Extremities cool but with palpable distal pulses. No focal neurologic deficit. He becomes more tachypneic when laid flat.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Decreased breath sounds with DULLNESS to percussion (not hyperresonance) over the left hemithorax after penetrating chest trauma, with mild hypotension, most strongly suggests which diagnosis?
options: Tension pneumothorax
Cardiac tamponade
Diaphragmatic rupture
Hemothorax
stages.explanation: Dullness to percussion with diminished breath sounds indicates fluid—blood—in the pleural space, i.e., a hemothorax, which fits penetrating trauma with mild hypotension from blood loss. Tension pneumothorax produces hyperresonance (not dullness), tracheal deviation away from the affected side, and distended neck veins—none of which are present. Cardiac tamponade causes muffled heart sounds, jugular venous distension, and hypotension (Beck's triad) but not unilateral dullness and reduced breath sounds. Diaphragmatic rupture can cause respiratory compromise after thoracoabdominal penetrating injury, but bowel sounds in the chest or herniated viscera on imaging would be expected, and dullness with hypotension here points to hemothorax as the immediate problem to address.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
eFAST: large anechoic fluid collection in the left pleural space; no pericardial effusion; abdomen negative
Chest X-ray (upright): opacification of the left hemithorax with loss of the costophrenic angle and a meniscus; mediastinum midline
Hemoglobin: 11.2 g/dL
Lactate: 3.1 mmol/L
INR: 1.0
ABG (on 4 L): pH 7.36, PaCO2 38 mmHg, PaO2 62 mmHg

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Imaging confirms a large left pleural fluid collection opacifying the hemithorax. What is the most appropriate immediate intervention?
options: Tube thoracostomy (chest tube) placement
CT chest before any intervention as monotherapy
Needle decompression in the second intercostal space
Immediate emergency thoracotomy as monotherapy
stages.explanation: Tube thoracostomy is the immediate treatment for hemothorax: a large-bore chest tube drains the blood, re-expands the lung to improve oxygenation, allows quantification of blood loss, and tamponades many low-pressure bleeders by re-apposing the pleural surfaces. Needle decompression treats tension pneumothorax (air), not a fluid collection, and would not drain blood effectively. Emergency thoracotomy is reserved for massive hemothorax with hemodynamic instability or ongoing high-volume output, criteria not yet met in this stabilized patient. Obtaining a CT before intervention is inappropriate when a symptomatic, large hemothorax is already documented by eFAST and chest X-ray—drainage should not be delayed for additional imaging in a patient with rising oxygen needs and borderline hemodynamics.

stages.label: PROCEDURE OUTPUT
stages.content:
Left chest tube placed in the fifth intercostal space, anterior axillary line
Immediate drainage: 900 mL of frank blood
Post-procedure: breath sounds improved on the left, SpO2 rises to 96% on 2 L
Ongoing output over next hour: 250 mL/hour for two consecutive hours
Repeat hemoglobin: 9.4 g/dL
BP 98/60 mmHg, HR 110 bpm after 1 L crystalloid and 1 unit PRBC

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: After initial drainage of 900 mL, the chest tube continues to put out about 250 mL/hour for two hours with persistent tachycardia. What is the most appropriate next step?
options: Surgical exploration for ongoing hemorrhage
Place a second chest tube and continue fluids
Clamp the chest tube to tamponade bleeding based on current risk
Continue observation and serial hemoglobins only
stages.explanation: Ongoing chest-tube output of roughly 150-200 mL/hour or more over 2-4 hours (or an initial output >1000-1500 mL) signals ongoing hemorrhage that will not stop spontaneously and meets criteria for operative intervention—surgical exploration via thoracotomy or VATS to identify and control the bleeding source. Continued observation alone risks exsanguination given his persistent tachycardia and sustained high output. Clamping the chest tube is dangerous: it can cause blood to re-accumulate, produce a tension physiology, and mask ongoing bleeding. Adding a second chest tube does not address an actively bleeding vessel and merely delays definitive control. The combination of high initial volume plus sustained hourly output is the trigger to go to the operating room.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: Suppose instead the bleeding had stopped, but follow-up imaging a few days later showed a persistent layer of clotted blood in the pleural space that the chest tube failed to evacuate. What complication is this, and how is it best managed?
options: Retained hemothorax
Simple pleural effusion; observe and repeat imaging in one month
Empyema; treat with antibiotics alone indefinitely as the leading concern
Chylothorax; start a low-fat diet as the leading concern
stages.explanation: A retained hemothorax—clotted blood not evacuated by the chest tube—is an important complication because it predisposes to empyema and fibrothorax (trapped lung). Early video-assisted thoracoscopic surgery (VATS), generally within the first several days, is the preferred management to evacuate the clot, re-expand the lung, and prevent these sequelae; it is more effective than additional tube drainage or fibrinolytics for organized clot. Calling it a simple effusion and merely observing risks progression to infection and lung entrapment. While empyema is a feared consequence, antibiotics alone do not clear an organized retained clot and are not curative without source control. Chylothorax produces milky, triglyceride-rich fluid—unrelated to this clotted blood—so a low-fat diet is irrelevant.

cases.diagnosis: Traumatic hemothorax from penetrating chest injury requiring tube thoracostomy and operative control of ongoing hemorrhage
pearls: Dullness to percussion with decreased breath sounds after chest trauma indicates hemothorax (blood); hyperresonance suggests pneumothorax (air).
Tube thoracostomy is both diagnostic and therapeutic for hemothorax and lets you quantify blood loss.
Operative exploration is indicated for initial output >1000-1500 mL or ongoing output >150-200 mL/hour for several hours.
Never clamp a chest tube draining a hemothorax—doing so can cause re-accumulation and tension physiology.

cases.title: Milky Drainage After Chest Surgery
cases.chiefComplaint: Increasing shortness of breath and an unusual milky appearance of the chest-tube fluid after recent thoracic surgery.
cases.history: A 60-year-old man on the surgical ward is one week out from an esophagectomy for esophageal adenocarcinoma. His postoperative chest tube initially drained serosanguineous fluid, but after he resumed enteral feeds the output increased in volume and turned opaque and milky-white. He reports mild dyspnea and fatigue but denies fever, chest pain, or productive cough. He has lost a few pounds and feels weaker. PMH: GERD, hypertension, type 2 diabetes, esophageal adenocarcinoma. Medications: home lisinopril 10 mg daily, metformin 1000 mg twice daily, perioperative enoxaparin prophylaxis, and a current high-fat enteral tube-feed formula. Allergies: none. Social: former smoker (25 pack-years, quit at surgery), minimal alcohol. Family history: noncontributory. The surgical team notes the chest-tube output rose to over a liter per day coinciding with the resumption of feeding. He is hemodynamically stable but appears mildly volume-depleted, and the nursing staff flagged the changed character of the drainage.
cases.exam: Thin, post-surgical man, comfortable at rest. Healing thoracotomy/laparotomy incisions without erythema or purulence. Chest tube in place draining copious milky-white, non-purulent fluid. Decreased breath sounds and dullness at the left base; trachea midline. Heart regular without murmur or rub. Abdomen soft with healing incision, mild tenderness, no peritoneal signs; bowel sounds present. Extremities without edema; mild dry mucous membranes suggesting volume depletion. No palpable lymphadenopathy. He is not septic-appearing and remains afebrile.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: The chest-tube fluid turned milky-white and surged after enteral feeding resumed following esophagectomy. What is the most useful initial test to confirm the suspected diagnosis?
options: Pleural fluid pH and glucose as the first step today
Pleural fluid Gram stain and culture
Pleural fluid cytology as the first step today
Pleural fluid triglyceride level
stages.explanation: A pleural fluid triglyceride level is the key initial test. A triglyceride concentration >110 mg/dL strongly supports chylothorax, and the presence of chylomicrons (by lipoprotein analysis) is confirmatory when the level is equivocal (50-110 mg/dL). The milky appearance plus a dramatic rise in output after resuming (especially fatty) enteral feeds is classic for thoracic duct injury during esophagectomy, since dietary fat increases chyle flow. Gram stain and culture evaluate empyema, but the fluid is non-purulent and the patient is afebrile, and pus—not chyle—would be expected. Cytology assesses malignant effusion, which would not surge with feeding. Pleural pH and glucose help characterize exudates broadly but do not specifically identify chyle the way triglycerides and chylomicrons do.

stages.label: PLEURAL FLUID ANALYSIS
stages.content:
Pleural fluid appearance: milky-white, opaque
Triglycerides: 320 mg/dL
Chylomicrons: present
Cholesterol: 65 mg/dL
Total protein (fluid/serum ratio): exudative
Gram stain: no organisms
Cell count: lymphocyte-predominant
Cytology: negative for malignant cells
Daily chest-tube output: 1.2 L

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Triglycerides are 320 mg/dL with chylomicrons present in a lymphocyte-predominant, milky exudate. What is the diagnosis and its underlying mechanism here?
options: Chylothorax from thoracic duct injury during esophagectomy
Empyema from postoperative infection in this clinical context
Pseudochylothorax from chronic inflammation
Cholesterol effusion from longstanding effusion
stages.explanation: This is a chylothorax: triglycerides >110 mg/dL with chylomicrons confirm chyle, and the cause is iatrogenic thoracic duct injury during esophagectomy, with leakage amplified by fat-containing enteral feeds (lymphocyte-predominant fluid further supports lymphatic origin). Empyema is excluded by the absence of organisms, lack of purulence, and the patient being afebrile. Pseudochylothorax (chyliform effusion) arises in chronic effusions (e.g., tuberculosis, rheumatoid pleuritis) and is driven by high cholesterol with cholesterol crystals, not chylomicrons, and typically has low triglycerides. A cholesterol effusion is the same chronic-process entity and does not surge acutely with feeding. The temporal link to surgery and feeding cements duct injury as the mechanism.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: His output is 1.2 L/day. What is the most appropriate initial management of this postoperative chylothorax?
options: Immediate thoracic duct ligation in all cases as initial therapy
Continue the current high-fat tube feeds and observe as initial therapy
Conservative therapy: low-fat/medium-chain triglyceride or NPO diet, drainage,
Pleurodesis as the first-line treatment as initial therapy
stages.explanation: Initial management of chylothorax is conservative: reduce chyle flow with a low-fat diet rich in medium-chain triglycerides (which are absorbed directly into portal blood, bypassing the lymphatics) or make the patient NPO with parenteral nutrition, while draining the pleural space and replacing fluid, protein, and immunoglobulin losses; octreotide/somatostatin may reduce output. Continuing high-fat feeds drives ongoing leakage and worsens the problem. Immediate duct ligation is not first-line for every patient; surgery (thoracic duct ligation or embolization) is reserved for high-volume leaks (often >1-1.5 L/day persisting) or failure of conservative therapy after several days. Pleurodesis addresses recurrent effusions but does not stop a high-output duct leak and is not first-line here.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: Despite NPO status, parenteral nutrition, and octreotide, his chyle leak persists at over 1 L/day after five days, and he is becoming malnourished and lymphopenic. What is the most appropriate next step?
options: Thoracic duct ligation or percutaneous thoracic duct embolization
Continue conservative therapy indefinitely as the leading concern
Repeated therapeutic thoracenteses only as the leading concern
High-dose corticosteroids as the leading concern
stages.explanation: A high-output chylothorax (>1-1.5 L/day) that persists despite optimal conservative therapy, especially with progressive malnutrition, immunosuppression from lymphocyte/immunoglobulin loss, and volume depletion, warrants definitive intervention: surgical thoracic duct ligation or interventional radiology thoracic duct embolization. Continuing conservative care indefinitely allows ongoing nutritional, immunologic, and fluid losses that increase morbidity and mortality. Repeated thoracenteses do not address the source of the leak and add infection and depletion risk. Corticosteroids have no role in stopping a traumatic duct leak. The decision to escalate is driven by the magnitude and persistence of output together with the patient's deteriorating nutritional and immune status.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: Which complication of a prolonged high-output chylothorax is most important to monitor and replace for during his course?
options: Iron-deficiency anemia from blood loss with appropriate follow-up
Hyperkalemia from cellular release with appropriate follow-up
Loss of fluid, protein, fat-soluble vitamins,
Respiratory alkalosis from hyperventilation with appropriate follow-up
stages.explanation: Chyle is rich in fat, fat-soluble vitamins, protein, electrolytes, and lymphocytes/immunoglobulins, so a prolonged high-output chylothorax causes malnutrition, hypoproteinemia, fat-soluble vitamin deficiency, dehydration, and—critically—lymphopenia with immunoglobulin depletion that predisposes to infection. Aggressive nutritional support and monitoring for these losses are essential. Iron-deficiency anemia is not the characteristic deficit; chyle is not blood. Hyperkalemia is not a hallmark; if anything, large-volume losses can disturb electrolytes in other directions. Respiratory alkalosis is unrelated to the chyle leak itself. Recognizing the immunologic and nutritional consequences guides timely escalation to definitive treatment before the patient becomes critically depleted and infection-prone.

cases.diagnosis: Postoperative chylothorax from thoracic duct injury during esophagectomy
pearls: Milky pleural fluid with triglycerides >110 mg/dL and chylomicrons confirms chylothorax; output classically surges after fatty enteral feeds.
Thoracic duct injury during thoracic/esophageal surgery is a leading cause of chylothorax.
Conservative therapy is first-line: medium-chain triglyceride/low-fat or NPO diet, drainage, nutritional support, and octreotide.
High-output (>1-1.5 L/day) or refractory leaks need thoracic duct ligation or embolization; monitor for malnutrition, lymphopenia, and immunosuppression.

cases.title: An Effusion That Keeps Coming Back
cases.chiefComplaint: Recurrent shortness of breath from fluid around the lung that returns after each drainage.
cases.history: A 68-year-old woman presents to pulmonary clinic with progressive dyspnea on exertion over two months. She was treated for a known left breast cancer two years ago and has had a recent rise in tumor markers. She has now undergone two therapeutic thoracenteses for a left pleural effusion; each time her breathing improved, but the fluid reaccumulated within one to two weeks. She denies fever, productive cough, or pleuritic chest pain. She reports fatigue, decreased appetite, and a 10-pound weight loss. PMH: hormone-receptor-positive breast cancer (status post lumpectomy and chemoradiation), hypertension, osteoarthritis. Medications: anastrozole 1 mg daily, amlodipine 5 mg daily, acetaminophen as needed. Allergies: sulfa (hives). Social: never-smoker, social alcohol, retired teacher, lives with her husband. Family history: mother with ovarian cancer. Her functional status has declined; she now stops to rest after one block. She is frustrated by the recurrent drainages and wants a more durable solution.
cases.exam: Thin, fatigued woman, comfortable at rest but dyspneic with exertion. Dullness to percussion, decreased breath sounds, and reduced tactile fremitus over the left lower hemithorax; right lung clear. No wheeze. Trachea slightly deviated to the right with the large effusion. Heart regular without murmur. No jugular venous distension. Healed left mastectomy/lumpectomy scar; no chest-wall mass. Abdomen soft, nontender, no organomegaly or ascites. Mild left supraclavicular fullness. Extremities without edema. No clubbing.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: In this woman with recurrent effusion and a history of breast cancer, what is the most appropriate test to determine the cause of the effusion?
options: CT pulmonary angiography as the first step today
Empiric diuretics and reassessment in two weeks
Diagnostic thoracentesis with fluid analysis
Pleural biopsy as the first procedure
stages.explanation: Diagnostic thoracentesis with pleural fluid analysis—including chemistry to apply Light's criteria and cytology for malignant cells—is the appropriate first step to determine etiology, and it can be combined with a therapeutic tap. Given her cancer history, weight loss, and supraclavicular fullness, a malignant exudate is likely, and cytology can confirm it. Empiric diuretics are appropriate for a transudative, heart-failure-related effusion, but she has no signs of volume overload and an exudate is suspected, so this would delay diagnosis. CT pulmonary angiography evaluates pulmonary embolism, not suggested by her chronic course. Pleural biopsy (e.g., thoracoscopic) is reserved for when fluid cytology is non-diagnostic but malignancy is still suspected; it is not the first-line procedure before a simple thoracentesis.

stages.label: PLEURAL FLUID ANALYSIS
stages.content:
Pleural fluid appearance: serosanguineous
Fluid LDH 480 U/L (serum LDH 250 U/L); ratio 1.9
Fluid protein/serum protein ratio: 0.7
Fluid glucose: 70 mg/dL
Nucleated cells: lymphocyte-predominant
Cytology: positive for malignant epithelial cells consistent with metastatic breast carcinoma
Chest CT: large left pleural effusion with nodular pleural thickening; no PE

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: The fluid is exudative by Light's criteria with positive cytology and nodular pleural thickening on CT. What is the diagnosis?
options: Parapneumonic effusion
Transudative effusion from heart failure
Malignant pleural effusion
Tuberculous pleuritis
stages.explanation: This is a malignant pleural effusion: cytology positive for metastatic carcinoma plus nodular pleural thickening on CT in a patient with known breast cancer is diagnostic. The fluid meets Light's criteria for an exudate (LDH ratio >0.6, protein ratio >0.5, fluid LDH greater than two-thirds the upper limit of normal serum LDH), consistent with malignancy. A parapneumonic effusion would accompany pneumonia with fever, neutrophil predominance, and possibly low glucose/pH in complicated cases—absent here. A transudate from heart failure is excluded by the exudative chemistry and lack of volume overload. Tuberculous pleuritis can be a lymphocytic exudate but cytology would be negative for malignancy and the clinical context (cancer, positive cytology, pleural nodularity) points firmly to malignant effusion.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: She has a confirmed recurrent malignant pleural effusion that reaccumulates quickly, and she wants a durable solution. Which definitive options should be offered?
options: Long-term oral diuretics as initial therapy
Indwelling pleural catheter or chemical pleurodesis, with the choice guided by lung re-expansion
Repeated therapeutic thoracenteses indefinitely as the only option as initial therapy
Pleural biopsy and observation as initial therapy
stages.explanation: For a recurrent symptomatic malignant pleural effusion, definitive management is either an indwelling (tunneled) pleural catheter for intermittent home drainage or chemical pleurodesis (e.g., talc), with the choice individualized to whether the lung fully re-expands after drainage and to patient preference and prognosis. Both relieve dyspnea and reduce repeat procedures; an indwelling catheter is preferred when the lung does not re-expand (trapped lung) or for those favoring outpatient management, whereas pleurodesis requires apposition of pleural surfaces to succeed. Indefinite repeated thoracenteses are burdensome and not durable. Pleural biopsy adds nothing once the diagnosis is established. Diuretics treat transudative effusions and have no role in a malignant exudate.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: If a key reason to choose an indwelling pleural catheter over talc pleurodesis is identified on her post-drainage imaging, what finding would that be?
options: Absence of pleural nodularity based on current risk
A small, asymptomatic effusion based on current risk
Failure of the lung to re-expand after drainage
Complete lung re-expansion after drainage
stages.explanation: Failure of the lung to re-expand after drainage—'trapped lung,' often from a visceral pleural peel encasing tumor—favors an indwelling pleural catheter over talc pleurodesis, because pleurodesis requires the visceral and parietal pleura to appose and fuse, which cannot occur if the lung does not re-expand to fill the space. Complete lung re-expansion, by contrast, is exactly the situation in which pleurodesis is likely to succeed, so it would favor (not argue against) pleurodesis. A small, asymptomatic effusion may simply be observed and does not mandate either intervention. Absence of pleural nodularity does not determine catheter versus pleurodesis; the decisive factor is whether the lung re-expands and the pleural surfaces can appose.

cases.diagnosis: Recurrent malignant pleural effusion secondary to metastatic breast carcinoma
pearls: Light's criteria define an exudate; malignant effusions are exudative, and positive cytology with pleural nodularity confirms malignancy.
Definitive options for recurrent malignant pleural effusion are indwelling pleural catheter or chemical (talc) pleurodesis.
Trapped lung (failure to re-expand after drainage) favors an indwelling catheter, since pleurodesis needs pleural apposition to work.
A malignant pleural effusion indicates advanced disease and generally a poor prognosis, so management is palliative and preference-sensitive.

cases.title: Sudden Breathlessness in the Middle of the Night
cases.chiefComplaint: Sudden severe shortness of breath that woke him from sleep, with frothy cough.
cases.history: A 72-year-old man presents to the emergency department after waking abruptly two hours ago, gasping for air. He had to sit upright at the edge of the bed and felt he was suffocating. He coughed up frothy, pink-tinged sputum and feels his heart pounding. He admits to running out of his blood pressure medications a week ago and eating salty takeout food the night before. He denies chest pain, fever, or recent immobilization. PMH: hypertension, type 2 diabetes, prior myocardial infarction with known reduced ejection fraction, chronic kidney disease stage 3. Medications (lapsed): lisinopril 40 mg daily, metoprolol succinate 100 mg daily, furosemide 40 mg daily, atorvastatin 40 mg daily, aspirin 81 mg daily. Allergies: none. Social: former smoker (quit years ago), no alcohol or drugs. Family history: father died of heart disease. He appears acutely distressed and anxious, speaking in short phrases. He has noticed mild ankle swelling and weight gain over recent days but nothing as severe as tonight's episode.
cases.exam: Elderly man in severe respiratory distress, sitting bolt upright, diaphoretic, using accessory muscles and speaking in two- to three-word sentences. Diffuse bibasilar and mid-lung crackles extending upward, with scattered wheezes. Jugular venous pressure elevated to the angle of the jaw. Cardiac exam reveals tachycardia, an S3 gallop, and a laterally displaced apex; no new murmur appreciated over the noise. Abdomen soft. Extremities with 1-2+ pitting edema and cool, clammy skin. He is anxious and air-hungry. Peripheral pulses present and regular.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: An elderly man with prior low-EF heart failure presents with acute orthopnea, pink frothy sputum, bilateral crackles, severe hypertension, and an S3. What is the most likely diagnosis?
options: Acute cardiogenic pulmonary edema
Community-acquired pneumonia
Pulmonary embolism
Acute exacerbation of COPD
stages.explanation: Acute cardiogenic pulmonary edema is the diagnosis: abrupt nocturnal dyspnea (paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnea), orthopnea, pink frothy sputum, diffuse bilateral crackles, elevated JVP, an S3 gallop, and severe hypertension in a patient with known reduced-EF heart failure who stopped his medications and ate a high-salt meal all point to acute decompensation with flash pulmonary edema. Pneumonia would typically cause fever, focal findings, and a productive purulent (not frothy pink) cough. A COPD exacerbation produces wheezing with prolonged expiration and a smoking-driven obstructive picture, not bilateral crackles with an S3 and elevated JVP. Pulmonary embolism causes acute hypoxemia but usually presents with pleuritic pain, clear lungs, and risk factors for thrombosis rather than this volume-overload picture.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
Chest X-ray: cardiomegaly, bilateral perihilar 'bat-wing' alveolar opacities, Kerley B lines, vascular cephalization
Lung ultrasound: diffuse bilateral B-lines
BNP: 1850 pg/mL
Troponin: mildly elevated, trending flat
Creatinine: 1.8 mg/dL (baseline 1.5)
ECG: sinus tachycardia, old anterior Q waves, LV strain pattern
ABG (RA): pH 7.30, PaCO2 48 mmHg, PaO2 54 mmHg

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which combination of findings best confirms a cardiogenic (rather than noncardiogenic) cause of his pulmonary edema?
options: Markedly elevated BNP plus cardiomegaly, an S3, elevated JVP,
A mildly elevated, flat troponin as the primary diagnosis
Diffuse B-lines on lung ultrasound alone as the primary diagnosis
Severe hypertension by itself as the primary diagnosis
stages.explanation: A cardiogenic cause is best supported by the constellation of a markedly elevated BNP (reflecting ventricular wall stress/volume overload) together with cardiomegaly, an S3 gallop, elevated jugular venous pressure, and interstitial congestion (Kerley B lines) on chest X-ray—signs of elevated cardiac filling pressures. Diffuse B-lines on lung ultrasound indicate interstitial fluid but are nonspecific, occurring in both cardiogenic and noncardiogenic (ARDS) edema, so they alone cannot distinguish the two. A mildly elevated, flat troponin reflects demand-related strain or chronic kidney disease and does not establish the mechanism of edema. Severe hypertension is a precipitant of flash edema but, by itself, does not prove a cardiogenic origin; the integrated picture of cardiac dysfunction and elevated filling pressures does.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: While confirming the diagnosis, what is the most appropriate combination of immediate therapy for this hypertensive flash pulmonary edema?
options: Aggressive IV normal saline bolus as initial therapy
Immediate systemic thrombolysis as initial therapy
IV antibiotics and bronchodilators as initial therapy
Supplemental oxygen/noninvasive positive-pressure ventilation, IV loop diuretic,
stages.explanation: The mainstays are oxygenation/ventilation and rapid reduction of preload and afterload: noninvasive positive-pressure ventilation (CPAP/BiPAP) improves oxygenation and decreases work of breathing and preload; an IV loop diuretic (furosemide) treats volume overload; and IV nitroglycerin is especially valuable in hypertensive flash edema to reduce afterload and preload, rapidly improving congestion. Antibiotics and bronchodilators treat pneumonia or COPD, which this is not. Aggressive IV saline is exactly wrong—it would worsen pulmonary congestion in a fluid-overloaded patient. Systemic thrombolysis treats massive pulmonary embolism or STEMI, neither of which is present; his troponin is only mildly and flatly elevated from demand, and the picture is decompensated heart failure, not an acute coronary occlusion.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: He improves rapidly with NIPPV, nitroglycerin, and diuresis. Beyond acute stabilization, what intervention most reduces his risk of recurrent episodes?
options: Routine serial CT pulmonary angiograms with appropriate follow-up
Resuming and optimizing guideline-directed heart failure therapy with medication adherence, blood pressure control, and dietary sodium restriction
Strict bed rest and fluid liberalization with appropriate follow-up
Long-term prophylactic antibiotics with appropriate follow-up
stages.explanation: Recurrence is best prevented by resuming and optimizing guideline-directed medical therapy (ACE inhibitor/ARNI, beta-blocker, mineralocorticoid antagonist, and SGLT2 inhibitor as appropriate), ensuring medication adherence, controlling blood pressure, and restricting dietary sodium—precisely the lapses (stopped meds, salty meal, uncontrolled hypertension) that precipitated this flash edema. Reinforcing daily weights and follow-up further reduces readmission. Bed rest and fluid liberalization would worsen deconditioning and congestion. Prophylactic antibiotics have no role in heart failure. Routine CT pulmonary angiograms expose him to contrast nephrotoxicity (he has CKD) and radiation without addressing his cardiac decompensation. Addressing the modifiable precipitants and optimizing chronic therapy is the highest-yield strategy.

cases.diagnosis: Acute cardiogenic (flash) pulmonary edema from hypertensive heart failure decompensation
pearls: Pink frothy sputum, orthopnea/PND, diffuse crackles, elevated JVP, and an S3 with severe hypertension point to acute cardiogenic pulmonary edema.
Chest X-ray shows cardiomegaly, bat-wing opacities, Kerley B lines, and cephalization; lung ultrasound shows diffuse B-lines.
Treat with oxygen/NIPPV, IV loop diuretic, and IV nitroglycerin; avoid IV fluid boluses.
Identify and reverse precipitants (medication nonadherence, dietary indiscretion, hypertension) and optimize guideline-directed therapy to prevent recurrence.

cases.title: Hypoxia, a Rash, and Confusion After a Broken Leg
cases.chiefComplaint: Sudden drop in oxygen, confusion, and a new rash two days after a femur fracture.
cases.history: A 23-year-old man is in the ICU after a closed midshaft femur fracture sustained in a motorcycle crash two days ago; surgical fixation was delayed. Roughly 48 hours after injury he becomes acutely tachypneic and hypoxemic, develops new confusion and agitation, and nurses note a fine rash. He was previously alert with stable oxygenation. He denies chest pain. There is no prior cardiopulmonary disease. PMH: none. Medications: enoxaparin prophylaxis, acetaminophen, and a hydromorphone PCA. Allergies: none. Social: occasional alcohol, no tobacco, no other drugs. Family history: noncontributory. He had no head injury at the scene (GCS 15 on arrival) and his initial chest imaging was clear. His mental status has now deteriorated to disorientation with intermittent agitation, and his oxygen requirement has climbed sharply over a few hours. The orthopedic and ICU teams are at the bedside; sepsis and pulmonary embolism are being considered, but the constellation of findings is distinctive.
cases.exam: Young man, tachypneic and confused, intermittently agitated. Scattered petechiae are noted over the conjunctivae, axillae, and anterior chest/neck. Lungs with diffuse fine crackles bilaterally; no focal consolidation. Heart tachycardic, regular, no murmur or rub; no jugular venous distension. The fractured thigh is splinted, swollen, and tender without compartment tautness; distal pulses intact. Abdomen soft. Neurologic exam: disoriented to time and place, no focal motor deficit, no neck stiffness. No calf cords or unilateral leg swelling beyond the fracture site.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Hypoxemia, neurologic changes, and petechiae developing 24-72 hours after a long-bone fracture form a classic triad. What is the most likely diagnosis?
options: Pulmonary thromboembolism
Fat embolism syndrome
Hospital-acquired pneumonia
Alcohol withdrawal delirium
stages.explanation: The classic triad of hypoxemia (respiratory insufficiency), neurologic abnormalities (confusion/agitation), and a petechial rash arising 24-72 hours after a long-bone (especially femur) fracture is the hallmark of fat embolism syndrome, in which marrow fat and inflammatory mediators embolize to the lungs, brain, and skin. The latent interval and petechiae in the axillae/conjunctivae/upper body are characteristic. Pulmonary thromboembolism can cause hypoxemia but does not produce a petechial rash or this triad and usually presents differently. Hospital-acquired pneumonia would show focal consolidation and a more gradual course, not abrupt petechiae and encephalopathy. Alcohol withdrawal can cause agitation but not hypoxemia with petechiae, and his history does not support heavy dependence; the triad points squarely to fat embolism syndrome.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Because fat embolism syndrome is a clinical diagnosis, what is the appropriate diagnostic approach at this point?
options: Make a clinical diagnosis using criteria while excluding mimics with supportive testing
Withhold treatment until a confirmatory blood test returns as the primary diagnosis
Rely solely on a normal chest X-ray to exclude the disease as the primary diagnosis
Require a brain biopsy to confirm fat globules as the primary diagnosis
stages.explanation: Fat embolism syndrome is a clinical diagnosis (e.g., using Gurd's criteria—respiratory insufficiency, cerebral involvement, petechial rash as major features) made by recognizing the characteristic pattern in the right context while using supportive tests to exclude mimics such as pulmonary embolism and sepsis. There is no single confirmatory blood test, so withholding supportive treatment to wait for one is wrong and dangerous. Brain biopsy is invasive, nonspecific, and never required. A normal or initially clear chest X-ray does not exclude the disease, since pulmonary findings (a 'snowstorm' pattern) may lag; relying on it alone would miss the diagnosis. The correct approach pairs pattern recognition with judicious testing to rule out alternatives.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: The team wants to exclude pulmonary thromboembolism, the main mimic, before settling on fat embolism syndrome. Which test best serves this purpose given his presentation?
options: Ventilation-perfusion (V/Q) scan as the first-line test
D-dimer to rule out the diagnosis in this setting
CT pulmonary angiography
Lower-extremity venous Doppler only in this setting
stages.explanation: CT pulmonary angiography is the best test to exclude pulmonary thromboembolism in this hemodynamically stable patient: it directly visualizes or excludes clot in the pulmonary arteries and simultaneously characterizes the lung parenchyma (here showing diffuse ground-glass infiltrates rather than a large thrombus), supporting fat embolism syndrome. Lower-extremity venous Doppler can detect DVT but a negative study does not rule out PE, so it is insufficient alone. A D-dimer is essentially always elevated in a recent trauma/postoperative patient, so it lacks specificity and cannot be used to 'rule out' PE in this context. A V/Q scan is reserved for patients who cannot receive iodinated contrast (e.g., severe renal failure or contrast allergy); CTPA is the preferred first-line confirmatory study here.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
ABG (on 6 L): pH 7.46, PaCO2 30 mmHg, PaO2 52 mmHg (P/F ratio ~130)
Hemoglobin: 9.6 g/dL (down from 12.0)
Platelets: 95 ×10³/µL (down from 240)
Chest X-ray: bilateral patchy alveolar 'snowstorm' infiltrates
CT pulmonary angiography: no large thrombus; diffuse ground-glass infiltrates
MRI brain: scattered punctate hyperintensities ('starfield' pattern)
Fat in urine: present
Procalcitonin: low; blood cultures pending and ultimately negative

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: His CTPA shows no large clot, and labs show anemia, thrombocytopenia, and a 'snowstorm' chest film. What is the mainstay of management for fat embolism syndrome?
options: Systemic anticoagulation with heparin infusion as initial therapy
Supportive care—oxygenation/ventilatory support
Systemic thrombolysis as initial therapy
High-dose corticosteroids as definitive cure as initial therapy
stages.explanation: Treatment of fat embolism syndrome is supportive: ensure adequate oxygenation and ventilation (supplemental oxygen, escalating to noninvasive or mechanical ventilation with lung-protective settings for ARDS-like hypoxemia) and hemodynamic support, while the syndrome runs its course. The greatest impact comes from prevention and early operative fixation/stabilization of the long-bone fracture, which reduces ongoing fat embolization. Therapeutic anticoagulation does not treat fat emboli and adds bleeding risk, especially with thrombocytopenia and recent trauma. Corticosteroids have been studied for prophylaxis with mixed data but are not a definitive cure and are not standard therapy for established disease. Thrombolysis is for thromboembolism, not fat emboli, and would be harmful in this trauma patient with low platelets.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: His family asks about prognosis and prevention. Which statement is most accurate regarding fat embolism syndrome?
options: It is uniformly fatal once the triad appears with appropriate follow-up
It can be prevented reliably with prophylactic anticoagulation based on current risk
It is usually self-limited with supportive care,
Recovery requires lifelong corticosteroids with appropriate follow-up
stages.explanation: Fat embolism syndrome is generally self-limited, and most patients recover with timely supportive care; the key preventive measure is early immobilization and operative fixation of long-bone fractures, which limits ongoing marrow-fat release and lowers incidence. It is not uniformly fatal—mortality is relatively low with good supportive ICU care, though severe ARDS or cerebral involvement can be serious. Prophylactic anticoagulation does not reliably prevent fat embolism because the emboli are fat, not thrombus; venous thromboembolism prophylaxis serves a different purpose. Lifelong corticosteroids are not required; steroids have only a debated prophylactic role and no place as chronic therapy. Reassurance with emphasis on supportive care and early fixation is the accurate counsel.

cases.diagnosis: Fat embolism syndrome following long-bone (femur) fracture
pearls: Classic triad: hypoxemia, neurologic changes, and petechiae appearing 24-72 hours after a long-bone (especially femur) fracture.
It is a clinical diagnosis (e.g., Gurd's criteria) made while excluding PE and sepsis; no single confirmatory test exists.
Supportive findings include thrombocytopenia, anemia, a 'snowstorm' chest X-ray, and a 'starfield' MRI brain pattern.
Management is supportive (oxygen/ventilation); early fracture fixation is the key preventive measure and the condition is usually self-limited.

cases.title: Collapse During Delivery
cases.chiefComplaint: Sudden cardiorespiratory collapse during active labor with profuse bleeding.
cases.history: A 32-year-old woman, gravida 3 para 2, at 39 weeks gestation, suffers sudden collapse during active labor. Moments after artificial rupture of membranes and amid strong contractions, she became acutely dyspneic, agitated, and cyanotic, then lost consciousness with profound hypotension. CPR was briefly initiated for a transient pulseless episode; spontaneous circulation returned and she was intubated and transferred to the ICU. Within minutes she began bleeding heavily from her IV sites and developed vaginal hemorrhage that would not clot. PMH: two prior uncomplicated vaginal deliveries; otherwise healthy. Medications: prenatal vitamins; oxytocin was running for augmentation. Allergies: none. Social: nonsmoker, no alcohol or drugs during pregnancy. Family history: noncontributory. The pregnancy had been low-risk with normal prenatal care. The obstetric and critical-care teams note the abrupt timing in relation to labor, the rapid cardiorespiratory collapse, and the immediate onset of coagulopathic bleeding. There was no preceding fever, trauma, or known amniotic fluid infection.
cases.exam: Intubated, sedated woman on vasopressors, critically ill. Diffuse oozing from venipuncture and IV insertion sites; continued vaginal bleeding with poorly clotting blood. Lungs with diffuse crackles; ventilation requires high FiO2 and PEEP. Heart tachycardic; weak peripheral pulses; cool, mottled extremities. Abdomen gravid (post-delivery uterus boggy). No focal neurologic findings obtainable under sedation. Skin shows petechiae and ecchymoses at pressure points. She is hemodynamically unstable despite fluids and pressors, with both severe hypoxemia and active coagulopathic hemorrhage.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Sudden cardiorespiratory collapse during labor immediately followed by disseminated coagulopathic bleeding most strongly suggests which diagnosis?
options: Eclampsia with hemorrhage
Amniotic fluid embolism
Anaphylaxis to oxytocin
Pulmonary thromboembolism
stages.explanation: Amniotic fluid embolism (AFE) is the classic cause of abrupt cardiorespiratory collapse during labor or immediately postpartum, followed rapidly by disseminated intravascular coagulation and hemorrhage. The triad of sudden hypoxemia, hemodynamic collapse/cardiac arrest, and coagulopathy occurring around membrane rupture or delivery is highly characteristic. Pulmonary thromboembolism can cause peripartum collapse but does not typically produce immediate fulminant DIC with diffuse bleeding. Eclampsia presents with hypertension, proteinuria, and seizures, not sudden hypoxemic collapse with DIC, and she was normotensive without preeclampsia. Anaphylaxis can cause shock and hypoxemia but is associated with urticaria/bronchospasm and does not characteristically trigger fulminant consumptive coagulopathy. The temporal link to labor plus collapse-then-DIC points to AFE.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: How is amniotic fluid embolism diagnosed in this acute setting?
options: Clinical diagnosis of exclusion based on the characteristic presentation, treated empirically while excluding alternatives
Definitive lung biopsy demonstrating fetal squames as the primary diagnosis
Confirmatory serum biomarker that must result before treatment as the primary diagnosis
Echocardiography finding that is pathognomonic as the primary diagnosis
stages.explanation: Amniotic fluid embolism is a clinical diagnosis of exclusion: it is recognized by the characteristic, abrupt peripartum presentation (sudden hypoxemia/cardiovascular collapse and DIC) and managed empirically and immediately while excluding other causes such as pulmonary embolism, hemorrhage, sepsis, and anaphylaxis. There is no validated confirmatory serum biomarker available in real time, so waiting for one would be fatal. No echocardiographic finding is pathognomonic, though echo may show acute right ventricular strain and support the picture and guide resuscitation. Detection of fetal squamous cells in maternal pulmonary circulation is neither sensitive nor specific and cannot be relied upon, and biopsy is not part of acute care. Treatment must proceed on clinical grounds without delay.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
ABG: pH 7.18, PaCO2 50 mmHg, PaO2 48 mmHg, lactate 7 mmol/L
Platelets: 45 ×10³/µL
Fibrinogen: 80 mg/dL
PT/INR: markedly prolonged; PTT prolonged
D-dimer: markedly elevated
Hemoglobin: 7.0 g/dL after hemorrhage
Bedside echocardiography: acute right ventricular dilation and dysfunction with leftward septal bowing
CXR: diffuse bilateral pulmonary infiltrates

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Labs confirm severe DIC and echo shows acute RV failure. What is the most appropriate management strategy?
options: Immediate high-quality resuscitation: airway/oxygenation, hemodynamic
Therapeutic anticoagulation to treat the embolism as initial therapy
Systemic thrombolysis for presumed massive PE as initial therapy
Withhold blood products until coagulation studies normalize as initial therapy
stages.explanation: Management of amniotic fluid embolism is immediate, aggressive supportive resuscitation: secure the airway and oxygenation, support the failing right ventricle and hemodynamics (vasopressors, inotropes, pulmonary vasodilators, and consideration of ECMO in refractory cases), and treat DIC aggressively with massive transfusion—packed red cells, fresh frozen plasma, cryoprecipitate for fibrinogen, and platelets—often with obstetric hemorrhage measures (uterotonics, possibly hysterectomy). If the fetus is undelivered, prompt delivery improves maternal resuscitation and fetal outcome. Systemic thrombolysis and therapeutic anticoagulation are contraindicated in a patient with fulminant DIC and active hemorrhage and would worsen bleeding; AFE is not a thrombotic embolus. Withholding blood products while awaiting normalized labs would be fatal—replacement must be empiric and immediate.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: Her low fibrinogen of 80 mg/dL and diffuse oozing are critical. Which blood product most directly corrects the hypofibrinogenemia driving her bleeding?
options: Albumin 25% as the leading concern
Normal saline boluses
Cryoprecipitate
Packed red blood cells
stages.explanation: Cryoprecipitate (or fibrinogen concentrate) is the most concentrated source of fibrinogen and directly corrects the hypofibrinogenemia (fibrinogen 80 mg/dL; target generally >150-200 mg/dL in obstetric hemorrhage) that is fueling her coagulopathic bleeding in AFE-associated DIC. Fresh frozen plasma also replaces fibrinogen and other clotting factors but is less concentrated, so cryoprecipitate is preferred when fibrinogen is critically low and volume must be limited. Packed red blood cells restore oxygen-carrying capacity and treat anemia but contain essentially no fibrinogen, so they will not correct the clotting defect. Normal saline and albumin are volume expanders that contain no coagulation factors and, given in excess, cause dilutional coagulopathy that worsens the bleeding.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: Her family asks about her risk and outcomes. Which statement about amniotic fluid embolism is most accurate?
options: It is rare, unpredictable,
It rarely causes coagulopathy with appropriate follow-up
It is highly predictable and preventable with screening with appropriate follow-up
It is reliably prevented by anticoagulation in labor with appropriate follow-up
stages.explanation: Amniotic fluid embolism is a rare but catastrophic, unpredictable obstetric emergency with high maternal mortality and a substantial risk of long-term neurologic injury in survivors (from the period of hypoxemia and arrest), even with prompt, optimal multidisciplinary care. It cannot be predicted or prevented by screening because it is not caused by an identifiable, modifiable risk factor amenable to testing. Coagulopathy/DIC is in fact a hallmark feature, present in the great majority of cases, so the claim that it rarely causes coagulopathy is incorrect. Anticoagulation does not prevent AFE and would be dangerous given the associated DIC and hemorrhage. Honest counseling acknowledges the severity, unpredictability, and the importance of rapid resuscitation and supportive care.

cases.diagnosis: Amniotic fluid embolism with cardiorespiratory collapse and disseminated intravascular coagulation
pearls: Amniotic fluid embolism presents with abrupt peripartum hypoxemia, cardiovascular collapse, and DIC—often around membrane rupture or delivery.
It is a clinical diagnosis of exclusion; there is no confirmatory real-time test, and treatment must not be delayed.
Management is aggressive supportive resuscitation: oxygenation, RV/hemodynamic support (consider ECMO), massive transfusion for DIC, and prompt delivery of the fetus.
It is rare, unpredictable, and carries high maternal mortality and risk of neurologic injury; thrombolysis and anticoagulation are contraindicated due to DIC.

cases.title: Breathlessness and Frothy Sputum on a Climb
cases.chiefComplaint: Worsening shortness of breath, cough, and pink frothy sputum that began two days into a mountain expedition.
cases.history: A 27-year-old recreational mountaineer presents to a base-camp clinic at 4,200 m. He flew from sea level three days ago and ascended rapidly without acclimatization days.
